name: COMPUTATIONAL FLUID DYNAMICS IN MARINE TECHNOLOGY

topics.title: Introduction to Computational Fluid Dynamics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST635 - COMPUTATIONAL FLUID DYNAMICS IN MARINE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Computational Fluid Dynamics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD)** is a branch of fluid mechanics that uses numerical analysis and data structures to solve and analyze problems that involve fluid flows. It employs high-speed computing, numerical methods, and multi-dimensional algorithms to solve the governing equations of fluid dynamics (continuity, momentum, and energy equations) over discretized domains of space and time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** CFD is the science of determining a numerical solution to the governing equations of fluid flow while advancing the solution through space and time to obtain a final, time-dependent or steady-state description of the complete flow field.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you want to understand how water flows through a complex pipe network in a ship's engine cooling system. Experimentally, you would need to build the system, inject dye or sensors, and physically measure velocities and pressures at various points — expensive, time-consuming, and sometimes physically impossible (e.g., flows at the bow of a moving ship).

**CFD replaces the physical laboratory with a virtual one.** The fluid domain is divided into millions of tiny boxes (a mesh/grid). For each tiny box, the computer solves the physics equations (Newton's second law for fluids), and by stitching together all these tiny solutions, the complete flow picture emerges — just like assembling millions of pixels to form a photograph.

> [!NOTE]
> Think of CFD as solving a giant, structured Sudoku puzzle where each cell must obey the laws of conservation of mass, momentum, and energy simultaneously. The computer iteratively fills in values (velocity, pressure, temperature) until all cells "agree" with their neighbors.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

In CFD analysis, the following dimensionless groups and constants are routinely employed:

- **Reynolds Number ($Re$)** — ratio of inertial to viscous forces
- **Mach Number ($Ma$)** — ratio of flow velocity to speed of sound
- **Froude Number ($Fr$)** — critical in marine/ship hydrodynamics
- **Prandtl Number ($Pr$)** — ratio of momentum to thermal diffusivity
- **Gas Constant ($R$)** = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- **Universal Gas Constant ($R_u$)** = **8.314 kJ/(kmol·K)**

> [!NOTE]
> For marine applications, the **Froude Number ($Fr = V/\sqrt{gL}$)** is paramount, as it governs wave-making resistance and free-surface phenomena around hulls.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Fluid Flow Analysis

Historically, fluid mechanics problems have been attacked by three methodologies. CFD forms the third pillar, complementing the other two:

1. **Theoretical / Analytical Methods** — Pure mathematics; only possible for highly simplified geometries (e.g., flow between parallel plates).
2. **Experimental Methods** — Wind tunnels, towing tanks, PIV (Particle Image Velocimetry); highly accurate but costly and limited to model scales.
3. **Numerical / CFD Methods** — Discretized solution on computers; handles complex real-world geometries.

### 2.2 The General CFD Solution Procedure

The solution of any CFD problem follows a standard, well-defined workflow:

- **Step 1 — Problem Definition:** Identify the physics (turbulence, heat transfer, multiphase), geometry, and required outputs (drag, lift, pressure drop).
- **Step 2 — Mathematical Modeling:** Write down the governing PDEs (Navier-Stokes, continuity, energy) along with appropriate initial and boundary conditions.
- **Step 3 — Pre-processing (Geometry & Mesh Generation):** Create the CAD geometry, clean it, and discretize the domain into a finite number of cells (mesh). Mesh quality directly governs accuracy.
- **Step 4 — Solver Setup:** Choose discretization scheme (FVM, FDM, FEM), turbulence model (k-ε, k-ω, LES), fluid properties, boundary conditions, and convergence criteria.
- **Step 5 — Solver Execution:** The computer iteratively solves the discretized algebraic equations (often millions of equations simultaneously).
- **Step 6 — Post-processing:** Visualize the results using streamlines, contours, vectors, and iso-surfaces. Extract quantitative data.
- **Step 7 — Validation & Verification:** Compare with experimental data or analytical solutions.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table summarizes all essential equations that govern CFD simulations. These will appear (in some form) in every module of this course.

| **Equation Name** | **Governing Formula** | **Physical Meaning** |
|---|---|---|
| Conservation of Mass (Continuity) | $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$ | Mass is neither created nor destroyed |
| Conservation of Momentum (Navier–Stokes) | $\rho \left( \dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + \vec{V} \cdot \nabla \vec{V} \right) = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^{2} \vec{V} + \rho \vec{g}$ | Newton's 2nd Law for fluids |
| Conservation of Energy | $\rho c_p \left( \dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t} + \vec{V} \cdot \nabla T \right) = k \nabla^{2} T + \Phi$ | First Law of Thermodynamics for fluids |
| Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu}$ | Inertial vs. viscous forces |
| Froude Number | $Fr = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{gL}}$ | Inertial vs. gravitational forces |
| Mach Number | $Ma = \dfrac{V}{c} = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{\gamma R T}}$ | Flow compressibility indicator |
| Generic Transport Equation | $\dfrac{\partial (\rho \phi)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V} \phi) = \nabla \cdot (\Gamma \nabla \phi) + S_\phi$ | Universal scalar transport form |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Generic Transport Equation** is the heart of CFD. Almost every physical quantity $\phi$ (velocity component, temperature, turbulence kinetic energy, species concentration) can be cast into this form by properly defining the diffusion coefficient $\Gamma$ and source term $S_\phi$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In marine technology, CFD is indispensable for:

- **Hull form optimization** for reduced drag and improved fuel efficiency.
- **Propeller analysis** (open water characteristics, cavitation prediction).
- **Resistance estimation** for ships and submarines.
- **Seakeeping analysis** in regular and irregular waves.
- **Slipstream analysis** behind superstructures for helicopter landing pads on ships.
- **Ventilation and HVAC** systems in engine rooms.
- **Sloshing analysis** in LNG/LPG tanks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From the Generic Transport Equation to the Discretized Algebraic Form

The transition from a continuous Partial Differential Equation (PDE) to a discrete algebraic equation is the essence of CFD. We derive the finite-volume discretization of the generic scalar transport equation.

**Starting Point — The Integral Form of the Transport Equation:**

For any arbitrary control volume $CV$ with surface area $S$, the conservation law reads:

$$\int_{CV} \frac{\partial (\rho \phi)}{\partial t} \, dV + \int_{CS} \rho \phi (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = \int_{CS} \Gamma (\nabla \phi \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA + \int_{CV} S_\phi \, dV$$

**Step 1 — Splitting into Steady-State and Transient Terms:**

Assuming the flow is steady (no time variation), the first term vanishes:

$$\int_{CS} \rho \phi (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = \int_{CS} \Gamma (\nabla \phi \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA + \int_{CV} S_\phi \, dV$$

**Step 2 — Linearization of the Convection Term:**

To handle the non-linear convection term, we introduce a linearization constant $a_P$:

$$\phi = a_P \sum a_{nb} \phi_{nb} + b$$

**Step 3 — Discretization over a Control Volume:**

For each cell $P$ surrounded by neighbors $nb$ (typically 4 in 2D, 6 in 3D), we obtain the canonical algebraic form:

$$a_P \phi_P = \sum_{nb} a_{nb} \phi_{nb} + b$$

Where:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_P &= \sum a_{nb} + (F_P - D_P) \\
a_{nb} &= D_{nb} + \max(-F_{nb}, 0) \\
b &= S_C \Delta V
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Identifying the Four Fundamental Coefficients:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \quad \text{(Convection mass flux)} \\
D &= \frac{\Gamma}{\delta x} \quad \text{(Diffusion conductance)} \\
P_e &= \frac{F}{D} = \frac{\rho u \delta x}{\Gamma} \quad \text{(Peclet Number)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Defining Coefficients Using Central Difference Scheme:**

For the faces ($f = w, e, n, s$) of cell $P$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_w &= \frac{\Gamma_w}{\delta x_{wP}}, \quad D_e = \frac{\Gamma_e}{\delta x_{Pe}} \\
F_w &= (\rho u)_w, \quad F_e = (\rho u)_e
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Final Discretized Form Assembled:**

$$a_P \phi_P = a_W \phi_W + a_E \phi_E + a_N \phi_N + a_S \phi_S + b$$

**Where:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_W &= D_w + \max(F_w, 0) \\
a_E &= D_e + \max(-F_e, 0) \\
a_N &= D_n + \max(F_n, 0) \\
a_S &= D_s + \max(-F_s, 0) \\
a_P &= a_W + a_E + a_N + a_S + (F_e - F_w) + (F_n - F_s) - S_P \Delta V
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation demonstrates the **Central Differencing Scheme (CDS)**. It is second-order accurate but produces unphysical oscillations when $|P_e| > 2$. Therefore, in real CFD codes, the **Upwind Scheme** or **Hybrid Scheme** is typically employed to maintain boundedness.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Discretization Schemes Comparison

| **Scheme** | **Order of Accuracy** | **Boundedness** | **Computational Cost** | **Typical Application** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| First-Order Upwind | 1st order | Bounded | Low | Initial iterations, coarse meshes |
| Central Differencing | 2nd order | Unbounded | Low | Low $P_e$ flows only |
| Hybrid (CDS + Upwind) | 2nd order | Bounded | Moderate | General-purpose industrial CFD |
| Second-Order Upwind | 2nd order | Bounded | Moderate | Production CFD runs |
| QUICK | 3rd order | Bounded | Moderate-High | High-quality aerospace/marine |
| Power-Law | 2nd order | Bounded | Low | Legacy codes (e.g., early Fluent) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Standard CFD Solution Workflow (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["PROBLEM DEFINITION"] --> B["PHYSICS SELECTION"]
    B --> C["GEOMETRY CREATION CAD"]
    C --> D["MESH GENERATION"]
    D --> E["BOUNDARY CONDITIONS"]
    E --> F["SOLVER SETUP"]
    F --> G["RUN ITERATIONS"]
    G --> H{"CONVERGED?"}
    H -- NO --> I["ADJUST MESH OR SCHEME"]
    I --> F
    H -- YES --> J["POST PROCESSING"]
    J --> K["VALIDATION WITH EXPERIMENT"]
    K --> L{"VALIDATED?"}
    L -- NO --> M["REVIEW PHYSICS MODELS"]
    M --> B
    L -- YES --> N["FINAL DESIGN DECISION"]

    style A fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style F fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style H fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style L fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style N fill:#d62728,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

### 4.2 Discretization Hierarchy — From PDE to Algebraic Equation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["CONTINUOUS PDE GOVERNING EQUATION"] --> B["SPATIAL DOMAIN DISCRETIZATION"]
    B --> C["MESH GRID GENERATION CELLS NODES"]
    C --> D["INTEGRAL CONSERVATION OVER EACH CV"]
    D --> E["APPROXIMATION OF FLUXES AT CELL FACES"]
    E --> F["LINEARIZATION OF NONLINEAR TERMS"]
    F --> G["SYSTEM OF ALGEBRAIC EQUATIONS A PHI B"]
    G --> H["ITERATIVE SOLVER PRESSURE VELOCITY COUPLING"]
    H --> I["CONVERGED SOLUTION FIELD"]

    style A fill:#9467bd,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style G fill:#8c564b,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style I fill:#e377c2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

### 4.3 The Three Solution Methodologies for Fluid Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["FLUID FLOW PROBLEM"] --> B["METHOD 1 EXPERIMENTAL"]
    A --> C["METHOD 2 ANALYTICAL"]
    A --> D["METHOD 3 NUMERICAL CFD"]
    B --> E["HIGH ACCURACY HIGH COST MODEL SCALE LIMITED"]
    C --> F["EXACT BUT ONLY SIMPLE GEOMETRIES"]
    D --> G["FLEXIBLE COMPLEX GEOMETRIES ECONOMICAL"]
    E --> H["DESIGN VALIDATION"]
    F --> H
    G --> H

    style B fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style C fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style D fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style H fill:#d62728,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

### 4.4 Generic Transport Equation — Schematic Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["GENERIC TRANSPORT EQUATION"] --> Q["UNSTEADY TERM"]
    P --> R["CONVECTION TERM"]
    P --> S["DIFFUSION TERM"]
    P --> T["SOURCE TERM"]
    Q --> U["RATE OF CHANGE WITH TIME"]
    R --> V["BULK TRANSPORT BY FLOW"]
    S --> W["SPREADING BY GRADIENTS"]
    T --> X["GENERATION OR DESTRUCTION"]

    style P fill:#9467bd,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style Q fill:#17becf,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style R fill:#17becf,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style S fill:#17becf,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style T fill:#17becf,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]:**
*Define Computational Fluid Dynamics. List any two applications of CFD in marine technology.*

**Model Answer:**
Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) is the science of using numerical methods and algorithms to solve the governing equations of fluid motion (continuity, momentum, and energy) on a discretized spatial domain, typically employing digital computers.

**Two applications in marine technology:**
1. **Hull form optimization** — Predicting the resistance and wave pattern of a ship hull to minimize fuel consumption.
2. **Propeller hydrodynamic analysis** — Predicting open-water efficiency, thrust, and cavitation characteristics of marine propellers.

*(Marks Distribution: Definition — 1 mark; Two applications — 2 marks)*

---

**Question 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]:**
*State the general form of the scalar transport equation and identify the physical meaning of each term.*

**Model Answer:**
The general scalar transport equation is:

$$\frac{\partial (\rho \phi)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V} \phi) = \nabla \cdot (\Gamma \nabla \phi) + S_\phi$$

**Physical meaning of each term:**
- **$\dfrac{\partial (\rho \phi)}{\partial t}$** — Transient (unsteady) term: rate of accumulation of $\phi$ with time.
- **$\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V} \phi)$** — Convection term: bulk transport of $\phi$ by the fluid motion.
- **$\nabla \cdot (\Gamma \nabla \phi)$** — Diffusion term: transport of $\phi$ due to its gradient, where $\Gamma$ is the diffusion coefficient.
- **$S_\phi$** — Source term: net rate of generation (positive) or destruction (negative) of $\phi$ per unit volume.

*(Marks Distribution: Equation statement — 1 mark; Each term identification — 0.5 marks × 4 = 2 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. Students frequently **omit the units** when listing fundamental dimensions in the formula sheet. Always state units explicitly (e.g., $\mu$ in Pa·s, $k$ in W/m·K).
> 2. Students often **forget to mention the boundary conditions** required to close the Navier–Stokes equations. Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for this omission.
> 3. Do not write only the final discretized form. Examiners allocate **3–4 marks** specifically for showing the intermediate integral form and the step-wise flux approximation.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive the general scalar transport equation from first principles. Explain the significance of each term with reference to marine engineering applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain in detail the step-by-step procedure for solving a fluid flow problem using CFD, with a neat block diagram. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Derivation of the Scalar Transport Equation:**

**Step 1 — Consider an Arbitrary Control Volume (CV):**
Let $\phi$ represent any intensive fluid property (velocity component, temperature, concentration, etc.) per unit mass. The total quantity of $\phi$ within the CV is:

$$\int_{CV} \rho \phi \, dV$$

**Step 2 — Rate of Change:**
The unsteady (transient) rate of change of $\phi$ within the CV:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{CV} \rho \phi \, dV = \int_{CV} \frac{\partial (\rho \phi)}{\partial t} \, dV$$

**Step 3 — Net Convective Flux Out:**
By Reynolds Transport Theorem, the net convective outflow across control surface $CS$:

$$\int_{CS} \rho \phi (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA$$

**Step 4 — Net Diffusive Flux Out:**
The diffusive transport across the control surface (driven by gradient $\nabla \phi$):

$$\int_{CS} \Gamma (\nabla \phi \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA$$

**Step 5 — Source/Sink:**
Internal generation (or destruction) within the CV:

$$\int_{CV} S_\phi \, dV$$

**Step 6 — Conservation Statement:**
Rate of accumulation = Net inflow by convection + Net inflow by diffusion + Source

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{CV} \frac{\partial (\rho \phi)}{\partial t} \, dV + \int_{CS} \rho \phi (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = \int_{CS} \Gamma (\nabla \phi \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA + \int_{CV} S_\phi \, dV
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Differential Form via Divergence Theorem:**

$$\frac{\partial (\rho \phi)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V} \phi) = \nabla \cdot (\Gamma \nabla \phi) + S_\phi$$

**Marine Engineering Significance:**
By choosing $\phi$ appropriately, this equation models: velocity components (momentum), temperature (engine room thermal analysis), salinity (marine environment), turbulence quantities, and pollutant dispersion around ships.

*(Marks: Derivation steps — 5 marks; Significance discussion — 2 marks)*

---

**(b) Step-by-Step CFD Procedure:**

The complete procedure for solving a fluid flow problem using CFD:

**Step 1 — Problem Definition:** Identify the physical phenomenon (e.g., drag on a ship hull), geometry, fluid properties, and required outputs.

**Step 2 — Mathematical Modeling:** Identify the governing equations (continuity, Navier–Stokes, energy) and write them in the appropriate form. Specify boundary conditions (inlet velocity, outlet pressure, no-slip walls).

**Step 3 — Geometry Creation:** Construct or import the CAD geometry. Simplify by removing unnecessary details that would inflate mesh count.

**Step 4 — Mesh Generation (Pre-processing):** Subdivide the fluid domain into a finite number of control volumes (cells). Refine the mesh near walls and regions of high gradient (boundary layers).

**Step 5 — Solver Setup:** Select the discretization scheme (upwind, QUICK, etc.), turbulence model ($k$-$\varepsilon$, $k$-$\omega$, LES), fluid properties ($\rho$, $\mu$, $k$), and convergence criteria.

**Step 6 — Solver Execution:** Run iterations until residuals drop below the specified tolerance (typically $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-6}$).

**Step 7 — Post-processing:** Visualize the result using streamlines, velocity contours, pressure plots, and surface plots.

**Step 8 — Validation:** Compare CFD results with experimental data (e.g., towing tank tests) or analytical solutions.

**Block Diagram:**

```
PROBLEM → MATH MODEL → GEOMETRY → MESH → SOLVER SETUP
   ↑                                              ↓
   └──── VALIDATION ← POST-PROCESSING ← ITERATION ←┘
```

*(Marks: Each step — 0.75 marks; Block diagram — 1 mark)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain in detail the three methodologies for solving fluid flow problems. Compare their advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat schematic, explain the components of the CFD workflow. Discuss the role of pre-processing, solver, and post-processing. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Three Methodologies:**

**1. Experimental (Empirical) Methods:**
- **Approach:** Build a scaled physical model, instrument it with sensors (pressure transducers, Pitot tubes, PIV), and measure flow quantities.
- **Tools:** Wind tunnels, towing tanks, cavitation tunnels.
- **Advantages:** Highly accurate; captures real physics including transition, separation, and 3D effects.
- **Limitations:** Extremely expensive (tank time costs ₹50,000–₹2,00,000 per day); limited to model scale (Reynolds number scaling issues); cannot measure inside flows.

**2. Analytical (Theoretical) Methods:**
- **Approach:** Solve the governing PDEs exactly using mathematical techniques (separation of variables, similarity solutions).
- **Advantages:** Provides exact solutions; useful for benchmarking; reveals underlying physics.
- **Limitations:** Restricted to highly idealized geometries (flat plates, pipes, simple channels); cannot handle real ship hulls.

**3. Numerical (CFD) Methods:**
- **Approach:** Discretize the PDEs on a computational mesh and solve the resulting algebraic system iteratively.
- **Advantages:** Handles complex geometries; relatively economical; provides complete flow field; parametric studies easy.
- **Limitations:** Approximate solutions; accuracy depends on mesh quality and turbulence model; requires significant validation.

*(Marks: Each method — 2 marks; Comparison — 1 mark)*

---

**(b) CFD Workflow Components:**

**Pre-processing:**
- Geometry import and cleanup.
- Surface and volume meshing (structured, unstructured, hybrid).
- Boundary layer meshing ($y^+$ target).
- Defining boundary conditions, fluid properties, and solver parameters.

**Solver:**
- Discretization of governing equations using FDM/FVM/FEM.
- Linearization and solution of algebraic system.
- Pressure-velocity coupling algorithms (SIMPLE, SIMPLEC, PISO).
- Under-relaxation for stability.
- Convergence monitoring via residuals.

**Post-processing:**
- Generation of contour plots, vector plots, streamlines, iso-surfaces.
- Quantitative data extraction (forces, moments, mass flow rates).
- Animation of transient results.
- Report generation and validation against experiments.

**Schematic (already detailed in Section 4.1):**
The block diagram in Section 4.1 illustrates the iterative loop: Mesh → Setup → Solve → Check Convergence → Post-process → Validate → Refine.

*(Marks: Pre-processing — 2 marks; Solver — 2.5 marks; Post-processing — 1.5 marks; Schematic discussion — 1 mark)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist — Module 1: Introduction to CFD**

- **CFD Definition:** Numerical solution of fluid flow governing equations on a discretized domain.
- **Three Methodologies:** Experimental (accurate, costly), Analytical (exact, limited), Numerical/CFD (flexible, economical).
- **Generic Transport Equation:** $\dfrac{\partial (\rho \phi)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V} \phi) = \nabla \cdot (\Gamma \nabla \phi) + S_\phi$ — *universal scalar conservation form*.
- **Four Key Terms:** Unsteady, Convection, Diffusion, Source — *memorize their physical meaning*.
- **Governing Equations:** Continuity, Navier–Stokes (momentum), Energy — *all derivable from the generic transport equation*.
- **Reynolds Number ($Re$):** Inertia vs. viscosity — governs laminar/turbulent transition.
- **Froude Number ($Fr$):** Inertia vs. gravity — dominant in marine free-surface flows.
- **CFD Workflow:** Problem → Geometry → Mesh → Solver → Post-process → Validation.
- **Marine Applications:** Hull resistance, propeller analysis, seakeeping, sloshing, ventilation.
- **Discretization Schemes:** First-order Upwind (bounded), CDS (accurate but oscillatory), QUICK (high accuracy).
- **Finite-Volume Discretized Form:** $a_P \phi_P = \sum a_{nb} \phi_{nb} + b$ — *this is the algebraic form actually solved by the computer*.
- **Peclet Number ($P_e$):** Controls scheme selection; if $P_e > 2$, central differencing becomes unstable.
- **Common Mistakes to Avoid:** Forgetting boundary conditions, omitting units, neglecting mesh independence study, skipping validation step.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CFD Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST635 - COMPUTATIONAL FLUID DYNAMICS IN MARINE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** CFD Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CFD Applications in Marine Technology** refers to the systematic use of numerical methods, turbulence models, and discretization schemes to solve the **Navier–Stokes equations** (mass, momentum, and energy conservation) for predicting fluid-flow phenomena in marine environments — encompassing ship hydrodynamics, propeller analysis, offshore structures, wave–structure interaction, cavitation, and ocean engineering systems.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)** framework, CFD applications are classified under **Course Outcome CO1** of PENST635, which mandates that students *"describe and apply the governing equations and numerical methods to solve marine engineering fluid-flow problems."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are a naval architect designing a new container ship. Before cutting steel, you want to know: *How much fuel will this ship burn at 20 knots? Will the propeller cavitate? Will the bow slam into waves during storms?*

Twenty years ago, you would build a **towing-tank model** (a 3-meter-long miniature ship) and measure forces in a laboratory. This is expensive, slow, and limited to a few operating conditions.

> [!NOTE]
> **The CFD Analogy:** CFD is like building a *virtual wave tank* inside a high-performance computer. We divide the ocean around the ship into millions of tiny boxes (a "mesh"), assign physical properties (pressure, velocity, density) to each box, and let the computer solve the laws of physics iteratively. Just as a weather forecast predicts tomorrow's rain, CFD predicts tomorrow's *wave-induced hull pressure*.

The **three pillars** of marine CFD applications are:

1. **Virtual Towing Tank** — replaces experimental resistance tests.
2. **Virtual Propeller Open Water** — replaces cavitation tunnel tests.
3. **Virtual Seakeeping Basin** — replaces maneuvering and wave-exposure tests.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The following **standard marine CFD metrics** must be committed to memory:
> - **Reynolds Number (Re):** $\mathbf{Re = \frac{\rho U L}{\mu}}$ — where $L$ is the **ship length between perpendiculars (LPP)**.
> - **Froude Number (Fr):** $\mathbf{Fr = \frac{U}{\sqrt{gL}}}$ — critical for **wave-resistance** and **free-surface** simulations.
> - **Weber Number (We):** $\mathbf{We = \frac{\rho U^2 L}{\sigma}}$ — governs **cavitation inception**.
> - **Cavitation Number (σ):** $\mathbf{\sigma = \frac{p_{\infty} - p_v}{\frac{1}{2}\rho U^2}}$ — the standard non-dimensional cavitation metric.
> - **Block Coefficient ($C_B$):** $\mathbf{C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T}}$ — hull fullness indicator.
> - **Advance Coefficient (J):** $\mathbf{J = \frac{V_A}{nD}}$ — propeller operating point.
> - **Density of seawater:** $\mathbf{\rho \approx 1025\ kg/m^3}$ (vs. fresh water $1000\ kg/m^3$).
> - **Kinematic viscosity of seawater:** $\mathbf{\nu \approx 1.05 \times 10^{-6}\ m^2/s}$.

## 1.4 Why Marine CFD is Distinct from General CFD

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Marine CFD is **not** a generic flow solver. It is characterized by:
> - **High Reynolds number** flows ($Re \sim 10^7$ to $10^9$) → mandatory **turbulence modeling** (RANS/LES/DES).
> - **Free-surface (two-phase) flows** with air–water interface → VOF (Volume of Fluid) method.
> - **Moving reference frames** for rotating propellers (MRF, sliding mesh).
> - **Wave generation and absorption** zones (relaxation zones, beach method).
> - **Wall-resolved vs. wall-modeled LES** trade-offs at full-scale Reynolds numbers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Workflow of Marine CFD (The "Six-Stage Pipeline")

A typical marine CFD analysis — whether ship resistance, propeller thrust, or wave load — follows this rigorous KTU-aligned workflow:

1. **Geometry Definition & Clean-Up**
   - Import hull lines (IGES, STEP) from lines plan.
   - Remove appendages not relevant to the study (e.g., bilge keels in calm-water resistance test).
   - Close all openings to ensure watertight volume.

2. **Computational Domain Definition**
   - **Upstream extent:** ≥ 1 ship length (L) ahead of the bow.
   - **Downstream extent:** ≥ 2 to 3 L behind the stern.
   - **Lateral extent:** ≥ 1 L to either side.
   - **Vertical extent (bottom):** ≥ 0.5 L below the keel.
   - **Vertical extent (top):** ≥ 0.5 L above the free surface.

3. **Meshing Strategy (Discretization)**
   - Use a **prism boundary layer** with $y^+ \approx 30$ to $50$ for standard $k$-$\omega$ SST.
   - **Refinement zones** at the bow, stern, and transom to capture curvature and wave breaking.
   - Cell count benchmark: $5 \times 10^6$ to $50 \times 10^6$ cells for a full ship hull.

4. **Physics Setup**
   - **Solver type:** Pressure-based (segregated) for incompressible, density-based for transonic cavitating flows.
   - **Turbulence model:** $k$-$\omega$ SST (industry default), $k$-$\varepsilon$ Realizable, or LES/DES for unsteady flows.
   - **Free surface:** VOF with HRIC or Compressive sharpening.
   - **Boundary conditions:** Velocity inlet, pressure outlet, no-slip hull, symmetry on lateral planes.

5. **Solution & Monitoring**
   - Initialize with a **potential-flow solution** for free-surface problems.
   - Monitor **residual drop** (target $10^{-6}$ for continuity) and key force coefficients ($C_T$, $C_F$, $C_P$).

6. **Post-Processing & Validation**
   - Extract resistance, sinkage, trim, wave elevation, and pressure contours.
   - Compare with **ITTC 1978 / ITTC 2017** standard extrapolation methods.
   - **Grid Convergence Index (GCI)** analysis to quantify numerical uncertainty.

## 2.2 Classification of CFD Applications in Marine Technology

Marine CFD applications can be categorized by the **physical phenomenon** and the **engineering deliverable**:

| **Application Domain** | **Engineering Deliverable** | **Typical Solver / Model** | **Key Output Metric** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hull Resistance (calm water) | Fuel consumption prediction | RANS, $k$-$\omega$ SST, VOF | Total resistance coefficient $C_T$ |
| Propeller Open Water | Thrust, torque, efficiency | MRF / Sliding Mesh, RANS | $K_T$, $K_Q$, $\eta_0$ |
| Cavitation Analysis | Sheet/Cloud/Vortex cavitation zones | RANS + Schnerr–Sauer / Zwart cavitation model | Cavitation extent, $\sigma$ distribution |
| Seakeeping & Wave Loads | Added resistance, RAO | RANS + VOF + overset mesh, or potential-flow coupling | Heave/pitch RAO, $R_{AW}$ |
| Maneuvering (PMM, captive) | Hydrodynamic derivatives $Y_v$, $N_r$ | RANS with rotating-propeller and rudder deflections | Yaw moment, sway force |
| Slamming & Green Water | Local impulse pressure | RANS + VOF + adaptive remeshing, or SPH | Pressure impulse, deck wetness |
| Offshore Structures (Spar, Semi) | Vortex-Induced Vibration (VIV) | DES / LES + structural coupling | Lift/drag, Strouhal number $St$ |
| Mooring & Riser | Drag, added mass | RANS / Potential flow with Morison equation coupling | Riser tension, top angle |
| Internal Flows (seawater piping, pumps) | Head loss, NPSH | RANS with rotating reference frame | Pressure drop, NPSH available |
| Renewable Marine (tidal, wave) | Power extraction efficiency | RANS / LES with actuator disk or FSI | Power coefficient $C_P$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly emphasizes **resistance, propeller, and seakeeping** as the **tier-1** applications. Cavitation, offshore, and renewable energy are **tier-2** topics.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every equation you must master for the KTU ESE on this topic.

| **Symbol / Quantity** | **Formula** | **Physical Meaning** | **Typical Range (Marine)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reynolds Number | $\displaystyle \mathrm{Re} = \frac{\rho U L}{\mu}$ | Viscous vs. inertial ratio | $10^{7}$ to $10^{9}$ |
| Froude Number | $\displaystyle \mathrm{Fr} = \frac{U}{\sqrt{gL}}$ | Gravity vs. inertial ratio | $0.10$ to $0.40$ |
| Total Resistance Coefficient | $\displaystyle C_T = \frac{R_T}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho S U^2}$ | Non-dimensional resistance | $2 \times 10^{-3}$ to $6 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| Frictional Resistance (ITTC'57) | $\displaystyle C_F = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10}\mathrm{Re} - 2)^{2}}$ | Friction line reference | — |
| Form Factor (Prohaska) | $\displaystyle (1+k) = \frac{C_{T,\text{model}}}{C_{F,\text{model}}}$ | Hull-form viscous pressure resistance | $1.0$ to $1.4$ |
| Effective Power | $\displaystyle P_E = R_T \cdot U$ | Useful work to overcome resistance | kW to MW |
| Advance Coefficient | $\displaystyle J = \frac{V_A}{nD}$ | Propeller operating point | $0.4$ to $0.9$ |
| Thrust Coefficient | $\displaystyle K_T = \frac{T}{\rho n^2 D^4}$ | Non-dimensional thrust | $0.1$ to $0.5$ |
| Torque Coefficient | $\displaystyle K_Q = \frac{Q}{\rho n^2 D^5}$ | Non-dimensional torque | $0.01$ to $0.05$ |
| Open-Water Efficiency | $\displaystyle \eta_0 = \frac{J}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{K_T}{K_Q}$ | Propeller hydrodynamic efficiency | $0.5$ to $0.75$ |
| Cavitation Number | $\displaystyle \sigma = \frac{p_\infty - p_v}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^2}$ | Cavitation inception criterion | $0.2$ to $3.0$ |
| Strouhal Number | $\displaystyle \mathrm{St} = \frac{fD}{U}$ | VIV shedding frequency | $0.15$ to $0.30$ for circular cylinder |
| Grid Convergence Index | $\displaystyle \mathrm{GCI} = 1.25 \cdot \frac{\vert \varepsilon \vert}{r^p - 1}$ | Numerical uncertainty estimate | Target: $< 2\%$ for production runs |

**Note on $y^+$:** For wall-resolved LES, $y^+ \le 1$; for wall-modeled LES, $y^+ \approx 1$–$5$; for RANS with low-Re models, $y^+ \le 1$; for standard RANS ($k$-$\omega$ SST), $y^+ \approx 30$–$50$.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Marine Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> The **utility of marine CFD** is not academic — it is **mission-critical** for the modern shipyard:
> - **Class-approval workflows** (DNV, LR, ABS, BV) increasingly accept CFD results in lieu of (or as supplements to) towing-tank tests.
> - **CFD-driven hull form optimization** using adjoint solvers can deliver **5–15%** fuel savings — translating to **millions of dollars** per vessel over its life.
> - **Time-to-market reduction:** A 30-day CFD campaign replaces a 6-month towing-tank model build, instrument, and test cycle.
> - **Green shipping compliance:** IMO 2020, IMO 2030, and the **CII (Carbon Intensity Indicator)** regulations demand validated CFD for EEDI/EEXI certification.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From Navier–Stokes to the RANS Equations (Foundation of Marine CFD)

> [!NOTE]
> Every marine CFD solver (Fluent, OpenFOAM, Star-CCM+, COMSOL) ultimately solves the **RANS equations**. This derivation is the *spine* of Module 1 and is a high-probability Part A question.

### Step 1 — Start with the instantaneous continuity and momentum equations

The incompressible continuity equation:

$$\nabla \cdot \mathbf{V} = 0$$

The incompressible Navier–Stokes momentum equation:

$$\rho \left( \frac{\partial \mathbf{V}}{\partial t} + \mathbf{V} \cdot \nabla \mathbf{V} \right) = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^2 \mathbf{V} + \mathbf{f}$$

where $\mathbf{V}$ is the velocity vector, $p$ is the pressure, $\mu$ is the dynamic viscosity, and $\mathbf{f}$ represents body forces (gravity).

### Step 2 — Apply the Reynolds decomposition

Decompose the instantaneous flow variables into a **time-averaged (mean)** component and a **fluctuating** component:

$$\mathbf{V}(x,t) = \overline{\mathbf{V}}(x) + \mathbf{V}'(x,t)$$

$$p(x,t) = \overline{p}(x) + p'(x,t)$$

The overbar denotes a time-average; the prime denotes the fluctuation.

### Step 3 — Substitute into Navier–Stokes and apply the time-average operator

Substituting the decomposition into the momentum equation and taking the time average gives:

$$\rho \left( \frac{\partial \overline{\mathbf{V}}}{\partial t} + \overline{\mathbf{V}} \cdot \nabla \overline{\mathbf{V}} \right) = -\nabla \overline{p} + \mu \nabla^2 \overline{\mathbf{V}} + \rho \mathbf{g} - \nabla \cdot \left( \rho \overline{\mathbf{V}' \mathbf{V}'} \right)$$

### Step 4 — Define the Reynolds stress tensor

The new term arising from the non-linearity of the convective acceleration is:

$$\boldsymbol{\tau}_{R} = -\rho \overline{\mathbf{V}' \mathbf{V}'}$$

This tensor is **symmetric** and has 6 independent components (3 normal + 3 shear). It represents the **momentum flux** caused by turbulent eddies.

### Step 5 — Apply the Boussinesq eddy-viscosity hypothesis (closure)

To close the system, Boussinesq (1877) proposed an analogy with viscous stresses:

$$\boldsymbol{\tau}_R = \mu_t \left( \nabla \overline{\mathbf{V}} + \nabla \overline{\mathbf{V}}^T \right) - \tfrac{2}{3} \rho k \mathbf{I}$$

where $\mu_t$ is the **eddy viscosity** and $k = \tfrac{1}{2} \overline{V_i' V_i'}$ is the **turbulent kinetic energy**.

### Step 6 — Final RANS form (foundation of all practical marine CFD)

The governing RANS equation becomes:

$$\rho \frac{D \overline{\mathbf{V}}}{Dt} = -\nabla \overline{p} + \left( \mu + \mu_t \right) \nabla^2 \overline{\mathbf{V}} + \rho \mathbf{g}$$

This is the **canonical equation** solved in ship-resistance, propeller, and seakeeping CFD codes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for the KTU exam:** The choice of $\mu_t$ is what differentiates turbulence models. The $k$-$\omega$ SST model used in $90\%$ of marine CFD applications uses $\mu_t = \rho \dfrac{a_1 k}{\max(a_1 \omega, \; S F_2)}$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: ITTC-1978 Friction Line and Form Factor Extraction

> [!NOTE]
> The **form factor $1+k$** is the *single most important quantity* in hull-form design and is a guaranteed Part B question in the KTU ESE.

### Step 1 — The resistance decomposition (Froude, 1868)

Total resistance decomposes into:

$$R_T = R_F + R_R + R_{APP}$$

where $R_F$ is frictional, $R_R$ is residual (wave + viscous pressure), and $R_{APP}$ is the appendage resistance. CFD computes the **integrated viscous + pressure** force directly.

### Step 2 — Prohaska's method (1968) for form factor

Measure (or compute via CFD) $C_T$ at two or more low Froude numbers where wave resistance is negligible (e.g., $\mathrm{Fr} < 0.15$). Plot $C_T$ vs. $C_F$:

$$C_T = (1+k) \cdot C_F + \text{const}$$

The **slope** of this line is the form factor $(1+k)$.

### Step 3 — Express in terms of the ITTC'57 friction line

For seawater at $20^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$, the ITTC-57 friction line is:

$$C_F = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} \mathrm{Re} - 2)^2}$$

### Step 4 — The corresponding speed-power relation

Effective power:

$$P_E = R_T \cdot U = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho S U^3 \cdot C_T$$

Delivered power (after hydrodynamic losses):

$$P_D = \frac{P_E}{\eta_H \cdot \eta_R \cdot \eta_0}$$

where $\eta_H$ is hull efficiency, $\eta_R$ is relative rotative efficiency, and $\eta_0$ is open-water propeller efficiency.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Turbulence Model

| **Application** | **Recommended Model** | **Justification** |
|---|---|---|
| Steady ship resistance (calm water) | $k$-$\omega$ SST | Industry standard; robust on adverse pressure gradients |
| Propeller open water (RANS) | $k$-$\omega$ SST or Realizable $k$-$\varepsilon$ | SST gives better separation prediction near blade tip |
| Cavitation | $k$-$\omega$ SST + Schnerr–Sauer | Schnerr–Sauer is most stable for marine cavitation |
| Unsteady free-surface (seakeeping) | $k$-$\omega$ SST + VOF | Computational cost constraint |
| Vortex-Induced Vibration (VIV) | DES / SAS or wall-resolved LES | Must resolve shedding frequency |
| Slamming / Green water | LES with adaptive mesh refinement | Transient impact physics |
| Tidal turbine (unsteady) | LES or IDDES | Captures turbine wake turbulence |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (CFD-Driven Power Estimate)

> [!NOTE]
> This worked example is representative of a **Part B (14 mark)** question style.

**Problem:** A bulk carrier of **LPP = 200 m**, **B = 32 m**, **T = 14.5 m**, **$C_B$ = 0.82** is to be analyzed at design speed $U = 14$ knots. CFD predicts a total resistance coefficient $C_T = 3.5 \times 10^{-3}$ at $\mathrm{Re} = 1.2 \times 10^9$. The form factor from low-Froude CFD runs is $(1+k) = 1.18$. The propeller (4-bladed, $D = 7.5$ m) operates at $n = 90$ rpm with open-water efficiency $\eta_0 = 0.68$, hull efficiency $\eta_H = 1.05$, relative rotative efficiency $\eta_R = 1.00$. Compute the total resistance, effective power, and brake power.

**Step 1 — Wetted surface area (WSA) estimate using the Harvald formula:**

$$\mathrm{WSA} = L (2T + B) \sqrt{C_M} \cdot C_{\mathrm{spr}}$$

where $C_M = 0.5 C_B + 0.3$ and $C_{\mathrm{spr}} = 0.95$ for typical merchant hulls.

$$C_M = 0.5(0.82) + 0.3 = 0.71$$

$$\mathrm{WSA} = 200 \cdot (2 \times 14.5 + 32) \cdot \sqrt{0.71} \cdot 0.95$$

$$= 200 \cdot 61 \cdot 0.8426 \cdot 0.95 = 9{,}758 \ \mathrm{m^2}$$

**Step 2 — Convert speed to SI units:**

$$U = 14 \text{ knots} = 14 \times 0.5144 = 7.2016 \ \mathrm{m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Total resistance from the CFD $C_T$:**

$$R_T = C_T \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \rho S U^2$$

$$R_T = 3.5 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.5 \cdot 1025 \cdot 9758 \cdot (7.2016)^2$$

$$R_T = 3.5 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 512.5 \cdot 9758 \cdot 51.86$$

$$R_T = 3.5 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 2.59 \times 10^8$$

$$R_T \approx 9.08 \times 10^5 \ \mathrm{N} = 908 \ \mathrm{kN}$$

**Step 4 — Effective power:**

$$P_E = R_T \cdot U = 908 \times 10^3 \times 7.2016 = 6.54 \times 10^6 \ \mathrm{W} = 6.54 \ \mathrm{MW}$$

**Step 5 — Brake power (using quasi-propulsive coefficient $QPC$):**

$$\eta_D = \eta_H \cdot \eta_R \cdot \eta_0 = 1.05 \cdot 1.00 \cdot 0.68 = 0.714$$

$$P_B = \frac{P_E}{\eta_D} = \frac{6.54}{0.714} = 9.16 \ \mathrm{MW}$$

**Step 6 — Verify advance coefficient and propeller loading:**

$$V_A = U (1 - w), \quad w \approx 0.35 \ \text{(typical for full-form bulk carrier)}$$

$$V_A = 7.2016 \cdot 0.65 = 4.68 \ \mathrm{m/s}$$

$$J = \frac{V_A}{nD} = \frac{4.68}{(90/60) \cdot 7.5} = \frac{4.68}{11.25} = 0.416$$

This $J$ value sits in the typical B-Series design envelope — confirming consistency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The vessel requires **9.16 MW of brake power** at the design speed. This single computation links the **CFD-computed $C_T$** to a **real-world engine selection** — the essence of marine CFD utility.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Marine CFD Workflow Block Diagram

The following block diagram maps the **six-stage computational pipeline** for any marine CFD application, from geometry input to validated resistance / power output.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Geometry Input] --> B[Lines Plan IGES STEP STL]
    B --> C[Domain Definition]
    C --> D[Upstream Downstream Lateral Vertical Extents]
    D --> E[Meshing Stage]
    E --> F[Surface Mesh Prism BL Tetrahedral Core]
    F --> G[Physics Setup]
    G --> H[Solver Type Turbulence Free Surface Reference Frame]
    H --> I[Solution and Monitoring]
    I --> J[Residuals Forces Wave Elevation]
    J --> K[Post Processing]
    K --> L[Validation against ITTC 1978 2017]
    L --> M[Engineering Output]
    M --> N[Resistance Power Cavitation VIV Seakeeping]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each block is a *gate* — a poorly executed block (e.g., insufficient domain extent) **propagates error** into every downstream block. This is the **principle of error cascading** in marine CFD.

## 4.2 Turbulence Model Selection Logic Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Marine CFD Application] --> B{Is the flow steady}
    B -- Yes --> C{Is wall resolution feasible}
    B -- No --> D{Is VIV or wake resolved}
    C -- Yes --> E[Use k-omega SST]
    C -- No --> F{Is the flow high Re wall-modeled}
    F -- Yes --> G[Use k-omega SST with y+ 30 to 50]
    F -- No --> H[Use low-Re k-omega SST or transition SST]
    D -- Yes --> I[Use DES or wall-resolved LES]
    D -- No --> J{Is the problem transient impact or sloshing}
    J -- Yes --> K[Use LES with adaptive mesh refinement]
    J -- No --> L[Use SAS or DDES]
    E --> M[End: Solution Ready]
    G --> M
    H --> M
    I --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
```

**Reading the diagram:** This decision tree is the **operational logic** that a KTU examiner expects you to be able to **reproduce from memory** in a viva or Part A question.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

### **Q1. Define the Froude number and explain its significance in marine CFD.**

> **Model Answer:**
> The Froude number is defined as the ratio of inertial to gravitational forces:
> $$\displaystyle \mathrm{Fr} = \frac{U}{\sqrt{gL}}$$
> where $U$ is the ship speed, $L$ is the length between perpendiculars, and $g$ is gravitational acceleration. In marine CFD, the Froude number is **critical for free-surface flows** because it governs the **wave-resistance** component and determines whether the flow is **sub-critical** ($\mathrm{Fr} < 0.4$) or **super-critical** ($\mathrm{Fr} > 0.4$). For $\mathrm{Fr} > 0.4$, **transom stern wave breaking** occurs, demanding fine VOF mesh resolution.

### **Q2. What is the ITTC-1978 friction line? Why is it used in CFD validation?**

> **Model Answer:**
> The ITTC-1978 friction line is a standard non-dimensional frictional resistance correlation:
> $$\displaystyle C_F = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10}\mathrm{Re} - 2)^2}$$
> It is used as the **benchmark** to compare CFD-predicted frictional resistance. The deviation between CFD $C_F$ and the ITTC line is used to extract the **form factor $(1+k)$** and to validate the wall treatment (boundary layer mesh, $y^+$ values) in the CFD setup.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **(a)** *Discuss the role of the turbulence model selection in marine CFD applications. With a suitable flow diagram, explain the decision logic for choosing between RANS, DES, and LES for typical marine applications.* **(7 marks)**

#### **(b)** *A container ship of LPP = 280 m, B = 40 m, T = 14 m, $C_B$ = 0.65 sails at design speed of 25 knots. CFD predicts $C_T = 2.4 \times 10^{-3}$ at $\mathrm{Re} = 2.1 \times 10^9$. Estimate the effective power and the brake power, given $\eta_0 = 0.70$, $\eta_H = 1.08$, $\eta_R = 0.98$. Take seawater density $\rho = 1025 \ \mathrm{kg/m^3}$.* **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution to Question A:**

#### **Part (a) — Turbulence Model Selection in Marine CFD**

**Step 1 — Why turbulence modeling is non-negotiable in marine CFD:**
All practical marine flows operate at $\mathrm{Re} > 10^7$, which is **orders of magnitude** above the laminar-to-turbulent transition. Direct Numerical Simulation (DNS) of such flows would require $N \sim \mathrm{Re}^{9/4}$ grid points — for a full ship at $\mathrm{Re} = 10^9$, this exceeds $10^{21}$ cells, which is **computationally infeasible** on any current or near-future supercomputer. We therefore must model the turbulence.

**Step 2 — The RANS family (steady-state, single-point closure):**
RANS models assume $\overline{\mathbf{V}' \mathbf{V}'} \approx f(\overline{\mathbf{V}})$ and add a **single additional equation** (or two) for the turbulent scales.

- **$k$-$\varepsilon$ Realizable** — best for free shear flows (propeller slipstream, wake).
- **$k$-$\omega$ SST** — best for adverse pressure gradients and separation (hull, rudder, appendage).
- **RSM (Reynolds Stress Model)** — used for highly 3D flows with strong curvature (high-skew propellers).

**Step 3 — The DES / hybrid family (unsteady RANS + LES):**
DES (Spalart, 1997) and its variants (DDES, IDDES, SAS) blend RANS near walls with LES in separated regions. They are the **workhorse for VIV, cavitation-vortex interaction, and ship-airwake problems**.

**Step 4 — The LES family (large-eddy simulation):**
Wall-resolved LES at full-scale marine $\mathrm{Re}$ is **barely feasible** even on the world's largest HPC clusters. Wall-modeled LES is an emerging compromise. LES is mandated for **unsteady impact problems** (slamming) and **acoustic signature prediction**.

**Step 5 — Decision logic (to be illustrated as a flowchart — see Section 4.2 of these notes):**

| **Question** | **Default Answer** |
|---|---|
| Steady, attached flow? | $k$-$\omega$ SST |
| Steady, massive separation? | Realizable $k$-$\varepsilon$ |
| Unsteady, free shear? | DES or SAS |
| Slamming / impulsive load? | Wall-resolved LES |
| VIV wake? | DDES / wall-modeled LES |

> **Conclusion:** The choice of turbulence model is **the most consequential decision** in any marine CFD study. A wrong choice can give qualitatively correct but quantitatively meaningless results.

---

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Power Estimation**

**Step 1 — Wetted Surface Area (Turtle-Schneekluth approximation):**

$$S = L^{0.5} \cdot T^{0.5} \cdot B^{0.75} \cdot (3.4 + 0.5 C_B)$$

$$S = (280)^{0.5} \cdot (14)^{0.5} \cdot (40)^{0.75} \cdot (3.4 + 0.5 \cdot 0.65)$$

$$S = 16.73 \cdot 3.74 \cdot 15.99 \cdot 3.725$$

$$S = 3{,}725 \ \mathrm{m^2}$$

**Step 2 — Convert ship speed to SI:**

$$U = 25 \times 0.5144 = 12.86 \ \mathrm{m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Total resistance:**

$$R_T = C_T \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \rho S U^2 = 2.4 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.5 \cdot 1025 \cdot 3725 \cdot (12.86)^2$$

$$R_T = 2.4 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 512.5 \cdot 3725 \cdot 165.4$$

$$R_T = 2.4 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 3.158 \times 10^8$$

$$R_T = 7.58 \times 10^5 \ \mathrm{N} = 758 \ \mathrm{kN}$$

**Step 4 — Effective power:**

$$P_E = R_T \cdot U = 758 \times 10^3 \cdot 12.86 = 9.75 \ \mathrm{MW}$$

**Step 5 — Quasi-propulsive coefficient:**

$$\eta_D = \eta_H \cdot \eta_R \cdot \eta_0 = 1.08 \cdot 0.98 \cdot 0.70 = 0.741$$

**Step 6 — Brake power:**

$$P_B = \frac{P_E}{\eta_D} = \frac{9.75}{0.741} = 13.16 \ \mathrm{MW}$$

> **Final Answer:** $R_T = 758$ kN, $P_E = 9.75$ MW, $P_B = 13.16$ MW.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part A — Q.A1):**
> - Students frequently omit the **physical meaning** of turbulence-model selection (i.e., *why* we cannot use DNS). Always begin with the **infeasibility argument** (computational cost scales as $\mathrm{Re}^{9/4}$).
> - Do **not** confuse RANS with LES — they have fundamentally different closure philosophies.
> - Vague statements like "use the best model" will be penalized; **name the model and the application**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part A — Q.A2):**
> - **Forgetting unit conversions** (knots → m/s) is the #1 source of mark loss. Always show the conversion explicitly.
> - **Wetted Surface Area (WSA)** must be computed or stated — never use $\nabla$ (volume) in $C_T$ unless the question explicitly gives $C_T$ on a volumetric basis.
> - **Quasi-propulsive coefficient:** students often forget that $\eta_H > 1$ is *physically valid* — it represents the **wake-fraction gain**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **(a)** *With a neat block diagram, explain the major application areas of marine CFD. For each application, state the typical solver type, the relevant non-dimensional numbers, and the standard output metrics.* **(7 marks)**

#### **(b)** *Explain the role of the VOF (Volume of Fluid) method in free-surface marine CFD. Why is VOF preferred over the level-set method for ship resistance and seakeeping simulations? State the key boundary conditions and mesh requirements for a typical ship resistance CFD setup.* **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution Outline to Question B:**

#### **Part (a) — Application Areas Block Diagram:**
Reproduce the **functional architecture diagram** in Section 4.3. Tabulate as in Section 2.2 of these notes.

#### **Part (b) — VOF Method:**

- **VOF principle:** tracks the volume fraction $\alpha$ of water in each cell. $\alpha = 1$ (pure water), $\alpha = 0$ (pure air), $0 < \alpha < 1$ (interface).
- **Governing equation:**
$$\frac{\partial \alpha}{\partial t} + \mathbf{V} \cdot \nabla \alpha = 0$$
- **Why VOF > Level-set for marine CFD:** mass conservation is **rigorously preserved** in VOF (a key requirement for ship-resistance and seakeeping), whereas the level-set method suffers from mass-loss / mass-gain during long transients.
- **Key boundary conditions:** atmospheric pressure at the top, symmetry on lateral planes, no-slip on hull, $k$-$\omega$ SST at hull with $y^+ = 30$ to $50$.
- **Mesh requirements:** refinement zone at the free surface of thickness $\approx 0.05 L$ and at the bow/stern for wave breaking.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — CFD Applications in Marine Technology (Module 1):**
> - [ ] **CFD applications are classified into 5 domains:** Ship Hydrodynamics, Propulsion, Offshore, Internal Flows, and Renewable Marine Energy.
> - [ ] **Six-stage CFD pipeline:** Geometry → Domain → Mesh → Physics Setup → Solution → Post-Processing.
> - [ ] **Domain size rule of thumb:** upstream ≥ $1L$, downstream ≥ $2L$–$3L$, lateral ≥ $1L$, vertical ≥ $0.5L$ above and below.
> - [ ] **Default turbulence model for marine RANS:** $k$-$\omega$ SST (industry standard).
> - [ ] **Wall treatment:** $y^+ \approx 30$–$50$ for standard $k$-$\omega$ SST; $y^+ \le 1$ for low-Re or wall-resolved LES.
> - [ ] **Three pillars of marine CFD:** Virtual Towing Tank, Virtual Propeller Open Water, Virtual Seakeeping Basin.
> - [ ] **Froude number** governs wave resistance; **Reynolds number** governs viscous resistance; **Weber number** governs cavitation inception.
> - [ ] **ITTC-1978 friction line** is the validation benchmark: $C_F = 0.075 / (\log_{10}\mathrm{Re} - 2)^2$.
> - [ ] **Form factor $(1+k)$** is extracted from low-Froude CFD runs via Prohaska's method (slope of $C_T$ vs. $C_F$).
> - [ ] **VOF (Volume of Fluid)** is the standard free-surface method for ship resistance and seakeeping.
> - [ ] **MRF (Moving Reference Frame)** for steady propeller; **sliding mesh** for unsteady.
> - [ ] **Schnerr–Sauer** is the default cavitation model in marine CFD.
> - [ ] **DES / SAS** are hybrid RANS–LES models — used for VIV, ship airwake, and cavitation-vortex problems.
> - [ ] **Validation metrics:** GCI $< 2\%$, residual drop to $10^{-6}$, agreement with ITTC'78 within $\pm 5\%$.
> - [ ] **Class-approval context:** DNV, LR, ABS, BV increasingly accept CFD for EEDI / EEXI / CII compliance.
> - [ ] **CFD-driven hull-form optimization** (adjoint method) delivers $5$–$15\%$ fuel savings.
> - [ ] **Final delivered power formula:** $P_B = \dfrac{R_T \cdot U}{\eta_H \cdot \eta_R \cdot \eta_0}$ — memorize this in symbolic form.
> - [ ] **Cavitation number** $\sigma = (p_\infty - p_v) / (\tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^2)$ — cavitation occurs when $\sigma < \sigma_{\text{critical}}$.
> - [ ] **Advance coefficient** $J = V_A / nD$ — design point is typically $J \approx 0.5$–$0.7$ for merchant ships.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Numerical vs Analytical vs Experimental
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST635 - COMPUTATIONAL FLUID DYNAMICS IN MARINE TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Numerical vs Analytical vs Experimental # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is the Triad of Solution Methods? In engineering — and especially in **Marine Technology** and **Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD)** — virtually every physical problem involving fluid flow, heat transfer, structural loading of a hull, or propeller hydrodynamics can be tackled using one of **three complementary approaches**: > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition** > **Analytical Method:** A closed-form, exact mathematical solution derived directly from governing differential equations using calculus, series expansion, or integral transforms. > **Numerical Method:** An approximate solution obtained by discretizing the governing equations into algebraic form and solving iteratively on a computer (the heart of CFD). > **Experimental Method:** A physical, laboratory- or field-based investigation using scale models, towing tanks, wind tunnels, Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV), or pressure transducers. ## 1.2 The Real-World Analogy — Predicting a Ship's Resistance Imagine three engineers are asked: *"What is the wave-making resistance of a 200 m LNG carrier cruising at 18 knots?"* - **Engineer A (Analytical)** picks up a pen and solves Michell's thin-ship theory integral on paper — exact, elegant, but works only for very simple hull shapes. - **Engineer B (Numerical)** builds a 3-D RANS mesh around the hull, runs a solver (Fluent/OpenFOAM/Star-CCM+) for 24 hours on a cluster, and produces a coloured pressure contour plot. - **Engineer C (Experimental)** takes a 1:30 scale wooden hull to the **towing tank** at IIT Madras or KRISO, attaches a dynamometer, and physically measures the drag force at the exact Froude number. All three are *correct paths* — but with very different accuracy, cost, and time trade-offs. The modern marine engineer uses a **hybrid of all three** (a fact we will see in **Validation & Verification** in later modules). ## 1.3 Why This Topic Comes First in Module 1 Before a student writes a single line of Fortran, Python, or opens ANSYS, the KTU 2024 syllabus expects a **clear philosophical and mathematical positioning** of CFD within the wider engineering landscape. CFD is **not a replacement** for analytical and experimental work — it is a **third pillar** that complements them. > [!NOTE] > **Standard CFD Metric to Remember** > The **National Physical Laboratory (NPL)** UK standard deviation for a well-validated CFD result is typically **< 2 %** when benchmarked against a towing-tank experiment for a simple Wigley hull. This is the benchmark used globally in ship hydrodynamics. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Approaches — Operational Breakdown ### 2.1.1 Analytical (Closed-Form) Method - **How:** Starts from the governing PDE (Navier–Stokes, Laplace, Euler). Uses symmetry, linearity, separation of variables, or conformal mapping. - **Why it works:** Mathematics is exact; the Navier–Stokes equations become tractable only for very simplified geometries (flat plate, inviscid flow, potential flow). - **When it fails:** Realistic ship hulls have curved, complex 3-D boundaries; no closed-form solution exists for the full viscous Navier–Stokes equations (Millennium Prize problem!). ### 2.1.2 Numerical (Discretised) Method - **How:** Replace continuous derivatives with algebraic approximations (Finite Difference, Finite Volume, or Finite Element). Solve the resulting large linear/non-linear system on a CPU/GPU. - **Why it works:** Computers handle millions of algebraic equations cheaply; geometry is discretised into a mesh. - **Key sub-branches in Marine CFD:** FVM (industry standard), FEM (for structural FEA coupling), Spectral methods (for seakeeping potential-flow solvers). ### 2.1.3 Experimental (Empirical) Method - **How:** Build a scaled model, measure forces/pressures/velocities in a controlled environment, then use **dynamic similarity** (Reynolds, Froude, Weber scaling) to extrapolate to full scale. - **Why it works:** Physics is real — no mathematical idealisation is required. - **Limitations:** Expensive (a single cavitation tunnel test costs **₹ 5–10 lakh / day** at leading Indian facilities); scale effects cannot be fully eliminated; some quantities (e.g., instantaneous turbulent eddies) are hard to measure. ## 2.2 The KTU 2024 High-Yield Comparison Matrix | Parameter | Analytical Method | Numerical Method (CFD) | Experimental Method | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Nature of Solution** | Exact (closed form) | Approximate (discrete) | Real measurement | | **Governing Equations** | Solved in their original PDE form | Converted to algebraic system via discretisation | Not solved; physical law observed | | **Geometry Handling** | Only simple shapes (flat plate, cylinder, sphere) | Arbitrarily complex 3-D CAD geometry | Limited by model-making and tank size | | **Cost** | Very low (pen + paper) | Moderate to high (HPC cluster licence) | Very high (model + facility + manpower) | | **Time** | Hours to days | Days to weeks (mesh + solve) | Months (model + campaign) | | **Accuracy** | Exact for the model assumed | **Mesh & scheme dependent** (validated) | Best, but scale-effect errors present | | **Repeatability** | Perfect (deterministic) | Perfect (same input = same output) | Limited by instrumentation noise | | **Output Type** | Equation for $C_D$, $C_L$ | Full 3-D flow field, $p$, $u$, $v$, $w$, $k$, $\varepsilon$ | Drag, pressure, velocity at discrete probes | | **Turbulence Treatment** | Almost always neglected (laminar) | RANS, LES, DNS — user choice | Naturally captured | | **Example in Marine Tech** | Michell's integral for wave resistance | Full RANS of KCS hull in OpenFOAM | Towing tank test at SIREH, Hyderabad | ## 2.3 Governing Equations of Marine CFD (Reference) The three methods all attempt to solve the same underlying physics — the **Navier–Stokes equations** for incompressible flow: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Continuity:} \quad & \nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0 \\ \text{Momentum:} \quad & \rho \left( \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + \vec{V} \cdot \nabla \vec{V} \right) = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^2 \vec{V} + \rho \vec{g} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > The **choice of method** (analytical / numerical / experimental) is *not* a choice of physics. It is a choice of **how to satisfy** these equations. All three methods, in principle, must satisfy the same mass and momentum conservation principles. ## 2.4 The Hierarchy of Truth in Marine Hydrodynamics Industry practice (followed by IACS, DNV, Lloyd's Register) follows a **Validation Pyramid**: 1. **Tier 1 — Validation:** Numerical solution is benchmarked against a *high-quality* experiment (e.g., KCS, KVLCC2, DTMB 5415 hull-form workshops). 2. **Tier 2 — Verification:** Numerical solution is checked for grid-independence, time-step independence, and conservation errors. 3. **Tier 3 — Analytical Cross-Check:** Limit cases (e.g., very low Froude number) are compared with thin-ship theory. This is why **no method is sufficient alone** — they form a triangle of mutual verification. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Finite-Difference Approximations Using Taylor Series This is the **mathematical backbone** that connects the analytical world (smooth derivatives) to the numerical world (algebra on a grid) — a guaranteed KTU Part-B favourite. ### 3.1.1 Forward-Difference Approximation of the First Derivative **Step 1 — Write the Taylor series expansion of $f(x+h)$ about the point $x$:** $$ \begin{aligned} f(x+h) = f(x) + h f'(x) + \frac{h^2}{2!} f''(x) + \frac{h^3}{3!} f'''(x) + \frac{h^4}{4!} f^{(4)}(x) + \ldots \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Isolate the first derivative $f'(x)$ by rearranging:** $$ \begin{aligned} f'(x) = \frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h} - \frac{h}{2!} f''(x) - \frac{h^2}{3!} f'''(x) - \ldots \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Truncate the series and write the Forward-Difference Formula:** $$ \begin{aligned} \left[ \frac{df}{dx} \right]_{\text{FD}} = \frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Identify the Truncation Error $\tau$:** $$ \begin{aligned} \tau_{\text{FD}} = -\frac{h}{2} f''(x) - \frac{h^2}{6} f'''(x) - \ldots \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \tau_{\text{FD}} = \mathcal{O}(h) \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** The forward-difference formula is **first-order accurate** $\mathcal{O}(h)$ — halving the grid spacing $h$ halves the error. This matters massively in marine CFD when refining the boundary-layer mesh around a ship hull. ### 3.1.2 Backward-Difference Approximation of the First Derivative **Step 1 — Write the Taylor series for $f(x-h)$:** $$ \begin{aligned} f(x-h) = f(x) - h f'(x) + \frac{h^2}{2!} f''(x) - \frac{h^3}{3!} f'''(x) + \frac{h^4}{4!} f^{(4)}(x) - \ldots \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Rearrange to isolate $f'(x)$:** $$ \begin{aligned} f'(x) = \frac{f(x) - f(x-h)}{h} + \frac{h}{2!} f''(x) - \frac{h^2}{3!} f'''(x) + \ldots \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Backward-Difference Formula and its Truncation Error:** $$ \begin{aligned} \left[ \frac{df}{dx} \right]_{\text{BD}} = \frac{f(x) - f(x-h)}{h} + \mathcal{O}(h) \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.1.3 Central-Difference Approximation of the First Derivative **Step 1 — Subtract the $f(x-h)$ expansion from the $f(x+h)$ expansion:** $$ \begin{aligned} f(x+h) - f(x-h) = 2h f'(x) + \frac{2h^3}{3!} f'''(x) + \frac{2h^5}{5!} f^{(5)}(x) + \ldots \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Solve for $f'(x)$:** $$ \begin{aligned} f'(x) = \frac{f(x+h) - f(x-h)}{2h} - \frac{h^2}{6} f'''(x) - \frac{h^4}{120} f^{(5)}(x) - \ldots \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Central-Difference Formula and its Truncation Error:** $$ \begin{aligned} \left[ \frac{df}{dx} \right]_{\text{CD}} = \frac{f(x+h) - f(x-h)}{2h} + \mathcal{O}(h^2) \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Takeaway:** The central-difference scheme is **second-order accurate** $\mathcal{O}(h^2)$ — it has an even-error structure because the odd terms cancel. In marine CFD, central differences are widely used in the **interior of the domain** for momentum equations, while upwind schemes are used near inlets to avoid odd-even decoupling. ### 3.1.4 Second-Derivative Central-Difference (The Diffusion Operator) **Step 1 — Add the two Taylor expansions:** $$ \begin{aligned} f(x+h) + f(x-h) = 2f(x) + h^2 f''(x) + \frac{h^4}{12} f^{(4)}(x) + \ldots \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Solve for $f''(x)$:** $$ \begin{aligned} \left[ \frac{d^2 f}{dx^2} \right]_{\text{CD}} = \frac{f(x+h) - 2f(x) + f(x-h)}{h^2} + \mathcal{O}(h^2) \end{aligned} $$ This is the **discrete Laplacian** — the numerical operator that handles **viscous diffusion** in the Navier–Stokes momentum equation. It is second-order accurate and is the cornerstone of every CFD solver on Earth. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Application to Marine Problem) **Problem:** The velocity profile over a flat plate is given at three points: - $u(0) = 0 \text{ m/s}$ - $u(0.1) = 0.985 \text{ m/s}$ - $u(0.2) = 1.951 \text{ m/s}$ Compute $\frac{du}{dy}$ at $y = 0.1$ m using **all three** finite-difference formulae (take $h = 0.1$ m). **Forward Difference:** $$ \begin{aligned} \left. \frac{du}{dy} \right|_{y=0.1} &\approx \frac{u(0.2) - u(0.1)}{0.1} \\ &= \frac{1.951 - 0.985}{0.1} = 9.66 \text{ s}^{-1} \end{aligned} $$ **Backward Difference:** $$ \begin{aligned} \left. \frac{du}{dy} \right|_{y=0.1} &\approx \frac{u(0.1) - u(0.0)}{0.1} \\ &= \frac{0.985 - 0.000}{0.1} = 9.85 \text{ s}^{-1} \end{aligned} $$ **Central Difference (most accurate):** $$ \begin{aligned} \left. \frac{du}{dy} \right|_{y=0.1} &\approx \frac{u(0.2) - u(0.0)}{2 \times 0.1} \\ &= \frac{1.951 - 0.000}{0.2} = 9.755 \text{ s}^{-1} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Interpretation:** The velocity gradient at $y = 0.1$ m is approximately $9.75 \text{ s}^{-1}$. For a fluid of dynamic viscosity $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$, the **wall shear stress** is $\tau_w = \mu \cdot (du/dy) \approx 9.77 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa. This shear stress, integrated over the wetted hull, gives the **frictional resistance** $R_F$ — the single largest component of ship drag at low Froude numbers. The whole pipeline — analytical, numerical, experimental — feeds into this calculation. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Triad of Solution Methods — Conceptual Map ```mermaid flowchart TB A["MARINE FLUID FLOW PROBLEM
Example: Drag on a KCS Hull"] A --> B["METHOD 1 ANALYTICAL"] A --> C["METHOD 2 NUMERICAL CFD"] A --> D["METHOD 3 EXPERIMENTAL"] B --> B1["Tools Calculus Series Transforms"] B1 --> B2["Output Closed Form Equation"] B2 --> B3["Limitation Simple Geometry Only"] C --> C1["Discretisation FDM FVM FEM"] C1 --> C2["Solver RANS LES DNS"] C2 --> C3["Output Pressure Velocity Field"] C3 --> C4["Limitation Mesh Convergence Required"] D --> D1["Facility Towing Tank Cavitation Tunnel"] D1 --> D2["Instrumentation PIV LDV Dynamometer"] D2 --> D3["Output Drag Force Pressure Data"] D3 --> D4["Limitation Scale Effects High Cost"] B3 --> E["VALIDATION AND VERIFICATION"] C4 --> E D4 --> E E --> F["HYBRID SOLUTION
Industry Standard for Marine CFD"] ``` ## 4.2 The CFD Solution Pipeline (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR P1["PHYSICAL PROBLEM
Navier Stokes Equations"] --> P2["GOVERNING EQUATIONS
PDE Form"] P2 --> P3["DISCRETISATION
FVM on Mesh"] P3 --> P4["LINEAR SOLVER
Algebraic System"] P4 --> P5["DISCRETE SOLUTION
Numerical Field"] P5 --> P6["VERIFICATION
Mesh Independence"] P6 --> P7["VALIDATION
Compare to Experiment"] P7 --> P8["ENGINEERING ANSWER
Resistance Propeller Thrust"] P2 -.Analytical check.-> A1["Thin Ship Theory"] P7 -.Benchmark.-> E1["Towing Tank Data"] ``` ## 4.3 When to Use Which Method — Decision Logic ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1["Is geometry very simple
flat plate cylinder potential flow"] -->|YES| Q2["Use ANALYTICAL METHOD
exact closed form"] Q1 -->|NO| Q3["Is the result safety critical
IMO regulatory"] Q3 -->|YES| Q4["Use NUMERICAL plus EXPERIMENTAL
hybrid validation"] Q3 -->|NO| Q5["Is turnaround time less than 1 week"] Q5 -->|YES| Q6["Use NUMERICAL CFD only
with mesh study"] Q5 -->|NO| Q7["Use EXPERIMENTAL METHOD
highest physical fidelity"] ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 SET A]** *Distinguish between the analytical method and the numerical method of solving fluid flow problems. State one advantage of each in the context of marine hydrodynamics.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - The **analytical method** solves the governing differential equations in closed form using mathematical techniques such as separation of variables, conformal mapping, or integral methods. It yields an **exact** solution but is restricted to simple geometries (e.g., potential flow around a 2-D cylinder). - The **numerical method (CFD)** discretises the governing PDEs into a system of algebraic equations using FDM, FVM, or FEM, and solves them iteratively on a computer. - *Advantage (Analytical):* Gives deep physical insight and a benchmark for validation; e.g., Michell's thin-ship integral for wave resistance. - *Advantage (Numerical):* Handles arbitrarily complex 3-D hull geometries that have no closed-form solution, providing full flow-field details. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 SET B]** *List any three limitations of the experimental approach in ship hydrodynamics.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Scale effects** — Reynolds and Froude numbers cannot be matched simultaneously between model and full scale; viscous and wave effects scale differently. 2. **High cost and time** — model construction, towing tank rental (₹ 5–10 lakh/day), and skilled manpower make it expensive. 3. **Limited spatial resolution** — measurements are typically at discrete probe points; the full turbulent field is not captured. 4. (Optional 4th) Wall and blockage effects in the test section. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern) ### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]** #### (a) Compare and contrast the analytical, numerical, and experimental approaches to solving marine fluid flow problems. Discuss the role of each in the modern ship design cycle. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** The three approaches form the **triad of fluid mechanics** and are used in combination in any serious ship design project. | Aspect | Analytical | Numerical (CFD) | Experimental | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Foundation** | Pure mathematics | Computational algorithm | Physical measurement | | **Cost** | Negligible | Moderate (HPC) | Very high | | **Time** | Hours | Days to weeks | Months | | **Geometry** | Simple only | Arbitrarily complex | Limited by tank | | **Output** | Equation | Full 3-D field | Probe data | | **Validation Role** | Benchmark for limit cases | The actual design tool | Gold standard for validation | **In the modern ship design cycle (7-marks structure expected):** 1. **Concept Design Stage:** Analytical methods (Holtrop–Mullen, Series 60) used for first-cut resistance estimation. 2. **Preliminary Design Stage:** CFD (RANS) used to refine hull form and optimise the bulbous bow; experimental data from sister-ship classes used for validation. 3. **Detailed/Contract Design Stage:** Final CFD validation + selected model tests (e.g., self-propulsion, seakeeping) at ITTC-affiliated facilities like SIREH or HSVA. 4. **IMO / Class Certification:** Experimental results (when available) take precedence; CFD is used as supplementary evidence. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "CFD has *replaced* experiments" — this is **factually wrong** and will cost 2 marks. Modern IACS Unified Requirements (UR) and DNV rules still mandate model tests for most large vessel classes. CFD is *complementary*, not a replacement. --- #### (b) Starting from the Taylor series expansion, derive the forward-difference and central-difference approximations of the first derivative. State the order of accuracy of each. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Taylor expansion of $f(x+h)$ about $x$:** $$ \begin{aligned} f(x+h) = f(x) + h f'(x) + \frac{h^2}{2!} f''(x) + \frac{h^3}{3!} f'''(x) + \mathcal{O}(h^4) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Forward Difference Derivation:** $$ \begin{aligned} f'(x) &= \frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h} - \frac{h}{2} f''(x) - \frac{h^2}{6} f'''(x) - \ldots \\ \therefore \quad \left[ \frac{df}{dx} \right]_{\text{FD}} &= \frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h} + \mathcal{O}(h) \end{aligned} $$ **Order of accuracy: $\mathcal{O}(h)$ — first order.** **Step 3 — Taylor expansion of $f(x-h)$ about $x$:** $$ \begin{aligned} f(x-h) = f(x) - h f'(x) + \frac{h^2}{2!} f''(x) - \frac{h^3}{3!} f'''(x) + \mathcal{O}(h^4) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Central Difference Derivation (Subtracting the two series):** $$ \begin{aligned} f(x+h) - f(x-h) &= 2h f'(x) + \frac{2h^3}{3!} f'''(x) + \mathcal{O}(h^5) \\ f'(x) &= \frac{f(x+h) - f(x-h)}{2h} - \frac{h^2}{6} f'''(x) + \mathcal{O}(h^4) \\ \therefore \quad \left[ \frac{df}{dx} \right]_{\text{CD}} &= \frac{f(x+h) - f(x-h)}{2h} + \mathcal{O}(h^2) \end{aligned} $$ **Order of accuracy: $\mathcal{O}(h^2)$ — second order.** **Final Conclusion:** The central-difference scheme is more accurate (2nd order) because the odd-power error terms cancel, leaving only the $h^2$ leading term. In marine CFD, central differences are therefore preferred in the **interior** of the domain, while one-sided differences are used at boundaries. --- ### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]** #### (a) Explain the validation pyramid used in marine CFD. Discuss why no single method (analytical, numerical, or experimental) is sufficient for ship design certification. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** The **Validation Pyramid** in marine CFD places the three methods in a hierarchy of trust: - **Apex — Experiment (highest trust):** Provides physical truth; used as the ultimate benchmark. For ship resistance, the towing tank is the gold standard. - **Middle — Numerical (CFD):** Provides full flow-field details; must be benchmarked against experiments to gain certification-grade credibility. - **Base — Analytical:** Provides insight, limit-case checks, and rough first-cut estimates. **Why a single method is insufficient:** 1. **Analytical alone is inadequate:** Real ship hulls have 3-D, viscous, turbulent flows with free-surface effects — no closed-form solution exists. Thin-ship theory under-predicts wave resistance by 20–40% for full-form hulls. 2. **Numerical alone is inadequate:** CFD is an *approximation*. Without experimental validation, the solver may give a beautifully-coloured but unphysical result (e.g., wrong turbulence model choice, mesh not independent, boundary-layer under-resolved). IACS UR M60 requires validation evidence. 3. **Experimental alone is inadequate:** Towing tank tests are expensive, slow, and suffer scale effects (Reynolds scaling mismatch); they provide only discrete probe data, not the full 3-D field, and cannot easily explore parametric variations. **Therefore, the modern ship design office uses a hybrid loop:** analytical for early estimates → CFD for design iteration → selected model tests for class certification and final validation. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students wrongly equate "CFD" with "true solution". The examiner will deduct 2 marks if the answer does not clearly state that **CFD requires validation against an experiment** before it can be trusted for design decisions. --- #### (b) A ship hull boundary-layer velocity profile is measured at three stations with $h = 0.05$ m: $u(0) = 0$, $u(0.05) = 0.487$, $u(0.10) = 0.946$ m/s. Calculate the velocity gradient at $y = 0.05$ m using forward, backward, and central differences. Compute the percentage error relative to the true gradient (analytical value of $du/dy = 9.4$ s⁻¹ at $y = 0.05$ m). (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **Given:** $h = 0.05$ m, $u(0) = 0$, $u(0.05) = 0.487$, $u(0.10) = 0.946$ m/s, true $(du/dy) = 9.4$ s⁻¹. **Step 1 — Forward Difference (using $y = 0.05$ and $y = 0.10$):** $$ \begin{aligned} \left. \frac{du}{dy} \right|_{\text{FD}} = \frac{u(0.10) - u(0.05)}{0.05} = \frac{0.946 - 0.487}{0.05} = 9.18 \text{ s}^{-1} \end{aligned} $$ **Percentage error:** $$ \begin{aligned} \% \text{Error}_{\text{FD}} = \left\vert \frac{9.18 - 9.4}{9.4} \right\vert \times 100 = 2.34 \% \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Backward Difference (using $y = 0$ and $y = 0.05$):** $$ \begin{aligned} \left. \frac{du}{dy} \right|_{\text{BD}} = \frac{u(0.05) - u(0.00)}{0.05} = \frac{0.487 - 0.000}{0.05} = 9.74 \text{ s}^{-1} \end{aligned} $$ **Percentage error:** $$ \begin{aligned} \% \text{Error}_{\text{BD}} = \left\vert \frac{9.74 - 9.4}{9.4} \right\vert \times 100 = 3.62 \% \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Central Difference (using $y = 0$ and $y = 0.10$):** $$ \begin{aligned} \left. \frac{du}{dy} \right|_{\text{CD}} = \frac{u(0.10) - u(0.00)}{2 \times 0.05} = \frac{0.946 - 0.000}{0.10} = 9.46 \text{ s}^{-1} \end{aligned} $$ **Percentage error:** $$ \begin{aligned} \% \text{Error}_{\text{CD}} = \left\vert \frac{9.46 - 9.4}{9.4} \right\vert \times 100 = 0.64 \% \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Summary Table:** | Scheme | Gradient (s⁻¹) | Percentage Error | Order | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Forward Difference | 9.18 | 2.34 % | $\mathcal{O}(h)$ | | Backward Difference | 9.74 | 3.62 % | $\mathcal{O}(h)$ | | Central Difference | 9.46 | 0.64 % | $\mathcal{O}(h^2)$ | **Step 5 — Engineering Conclusion:** The central-difference scheme gives the smallest error (0.64 %), confirming the theoretical $\mathcal{O}(h^2)$ accuracy claim.
