In marine CFD, this is why central differences are used in the interior of the boundary layer mesh, while one-sided differences are reserved for the wall and free-surface boundaries. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write the percentage error formula incorrectly (e.g., forgetting the absolute value, or dividing by the approximate value instead of the true value). KTU valuation key gives **zero credit** for a correctly computed numerical value if the error formula is wrong. Use: $\% \text{Error} = \vert ( \text{Approx} - \text{True} ) / \text{True} \vert \times 100$. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **Three approaches to fluid flow problems:** Analytical (exact, simple geometry), Numerical / CFD (approximate, complex geometry), Experimental (real, costly). - **CFD is the numerical method** — it is *one of three* pillars, not the only one. The full engineering answer requires all three. - **Validation Pyramid:** Experiment at the top (gold standard) → CFD in the middle (must be validated) → Analytical at the base (insight + limit cases). - **Forward difference:** $f'(x) \approx [f(x+h) - f(x)] / h$, accuracy $\mathcal{O}(h)$. - **Backward difference:** $f'(x) \approx [f(x) - f(x-h)] / h$, accuracy $\mathcal{O}(h)$. - **Central difference:** $f'(x) \approx [f(x+h) - f(x-h)] / 2h$, accuracy $\mathcal{O}(h^2)$. - **Second derivative (Laplacian):** $f''(x) \approx [f(x+h) - 2f(x) + f(x-h)] / h^2$, accuracy $\mathcal{O}(h^2)$ — this is the discrete diffusion operator in Navier–Stokes. - **Central differences are more accurate** because odd-power error terms cancel — the dominant remaining error is proportional to $h^2$, not $h$. - **Mesh-independence study is mandatory** before trusting any CFD result — change $h$ by a factor of 2, and the central-difference result should change by a factor of 4 (since error $\propto h^2$). - **Marine applications:** Hull resistance, propeller open-water characteristics, seakeeping, slam loading, sloshing in LNG tanks, and cavitation prediction — all benefit from the hybrid Analytical + CFD + Experimental approach. - **Industry benchmark:** A well-validated CFD result is accepted by IACS / DNV when it is within **2 % of towing-tank data** for a benchmark hull like KCS or KVLCC2. - **Common exam traps:** Saying "CFD has replaced experiments" (wrong — complementary only); forgetting absolute value in percentage error; writing finite-difference formula with wrong sign; missing the order-of-accuracy statement (always quote $\mathcal{O}(h)$ or $\mathcal{O}(h^2)$ explicitly).

topics.title: Modelling vs Experimentation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST635 - COMPUTATIONAL FLUID DYNAMICS IN MARINE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Modelling vs Experimentation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In marine hydrodynamics and ship design, two complementary engineering approaches exist for predicting fluid flow behaviour around vessels and marine structures:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modelling (Numerical/CFD Approach):** The mathematical representation of physical fluid flow phenomena through numerical solution of the governing partial differential equations (typically the Navier-Stokes equations) on a discretized computational domain, using a digital computer as the primary investigative tool.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Experimentation (Physical/Empirical Approach):** The investigation of fluid flow behaviour through direct measurement and observation on a physical prototype, scale model, or full-scale vessel, employing sensors, flow visualisation, and towing/wave tanks as investigative tools.

Both approaches aim to quantify the same physical reality — the interaction of water with a moving ship hull — but achieve this goal through fundamentally different epistemological pathways.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Marine Engineer's Dilemma

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Navigating Uncharted Waters**

Imagine you are a harbour master designing a new 300 m container ship and need to know its total resistance at 24 knots service speed. You have two tools at your disposal:

- **Experimentation** is like **sailing an actual 3-metre model of the ship in a model-testing basin** at RIVA, Trivandrum. You watch it move through real water, you feel the resistance through a force balance, you measure waves with capacitance gauges. It is **direct, real, and trusted** — but expensive (a single model costs ₹15–30 lakhs to build) and limited to the conditions you test.

- **Modelling (CFD)** is like **simulating the ship in STAR-CCM+ or OpenFOAM on your laptop**. You set up the geometry, define the free surface, choose a turbulence model (say, *k-ω SST*), and let the solver iterate until convergence. It is **fast, cheap to re-run with design changes, and provides the full 3-D flow field** — but every result is contingent on the validity of your turbulence closure and mesh quality.

A skilled naval architect uses **both**: CFD for early-stage design exploration, and model testing for final validation and regulatory certification.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Governing Metrics

The following dimensionless numbers govern the choice of modelling vs experimentation in marine flows:

- **Reynolds Number (Re)** — ratio of inertial to viscous forces
- **Froude Number (Fr)** — ratio of inertial to gravitational forces
- **Weber Number (We)** — ratio of inertial to surface tension forces
- **Cavitation Number (σ)** — ratio of pressure to dynamic head
- **Mach Number (M)** — ratio of flow to sound speed (relevant for propeller tips)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Philosophical Distinction

The choice between modelling and experimentation is not a matter of one being superior; rather, each possesses intrinsic strengths and blind spots dictated by the **epistemology of measurement**.

### 2.1.1 The Modelling (CFD) Approach — Path of Mathematical Idealisation

The computational modeller proceeds through a structured pipeline:

1. **Geometric Idealisation** — The continuous, complex hull surface is approximated by a finite set of surface patches (NURBS, IGES/STEP import).
2. **Governing Equation Selection** — The Navier-Stokes equations (mass, momentum) plus turbulence closure (RANS, LES, DES) are chosen.
3. **Discretisation** — The continuous domain is divided into a finite number of control volumes or finite elements (**mesh generation**).
4. **Boundary & Initial Conditions** — Inlet/outlet velocity profiles, no-slip walls, free-surface boundary, initial field.
5. **Numerical Solution** — Iterative algorithms (SIMPLE, PISO, Fractional Step) march toward a converged solution.
6. **Post-Processing** — Pressure, velocity, shear stress, wave elevation, cavitation risk are extracted.

### 2.1.2 The Experimentation Approach — Path of Empirical Observation

The experimentalist proceeds through:

1. **Scale Model Fabrication** — A geometrically similar model (typically 1:10 to 1:40 for ship models) is built, often from paraffin wax or fibre-reinforced plastic.
2. **Towing Tank / Circulating Water Channel** — The model is subjected to controlled flow at a known speed.
3. **Instrumentation** — Force balances (resistance), pressure transducers, PIV (Particle Image Velocimetry), LDV (Laser Doppler Velocimetry).
4. **Data Acquisition** — Time-averaged and instantaneous measurements are recorded.
5. **Dimensional Scaling & Correlation** — Results are extrapolated to full-scale via similarity laws (Froude, Reynolds).

## 2.2 Comparative Analysis — Advantages & Limitations

| Aspect | Modelling (CFD) | Experimentation (Physical) |
|---|---|---|
| **Cost per Run** | Low (only CPU-time) | High (model fabrication + facility fees) |
| **Information Content** | Complete 3-D + time-resolved field | Limited to instrumented locations |
| **Iterative Design Changes** | Trivial (geometry edit) | Costly (new model build) |
| **Real Fluid Effects (cavitation, sheet cavitation)** | Partially captured (with limits) | Fully captured |
| **Full-Scale Replicability** | Direct (no scaling required) | Requires similarity laws |
| **Boundary Layer Resolution** | Depends on mesh y+ | Captured naturally |
| **Reynolds Number Realism** | Possible if model scale = 1:1 | Model Re ≪ full-scale Re |
| **Verification Difficulty** | Grid-independence studies | Repeatability tests |
| **Validation Difficulty** | Needs comparison data | IS the comparison data |
| **Time per Study** | Hours to weeks | Days to months |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Similarity & Scaling Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarises every formula a KTU student must master for the *Modelling vs Experimentation* topic. Critical similarity parameters are highlighted.

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reynolds Number (viscous similarity) | $Re = \dfrac{\rho U L}{\mu} = \dfrac{U L}{\nu}$ | $\rho$: density (kg/m³), $U$: velocity (m/s), $L$: characteristic length (m), $\mu$: dynamic viscosity (Pa·s), $\nu$: kinematic viscosity (m²/s) |
| 2 | Froude Number (gravity similarity) | $Fr = \dfrac{U}{\sqrt{g L}}$ | $g$: acceleration due to gravity ≈ **9.81 m/s²** |
| 3 | Weber Number (surface tension) | $We = \dfrac{\rho U^{2} L}{\sigma}$ | $\sigma$: surface tension (N/m) |
| 4 | Euler Number (pressure coefficient) | $Eu = \dfrac{p}{\rho U^{2}}$ | Dimensionless pressure |
| 5 | Cavitation Number | $\sigma_c = \dfrac{p_\infty - p_v}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^{2}}$ | $p_v$: vapour pressure |
| 6 | Geometric Scale Ratio | $\lambda = L_m / L_p$ | Subscript m: model, p: prototype |
| 7 | Velocity Scale (Froude) | $U_m = U_p \sqrt{\lambda}$ | From Fr similarity |
| 8 | Velocity Scale (Reynolds) | $U_m = U_p / \lambda$ | From Re similarity |
| 9 | Force Scale (geometric) | $F_m / F_p = \lambda^{2}$ (Froude, wetted) | Wave-making scales with $\lambda^{2}$ |
| 10 | Resistance Coefficient | $C_T = \dfrac{R}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho S U^{2}}$ | $S$: wetted surface area |
| 11 | ITTC 1978 Friction Line | $C_F = \dfrac{0.075}{(\log_{10} Re - 2)^{2}}$ | Empirical full-scale form drag |
| 12 | Skin Friction Form Factor | $(1 + k) = C_F (\text{model}) / C_F (\text{plate})$ | Used in Reynolds number effects extrapolation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The Froude number is the **dominant similarity parameter in ship model testing** because gravity-driven wave-making resistance dominates viscous resistance for most merchant vessels. This is why ship resistance is routinely scaled by **Froude similarity**, while Reynolds number effects are corrected post-hoc using the **ITTC 1978 method**.

## 2.4 The Verification & Validation (V&V) Paradigm

A critical bridge between modelling and experimentation:

- **Verification** — *"Are we solving the equations right?"* (deals with numerical error: mesh, timestep, iterative convergence).
- **Validation** — *"Are we solving the right equations?"* (deals with physical model error: comparison with benchmark experiment).

The **ASME V&V 20-2009 standard** provides the rigorous framework for uncertainty quantification in CFD.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the marine industry, the modelling–experimentation duality manifests across the entire ship design lifecycle:

- **Concept Design:** CFD (RANS) for hull-form parametric studies — iterate 50 designs in one week.
- **Contract Design:** CFD wave-pattern validation + EFD (Experimental Fluid Dynamics) for powering prediction.
- **Detailed Design:** EFD model tests for resistance, propulsion, seakeeping, manoeuvring (mandatory by classification societies — IRS, DNV, Lloyd's Register).
- **Cavitation & Propeller Design:** CFD + cavitation tunnel experiments (essential, since sheet cavitation onset is hard to predict numerically).
- **Full-Scale Trials:** Sea trials on the actual vessel for final validation (gives the *Validation Database* used to certify future CFD codes).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Why Froude Similarity Dominates Ship Model Testing

We begin from the **incompressible Navier-Stokes equations** governing the fluid motion around a ship hull:

$$
\rho \left( \frac{\partial \mathbf{u}}{\partial t} + \mathbf{u} \cdot \nabla \mathbf{u} \right) = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^{2} \mathbf{u} + \rho \mathbf{g}
$$

$$
\nabla \cdot \mathbf{u} = 0
$$

where $\mathbf{u}$ is the velocity vector, $p$ is the static pressure, $\rho$ is the fluid density, $\mu$ is the dynamic viscosity, and $\mathbf{g}$ is the gravitational acceleration vector.

**Step 1 — Define dimensionless (non-dimensional) variables.**

We non-dimensionalise every variable using a characteristic length $L$, a characteristic velocity $U$, the fluid density $\rho$, and a characteristic time scale $L/U$.

$$
\tilde{\mathbf{x}} = \frac{\mathbf{x}}{L}, \quad \tilde{\mathbf{u}} = \frac{\mathbf{u}}{U}, \quad \tilde{t} = \frac{U t}{L}, \quad \tilde{p} = \frac{p}{\rho U^{2}}, \quad \tilde{\nabla} = L \nabla
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the momentum equation.**

The left-hand side (inertia term) becomes:

$$
\rho \frac{U^{2}}{L}\left( \frac{\partial \tilde{\mathbf{u}}}{\partial \tilde{t}} + \tilde{\mathbf{u}} \cdot \tilde{\nabla} \tilde{\mathbf{u}} \right)
$$

The pressure gradient becomes:

$$
- \frac{\rho U^{2}}{L} \tilde{\nabla} \tilde{p}
$$

The viscous diffusion term becomes:

$$
\frac{\mu U}{L^{2}} \tilde{\nabla}^{2} \tilde{\mathbf{u}}
$$

The body force (gravity) term becomes:

$$
\rho g = \frac{\rho U^{2}}{L} \cdot \frac{g L}{U^{2}} \cdot \tilde{\mathbf{g}} = \frac{\rho U^{2}}{L} \cdot \frac{1}{Fr^{2}} \, \tilde{\mathbf{g}}
$$

**Step 3 — Divide through by the inertial scale $\rho U^{2}/L$.**

We obtain the **dimensionless Navier-Stokes equation**:

$$
\frac{\partial \tilde{\mathbf{u}}}{\partial \tilde{t}} + \tilde{\mathbf{u}} \cdot \tilde{\nabla} \tilde{\mathbf{u}} = -\tilde{\nabla} \tilde{p} + \frac{1}{Re}\tilde{\nabla}^{2}\tilde{\mathbf{u}} + \frac{1}{Fr^{2}}\tilde{\mathbf{g}}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the dimensionless coefficients.**

For dynamic similarity between a model and a prototype, **every dimensionless coefficient in the governing equations must match**. This produces two conditions:

$$
Re_{m} = Re_{p} \quad \text{(viscous similarity)}, \qquad Fr_{m} = Fr_{p} \quad \text{(gravity similarity)}
$$

**Step 5 — Impose the constraints simultaneously.**

From $Fr_m = Fr_p$:

$$
\frac{U_m}{\sqrt{g L_m}} = \frac{U_p}{\sqrt{g L_p}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{U_m = U_p \sqrt{\lambda}}
$$

where $\lambda = L_m / L_p$.

From $Re_m = Re_p$:

$$
\frac{U_m L_m}{\nu_m} = \frac{U_p L_p}{\nu_p} \quad \Rightarrow \quad U_m = U_p \cdot \frac{L_p}{L_m} \cdot \frac{\nu_m}{\nu_p}
$$

For a **fresh-water model tested in a fresh-water tank** against a **salt-water full-scale ship**, we get $\nu_m / \nu_p \approx 1.07$ — close enough that the Reynolds mismatch is acceptable.

**Step 6 — Why is matching both impossible in practice?**

Equating the two expressions for $U_m$:

$$
U_p \sqrt{\lambda} = U_p \cdot \frac{1}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{\nu_m}{\nu_p}
$$

This requires $\lambda^{3/2} = \nu_m / \nu_p$, which for $\lambda \ll 1$ demands $\nu_m \ll \nu_p$ — physically impossible for the same fluid. **Therefore, exact dynamic similarity is unachievable for ships**, and we compromise by matching the **dominant** parameter (Froude for wave-making) and **correcting** the residual viscous mismatch with the ITTC 1978 line.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Resistance Scaling from Froude Similarity

The total resistance $R$ scales with dynamic pressure and wetted surface area. Using Froude-similar velocity $U_m = U_p \sqrt{\lambda}$ and wetted area scaling $S_m = \lambda^{2} S_p$:

$$
R_m = C_{T,m} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U_m^{2} S_m = C_{T,m} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho \, U_p^{2} \lambda \cdot \lambda^{2} S_p
$$

$$
R_p = C_{T,p} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U_p^{2} S_p
$$

Taking the ratio:

$$
\boxed{\frac{R_m}{R_p} = C_{T,m} \cdot \lambda^{3}}
$$

This is the **fundamental ship-model resistance scaling law** — a *must-memorise* formula for KTU exams.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — ITTC 1978 Extrapolation of Frictional Resistance

The **ITTC 1978 performance prediction method** separates total resistance into frictional and residual components:

$$
C_T = C_F + C_R + C_{AA}
$$

where $C_F$ is the frictional coefficient, $C_R$ is the residual (wave + viscous pressure), and $C_{AA}$ is the air-resistance correction.

The frictional coefficient uses the **Schoenherr** form, integrated by Granville to yield the **ITTC 1978 line**:

$$
\boxed{C_F = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} Re - 2)^{2}}}
$$

**Step-by-step usage:**

1. Compute $Re_p = U_p L_{pp} / \nu_p$ at full scale.
2. Compute $C_{F,p}$ from the ITTC formula.
3. Compute $C_{F,m}$ at model scale.
4. Compute the form factor $k$ from the Prohaska method or the **ITTC 1957 standard**:

$$
k = \frac{C_{F,m} - C_{F,plate}}{C_{F,plate}}
$$

5. Extrapolate residual coefficient:

$$
C_R = \frac{C_{T,m} - C_{F,m}}{1 + k + (C_{AA}/C_{F,m})}
$$

6. Compute full-scale $C_{T,p} = (1 + k) C_{F,p} + C_R + C_{AA}$.

This is **the gold-standard procedure** used by IACS classification societies for resistance prediction.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Choose What?

| Decision Criterion | Choose Experimentation | Choose Modelling (CFD) |
|---|---|---|
| **Iterative design loop** | Avoid (cost-prohibitive) | Preferred |
| **Cavitation onset** | Preferred (tunnel data) | Use with caution |
| **Wave pattern visualisation** | Both work | CFD excels |
| **Final regulatory approval** | Often mandatory | Increasingly accepted with V&V |
| **Reynolds number effects** | Inherently captured | Needs wall-resolved LES or correction |
| **Free-surface dynamics** | Captured naturally | Needs VOF / level-set |
| **Time-to-result** | Weeks | Days |
| **Cost per data point** | High (₹1000s) | Low (₹10s of CPU) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Modelling–Experimentation–Validation Trinity

The following schematic captures the interrelationship between physical experimentation, numerical modelling, and the validation cycle used in modern marine CFD practice.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["PROBLEM DEFINITION"] --> B["SHIP DESIGN REQUIREMENT"]
    B --> C{"CHOICE OF PATH"}
    C -->|"Fast, low-cost, parametric"| D["NUMERICAL MODELLING CFD"]
    C -->|"High-fidelity, final approval"| E["PHYSICAL EXPERIMENTATION"]
    D --> D1["Geometry & Mesh Generation"]
    D1 --> D2["Solver Setup RANS LES"]
    D2 --> D3["Solution & Convergence Check"]
    D3 --> D4["Post Processing Resistance Waves"]
    E --> E1["Model Fabrication 1 by Lambda"]
    E1 --> E2["Towing Tank or Cavitation Tunnel"]
    E2 --> E3["Instrumentation Force Balance PIV"]
    E3 --> E4["Data Acquisition & Scaling"]
    D4 --> F["COMPARISON & VALIDATION"]
    E4 --> F
    F --> G{"AGREEMENT WITHIN TOLERANCE?"}
    G -->|"Yes"| H["VERIFIED & VALIDATED RESULT"]
    G -->|"No"| I["ROOT CAUSE ANALYSIS"]
    I --> I1["Numerical: Mesh BCs Turbulence Model"]
    I -->|"Experimental: Instrumentation Drift Scale Effects"| I2["Modelling: Closure Hypothesis Simplification"]
    I1 --> D2
    I2 --> D2
    H --> J["ENGINEERING DECISION"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — The V&V Workflow in Marine CFD

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    K["VERIFICATION"] --> K1["Code Verification"]
    K --> K2["Solution Verification"]
    K1 --> K1A["Method of Manufactured Solutions"]
    K1 --> K1B["Symmetry Checks"]
    K2 --> K2A["Grid Independence Study"]
    K2 --> K2B["Time Step Independence"]
    L["VALIDATION"] --> L1["Benchmark Experiments"]
    L --> L2["Full-Scale Trials"]
    L1 --> L1A["Tokyo 2015 Workshop Hull"]
    L1 --> L1B["KCS KRISO Container Ship"]
    L2 --> L2A["Sea Trial Data"]
    K --> M["UNCERTAINTY QUANTIFICATION UQ"]
    L --> M
    M --> N["ENGINEERING CONFIDENCE & CERTIFICATION"]
```

## 4.3 Process-Flow — The ITTC Recommended Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0["Model Test at Scale Lambda"] --> P1["Measure R_T_m, C_T_m"]
    P1 --> P2["Compute Re_m and C_F_m"]
    P2 --> P3["Prohaska or Telfer Test for Form Factor k"]
    P3 --> P4["Compute C_R residual"]
    P4 --> P5["Compute Full-Scale Re_p and C_F_p"]
    P5 --> P6["Predict Full-Scale C_T_p"]
    P6 --> P7["R_T_p = C_T_p times 0.5 times rho times U_p squared times S_p"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

### Question 1
**Differentiate between physical modelling and numerical modelling in marine hydrodynamics. Give one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer:**

Physical modelling (experimentation) investigates the fluid flow around a **scale model** of the vessel (typically $\lambda = 1{:}10$ to $1{:}40$) in a towing tank, circulating water channel, or cavitation tunnel, with direct measurement of forces and flow features.

Numerical modelling (CFD) investigates the fluid flow by **solving the governing equations** (Navier-Stokes with turbulence closure) on a discretised computational domain using numerical algorithms.

- **Advantage of Physical Modelling:** It naturally captures all real-fluid effects — turbulence, cavitation, free-surface deformation — without requiring constitutive assumptions.
- **Advantage of Numerical Modelling:** It provides the **complete 3-D flow field** at every point in the domain and is highly economical for parametric design studies.

*(3 marks: 1.5 for definition of each + 1 for advantages)*

### Question 2
**Define Reynolds number and Froude number. State the significance of each in ship model testing.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Reynolds Number:** $Re = UL/\nu$, the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces. In ship model testing, it governs the **boundary layer development** and viscous (frictional) resistance. Similarity in Reynolds number ensures dynamic similarity in the viscous-dominated near-wall flow.

- **Froude Number:** $Fr = U/\sqrt{gL}$, the ratio of inertial forces to gravitational forces. In ship model testing, it governs the **wave-making resistance**, which is dominant for most displacement-type merchant vessels. Froude similarity is the **primary similarity criterion** used in ship-model resistance tests.

*(3 marks: 1 for each definition, 1 for significance)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the procedure of **physical model testing of a ship in a towing tank**. Discuss the steps involved from model preparation to full-scale resistance prediction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **dimensionless form of the Navier-Stokes equations** and explain the conditions for dynamic similarity between a ship model and its prototype. Why is it impossible to achieve complete similarity in practice? **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer for Question A(a) — Physical Model Testing Procedure

**1. Model Fabrication**

A geometrically similar model of the ship is built, typically to a scale of $\lambda = L_m / L_p = 1/20$ to $1/40$, using paraffin wax, wood, or fibre-reinforced plastic. The hull form is accurately reproduced within ITTC tolerances (typically ±0.5 mm for a 5 m model).

**2. Model Instrumentation**

The model is fitted with a **dynamometric force balance** at its support strut to measure total resistance $R_{T,m}$. Resistance components are measured via:
- Longitudinal force balance → total resistance
- Pressure taps on the hull → pressure distribution
- Wave probes in the tank → wave-pattern measurement
- PIV (when available) → velocity field visualisation

**3. Towing Test Procedure**

The model is towed at discrete speeds covering the required **Froude number range**:

$$
Fr = \frac{U}{\sqrt{g L_{pp}}}
$$

where $L_{pp}$ is the length between perpendiculars. Resistance is recorded after the model reaches steady state (transients die out within 2–3 model lengths of travel).

**4. Data Reduction — ITTC 1978 Method**

The measured model resistance is separated into frictional and residual components:

$$
C_{T,m} = C_{F,m} + C_R + C_{AA}
$$

The **form factor** $k$ is determined experimentally by the Prohaska method (testing two models of different lengths but similar form) or from the Telfer friction line.

**5. Full-Scale Extrapolation**

Using $C_F$ from the ITTC 1978 line:

$$
C_{T,p} = (1 + k)\, C_{F,p} + C_R + C_{AA}
$$

The full-scale resistance is then:

$$
R_{T,p} = C_{T,p} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho S_p U_p^{2}
$$

**6. Propulsion Prediction**

Self-propulsion tests with a model propeller in a separate set-up provide the **effective power**, which, combined with the still-water resistance, yields the **brake power** required by the parent engine.

---

### Model Answer for Question A(b) — Derivation of Dynamic Similarity Conditions

We begin from the incompressible Navier-Stokes equations:

$$
\rho \left( \frac{\partial \mathbf{u}}{\partial t} + \mathbf{u}\cdot\nabla\mathbf{u} \right) = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^{2}\mathbf{u} + \rho\mathbf{g}
$$

Non-dimensionalise with $L$, $U$, $L/U$, $\rho U^2$:

$$
\tilde{\mathbf{x}}=\frac{\mathbf{x}}{L}, \quad \tilde{\mathbf{u}}=\frac{\mathbf{u}}{U}, \quad \tilde{t}=\frac{U t}{L}, \quad \tilde{p}=\frac{p}{\rho U^{2}}
$$

Substituting and dividing by $\rho U^2/L$:

$$
\frac{\partial \tilde{\mathbf{u}}}{\partial \tilde{t}} + \tilde{\mathbf{u}}\cdot\tilde{\nabla}\tilde{\mathbf{u}} = -\tilde{\nabla}\tilde{p} + \frac{1}{Re}\tilde{\nabla}^{2}\tilde{\mathbf{u}} + \frac{1}{Fr^{2}}\tilde{\mathbf{g}}
$$

For **dynamic similarity**, the dimensionless coefficients in the equation must be identical for model and prototype. Hence:

$$
Re_m = Re_p \quad \text{and} \quad Fr_m = Fr_p
$$

**Why exact similarity is impossible:**

Equating velocity scales from both conditions:

- From Froude: $U_m = U_p\sqrt{\lambda}$
- From Reynolds: $U_m = U_p \cdot (1/\lambda)\cdot(\nu_m/\nu_p)$

These are simultaneously satisfied only if $\lambda^{3/2} = \nu_m/\nu_p$, which is impossible for typical ship model scales ($\lambda \ll 1$). Therefore, **Froude similarity is enforced** (dominant physical effect), and the **Reynolds number mismatch is corrected post-hoc** via the ITTC 1978 line.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Discuss the advantages and limitations of **Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD)** compared with physical model testing in ship hydrodynamics. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 200 m full-scale ship ($\lambda = L_p$) is to be tested at a model scale of $\lambda = 1/25$. If the full-scale speed is 25 knots, determine: **(i)** the model speed using Froude similarity, **(ii)** the model Reynolds number if seawater at full scale has $\nu_p = 1.05 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s and fresh water at model scale has $\nu_m = 1.10 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s, and **(iii)** the ratio of model to full-scale total resistance assuming $C_T$ remains constant. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer for Question B(a) — CFD vs EFD Comparison

**Advantages of CFD over physical testing:**

1. **Economy and Speed** — Once validated, CFD allows rapid parametric hull-form studies (typically 20–50 designs in the time taken for one model fabrication and test cycle).
2. **Complete Field Information** — CFD provides the **full 3-D velocity, pressure, and turbulence field**; experiments sample only instrumented locations.
3. **No Scale Effects** — CFD can be run at the actual full-scale Reynolds number; experiments are always at reduced $Re$.
4. **Ideal Conditions** — CFD isolates individual effects (e.g., viscosity alone) without ambient disturbances.
5. **Cost per Iteration** — CPU-time cost is far lower than model fabrication + tank hire costs.

**Limitations of CFD:**

1. **Turbulence Closure** — RANS models (k-ω SST, k-ε) approximate turbulence; LES/DES remain computationally expensive for full ships.
2. **Free Surface** — Capturing complex wave breaking, green-water, and air entrainment is numerically challenging (VOF issues).
3. **Cavitation Modelling** — Sheet cavitation onset is hard to predict; specialised solvers (Schnerr-Sauer) are still under development.
4. **Grid Dependence** — Solution quality depends on mesh resolution, wall $y^+$, and time-step choice.
5. **Validation Required** — Without experimental data, CFD is unverified for novel hull forms; IACS acceptance still requires model tests for final approval.

---

### Model Answer for Question B(b) — Numerical Problem Solution

**Given:**
- $L_p = 200$ m, $L_m = 200/25 = 8$ m, $\lambda = 1/25$
- $U_p = 25$ knots = $25 \times 0.5144 = 12.86$ m/s
- $\nu_p = 1.05 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s, $\nu_m = 1.10 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s

**(i) Model Speed from Froude Similarity:**

$$
U_m = U_p \sqrt{\lambda} = 12.86 \times \sqrt{1/25} = 12.86 \times 0.2
$$

$$
\boxed{U_m = 2.572 \text{ m/s} = 5.0 \text{ knots}}
$$

**(ii) Model Reynolds Number:**

$$
Re_m = \frac{U_m L_m}{\nu_m} = \frac{2.572 \times 8}{1.10 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{20.576}{1.10 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
\boxed{Re_m \approx 1.87 \times 10^{7}}
$$

For comparison, full-scale $Re_p = (12.86 \times 200) / (1.05 \times 10^{-6}) \approx 2.45 \times 10^{9}$.

The Reynolds ratio is $Re_m/Re_p \approx 1/131$, demonstrating why Reynolds number effects must be corrected using the ITTC line.

**(iii) Resistance Ratio at constant $C_T$:**

$$
R_m / R_p = C_{T,m} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_m U_m^{2} S_m \; / \; C_{T,p} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_p U_p^{2} S_p
$$

For the same fluid and constant $C_T$:

$$
R_m / R_p = (U_m/U_p)^{2} \cdot (S_m/S_p) = \lambda \cdot \lambda^{2} = \lambda^{3}
$$

$$
\boxed{R_m / R_p = (1/25)^{3} = 6.4 \times 10^{-5}}
$$

This means the model resistance is only about **0.0064%** of the full-scale value — illustrating the extreme care needed in low-force measurement at model scale.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU Examinations**

1. **Confusing Form Factor $k$ with Factor $(1+k)$** — Students routinely forget that the residual coefficient formula uses $(1+k)$ in the denominator. **Penalty: –1 mark.**

2. **Mixing Up Density and Kinematic Viscosity** — In Reynolds number, $\nu$ is kinematic viscosity (m²/s), not density. Use $\rho$ only in dynamic pressure.

3. **Forgetting to Convert Knots to m/s** — KTU problems often give ship speeds in knots; failure to convert (1 knot = 0.5144 m/s) is the single most common computational error. **Penalty: –1 mark, even when the rest of the derivation is correct.**

4. **Confusing Geometric Scale $\lambda$ with its Reciprocal** — When the question states "model is built at a scale of 1:25", $\lambda = L_m/L_p = 1/25$ (not 25). Reading this backwards produces $R_m/R_p = 25^{3} = 15625$, which is physically absurd. **Penalty: –2 marks.**

5. **Writing "Froude Number governs resistance" without qualifying "wave-making resistance"** — Examiners reward precise language: Froude similarity governs **wave-making**, Reynolds similarity governs **viscous/frictional** components.

6. **In CFD questions, omitting the verification vs validation distinction** — KTU 2024 examiners increasingly test this fundamental OBE-level concept. Skipping the V&V discussion in CFD essays loses **2 marks** at minimum.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — *Modelling vs Experimentation***

- [ ] **Modelling** = numerical (CFD); **Experimentation** = physical (towing tank).
- [ ] Two primary similarity numbers in ship model testing: **Froude** (gravity, dominant for merchant ships) and **Reynolds** (viscous, corrected via ITTC).
- [ ] The **Navier-Stokes equations** non-dimensionalised yield $Re$ and $Fr$ as the natural dimensionless groups.
- [ ] **Exact dynamic similarity is impossible** for ship models because $Re$ and $Fr$ similarity demand incompatible velocity scaling.
- [ ] **Froude scaling** gives $U_m = U_p \sqrt{\lambda}$ and $R_m / R_p = \lambda^{3} C_{T,m}/C_{T,p}$.
- [ ] The **ITTC 1978 method** is the standard for full-scale resistance prediction from model tests.
- [ ] **Verification** = solving equations right (numerical); **Validation** = solving the right equations (physical, against benchmark experiments).
- [ ] CFD provides **complete 3-D flow field**; experiments sample only instrumented points.
- [ ] Experiments capture **all real-fluid effects** (cavitation, breaking waves) naturally; CFD requires special modelling.
- [ ] **CFD advantages:** cost-effective iteration, no scale effects, complete field; **CFD limitations:** turbulence closure dependence, grid dependence, free-surface modelling challenges.
- [ ] **1 knot = 0.5144 m/s** — always convert in KTU numerical problems.
- [ ] **ASME V&V 20-2009** is the international standard for CFD verification & validation uncertainty quantification.
- [ ] **Tokyo 2015 Workshop** and **KCS (KRISO Container Ship)** are the gold-standard CFD benchmark hulls.
- [ ] IACS classification societies (IRS, DNV, Lloyd's) still require model tests for final ship approval — CFD is supplementary, not yet fully replacing EFD for regulatory certification.
- [ ] The **form factor $k$** is determined from model tests (Prohaska or Telfer method) and is essential for full-scale extrapolation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fundamental Principles of Conservation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST635 - COMPUTATIONAL FLUID DYNAMICS IN MARINE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fundamental Principles of Conservation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Three Pillars of CFD: Conservation Laws

**Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD)** is built upon three fundamental physical principles — the **Conservation of Mass**, the **Conservation of Momentum**, and the **Conservation of Energy**. In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for Marine Technology, these are formally introduced as the **Governing Equations of Fluid Flow**, and they form the mathematical backbone of every numerical simulation performed on marine vessels, propellers, hulls, and offshore structures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 1):**
> *"The fundamental principles of conservation state that the physical properties of a fluid system — namely mass, momentum, and energy — can neither be created nor destroyed, but only transformed or transported across the boundaries of a defined control volume."*

In a marine engineering context, this means when seawater flows past a ship hull, the **mass of fluid entering** a control volume around the hull must equal the **mass leaving it** (plus any accumulation). Similarly, the **momentum change** of the fluid equals the **net force** exerted on it (viscous, pressure, and body forces), and the **energy entering** the system minus energy leaving equals the **work done plus heat transfer**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The "Bank Account" Model

Imagine a fluid domain as a **bank account**:

- **Money in the account** = Total mass, momentum, or energy inside the control volume at a given instant.
- **Deposits** = Inflow of that property across the control surface.
- **Withdrawals** = Outflow across the control surface.
- **Interest earned / Loan taken** = Sources or sinks within the volume (e.g., body forces, heat addition).

> **Conservation Law (Universal Statement):**
> $$\text{Rate of Change inside CV} = \text{Net Inflow} - \text{Net Outflow} + \text{Generation} - \text{Consumption}$$

Just as a bank will never invent or destroy money out of thin air, the fluid will never spontaneously gain or lose mass, momentum, or energy. The CFD solver's job is simply to **balance this account** at every point in space and every instant in time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants in Marine CFD:**
> - **Density of seawater:** $\rho \approx 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> - **Dynamic viscosity of seawater:** $\mu \approx 1.08 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$
> - **Kinematic viscosity:** $\nu = \mu / \rho \approx 1.05 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$
> - **Gravitational acceleration:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

## 1.3 Why These Laws Matter in Marine Technology

For a marine engineer simulating a **KRISO container ship** or a **Wigley hull**, these conservation principles translate directly into:

1. **Hull resistance prediction** — momentum conservation gives the viscous + pressure drag.
2. **Wake field analysis** — mass conservation (continuity) ensures the propeller inflow is correctly captured.
3. **Cavitation risk assessment** — energy conservation (Bernoulli-coupled) reveals low-pressure zones.
4. **Free-surface flow** — energy and mass conservation together govern wave generation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Control Volume Concept

A **Control Volume (CV)** is a fixed region in space through which fluid flows. Its bounding surface is called the **Control Surface (CS)**. Two perspectives are used:

| Perspective | Reference Frame | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Eulerian** | Fixed in space | CFD solvers, Reynolds Transport Theorem |
| **Lagrangian** | Moving with fluid particle | Theoretical derivation, particle tracking |

The bridge between these two viewpoints is the **Reynolds Transport Theorem (RTT)**.

## 2.2 The Reynolds Transport Theorem (RTT) — The Heart of Conservation

RTT converts a Lagrangian statement ("follow a fluid parcel") into an Eulerian statement ("look at a fixed region in space"). It is the **single most important tool** for deriving all conservation laws in integral form.

For any extensive fluid property $N$ (mass, momentum, energy) with intensive counterpart $\eta$ (per unit mass):

$$\frac{D}{Dt} \int_{CV} \eta \, \rho \, dV = \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{CV} \eta \, \rho \, dV + \oint_{CS} \eta \, \rho \, (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA$$

Where:
- $\frac{D}{Dt}$ = **Material (substantial) derivative** — rate of change following a fluid particle
- $\frac{\partial}{\partial t}$ = **Local derivative** — rate of change at a fixed point
- $\oint_{CS}$ = Surface integral over the closed control surface
- $\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}$ = Velocity component normal to the control surface
- $\hat{n}$ = Outward unit normal vector

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** RTT is the **starting point** for deriving the integral forms of all three conservation equations. Examiner questions often ask: *"State and explain the Reynolds Transport Theorem."* (Typical 7-mark question.)

## 2.3 The Three Conservation Laws in Compact Form

### (a) Conservation of Mass (Continuity Equation)
With $N = m$ and $\eta = 1$:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{CV} \rho \, dV + \oint_{CS} \rho \, (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = 0$$

Differential (incompressible) form:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$$

Differential (compressible) form:

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$$

### (b) Conservation of Momentum (Navier-Stokes Equation)
With $N = m\vec{V}$ and $\eta = \vec{V}$:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{CV} \rho \vec{V} \, dV + \oint_{CS} \rho \vec{V} (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = \sum \vec{F}_{ext}$$

Differential form for a Newtonian fluid:

$$\rho \left( \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + \vec{V} \cdot \nabla \vec{V} \right) = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^2 \vec{V} + \rho \vec{g}$$

### (c) Conservation of Energy
With $N = E$ (total energy per unit mass):

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{CV} \rho e_t \, dV + \oint_{CS} \rho e_t (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = \dot{Q} - \dot{W}$$

Differential form:

$$\rho \frac{De_t}{Dt} = \nabla \cdot (k \nabla T) - p (\nabla \cdot \vec{V}) + \Phi + \rho \vec{g} \cdot \vec{V}$$

Where $\Phi$ is the **viscous dissipation function**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **exam-critical formulas** for Module 1. Master these before attempting Part B numerical derivations.

| # | Law / Concept | Integral Form (Eulerian) | Differential Form | Key Assumptions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reynolds Transport Theorem | $\frac{D}{Dt}\int \eta\rho\,dV = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int \eta\rho\,dV + \oint \eta\rho(\vec V\cdot\hat n)\,dA$ | — | Fixed CV, smooth flow |
| 2 | Mass Conservation | $\frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int \rho\,dV + \oint \rho(\vec V\cdot\hat n)\,dA = 0$ | $\partial_t \rho + \nabla\cdot(\rho\vec V) = 0$ | None (universal) |
| 3 | Incompressible Continuity | — | $\nabla\cdot\vec V = 0$ | $\rho = $ const |
| 4 | Momentum Conservation (Newtonian) | $\partial_t\int\rho\vec V\,dV + \oint\rho\vec V(\vec V\cdot\hat n)\,dA = \sum\vec F$ | $\rho(\partial_t\vec V + \vec V\cdot\nabla\vec V) = -\nabla p + \mu\nabla^2\vec V + \rho\vec g$ | Newtonian, constant $\mu$ |
| 5 | Energy Conservation | $\partial_t\int\rho e_t\,dV + \oint\rho e_t(\vec V\cdot\hat n)\,dA = \dot Q - \dot W$ | $\rho De_t/Dt = \nabla\cdot(k\nabla T) + \Phi$ | First Law of Thermo |
| 6 | Body Force per unit volume | $\vec f_b = \rho \vec g$ | — | Gravity only |
| 7 | Surface Force (pressure) | $\vec f_p = -p\hat n\,dA$ | $-\nabla p$ | Isotropic pressure |
| 8 | Viscous Force (Newtonian) | — | $\mu\nabla^2\vec V$ | Stokes' hypothesis |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In ship hydrodynamics, these equations are not abstract — they are **computational tools** that predict:

- **Total resistance** $R_T$ of a hull at service speed $V_s$
- **Propulsive efficiency** $\eta_D$ from wake fraction $w$ and thrust deduction $t$
- **Slamming loads** on bow sections during wave impacts
- **Slipstream velocity** behind rotating propellers (uses mass + momentum balance)

> [!NOTE]
> The **viscous term** $\mu\nabla^2\vec V$ in Navier-Stokes is what generates the **frictional resistance** $R_F$ — typically 60–80% of the total resistance of a modern cargo ship at design speed.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Reynolds Transport Theorem (RTT)

We start with a moving **material volume** (always containing the same fluid particles) $V_m(t)$ at time $t$. After a small time $dt$, the system has moved to a new region $V_m(t+dt)$. We want to relate the rate of change of an integral over the material volume to one over a fixed control volume.

**Step 1: Definition of material derivative for a generic extensive property $N$.**

$$\frac{DN_{sys}}{Dt} = \frac{D}{Dt} \int_{V_m(t)} \eta \rho \, dV$$

This is the rate at which property $N$ changes for the **same fluid particles** as they move.

**Step 2: Express the change between time $t$ and $t + dt$.**

$$\frac{D}{Dt} \int_{V_m} \eta \rho \, dV = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{1}{\Delta t} \left[ \int_{V_m(t+\Delta t)} \eta \rho \, dV - \int_{V_m(t)} \eta \rho \, dV \right]$$

**Step 3: Add and subtract the integral over a fixed control volume $V_{CV}$ that encloses the system at time $t$.**

We decompose the change into three regions:
- **Region I:** Inside $V_{CV}$ but outside $V_m(t+\Delta t)$ → the fluid that **left** the system.
- **Region II:** Common to both $V_{CV}$ and $V_m(t+\Delta t)$ → the fluid that **stayed**.
- **Region III:** Inside $V_m(t)$ but outside $V_{CV}$ → not present here, but by definition zero.

After algebraic manipulation (see standard fluid mechanics texts), we obtain:

**Step 4: Resulting Eulerian RTT statement.**

$$\frac{D}{Dt} \int_{CV} \eta \rho \, dV = \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{CV} \eta \rho \, dV + \oint_{CS} \eta \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA$$

The first term on the right is the **local (storage) rate**, and the second is the **net efflux** (or flux) through the control surface.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Continuity Equation from RTT

**Step 1: Set $\eta = 1$ (mass per unit mass = 1) in the RTT.**

$$\frac{D}{Dt} \int_{CV} (1) \rho \, dV = \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{CV} \rho \, dV + \oint_{CS} (1) \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA$$

**Step 2: Recognize the left side is the rate of change of system mass.**

By conservation of mass, the system mass cannot change (mass is neither created nor destroyed):

$$\frac{Dm_{sys}}{Dt} = 0$$

**Step 3: Equate and simplify.**

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{CV} \rho \, dV + \oint_{CS} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = 0$$

**Step 4: Use the Divergence Theorem to convert the surface integral to a volume integral.**

By Gauss's theorem: $\oint_{CS} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = \int_{CV} \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) \, dV$

**Step 5: Combine under a single volume integral.**

$$\int_{CV} \left[ \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) \right] dV = 0$$

**Step 6: Since the CV is arbitrary, the integrand itself must vanish (this is the localization principle).**

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$$

This is the **compressible continuity equation**.

**Step 7: For incompressible flow ($\rho = $ constant), the equation reduces to:**

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0 \quad \text{or in Cartesian:} \quad \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Differential Momentum Equation (Navier-Stokes)

**Step 1: Apply RTT with $\eta = \vec{V}$ (momentum per unit mass).**

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{CV} \rho \vec{V} \, dV + \oint_{CS} \rho \vec{V} (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = \sum \vec{F}_{ext}$$

**Step 2: Identify the external forces.** Two categories:

(a) **Body forces** (gravity, electromagnetic): $\vec{F}_b = \int_{CV} \rho \vec{g} \, dV$

(b) **Surface forces** (pressure + viscous stresses):

$$\vec{F}_s = \oint_{CS} \boldsymbol{\tau} \cdot \hat{n} \, dA = - \oint_{CS} p \hat{n} \, dA + \oint_{CS} \boldsymbol{\tau}_{visc} \cdot \hat{n} \, dA$$

**Step 3: Apply Divergence Theorem to convert surface forces to volume integrals.**

$$\oint_{CS} (-p\hat{n}) \, dA = -\int_{CV} \nabla p \, dV$$

$$\oint_{CS} \boldsymbol{\tau}_{visc} \cdot \hat{n} \, dA = \int_{CV} \nabla \cdot \boldsymbol{\tau}_{visc} \, dV$$

**Step 4: For a Newtonian fluid with constant viscosity, the viscous stress tensor divergence simplifies to:**

$$\nabla \cdot \boldsymbol{\tau}_{visc} = \mu \nabla^2 \vec{V}$$

This uses **Stokes' hypothesis**: $\lambda = -\frac{2}{3}\mu$ (bulk viscosity).

**Step 5: Substitute all into the momentum RTT and apply the localization principle.**

$$\frac{\partial (\rho \vec{V})}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V} \vec{V}) = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^2 \vec{V} + \rho \vec{g}$$

**Step 6: For constant $\rho$ (incompressible), simplify using $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$:**

$$\rho \left( \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + \vec{V} \cdot \nabla \vec{V} \right) = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^2 \vec{V} + \rho \vec{g}$$

This is the famous **Navier-Stokes equation** — the cornerstone of viscous marine hydrodynamics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The non-linear advection term $\vec{V} \cdot \nabla \vec{V}$ is what makes Navier-Stokes notoriously difficult to solve analytically. This is precisely why **CFD discretization techniques** (FVM, FEM, FDM) are essential.

## 3.4 Non-Dimensionalization: The Reynolds Number

**Step 1: Introduce characteristic scales** — length $L_c$, velocity $V_c$, time $t_c = L_c / V_c$, pressure $p_c = \rho V_c^2$.

**Step 2: Define dimensionless variables.** $\vec{V}^* = \vec{V}/V_c$, $\nabla^* = L_c \nabla$, $t^* = t/t_c$, $p^* = p/p_c$.

**Step 3: Substitute into the incompressible Navier-Stokes equation.**

$$\frac{\partial \vec{V}^*}{\partial t^*} + \vec{V}^* \cdot \nabla^* \vec{V}^* = -\nabla^* p^* + \frac{1}{Re} \nabla^{*2} \vec{V}^* + \frac{1}{Fr^2} \hat{g}$$

**Step 4: Identify the dimensionless groups.**

$$Re = \frac{\rho V_c L_c}{\mu} \quad \text{(Reynolds number — inertia / viscous ratio)}$$

$$Fr = \frac{V_c}{\sqrt{g L_c}} \quad \text{(Froude number — inertia / gravity ratio)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Marine relevance:** A modern VLCC at $V_s = 15$ knots with $L_{pp} = 300$ m has $Re \approx 2 \times 10^9$ — firmly in the **turbulent regime**. The Froude number for the same ship is $Fr \approx 0.25$, classifying the flow in the **displacement regime** (no significant wave-making). Both non-dimensional numbers directly emerge from the conservation equations.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Architecture of the Three Conservation Laws

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Reynolds Transport Theorem RTT Core Engine"] --> B["Conservation of Mass Continuity Equation"]
    A --> C["Conservation of Momentum Navier Stokes"]
    A --> D["Conservation of Energy First Law"]

    B --> B1["Incompressible Del dot V equals zero"]
    B --> B2["Compressible D rho Dt plus Del dot rho V equals zero"]

    C --> C1["Inertia Term rho D V Dt"]
    C --> C2["Pressure Force minus Del p"]
    C --> C3["Viscous Force mu Del squared V"]
    C --> C4["Body Force rho g"]

    D --> D1["Conduction Del dot k Del T"]
    D --> D2["Viscous Dissipation Phi"]
    D --> D3["Pressure Work p Del dot V"]

    C1 --> E["Marine Applications"]
    C2 --> E
    C3 --> E
    C4 --> E

    E --> E1["Hull Resistance Prediction"]
    E --> E2["Propeller Wake Analysis"]
    E --> E3["Wave Load Computation"]
    E --> E4["Cavitation Risk Mapping"]

    style A fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#0e7490,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#0e7490,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#0e7490,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style E1 fill:#fef3c7,color:#000000
    style E2 fill:#fef3c7,color:#000000
    style E3 fill:#fef3c7,color:#000000
    style E4 fill:#fef3c7,color:#000000
```

## 4.2 Block-Level CFD Solution Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Physical Problem Ship Hull Flow"] --> P2["Define Control Volume CV"]
    P2 --> P3["Apply RTT to each property"]
    P3 --> P4["Derive Integral Conservation Laws"]
    P4 --> P5["Apply Divergence Theorem"]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**State and explain the Reynolds Transport Theorem. Why is it important in CFD?**

**Model Answer:**

The Reynolds Transport Theorem (RTT) is a mathematical statement that relates the rate of change of an extensive property $N$ for a system (material volume of fluid particles) to the rate of change within a fixed control volume (Eulerian frame) plus the net flux of that property across the control surface.

$$ \frac{DN_{sys}}{Dt} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{CV} \eta \rho \, dV + \oint_{CS} \eta \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA $$

**Importance in CFD:**
- It allows formulation of conservation laws in a **fixed, stationary** reference frame, which is essential because CFD grids are Eulerian (space-fixed).
- It is the **starting point** for deriving the integral and differential forms of the continuity, momentum, and energy equations.
- It enables the use of the **Divergence Theorem** to convert surface fluxes to volume integrals, leading to the governing PDEs solved numerically.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner tip:** Mention both the Lagrangian (system/material) and Eulerian (CV) perspectives, and explicitly state that CFD is fundamentally an **Eulerian** methodology.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Write the integral form of the continuity equation and interpret each term physically.**

**Model Answer:**

$$ \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{CV} \rho \, dV + \oint_{CS} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = 0 $$

**Physical interpretation of each term:**

| Term | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|
| $\frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{CV} \rho \, dV$ | Rate of accumulation of mass inside the control volume (storage term) |
| $\oint_{CS} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA$ | Net rate of mass efflux leaving through the control surface |
| Sum = 0 | Mass can neither be created nor destroyed (conservation principle) |

For **steady flow**, the first term vanishes, and **inflow must equal outflow** through the control surface — a critical condition used to set **mass-flow boundary conditions** at hull inlets and outlets in marine CFD.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Choose **ONE** of the following:

---

### **Question A. (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

**(a)** Starting from the Reynolds Transport Theorem, derive the **differential form of the continuity equation** for a compressible fluid. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **incompressible Navier-Stokes equation** from the momentum form of RTT. Identify and physically interpret each term in the final equation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1:** Apply RTT with $\eta = 1$:

$$ \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{CV} \rho \, dV + \oint_{CS} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = 0 $$

**Step 2:** Apply Divergence Theorem:

$$ \oint_{CS} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = \int_{CV} \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) \, dV $$

**Step 3:** Combine and apply localization:

$$ \int_{CV} \left[ \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) \right] dV = 0 \implies \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0 $$

**Assumptions:**
- Fluid is a continuum.
- No mass sources or sinks inside the CV.
- Smooth, single-phase flow.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1:** Apply RTT with $\eta = \vec{V}$:

$$ \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{CV} \rho \vec{V} \, dV + \oint_{CS} \rho \vec{V} (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = \sum \vec{F}_{ext} $$

**Step 2:** Surface forces: $-p\hat{n} + \boldsymbol{\tau}_{visc} \cdot \hat{n}$

**Step 3:** Body forces: $\rho \vec{g}$

**Step 4:** Apply Divergence Theorem, use Stokes' hypothesis, assume constant $\mu$ and incompressible flow ($\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$):

$$ \rho \left( \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + \vec{V} \cdot \nabla \vec{V} \right) = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^2 \vec{V} + \rho \vec{g} $$

**Physical interpretation:**

| Term | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|
| $\rho \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t}$ | Local (unsteady) acceleration of fluid |
| $\rho \vec{V} \cdot \nabla \vec{V}$ | Convective acceleration (inertial non-linearity) |
| $-\nabla p$ | Pressure gradient force per unit volume |
| $\mu \nabla^2 \vec{V}$ | Viscous (diffusive) momentum transfer |
| $\rho \vec{g}$ | Gravitational body force per unit volume |

---

### **Question B. (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]

**(a)** What are the **three fundamental conservation principles** governing fluid flow? Write the integral form of each and explain their physical significance in marine hydrodynamics. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 2D control volume of a marine CFD domain has an inlet on the left (area 0.5 m²) with $\vec{V}_1 = (2, 0)$ m/s and $\rho_1 = 1025$ kg/m³, and an outlet on the right (area 0.5 m²) with $\vec{V}_2 = (1.8, 0)$ m/s and $\rho_2 = 1025$ kg/m³. Top and bottom walls are impermeable. **Verify conservation of mass** and compute the steady-state mass imbalance error. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The three conservation principles are:

1. **Conservation of Mass (Continuity):** $\frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int \rho \, dV + \oint \rho (\vec V \cdot \hat n) \, dA = 0$

   *Marine significance:* Ensures correct mass flux through propeller disc, wake uniformity, and avoids non-physical accumulation near the hull.

2. **Conservation of Momentum (Newton's 2nd Law):** $\frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int \rho \vec V \, dV + \oint \rho \vec V(\vec V \cdot \hat n)\,dA = \sum \vec F$

   *Marine significance:* Predicts total resistance (viscous + pressure) experienced by a ship hull; basis for ITTC 1978 / 1957 friction line correlations.

3. **Conservation of Energy (First Law of Thermodynamics):** $\frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int \rho e_t \, dV + \oint \rho e_t (\vec V \cdot \hat n)\,dA = \dot Q - \dot W$

   *Marine significance:* Captures thermal effects, viscous dissipation heating, and free-surface energy balance for wave-induced loads.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1: Mass flow rate at inlet.**

$$ \dot{m}_1 = \rho_1 (\vec{V}_1 \cdot \hat{n}_1) A_1 = 1025 \times 2.0 \times 0.5 = 1025 \text{ kg/s} $$

$\hat{n}_1 = (-1, 0)$ (outward normal on left face), but $\vec{V}_1 \cdot \hat{n}_1 = 2.0 \times (-1) = -2$ — so $\dot m_{in} = 1025$ kg/s enters.

**Step 2: Mass flow rate at outlet.**

$$ \dot{m}_2 = \rho_2 (\vec{V}_2 \cdot \hat{n}_2) A_2 = 1025 \times 1.8 \times 0.5 = 922.5 \text{ kg/s} $$

$\hat{n}_2 = (+1, 0)$ (right face), so $\vec{V}_2 \cdot \hat{n}_2 = 1.8$ — so $\dot m_{out} = 922.5$ kg/s leaves.

**Step 3: Top and bottom walls.**

$\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n} = 0$ → no contribution.

**Step 4: Mass imbalance.**

$$ \Delta \dot{m} = \dot{m}_{in} - \dot{m}_{out} = 1025 - 922.5 = 102.5 \text{ kg/s} $$

**Step 5: Relative error.**

$$ \text{Error \%} = \frac{\Delta \dot{m}}{\dot{m}_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{102.5}{1025} \times 100 = 10\% $$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign convention trap:** Students frequently forget the **outward normal** direction. A positive $\vec V \cdot \hat n$ means **outflow**; negative means **inflow**. Many mark deductions occur here.
> 2. **Localization principle misuse:** When applying the Divergence Theorem, you must emphasize that the integrand vanishes because **the CV is arbitrary**, not because the integral is zero.
> 3. **Units mismatch:** Pressure must be in **Pa** (N/m²) and density in **kg/m³**. Mixing CGS and SI units loses at least 1 mark.
> 4. **Skipping assumptions:** Examiner expects explicit listing of assumptions (Newtonian fluid, constant $\mu$, incompressible, Stokes' hypothesis) — failure to list them costs 1–2 marks even if the derivation is correct.
> 5. **Confusing material vs. local derivative:** The term $\frac{D\vec V}{Dt} = \frac{\partial \vec V}{\partial t} + \vec V \cdot \nabla \vec V$ must be written **expanded**; collapsing it loses clarity marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Fundamental Principles of Conservation**

- **RTT is the master key** — every integral conservation law is derived from it. Memorize the form: $\frac{D}{Dt}\int \eta\rho\,dV = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int \eta\rho\,dV + \oint \eta\rho(\vec V\cdot\hat n)\,dA$.
- **Three conservation laws** to remember: Mass ($\eta=1$), Momentum ($\eta=\vec V$), Energy ($\eta=e_t$).
- **Continuity equation** (incompressible) reduces to $\nabla \cdot \vec V = 0$. In Cartesian: $\partial u/\partial x + \partial v/\partial y + \partial w/\partial z = 0$.
- **Navier-Stokes** has five terms: unsteady, convective (inertia), pressure, viscous, body force.
- **Newtonian fluid** assumption + **Stokes' hypothesis** ($\lambda = -\frac{2}{3}\mu$) gives $\mu\nabla^2 \vec V$ for the viscous term.
- **Divergence Theorem** is the bridge from integral to differential form.
- **Localization principle:** integrand of an arbitrary volume integral = 0 implies the integrand is identically zero.
- **Dimensional analysis** of Navier-Stokes gives **Reynolds number** $Re = \rho V_c L_c / \mu$ and **Froude number** $Fr = V_c / \sqrt{g L_c}$.
- **Marine relevance:** A VLCC at $V_s = 15$ knots has $Re \sim 10^9$ (turbulent) and $Fr \sim 0.25$ (displacement regime).
- **Sign convention:** Positive $\vec V \cdot \hat n$ → **outflow**; negative → **inflow**. Always include the outward normal in surface integrals.
- **Steady flow simplification:** $\partial/\partial t \equiv 0$ for all terms.
- **Units check:** $\rho$ in kg/m³, $\vec V$ in m/s, $p$ in Pa, $\mu$ in Pa·s, $\mu \nabla^2 \vec V$ in N/m³ (consistent with $\rho \vec a$).
- **CFD consequence:** All three laws together form a closed system of 5 equations (1 continuity + 3 momentum + 1 energy) for 5 unknowns ($\rho, u, v, w, p, T$) in 3D flow.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
