topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST635 - COMPUTATIONAL FLUID DYNAMICS IN MARINE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Reynolds Transport Theorem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Reynolds Transport Theorem (RTT)** is a fundamental mathematical statement in continuum mechanics and Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) that bridges the kinematics of a **material (Lagrangian) system** with the kinematics of a **fixed or moving control volume (Eulerian frame)**. It allows the time rate of change of any **extensive property** of a fluid system to be expressed as a volume integral over a control volume plus a flux integral across its control surface.

In its most general differential form, RTT states:

$$\frac{dB_{sys}}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}\int_{CV} \rho \phi \, dV + \int_{CS} \rho \phi \, (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** RTT is the *mathematical foundation* of the integral form of all governing equations of fluid flow — **Conservation of Mass, Momentum (Navier–Stokes), and Energy**. Every CFD code (FVM, FDM, FEM) ultimately solves discretized versions of these RTT-derived equations. Without RTT, CFD has no governing system.

Where:
- $B_{sys}$ = Extensive property of the **system** (e.g., total mass, total momentum, total energy)
- $\phi$ = **Intensive** (per-unit-mass) counterpart of $B$, such that $B = \int \rho \phi \, dV$
- $\rho$ = Fluid density $[kg/m^3]$
- $\vec{V}$ = Fluid velocity vector $[m/s]$
- $\hat{n}$ = Outward-pointing unit normal to the Control Surface (CS)
- $CV$ = Control Volume (fixed region in space)
- $CS$ = Control Surface (boundary of the CV)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Aquarium vs. The Fish Tank Frame"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for First-Time Learners:**
> Imagine you are tracking the **total number of goldfish** in an aquarium.

- **System (Lagrangian) View:** You follow a *specific parcel of water* as it flows. The fish inside that exact blob of water are always "yours" — you move *with* the water. This is natural, but mathematically expensive because the parcel deforms.

- **Control Volume (Eulerian) View:** You stand still and watch a *fixed window* (say, a glass box) inserted into a flowing river. Fish swim in through the upstream face and out through the downstream face. The number of fish inside your window changes due to (a) fish leaving/entering through the faces, and (b) any fish being born or dying inside the box.

**Reynolds Transport Theorem is the accounting identity that says:**

> *The change of fish following the water = (Change of fish inside the fixed window) + (Net fish flowing out through the window faces).*

In engineering, "fish" can be replaced with **mass, momentum, energy, or any conserved scalar** — and the "aquarium" becomes any region of fluid (a nozzle, ship hull boundary layer, marine propeller wake, etc.).

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics Used in RTT Applications:**
> - **Density of seawater:** $\rho_{sw} \approx \mathbf{1025 \ kg/m^3}$
> - **Density of fresh water:** $\rho_{fw} \approx \mathbf{1000 \ kg/m^3}$
> - **Standard atmospheric pressure:** $P_{atm} = \mathbf{101325 \ Pa}$
> - **Gravitational acceleration:** $g = \mathbf{9.81 \ m/s^2}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Reference Frames — A Comparative Breakdown

| Aspect | Material System (Lagrangian) | Control Volume (Eulerian) |
|---|---|---|
| **Observer** | Moves *with* the fluid | Fixed in space (or moves arbitrarily) |
| **Mass** | Always constant ($\frac{dm_{sys}}{dt}=0$ for closed system) | Can change as fluid crosses CS |
| **Mathematical Identity** | Following a fixed identity of matter | Fixed region; matter enters and leaves |
| **Use Case** | Solid mechanics, structural analysis | Fluid dynamics, CFD, marine hydrodynamics |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Do We Need RTT?**
> Engineers design equipment (pumps, propellers, ship hulls, heat exchangers) that occupy *fixed locations in space*. Therefore, the natural frame for analysis is **Eulerian**. However, the conservation laws are *fundamentally stated* in the **Lagrangian (system) frame**. RTT is the *translator* between these two frames — it is the bridge from fundamental physics to engineering practice.

## 2.2 Logical Steps of the RTT Derivation Strategy

The RTT is constructed through the following structured logic:

- **Step 1 — Define an Extensive Property:** Begin with a system property $B_{sys}(t)$ that can be written as a volume integral:
$$B_{sys}(t) = \int_{V_{sys}(t)} \rho(\vec{r},t) \, \phi(\vec{r},t) \, dV$$

- **Step 2 — Differentiate with Respect to Time:** Apply the **Leibniz Rule** for differentiating integrals with moving, deforming boundaries. The boundary moves with the *fluid velocity* $\vec{V}$ at the surface.

- **Step 3 — Decompose the Velocity:** Express the fluid velocity as:
$$\vec{V} = \vec{V}_{CV} + \vec{V}_{rel}$$
where $\vec{V}_{CV}$ is the velocity of the control surface, and $\vec{V}_{rel} = \vec{V} - \vec{V}_{CV}$ is the fluid velocity *relative to* the CS.

- **Step 4 — Identify the Geometric Term:** The Leibniz term involving the surface motion collapses to a **flux integral** $\int_{CS} \rho \phi (\vec{V}_{rel} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA$ via the **Reynolds Decomposition** and the **Transport (Divergence) Theorem**.

- **Step 5 — Arrive at the Final RTT Form:** Combine the *local (unsteady) accumulation* and the *convective flux* into the canonical RTT statement.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the high-yield reference for all RTT-based derivations. Memorize the forms, not just the symbols.**

| # | Form / Variant | Mathematical Statement | Physical Meaning | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **General RTT** | $\frac{dB_{sys}}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}\int_{CV} \rho \phi \, dV + \int_{CS} \rho \phi (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA$ | Rate of change of $B$ for system = unsteady accumulation + net outflow | Master equation template |
| 2 | **For Fixed (Non-Moving) CV** | $\frac{dB_{sys}}{dt} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{CV} \rho \phi \, dV + \int_{CS} \rho \phi (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA$ | $\vec{V}_{CV}=0$, so $\vec{V}_{rel}=\vec{V}$ | Most CFD problems (ship hulls, nozzles) |
| 3 | **For Moving / Deforming CV** | $\frac{dB_{sys}}{dt} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{CV} \rho \phi \, dV + \int_{CS} \rho \phi (\vec{V}_{rel} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA$ | Includes piston/boundary motion | Propeller pitch control, piston engines |
| 4 | **Mass Conservation (Continuity)** | Set $\phi = 1$ → $0 = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{CV} \rho \, dV + \int_{CS} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA$ | $\frac{dm_{sys}}{dt}=0$ for closed system | Incompressible/compressible flow |
| 5 | **Linear Momentum** | Set $\phi = \vec{V}$ → $\sum \vec{F} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{CV} \rho \vec{V} \, dV + \int_{CS} \rho \vec{V} (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA$ | Newton's 2nd law in CV form | Hull resistance, propeller thrust |
| 6 | **Energy Equation** | Set $\phi = e + \tfrac{V^2}{2} + gz$ → $\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{CV} \rho (e + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz) dV + \int_{CS} \rho (e + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz)(\vec{V}\cdot\hat{n}) dA$ | 1st Law of Thermodynamics | Heat exchangers, marine boilers |
| 7 | **Differential (Local) Form** | $\frac{D(\rho \phi)}{Dt} + \rho \phi (\nabla \cdot \vec{V}) = 0$ → $\frac{\partial (\rho \phi)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \phi \vec{V}) = 0$ | Apply Divergence Theorem to the flux term | Foundation of PDEs in CFD |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Note for Tables:** The table above uses `\cdot` instead of the literal `|` pipe character for any absolute-value or normal-vector notation, in order to avoid breaking KTU-compatible markdown rendering. Students copying these into LaTeX must remember to use `\vert` or `\mid` for norms.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **Marine Technology**, RTT is indispensable for:

- **Hull resistance & propulsion analysis:** Computing net momentum flux through a control volume surrounding a ship hull yields the total resistance force.
- **Cavitation modeling in propellers:** Mass conservation applied via RTT with phase-change source terms predicts vapor cavity growth.
- **Wave–structure interaction:** RTT over a deforming control surface (free-surface following) tracks total energy in ship-generated wave systems.
- **Slipstream analysis:** Momentum flux through a plane downstream of a propeller gives thrust using the **actuator disk theory**, derived directly from RTT.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Reynolds Transport Theorem (Fixed Control Volume)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is the **expected KTU 2024 long-answer content** for the topic. Every algebraic step is shown explicitly.

**Given:** An extensive property $B$ of a fluid system with intensive counterpart $\phi$ (per unit mass), such that:
$$B_{sys}(t) = \int_{V_{sys}(t)} \rho(\vec{r},t) \, \phi(\vec{r},t) \, dV$$

**Goal:** Express $\frac{dB_{sys}}{dt}$ entirely in terms of integrals over a *fixed* control volume $V_{CV}$ and its control surface $S_{CS}$.

---

**Step 1 — Differentiate the system integral with respect to time.**

By the Leibniz integral rule for a region whose boundary moves with the fluid:

$$\frac{dB_{sys}}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}\int_{V_{sys}(t)} \rho \phi \, dV = \int_{V_{sys}(t)} \frac{\partial(\rho \phi)}{\partial t} \, dV + \int_{S_{sys}(t)} \rho \phi \, (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA$$

*Explanation:* The first term captures the *local* (in-place) change of $\rho\phi$ within the deforming volume. The second term captures the rate at which mass carrying property $\phi$ crosses the moving system boundary.

---

**Step 2 — At an instant $t$, equate the system to the control volume.**

At any instant in time, the *system* and the *control volume* occupy the *same region of space* and the *same mass of fluid*. This is the **instantaneous identity condition**:

$$V_{sys}(t) \equiv V_{CV} \quad \text{and} \quad S_{sys}(t) \equiv S_{CV} \quad \text{at time } t$$

This allows us to rewrite the volume integral over the system using the fixed CV:

$$\int_{V_{sys}(t)} \frac{\partial(\rho \phi)}{\partial t} \, dV = \int_{V_{CV}} \frac{\partial(\rho \phi)}{\partial t} \, dV$$

---

**Step 3 — Rewrite the surface integral in terms of the fixed control surface.**

Because the system surface coincides with the CV surface *at the instant*, the surface integral is *also* the same integral over the CS:

$$\int_{S_{sys}(t)} \rho \phi \, (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = \int_{S_{CV}} \rho \phi \, (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA$$

> Here, $\vec{V}$ is the **absolute fluid velocity** and $\hat{n}$ is the outward unit normal of the CS.

---

**Step 4 — Assemble the result for a fixed control volume.**

Substituting Steps 2 and 3 into Step 1:

$$\frac{dB_{sys}}{dt} = \int_{V_{CV}} \frac{\partial(\rho \phi)}{\partial t} \, dV + \int_{S_{CV}} \rho \phi \, (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA$$

Converting the volume integral back into the standard RTT convention using the chain rule on the integrand:

$$\int_{V_{CV}} \frac{\partial(\rho \phi)}{\partial t} \, dV = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{V_{CV}} \rho \phi \, dV \quad \text{(since } V_{CV} \text{ is time-invariant)}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\frac{dB_{sys}}{dt} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{V_{CV}} \rho \phi \, dV + \int_{S_{CV}} \rho \phi \, (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA}$$

This is the **Reynolds Transport Theorem for a fixed control volume**. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation for a Moving / Deforming Control Volume

**Step 1 — Decompose the absolute fluid velocity.**

When the control surface moves with velocity $\vec{V}_{CV}$, the fluid velocity *relative to* the CS is:

$$\vec{V}_{rel} = \vec{V} - \vec{V}_{CV}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the velocity carried across the boundary.**

The flux of $\phi$ across the CS depends only on the *relative* motion between fluid and surface, not on the absolute motion. Therefore, the surface integral becomes:

$$\int_{S_{CV}} \rho \phi \, (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = \int_{S_{CV}} \rho \phi \, (\vec{V}_{rel} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA + \int_{S_{CV}} \rho \phi \, (\vec{V}_{CV} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA$$

**Step 3 — Show that the second term vanishes.**

Apply the **Reynolds Transport Theorem (geometric identity)** to the function $\phi = 1$:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\int_{V_{CV}} dV = \int_{S_{CV}} (\vec{V}_{CV} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA$$

But the time derivative of the volume of a *material* region (or any volume) must be consistent. Applying it carefully with mass conservation ($\rho = $ constant for the geometric motion), one obtains:

$$\int_{S_{CV}} \rho \phi \, (\vec{V}_{CV} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = \frac{d}{dt}\int_{V_{CV}} \rho \phi \, dV - \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{V_{CV}} \rho \phi \, dV$$

**Step 4 — Final result.**

After cancelling the unsteady terms, the surface integral reduces to the relative velocity form:

$$\boxed{\frac{dB_{sys}}{dt} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{V_{CV}} \rho \phi \, dV + \int_{S_{CV}} \rho \phi \, (\vec{V}_{rel} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA}$$

This is the **general RTT for a moving, deforming control volume**. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example — Continuity Equation Derivation

**Problem:** Starting from RTT, derive the integral form of the **conservation of mass** (continuity equation) for a fixed control volume.

**Solution:**

For mass conservation, the extensive property is **mass** $B = m$, and the intensive counterpart is $\phi = 1$ (mass per unit mass = 1). The system mass is conserved:

$$\frac{dm_{sys}}{dt} = 0$$

Apply the **RTT (Fixed CV form)** with $\phi = 1$:

$$0 = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{V_{CV}} \rho (1) \, dV + \int_{S_{CV}} \rho (1) \, (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{V_{CV}} \rho \, dV + \int_{S_{CV}} \rho \, (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = 0}$$

*Physical interpretation:* The rate of mass accumulation inside the CV equals the negative of the net mass flux leaving through the CS. This is the **starting point for the continuity equation used in all CFD solvers (FVM, FDM, FEM).**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: RTT and the Governing Equations of Fluid Flow

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram maps the *functional architecture* of how RTT generates every conservation law used in marine CFD.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Reynolds Transport Theorem RTT] --> B[Select Intensive Property phi]
    B --> C1[phi equals 1]
    B --> C2[phi equals V vector]
    B --> C3[phi equals e plus V squared over 2 plus gz]
    C1 --> D1[Conservation of Mass Continuity Equation]
    C2 --> D2[Conservation of Linear Momentum Navier Stokes]
    C3 --> D3[Conservation of Energy First Law]
    D1 --> E1[Incompressible and Compressible Flow Solvers]
    D2 --> E2[Hull Resistance Propeller Thrust CFD]
    D3 --> E3[Heat Exchanger Marine Boiler Analysis]
    E1 --> F[Computational Fluid Dynamics Marine Applications]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G[Ship Hull Design Cavitation Prediction Wake Analysis]
```

## 4.2 Concept Map: System vs. Control Volume — RTT as the Bridge

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SYS[Material System Lagrangian Frame] -->|Following the fluid| RTT[Reynolds Transport Theorem]
    RTT -->|Fixed in space| CV[Control Volume Eulerian Frame]
    SYS -->|Property B| B1[B sys as integral of rho phi dV]
    CV -->|CV + CS integrals| B2[Unsteady term plus Flux term]
    B1 --> RTT
    B2 --> FVM[Finite Volume Method in CFD]
    FVM --> SOL[Numerical Solution on Marine Geometry]
```

## 4.3 Functional Flow of the RTT Algorithm in CFD

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Identify the extensive property B of interest] --> S2[Step 2: Express B as an integral of rho phi over the system]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Apply the Leibniz rule to differentiate the system integral]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Apply the Reynolds decomposition V equals V CV plus V rel]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Use the Divergence Theorem to convert surface to volume integrals]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Obtain the integral conservation law over the control volume]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Discretize the CV into finite cells for CFD solution]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** All node identifiers above are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `A`, `B`, `C1`, `SYS`, `RTT`, `FVM`) and contain no reserved Mermaid keywords, ensuring safe compilation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State the Reynolds Transport Theorem in its general form for a fixed control volume. Clearly define each term in the equation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The RTT for a fixed control volume states that the time rate of change of an extensive property $B$ of a fluid system equals the local (unsteady) rate of change of $B$ inside the control volume plus the net convective flux of $B$ out of the control surface:

$$\frac{dB_{sys}}{dt} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{CV} \rho \phi \, dV + \int_{CS} \rho \phi \, (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA$$

where $B_{sys}$ is the system property, $\phi$ is the per-unit-mass intensive counterpart, $\rho$ is the fluid density, $\vec{V}$ is the fluid velocity, and $\hat{n}$ is the outward unit normal of the CS. **(3 Marks)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between a material (Lagrangian) system and a control volume (Eulerian) in the context of fluid mechanics.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A **material (Lagrangian) system** is a fixed identity of mass whose boundary always moves with the fluid; its mass is conserved by definition. A **control volume (Eulerian)** is a fixed region in space through which fluid may flow; its mass can change with time. RTT translates the rate of change of a property from the system frame to the control-volume frame, which is the natural choice for engineering problems such as ship hull or propeller analysis. **(3 Marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Starting from the definition of an extensive property, **derive the Reynolds Transport Theorem for a fixed control volume** in its general integral form. State clearly all assumptions used. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Using the RTT, **derive the integral form of the continuity equation** for a fixed control volume. Comment on the physical meaning of each term. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**
- **Step 1 (1 Mark):** Define $B_{sys}(t) = \int_{V_{sys}(t)} \rho \phi \, dV$.
- **Step 2 (2 Marks):** Apply Leibniz rule → $\frac{dB_{sys}}{dt} = \int \frac{\partial(\rho \phi)}{\partial t} dV + \int_{S} \rho \phi (\vec{V}\cdot\hat{n}) dA$.
- **Step 3 (2 Marks):** Invoke the instantaneous identity (system ≡ CV at time $t$).
- **Step 4 (2 Marks):** Write the final RTT: $\frac{dB_{sys}}{dt} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{CV} \rho \phi dV + \int_{CS} \rho \phi (\vec{V}\cdot\hat{n}) dA$.
- *Assumptions:* Newtonian continuum, single-phase fluid, CV is fixed in inertial frame.

**(b) Continuity Equation (7 Marks):**
- **Step 1 (1 Mark):** Identify $\phi = 1$ for mass, and $\frac{dm_{sys}}{dt} = 0$ (conservation of mass).
- **Step 2 (3 Marks):** Substitute into RTT: $0 = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{CV} \rho dV + \int_{CS} \rho (\vec{V}\cdot\hat{n}) dA$.
- **Step 3 (3 Marks):** Physical meaning: First term = rate of mass accumulation inside CV; second term = net mass efflux through CS. For steady flow, the first term vanishes, giving $\int_{CS} \rho (\vec{V}\cdot\hat{n}) dA = 0$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** **Derive the general Reynolds Transport Theorem for a moving, deforming control volume.** Discuss how it differs from the fixed-CV form and when the moving form is required in marine engineering. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Apply the RTT with $\phi = \vec{V}$ to obtain the **integral form of the linear momentum equation** for a fixed control volume, and identify each term. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Moving CV Derivation (7 Marks):**
- **Step 1 (1 Mark):** Decompose $\vec{V} = \vec{V}_{CV} + \vec{V}_{rel}$.
- **Step 2 (2 Marks):** Show that the surface motion contributes a geometric term $\int \rho \phi (\vec{V}_{CV}\cdot\hat{n}) dA$.
- **Step 3 (2 Marks):** Apply the divergence theorem / Leibniz identity to show the geometric term cancels the additional unsteady part, leaving only the relative velocity flux.
- **Step 4 (2 Marks):** Final result: $\frac{dB_{sys}}{dt} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{CV} \rho \phi dV + \int_{CS} \rho \phi (\vec{V}_{rel}\cdot\hat{n}) dA$.
- *Engineering relevance:* Required for **variable-pitch propellers, deforming wave-following CVs, and oscillating control surfaces** in marine applications.

**(b) Momentum Equation (7 Marks):**
- **Step 1 (1 Mark):** Set $\phi = \vec{V}$ and $\frac{d(m\vec{V})_{sys}}{dt} = \sum \vec{F}_{ext}$ (Newton's 2nd law for the system).
- **Step 2 (3 Marks):** Substitute into fixed-CV RTT: $\sum \vec{F} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{CV} \rho \vec{V} dV + \int_{CS} \rho \vec{V} (\vec{V}\cdot\hat{n}) dA$.
- **Step 3 (3 Marks):** Term identification — LHS = body + surface forces (pressure, viscous, gravity); 1st RHS term = unsteady momentum storage; 2nd RHS term = net convective momentum flux (used in thrust and resistance calculations).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Examiner-Verified):**
> 1. **Confusing $\vec{V}$ and $\vec{V}_{rel}$:** For a moving CV, the *relative* velocity $\vec{V}_{rel}$ MUST appear in the flux term, not the absolute $\vec{V}$. Students frequently copy the fixed-CV form and lose **3 to 4 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting the Leibniz Rule Justification:** Simply writing the final RTT equation without deriving the time derivative of the moving boundary integral is considered an *incomplete derivation* — expect a **2-mark penalty**.
> 3. **Mis-defining $\phi$:** The intensive property $\phi$ is *per unit mass* ($B/m$), not per unit volume. Conflating with $\rho\phi$ (per unit volume) leads to incorrect integral forms.
> 4. **Missing the instantaneous identity step:** The crucial step of equating the system and CV at an instant $t$ is often skipped — it is the **conceptual heart** of the theorem and worth **2 marks** explicitly.
> 5. **Sign convention errors:** $(\vec{V}\cdot\hat{n})$ is *positive for outflow* (outward normal). The negative sign in the continuity equation comes from the LHS, not from the integral. Be vigilant.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — RTT, Module 1:**

- **RTT bridges Lagrangian (system) and Eulerian (control volume) frames** — it is the *translator* between fundamental physics and engineering analysis.
- **Master Equation (Fixed CV):** $\frac{dB_{sys}}{dt} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{CV} \rho \phi \, dV + \int_{CS} \rho \phi (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA$
- **Master Equation (Moving CV):** Same form but replace $\vec{V}$ with $\vec{V}_{rel} = \vec{V} - \vec{V}_{CV}$.
- **$\phi$ is intensive, per unit mass:** $B = \int \rho \phi \, dV$. The system mass is always constant; the CV mass is not.
- **Substitute $\phi$ to get conservation laws:**
  - $\phi = 1$ → **Mass (Continuity)**
  - $\phi = \vec{V}$ → **Linear Momentum (Navier–Stokes)**
  - $\phi = e + \tfrac{V^2}{2} + gz$ → **Energy (1st Law of Thermodynamics)**
- **Instantaneous identity:** System and CV coincide at the chosen instant $t$ — this is the *conceptual key* of the derivation.
- **Leibniz Rule** is required to differentiate an integral whose domain is moving/deforming.
- **In CFD:** RTT is the *starting point* of the **Finite Volume Method (FVM)**. Every cell in a CFD mesh is a mini CV.
- **Marine Applications:** Hull resistance (momentum flux), propeller thrust (actuator disk theory from RTT), cavitation modeling (mass with source term), wave–structure interaction (deforming CV).
- **Sign convention:** $(\vec{V}\cdot\hat{n}) > 0$ means **outflow**; $(\vec{V}\cdot\hat{n}) < 0$ means **inflow**.
- **Memorize the four-step derivation:** Define $B$ → Differentiate → Apply identity → Write final integral form.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conservation Equations for Mass
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST635 - COMPUTATIONAL FLUID DYNAMICS IN MARINE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Conservation Equations for Mass

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Conservation Equation for Mass**, also known as the **Continuity Equation**, is the mathematical statement of the fundamental physical principle that **mass can neither be created nor destroyed** within a closed system (excluding relativistic effects and nuclear reactions). In the context of **Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD)**, it is the first and most fundamental governing equation solved for any fluid flow problem.

In its most general form, the continuity equation states that the **rate of change of mass within a control volume** plus the **net rate of mass efflux through the control surface** must equal **zero** for a closed system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The continuity equation forms the bedrock of the **Navier-Stokes equations** and is solved simultaneously with the momentum and energy equations in virtually every CFD code (Fluent, OpenFOAM, STAR-CCM+). Marine applications include wave-structure interaction, propeller cavitation analysis, and free-surface flow simulations.

### Mathematical Statement (Integral Form)

For a **Control Volume (CV)** with a closed **Control Surface (CS)**:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{CV} \rho \, dV + \int_{CS} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = 0$$

Where:
- $\rho$ = Fluid density ($\text{kg/m}^3$)
- $\vec{V}$ = Velocity vector field
- $\hat{n}$ = Outward unit normal vector to the control surface
- $dV$ = Differential volume element
- $dA$ = Differential surface area element

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 💡

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Water Tank with an Inlet and Outlet**
>
> Imagine a **cylindrical water tank** with water flowing in through an inlet pipe and out through an outlet pipe. If the **inlet flow rate** ($Q_{in}$) is **greater** than the **outlet flow rate** ($Q_{out}$), the water level inside the tank **rises** (mass accumulates). If $Q_{in} < Q_{out}$, the water level **falls** (mass depletes). Only when $Q_{in} = Q_{out}$ does the water level remain **constant** (steady-state mass conservation).
>
> The continuity equation is essentially this exact balance, but extended to **infinitesimally small fluid elements** moving through 3D space — making it a **point-wise** statement valid at every location in the flow field.

**Marine Engineering Context**: When simulating water flow past a ship's hull, the continuity equation ensures that the volume of water displaced by the bow is **exactly** equal to the volume of water that fills the void behind the stern — a critical condition for accurate resistance and wake field predictions.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Density of seawater** (at $20°C$): $\rho \approx \mathbf{1025 \; \text{kg/m}^3}$
- **Density of fresh water** (at $20°C$): $\rho \approx \mathbf{998 \; \text{kg/m}^3}$
- **Density of air** (at STP): $\rho \approx \mathbf{1.225 \; \text{kg/m}^3}$
- **Speed of sound in water** (reference for compressibility): $c \approx \mathbf{1500 \; \text{m/s}}$
- **Mach number threshold for compressibility** (in marine flows): $M < \mathbf{0.3}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compressibility Criterion:** Most marine engineering flows (ship hydrodynamics, offshore structures, propeller flows below cavitation) are treated as **incompressible** ($M < 0.3$) since the density variation is less than **5%**. However, high-speed propeller tip flows and underwater explosions require **compressible** treatment.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Fundamental Forms of the Continuity Equation

The mass conservation equation can be expressed in **three equivalent mathematical forms**, each suited for a specific type of analysis:

### 📌 Form 1: Integral Form (Control Volume Approach)

$$\frac{d}{dt} \int_{CV} \rho \, dV + \oint_{CS} \rho \vec{V} \cdot d\vec{A} = 0$$

**Physical Meaning:** This is the most intuitive form. It states that the rate of mass accumulation in a fixed control volume equals the net mass flow rate into the volume.

- **Best Used For:** Finite Volume Method (FVM) discretization in CFD, which is the foundation of codes like OpenFOAM and ANSYS Fluent.

### 📌 Form 2: Differential Form (Eulerian Approach)

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$$

**Physical Meaning:** This is the point-wise (infinitesimal) form, valid at every point in the flow field. It expresses local mass balance.

- **Best Used For:** Analytical solutions, Finite Difference Method (FDM), and deriving Navier-Stokes equations.

### 📌 Form 3: Material/Substantial Derivative Form (Lagrangian Approach)

$$\frac{D\rho}{Dt} + \rho (\nabla \cdot \vec{V}) = 0$$

Where the **material derivative** is:

$$\frac{D}{Dt} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t} + \vec{V} \cdot \nabla$$

**Physical Meaning:** This form follows a **specific fluid particle** as it moves through space, stating that the rate of density change following the particle plus the divergence of velocity gives zero.

- **Best Used For:** Compressible flow analysis, acoustics, and deriving equations along streamlines.

## 2.2 Special Cases & Their Engineering Significance

| **Case** | **Condition** | **Simplified Equation** | **Marine Application** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Incompressible Flow** | $\rho = \text{constant}$ | $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$ | Ship hull resistance, wave loads |
| **Steady Flow** | $\partial/\partial t = 0$ | $\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$ | Steady propeller flow, steady towing |
| **1-D Flow in a Pipe** | Area varies with $x$ | $\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$ | Pipe networks in ballast systems |
| **Compressible Flow** | $\rho \neq \text{constant}$ | Full continuity equation | Cavitation, underwater explosions |
| **2-D Incompressible** | $V_z = 0$, $\rho = \text{const}$ | $\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0$ | 2D hydrofoil analysis |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet 📋

| **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** | **Applicable To** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| $\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$ | General differential continuity | $\text{kg/(m}^3 \cdot \text{s)}$ | All flows |
| $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$ | Incompressible continuity | $\text{s}^{-1}$ | Low-speed marine flows |
| $\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$ | 1-D steady flow | $\text{kg/s}$ | Pipe/duct flow |
| $\frac{D\rho}{Dt} = -\rho \nabla \cdot \vec{V}$ | Material derivative form | $\text{kg/(m}^3 \cdot \text{s)}$ | Compressible flow |
| $\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial z} = 0$ | Cartesian expansion | $\text{kg/(m}^3 \cdot \text{s)}$ | Cartesian CFD |
| $\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (r \rho v_r)}{\partial r} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (\rho v_\theta)}{\partial \theta} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_z)}{\partial z} = 0$ | Cylindrical (steady) | $\text{kg/(m}^3 \cdot \text{s)}$ | Propeller, shaft flows |
| $\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$ | Steady compressible | $\text{kg/(m}^3 \cdot \text{s)}$ | High-speed marine flows |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRTICAL NOTE on Pipe Symbol in LaTeX:** In the above table, expressions like $\nabla \cdot \vec{V}$ use the **dot operator** ($\cdot$) for divergence, not the vertical bar ($\vert$) symbol. This preserves the markdown table integrity.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility in Marine Technology ⚓

1. **Ship Resistance Prediction:** The incompressible continuity equation ($\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$) is solved alongside the Navier-Stokes equations to compute the velocity field around a hull.
2. **Propeller Design:** Cylindrical form of continuity is used in Blade Element Momentum Theory (BEMT).
3. **Cavitation Analysis:** The full compressible continuity is required near cavitating regions where density changes drastically.
4. **Wave-Structure Interaction:** Used in Volume of Fluid (VOF) methods to track free-surface boundaries.
5. **Ballast System Design:** Mass flow rates in piping networks are computed using the 1-D continuity equation.
6. **Sloshing Analysis in LNG Carriers:** Continuity equation is coupled with momentum equations to simulate liquid motion in tanks.

## 2.5 Vector Calculus Foundations Required

> [!NOTE]
> **Prerequisites for KTU Exam Success:** Students must be fluent in the following identities:
>
> $$\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = \rho \nabla \cdot \vec{V} + \vec{V} \cdot \nabla \rho$$
>
> $$\nabla \cdot (\phi \vec{A}) = \phi \nabla \cdot \vec{A} + \vec{A} \cdot \nabla \phi$$
>
> $$\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} \quad \text{(Cartesian)}$$
>
> $$\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (r v_r)}{\partial r} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial \theta} + \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial z} \quad \text{(Cylindrical)}$$

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Integral Form (Reynolds Transport Theorem Approach)

### Step 1: State the Fundamental Conservation Principle

For any system (a collection of matter with fixed identity), the total mass is conserved. The mathematical statement is:

$$\frac{dm_{sys}}{dt} = 0$$

Where $m_{sys}$ is the total mass of the system at time $t$.

### Step 2: Express Mass as a Volume Integral

The mass of a fluid system occupying a volume $V(t)$ is:

$$m_{sys} = \int_{V(t)} \rho \, dV$$

> **Logic:** This step uses the fact that mass is the integral of density over the volume occupied by the system.

### Step 3: Apply the Reynolds Transport Theorem (RTT)

The Reynolds Transport Theorem links the system rate of change to a control volume rate of change:

$$\frac{d}{dt} \int_{V(t)} \rho \, dV = \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{CV} \rho \, dV + \oint_{CS} \rho (\vec{V}_{rel} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA$$

For a **fixed control volume** (the most common case in CFD), $\vec{V}_{rel} = \vec{V}$ (the fluid velocity relative to the control surface).

$$\frac{d}{dt} \int_{V(t)} \rho \, dV = \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{CV} \rho \, dV + \oint_{CS} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA$$

### Step 4: Set the Expression to Zero (Conservation)

Since mass is conserved, the system rate of change is zero:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{CV} \rho \, dV + \oint_{CS} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = 0$$

> **Result:** This is the **integral form of the continuity equation** for a fixed control volume.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Differential Form (Divergence Theorem Approach)

### Step 1: Start with the Integral Form

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{CV} \rho \, dV + \oint_{CS} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = 0$$

### Step 2: Convert the Surface Integral to a Volume Integral

Apply the **Divergence Theorem** (Gauss's Theorem):

$$\oint_{CS} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = \int_{CV} \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) \, dV$$

> **Logic:** The Divergence Theorem states that the flux of a vector field through a closed surface equals the divergence of that field integrated over the enclosed volume.

### Step 3: Substitute Back into the Integral Equation

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{CV} \rho \, dV + \int_{CV} \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) \, dV = 0$$

### Step 4: Combine the Integrals

Since the control volume is **arbitrary** (this is true for any CV in the flow), the integrands must be equal:

$$\int_{CV} \left[ \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) \right] dV = 0$$

### Step 5: Conclude the Differential Form

Since the volume $V$ is arbitrary, the integrand must vanish everywhere:

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0}$$

> **Result:** This is the **differential form of the continuity equation** — the workhorse of CFD solvers.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Material Derivative Form

### Step 1: Expand the Divergence Term

Using the vector identity:

$$\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = \vec{V} \cdot \nabla \rho + \rho \nabla \cdot \vec{V}$$

### Step 2: Substitute into the Differential Form

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \vec{V} \cdot \nabla \rho + \rho \nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$$

### Step 3: Recognize the Material Derivative

The **material derivative** (also called substantial derivative or convective derivative) is defined as:

$$\frac{D\rho}{Dt} = \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \vec{V} \cdot \nabla \rho$$

### Step 4: Rewrite the Continuity Equation

Substituting the material derivative:

$$\frac{D\rho}{Dt} + \rho \nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$$

$$\boxed{\frac{D\rho}{Dt} = -\rho \nabla \cdot \vec{V}}$$

> **Physical Interpretation:** The rate of density change of a fluid particle is proportional to the negative of the velocity divergence. If a fluid element is **expanding** ($\nabla \cdot \vec{V} > 0$), its density must be **decreasing** to conserve mass.

## 3.4 Derivation for Cartesian Coordinates (3D)

Starting from the general differential form:

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z} = 0$$

### Step-by-Step Expansion

**Step A:** Expand the flux derivatives using the product rule:

$$\frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} = u\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial x} + \rho\frac{\partial u}{\partial x}$$

$$\frac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} = v\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial y} + \rho\frac{\partial v}{\partial y}$$

$$\frac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z} = w\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial z} + \rho\frac{\partial w}{\partial z}$$

**Step B:** Group the density terms and velocity divergence terms:

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial z} + \rho\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z}\right) = 0$$

**Step C:** Recognize the material derivative of density:

$$\frac{D\rho}{Dt} + \rho \nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$$

This confirms consistency with the general material derivative form.

## 3.5 Incompressible Flow Simplification

### Step 1: Apply the Incompressibility Condition

For an **incompressible fluid**, density is constant for each fluid particle:

$$\frac{D\rho}{Dt} = 0$$

### Step 2: Substitute into the Material Derivative Form

$$0 + \rho \nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$$

### Step 3: Since $\rho \neq 0$, Divide Both Sides

$$\boxed{\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0}$$

In Cartesian coordinates:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Marine Application Note:** In the incompressible form, the continuity equation **decouples from** the density field and acts as a **kinematic constraint** on the velocity field. This is why in CFD solvers like **SIMPLE** and **PISO** algorithms, a pressure correction equation is derived from the incompressible continuity to enforce this constraint.

## 3.6 Derivation for Cylindrical Coordinates (2D Axisymmetric)

For a 2D axisymmetric flow (e.g., flow around a propeller hub), the continuity equation in cylindrical coordinates $(r, \theta, z)$ with **no swirl** ($\partial/\partial \theta = 0$) becomes:

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (r \rho v_r)}{\partial r} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_z)}{\partial z} = 0$$

### Derivation Steps:

**Step 1:** The divergence in cylindrical coordinates is:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (r v_r)}{\partial r} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial \theta} + \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial z}$$

**Step 2:** For axisymmetric flow, $\partial/\partial \theta = 0$ and $v_\theta = 0$:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (r v_r)}{\partial r} + \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial z}$$

**Step 3:** For steady, incompressible axisymmetric flow:

$$\boxed{\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (r v_r)}{\partial r} + \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial z} = 0}$$

> **Application:** This form is extensively used in **propeller hub vortex analysis** and **towing tank simulations**.

## 3.7 Worked Example: 1D Flow Through a Variable Area Duct

**Problem:** Air ($\rho = 1.2 \; \text{kg/m}^3$) flows steadily through a duct. At section 1, the area is $A_1 = 0.1 \; \text{m}^2$ and velocity is $V_1 = 20 \; \text{m/s}$. At section 2, the area is $A_2 = 0.05 \; \text{m}^2$. Find $V_2$ assuming incompressible flow.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Apply the 1-D incompressible continuity equation:

$$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $V_2$:

$$V_2 = \frac{A_1 V_1}{A_2} = \frac{0.1 \times 20}{0.05} = 40 \; \text{m/s}$$

**Step 3:** Verify the result using mass flow rate:

$$\dot{m} = \rho A_1 V_1 = 1.2 \times 0.1 \times 20 = 2.4 \; \text{kg/s}$$

$$\dot{m} = \rho A_2 V_2 = 1.2 \times 0.05 \times 40 = 2.4 \; \text{kg/s} \quad \checkmark$$

> **Marine Relevance:** This is the principle behind **Venturi flowmeters** used to measure seawater flow rates in engine cooling systems of marine vessels.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Control Volume Concept Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[System: Fixed Mass of Fluid] --> B[Identify Boundaries]
    B --> C[Define Control Volume CV]
    C --> D[Define Control Surface CS]
    D --> E{Inflow or Outflow?}
    E -->|Inflow| F[Mass Enters: Negative Flux]
    E -->|Outflow| G[Mass Leaves: Positive Flux]
    F --> H[Apply Conservation: dm/dt = 0]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Integral Continuity Equation]
    I --> J[Apply Divergence Theorem]
    J --> K[Differential Continuity Equation]
    K --> L[Solve in CFD Domain]
```

```mermaid
    D1 --> E
    D2 --> E

```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: CFD Solver Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Input: Geometry and Mesh] --> B[Initialize Flow Field]
    B --> C[Continuity Equation Module]
    C --> D[Momentum Equation Module]
    D --> E[Energy Equation Module]
    E --> F{Coupled?}
    F -->|Yes| G[SIMPLE or PISO Algorithm]
    F -->|No| H[Sequential Solver]

    H --> I
    I --> J[Convergence Check]
    J -->|Not Converged| C
    J -->|Converged| K[Output: Velocity and Pressure Fields]
    K --> L[Post Processing]

    M --> M2[Wake Field]

```

## 4.4 Derivation Logic Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Conservation of Mass: dm_sys/dt = 0] --> B[Express m as Volume Integral]
    B --> C[Apply Reynolds Transport Theorem]
    C --> D[Obtain Integral Form]
    D --> E[Apply Divergence Theorem]
    E --> F[Obtain Differential Form]
    F --> G[Expand Divergence Term]
    G --> H[Identify Material Derivative]
    H --> I[Obtain Material Form]
    F --> J[Apply rho = constant]
    J --> K[Obtain Incompressible Form]
    I --> L[Apply D rho/Dt = 0]
    L --> K
    K --> M[Solve with Navier Stokes]
    M --> N[CFD Solution]
```

## 4.5 Decision Tree for Choosing the Correct Form

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Start: Identify Flow Type] --> B{Compressible?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Use General Continuity]
    B -->|No| D{Steady?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Use Steady Incompressible]
    D -->|No| F[Use Unsteady Incompressible]
    C --> G{1D, 2D, or 3D?}
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G -->|1D| H[Use Area Velocity Form]
    G -->|2D| I[Use Cartesian 2D Form]
    G -->|3D Axisymmetric| J[Use Cylindrical Form]
    G -->|3D General| K[Use Full 3D Cartesian]
    H --> L[Apply Boundary Conditions]
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[Solve via Numerical Method]
```

# 📓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**State and explain the continuity equation for a compressible fluid in differential form. Write its physical significance in CFD.**

#### Model Answer:

The **continuity equation** for a compressible fluid in differential form is:

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$$

**Physical Significance:**
- It is the **mathematical expression of mass conservation**, stating that mass can neither be created nor destroyed in a fluid flow.
- The first term $\partial \rho/\partial t$ represents the **local (unsteady) rate of change** of density at a fixed point.
- The second term $\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V})$ represents the **net convective mass flux** per unit volume leaving a point.
- In **CFD**, it serves as one of the fundamental governing equations (along with momentum and energy equations) and is **always satisfied** in any valid numerical solution.

> [!NOTE]
> **3-Mark Answer Pattern:** Definition (1 mark) + Physical meaning of each term (1 mark) + CFD relevance (1 mark).

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Derive the continuity equation for steady, incompressible flow in 3D Cartesian coordinates. Why is this form widely used in marine hydrodynamics?**

#### Model Answer:

For **incompressible flow**, $\rho = \text{constant}$ and the continuity equation reduces to $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$.

In 3D Cartesian coordinates $(x, y, z)$ with velocity components $(u, v, w)$:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0$$

**Why widely used in Marine Hydrodynamics:**
1. Most ship and offshore flows have $M < 0.3$, making the incompressible assumption valid.
2. The equation becomes a **simple linear constraint** on velocity, independent of pressure and density.
3. It allows the use of efficient pressure-based solvers (SIMPLE, PISO) widely available in marine CFD codes.

> [!NOTE]
> **3-Mark Answer Pattern:** Final equation (1 mark) + Derivation logic (1 mark) + Marine application justification (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### 📝 Question A — Choice 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

#### (a) **Derive the integral form of the continuity equation using the Reynolds Transport Theorem. Clearly state all assumptions.** (7 marks)

#### Model Solution:

**Assumption 1:** Mass is conserved (no sources or sinks of mass within the system).

**Assumption 2:** The fluid is treated as a continuum.

**Assumption 3:** The control volume is fixed in space (Eulerian frame).

**Derivation:**

**Step 1:** The conservation principle states:

$$\frac{dm_{sys}}{dt} = 0$$

**Step 2:** Express the system mass as a volume integral over the system volume $V_{sys}(t)$:

$$m_{sys} = \int_{V_{sys}(t)} \rho \, dV$$

**Step 3:** Apply the Reynolds Transport Theorem to convert from system to control volume:

$$\frac{d}{dt} \int_{V_{sys}} \rho \, dV = \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{CV} \rho \, dV + \oint_{CS} \rho \vec{V}_{rel} \cdot \hat{n} \, dA$$

**Step 4:** For a **fixed control volume**, the relative velocity equals the fluid velocity: $\vec{V}_{rel} = \vec{V}$.

$$\frac{d}{dt} \int_{V_{sys}} \rho \, dV = \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{CV} \rho \, dV + \oint_{CS} \rho \vec{V} \cdot \hat{n} \, dA$$

**Step 5:** Setting this equal to zero (mass conservation):

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{CV} \rho \, dV + \oint_{CS} \rho \vec{V} \cdot \hat{n} \, dA = 0}$$

> **Mark Distribution (7 marks):** Assumptions (1) + Steps 1-2 (2) + Step 3-4 (2) + Step 5 + final boxed equation (2).

#### (b) **Starting from the general continuity equation, derive the incompressible continuity equation in cylindrical coordinates for steady, axisymmetric flow.** (7 marks)

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1:** The general continuity equation in cylindrical coordinates $(r, \theta, z)$ is:

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (r \rho v_r)}{\partial r} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (\rho v_\theta)}{\partial \theta} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_z)}{\partial z} = 0$$

**Step 2:** Apply the **steady-flow** condition:

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = 0$$

This gives:

$$\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (r \rho v_r)}{\partial r} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (\rho v_\theta)}{\partial \theta} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_z)}{\partial z} = 0$$

**Step 3:** Apply the **axisymmetric** condition. This means no variation in the $\theta$ direction:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta} = 0$$

And there is no swirl velocity: $v_\theta = 0$.

This simplifies to:

$$\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (r \rho v_r)}{\partial r} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_z)}{\partial z} = 0$$

**Step 4:** Apply the **incompressible** condition. Since $\rho = \text{constant}$, it can be taken out of the derivatives:

$$\frac{\rho}{r}\frac{\partial (r v_r)}{\partial r} + \rho \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial z} = 0$$

**Step 5:** Divide by $\rho$ (since $\rho \neq 0$):

$$\boxed{\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (r v_r)}{\partial r} + \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial z} = 0}$$

> **Mark Distribution (7 marks):** Step 1 (1) + Step 2 (1.5) + Step 3 (1.5) + Step 4 (1.5) + Step 5 + boxed equation (1.5).

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Many students forget to justify the **axisymmetric assumption** (i.e., explain that $v_\theta = 0$ and $\partial/\partial \theta = 0$). This is a **1-mark deduction** trigger. Also, failing to show the **division by $\rho$ step explicitly** costs another mark. Always state physical assumptions before mathematical simplifications.

---

### 📝 Question B — Choice 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

#### (a) **Explain the Reynolds Transport Theorem. Using it, derive the differential form of the continuity equation from the integral form.** (7 marks)

#### Model Solution:

**Reynolds Transport Theorem (RTT):**

The RTT provides a mathematical bridge between the **Lagrangian** (system-following) and **Eulerian** (control volume) descriptions of a fluid flow. For any extensive property $B$ with intensive (per unit mass) value $b$:

$$\frac{dB_{sys}}{dt} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{CV} \rho b \, dV + \oint_{CS} \rho b (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA$$

For mass conservation, $B = m$ and $b = 1$:

$$\frac{dm_{sys}}{dt} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{CV} \rho \, dV + \oint_{CS} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA$$

**Derivation of Differential Form:**

**Step 1:** Start with the integral form:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{CV} \rho \, dV + \oint_{CS} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = 0$$

**Step 2:** Apply the **Divergence Theorem** to the surface integral:

$$\oint_{CS} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = \int_{CV} \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) \, dV$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the integral equation:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{CV} \rho \, dV + \int_{CV} \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) \, dV = 0$$

**Step 4:** Bring the time derivative inside the volume integral (since CV is fixed):

$$\int_{CV} \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} dV + \int_{CV} \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) \, dV = 0$$

**Step 5:** Combine into a single volume integral:

$$\int_{CV} \left[ \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) \right] dV = 0$$

**Step 6:** Since this must hold for **any arbitrary** control volume, the integrand itself must vanish:

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0}$$

> **Mark Distribution (7 marks):** RTT definition with formula (2) + Step 2 (1) + Steps 3-4 (1.5) + Step 5 (1) + Step 6 + final equation (1.5).

#### (b) **A submarine is submerged in seawater ($\rho = 1025 \; \text{kg/m}^3$). The ballast tank inlet has an area of $0.05 \; \text{m}^2$ with an inflow velocity of $8 \; \text{m/s}$. The outlet has an area of $0.02 \; \text{m}^2$. If the tank volume is $2 \; \text{m}^3$ and initially empty, calculate: (i) the mass flow rate at the inlet, (ii) the outlet velocity, and (iii) the time to fill the tank.** (7 marks)

#### Model Solution:

**Given Data:**
- $\rho = 1025 \; \text{kg/m}^3$ (seawater density)
- $A_1 = 0.05 \; \text{m}^2$ (inlet area)
- $V_1 = 8 \; \text{m/s}$ (inlet velocity)
- $A_2 = 0.02 \; \text{m}^2$ (outlet area)
- $V_{tank} = 2 \; \text{m}^3$ (tank volume)
- Initial volume = 0

**Part (i): Mass Flow Rate at the Inlet**

The mass flow rate is:

$$\dot{m}_{in} = \rho A_1 V_1$$

Substituting values:

$$\dot{m}_{in} = 1025 \times 0.05 \times 8$$

$$\dot{m}_{in} = 1025 \times 0.4$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{in} = 410 \; \text{kg/s}}$$

**Part (ii): Outlet Velocity**

Using the incompressible continuity equation ($A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$):

$$V_2 = \frac{A_1 V_1}{A_2}$$

$$V_2 = \frac{0.05 \times 8}{0.02} = \frac{0.4}{0.02}$$

$$\boxed{V_2 = 20 \; \text{m/s}}$$

**Part (iii): Time to Fill the Tank**

The net volumetric flow rate into the tank is:

$$Q_{net} = A_1 V_1 - A_2 V_2 = 0.05 \times 8 - 0.02 \times 20 = 0.4 - 0.4 = 0 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 Critical Observation:** The net volumetric flow is **zero**! This means the tank will **never fill** if the flow is purely steady. This indicates that the problem setup is **inconsistent** for a filling scenario. In a real ballast system, either the outlet would be closed during filling, or the outlet would discharge to a different location (e.g., the sea). The correct interpretation: during **filling**, the outlet is **closed** ($V_2 = 0$), so:
>
> $$Q_{filling} = A_1 V_1 = 0.4 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$
>
> $$t_{fill} = \frac{V_{tank}}{Q_{filling}} = \frac{2}{0.4} = 5 \; \text{s}$$
>
> $$\boxed{t_{fill} = 5 \; \text{seconds}}$$

> **Mark Distribution (7 marks):** Part (i) (2) + Part (ii) (2) + Part (iii) (2) + Physical interpretation / sanity check (1).

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students who blindly calculate $t = \infty$ (or get confused) lose **2-3 marks** on Part (iii). Examiners reward the **physical insight** that a filling operation requires the outlet to be closed. Always cross-check your mathematical result against physical reality.

---

## 5.3 KTU 2024 Common Mark Distribution Pattern

| **Question Type** | **Marks** | **Cognitive Level (Bloom's)** | **Course Outcome (CO)** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Define / State continuity equation | 2-3 | Remember / Understand | CO1 |
| Derive integral form | 7 | Apply / Analyze | CO2 |
| Derive differential form | 7 | Apply / Analyze | CO2 |
| Special case simplification (incompressible, etc.) | 4-7 | Analyze / Evaluate | CO2 |
| Numerical problem (1D/2D flow) | 5-7 | Apply | CO3 |
| CFD application discussion | 3-5 | Evaluate / Create | CO4 |

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🎯

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📌 Rapid Revision Checklist — Conservation Equations for Mass**

### 🔑 Core Equations to Memorize

- ✅ **Integral Form:** $\frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{CV} \rho \, dV + \oint_{CS} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = 0$
- ✅ **Differential Form:** $\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$
- ✅ **Material Form:** $\frac{D\rho}{Dt} + \rho \nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$
- ✅ **Incompressible Form:** $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$
- ✅ **1D Form:** $\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$

### 🎯 Key Concepts

- ✅ The continuity equation is a **kinematic** constraint (it doesn't involve forces).
- ✅ For **incompressible** flows, it becomes a **divergence-free** condition on velocity.
- ✅ The **Reynolds Transport Theorem** is the bridge between system and control volume.
- ✅ The **Divergence Theorem (Gauss's Theorem)** converts surface integrals to volume integrals.
- ✅ The **material derivative** represents the rate of change **following a fluid particle**.

### ⚠️ Common Pitfalls in KTU Exams

- ❌ Forgetting the **outward normal** sign convention in flux terms.
- ❌ Confusing the **system** (Lagrangian) with the **control volume** (Eulerian).
- ❌ Applying the incompressible form to flows with $M > 0.3$.
- ❌ Missing the assumption justifications in derivation problems.
- ❌ Not specifying the coordinate system (Cartesian vs. Cylindrical).

### ⚓ Marine Technology Specific

- ✅ Ship hull flow: Use **incompressible 3D Cartesian** form.
- ✅ Propeller flow: Use **steady, incompressible cylindrical** form.
- ✅ Cavitation: Use **full compressible** form with equation of state.
- ✅ Wave-structure interaction: Use **VOF method** with incompressible continuity.
- ✅ Ballast systems: Use **1D incompressible** form for pipe flow.

### 🧠 Quick Mental Check Formula

> If a problem says "incompressible," immediately recall: **$\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$**
>
> If a problem says "steady," immediately drop **$\partial/\partial t$** terms.
>
> If a problem says "1D pipe flow," use **$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$** for incompressible.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Momentum and Energy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST635 - COMPUTATIONAL FLUID DYNAMICS IN MARINE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Momentum and Energy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD), the term **"Momentum and Energy"** refers to the two fundamental conservation laws — **Conservation of Linear Momentum** and **Conservation of Energy** — which, together with the **Conservation of Mass**, govern every physical fluid flow. In marine technology, these laws are expressed mathematically through the **Navier–Stokes Equations** (momentum) and the **Energy Equation** (thermal/first law of thermodynamics), and they form the governing Partial Differential Equations (PDEs) that every CFD solver discretizes and solves numerically.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Examiner-Verbatim):**
> *"Momentum conservation in a fluid continuum is the statement that the rate of change of linear momentum within a control volume equals the sum of surface forces (pressure + viscous) and body forces (gravity, buoyancy). Energy conservation states that the rate of change of total energy within a control volume equals the net heat added plus the work done by surface and body forces."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Water-Bucket Accountant"**
> Imagine you are an accountant tracking a bucket of seawater being poured over the side of a ship. Three ledgers must always balance:
>
> 1. **Mass Ledger** — Water entering the bucket = water leaving the bucket + water stored inside.
> 2. **Momentum Ledger** — If waves push the bucket harder from the front (pressure force) than the back, the bucket accelerates forward (Newton's 2nd Law: $F = ma$).
> 3. **Energy Ledger** — Heat from the sun, friction (viscous dissipation) heating the water, and work done by the ship's propeller must all be accounted for.
>
> CFD simply automates these three ledgers for **millions of tiny buckets** (control volumes) tiled across a ship hull, propeller, or ocean domain.

---

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Fluid Conservation

| Conservation Law | Mathematical Name | Physical Meaning | Marine Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mass | Continuity Equation | Volume of fluid is conserved | Seawater cannot disappear over a ship's wake |
| Momentum | Navier–Stokes Equations | $F = ma$ applied to a fluid element | Hull resistance, propeller thrust, slamming loads |
| Energy | First Law of Thermodynamics | Heat + Work = change in internal energy | Engine cooling, cavitation bubble temperature |

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic is High-Yield in KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - Every KTU 2024 Module 1 question on CFD begins with conservation laws.
> - The momentum equation alone accounts for **~60% of marks** in Part B derivations.
> - Marine-specific CFD (ship hydrodynamics, propeller analysis) is a *favourite* examiner topic because PENST635 is a marine-specialization paper.

---

## 1.5 Fundamental Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants and metrics will recur throughout your derivations:

- **Density of seawater:** $\rho = 1025 \, \text{kg/m}^3$
- **Dynamic viscosity of seawater:** $\mu = 1.08 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$
- **Kinematic viscosity:** $\nu = \mu / \rho \approx 1.05 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}$
- **Gravitational acceleration:** $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$
- **Standard sea-level pressure:** $p_{atm} = 101325 \, \text{Pa}$

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Substantial (Material) Derivative — The Heart of CFD

When you "ride along" with a fluid particle, the rate of change you observe is **not** the same as the rate of change a stationary observer sees. This is captured by the **substantial derivative** $D/Dt$.

$$
\frac{D}{Dt} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial}{\partial z}
$$

**Component breakdown:**

- $\partial/\partial t$ → **Local (unsteady) acceleration** — change at a fixed point in space.
- $u\,\partial/\partial x + v\,\partial/\partial y + w\,\partial/\partial z = (\vec{V}\cdot\nabla)$ → **Advective (convective) acceleration** — change due to fluid moving past a stationary observer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marine Analogy:** Standing on the deck of a ship in a storm, you feel wind changing due to (a) the storm evolving (local) and (b) the ship moving through the wind field (advective). Both contribute — exactly as the substantial derivative says.

---

## 2.2 Reynolds Transport Theorem (RTT) — The Bridge from System to Control Volume

RTT converts a statement about a **system** (a fixed collection of fluid particles) into a statement about a **control volume** (a fixed region in space) — the form CFD codes actually solve.

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\int_{CV} \eta\,\rho\,d\forall \;=\; \int_{CV} \frac{\partial(\eta\,\rho)}{\partial t}\,d\forall \;+\; \int_{CS} \eta\,\rho\,(\vec{V}\cdot\hat{n})\,dA
$$

**Plain-English interpretation:**

- **Left side:** Total rate of change inside the moving system (Lagrangian view).
- **First term on right:** Local (Eulerian) rate of change inside the fixed CV.
- **Second term on right:** Net flux of $\eta\,\rho$ leaving through the control surface.

**Choosing $\eta$ for each conservation law:**

| $\eta$ (per unit mass) | $\eta\,\rho$ | Law Obtained |
|---|---|---|
| $1$ | $\rho$ | Conservation of Mass |
| $\vec{V}$ | $\rho\vec{V}$ | Conservation of Momentum |
| $e + \tfrac{1}{2}V^2 + gz$ | $\rho(e + \tfrac{1}{2}V^2 + gz)$ | Conservation of Energy |

---

## 2.3 Conservation of Mass — The Continuity Equation

**Differential (point) form, incompressible flow:**

$$
\nabla\cdot\vec{V} \;=\; \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} \;=\; 0
$$

**Differential form, compressible flow:**

$$
\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla\cdot(\rho\vec{V}) \;=\; 0
$$

**Vector form (general):**

$$
\frac{D\rho}{Dt} + \rho\,\nabla\cdot\vec{V} \;=\; 0
$$

---

## 2.4 Conservation of Momentum — Navier–Stokes Equations

Applying Newton's Second Law $\sum F = m a$ to an infinitesimal fluid element yields:

**Vector form (incompressible, Newtonian fluid):**

$$
\rho\,\frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} \;=\; -\nabla p \;+\; \mu\,\nabla^{2}\vec{V} \;+\; \rho\,\vec{g}
$$

**Component form (x-direction, expanded):**

$$
\rho\!\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial u}{\partial z}\right) = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \mu\!\left(\frac{\partial^{2}u}{\partial x^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2}u}{\partial y^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2}u}{\partial z^{2}}\right) + \rho g_{x}
$$

**Term-by-term physical meaning:**

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Inertia (local + advective) | $\rho\,D\vec{V}/Dt$ | Acceleration of fluid element |
| Pressure force | $-\nabla p$ | Push from high to low pressure |
| Viscous force | $\mu\nabla^{2}\vec{V}$ | Internal friction (Newton's law of viscosity) |
| Body force | $\rho\vec{g}$ | Gravity, buoyancy, electromagnetic |

---

## 2.5 Newton's Law of Viscosity (Constitutive Relation)

$$
\tau_{xy} \;=\; \mu\,\frac{\partial u}{\partial y}
$$

- For **Newtonian fluids** (water, air, most oils): $\mu$ = constant.
- For **Non-Newtonian fluids** (mud, polymer solutions, biolubricants in marine creatures): $\mu$ depends on $\dot{\gamma}$.

---

## 2.6 Conservation of Energy — The Energy Equation

For a Newtonian fluid with Fourier heat conduction:

$$
\rho\,\frac{D h}{Dt} \;=\; \frac{D p}{Dt} \;+\; \nabla\cdot(k\,\nabla T) \;+\; \Phi
$$

Or, expanded in terms of internal energy $e$:

$$
\rho\,\frac{D e}{Dt} \;=\; -p\,\nabla\cdot\vec{V} \;+\; \nabla\cdot(k\,\nabla T) \;+\; \Phi
$$

Where the **viscous dissipation function** is:

$$
\Phi \;=\; \mu\!\left[2\!\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial x}\right)^{\!2} + 2\!\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial y}\right)^{\!2} + 2\!\left(\frac{\partial w}{\partial z}\right)^{\!2} + \left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial y}+\frac{\partial v}{\partial x}\right)^{\!2} + \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial z}+\frac{\partial w}{\partial y}\right)^{\!2} + \left(\frac{\partial w}{\partial x}+\frac{\partial u}{\partial z}\right)^{\!2}\right]
$$

**Physical meaning of each term:**

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $\rho\,De/Dt$ | Rate of change of internal energy |
| $-p\nabla\cdot\vec{V}$ | Pressure work (compression heating) |
| $\nabla\cdot(k\nabla T)$ | Heat conduction (Fourier's law) |
| $\Phi$ | Viscous dissipation (always $\geq 0$) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For marine flows, viscous dissipation $\Phi$ is often negligible** because velocities and velocity-gradients along most of a ship's hull are small. However, near the **boundary layer**, **propeller blades**, and in **cavitation bubbles**, $\Phi$ becomes significant and is the reason why high-speed marine flows can locally heat the water enough to flash-boil (a cause of cavitation erosion).

---

## 2.7 Boundary Conditions (KTU-Favourite Topic)

| Boundary | Velocity BC | Thermal BC | Marine Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| No-slip wall | $\vec{V}=0$ | $T = T_{wall}$ or $\partial T/\partial n = 0$ | Ship hull, rudder, propeller blade |
| Free surface | $\partial u/\partial n = 0$, kinematic | $T$ continuous | Ocean surface, wake |
| Inlet | $\vec{V}$ prescribed | $T$ prescribed | Bow of ship entering domain |
| Outlet | $\partial \vec{V}/\partial n = 0$ | $\partial T/\partial n = 0$ | Stern (far wake) |
| Symmetry | $\partial u_n/\partial n = 0$, $u_t = 0$ | $\partial T/\partial n = 0$ | Centerplane of a symmetric hull |

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Marine Application | Governing Law Used | CFD Output |
|---|---|---|
| Hull resistance prediction | Momentum (Navier–Stokes) | Drag coefficient $C_D$ |
| Propeller thrust & efficiency | Momentum + Energy | $K_T$, $K_Q$, $\eta_0$ |
| Cavitation risk analysis | Momentum (with phase change) | Vapour volume fraction |
| Heat exchanger design | Energy equation | Temperature distribution |
| Wave–structure interaction | Momentum (free-surface) | Slamming force, sea-keeping |
| LNG cargo cooling | Energy equation | Boil-off rate |

---

## 2.9 The KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Equation Name | Formula | Applicable Regime |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Substantial Derivative | $\dfrac{D}{Dt} = \dfrac{\partial}{\partial t} + (\vec{V}\cdot\nabla)$ | All flows |
| 2 | RTT | $\dfrac{d}{dt}\!\int_{CV}\!\eta\rho\,d\forall = \int_{CV}\!\dfrac{\partial(\eta\rho)}{\partial t}d\forall + \int_{CS}\!\eta\rho(\vec{V}\cdot\hat{n})\,dA$ | All flows |
| 3 | Continuity (incomp.) | $\nabla\cdot\vec{V} = 0$ | Liquid marine flows |
| 4 | Continuity (comp.) | $\dfrac{\partial\rho}{\partial t} + \nabla\cdot(\rho\vec{V}) = 0$ | Air, cavitation |
| 5 | Navier–Stokes (vector) | $\rho\,\dfrac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \mu\nabla^{2}\vec{V} + \rho\vec{g}$ | Newtonian, incompressible |
| 6 | Navier–Stokes (x-comp.) | $\rho\!\left(\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial z}\right) = -\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \mu\nabla^{2}u + \rho g_x$ | Same |
| 7 | Newton's Law of Viscosity | $\tau_{xy} = \mu\,\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y}$ | Newtonian |
| 8 | Energy Equation (Fourier) | $\rho\,\dfrac{D e}{Dt} = -p\nabla\cdot\vec{V} + \nabla\cdot(k\nabla T) + \Phi$ | Compressible heat transfer |
| 9 | Viscous Dissipation | $\Phi = \mu\!\left[2\!\left(\dfrac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_i}\right)^{\!2} + \sum\!\left(\dfrac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j}+\dfrac{\partial u_j}{\partial x_i}\right)^{\!2}\right]$ | All viscous flows |
| 10 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \dfrac{V L}{\nu}$ | All flows (dimensionless) |
| 11 | Prandtl Number | $Pr = \dfrac{\mu c_p}{k} = \dfrac{\nu}{\alpha}$ | Energy (dimensionless) |
| 12 | Euler Equation (inviscid) | $\rho\,\dfrac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \rho\vec{g}$ | $\mu = 0$ idealization |
| 13 | Bernoulli (steady, inviscid, irrotational) | $p + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} + \rho g z = \text{const.}$ | Ideal flow |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorization Tip:** Examiners expect you to write Reynolds Number $Re$, Prandtl Number $Pr$, and the substantial derivative **from memory** without referring to any formula sheet. Practice writing them 10 times the night before the exam.
