<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Finite Difference Method (FDM)** is a numerical technique for solving partial differential equations (PDEs) by approximating derivatives using finite difference quotients obtained from Taylor series expansions. The continuous computational domain is replaced by a **discrete grid (mesh)** of nodal points, and the governing differential equation is converted into a system of **algebraic equations** that can be solved iteratively or directly.

In marine technology applications, the FDM is the classical workhorse of early CFD codes used for:
- **Wave-structure interaction** problems (ship hulls, offshore platforms)
- **Viscous boundary layer** analysis over ship hulls
- **Propeller wake** field computation
- **Potential flow** problems governed by the **Laplace equation** ($\nabla^2 \phi = 0$)
- **Diffusion-dominated** flows governed by the **heat (diffusion) equation**

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Staircase on a Hill"

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine you are standing on a smooth, curved hillside and you want to tell a friend exactly how steep the slope is at a particular point. Instead of measuring the smooth tangent (which requires calculus), you walk a small, fixed step forward and a small step backward, and measure the change in elevation. The **difference in height divided by the horizontal step** is your *finite difference approximation* of the slope.

- The **smooth hill** = the continuous function $f(x)$
- The **fixed step length** = grid spacing $\Delta x$
- The **measured slope between two points** = finite difference quotient
- **Smaller steps** $\Rightarrow$ better approximation (but more points to compute)

### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus Anchors)

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Grid / Mesh** | Discrete set of points replacing the continuous domain |
| **Node** | A single point $(x_i, y_j)$ on the grid |
| **Stencil** | Pattern of neighbouring nodes used to approximate a derivative |
| **Grid spacing** $\Delta x, \Delta y$ | Distance between adjacent nodes |
| **Truncation error** | Error from replacing exact derivative by difference quotient |
| **Order of accuracy** | Power of $\Delta x$ in the truncation error term |
| **Explicit scheme** | Future value expressed directly from known past values |
| **Implicit scheme** | Future value coupled with neighbours, requires matrix solve |
| **CFL condition** | Courant–Friedrichs–Lewy stability criterion: $C = \frac{u \Delta t}{\Delta x} \leq 1$ |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of FDM

The entire FDM framework rests on three logical pillars:

**Pillar 1 — Discretisation of the Domain**
The continuous domain $\Omega \subset \mathbb{R}^2$ is replaced by a finite set of nodes:
$$\Omega \longrightarrow \Omega_h = \{(x_i, y_j) \;:\; x_i = i\Delta x,\; y_j = j\Delta y,\; i,j \in \mathbb{Z}\}$$

**Pillar 2 — Approximation of Derivatives**
Each differential operator is replaced by a difference operator using Taylor series. The order of accuracy depends on which neighbouring nodes are retained in the **stencil**.

**Pillar 3 — Assembly of Algebraic System**
Substituting the difference operators into the governing PDE produces a linear (or non-linear) algebraic system of the form:
$$[A]\{\phi\} = \{b\}$$
where $[A]$ is the coefficient matrix, $\{\phi\}$ contains the unknown nodal values, and $\{b\}$ encodes boundary/initial conditions.

### 2.2 Classification of Difference Schemes

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently test whether you can **classify a scheme** based on its stencil and time-coupling structure.

- **Forward Difference in Time, Central Difference in Space (FTCS)** — Explicit, first-order in time, second-order in space.
- **Backward Difference in Time, Central Difference in Space (BTCS)** — Implicit, unconditionally stable.
- **Crank–Nicolson** — Second-order in both time and space, implicit.
- **Lax–Wendroff** — Second-order in time, explicit, used for hyperbolic flows (e.g., shallow-water waves).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU Module-2 question.**

| Formula / Operator | Finite Difference Approximation | Truncation Error | Used For |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Forward $\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x}$ | $\dfrac{f_{i+1} - f_i}{\Delta x}$ | $O(\Delta x)$ | One-sided boundaries |
| Backward $\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x}$ | $\dfrac{f_i - f_{i-1}}{\Delta x}$ | $O(\Delta x)$ | One-sided boundaries |
| Central $\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x}$ | $\dfrac{f_{i+1} - f_{i-1}}{2\Delta x}$ | $O(\Delta x^2)$ | Interior nodes |
| Second derivative $\dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x^2}$ | $\dfrac{f_{i+1} - 2f_i + f_{i-1}}{\Delta x^2}$ | $O(\Delta x^2)$ | Diffusion/Laplace terms |
| Mixed $\dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x \partial y}$ | $\dfrac{f_{i+1,j+1} - f_{i+1,j-1} - f_{i-1,j+1} + f_{i-1,j-1}}{4\Delta x \Delta y}$ | $O(\Delta x^2, \Delta y^2)$ | Cross-derivative terms |
| Time derivative $\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial t}$ | $\dfrac{\phi^{n+1}_i - \phi^n_i}{\Delta t}$ | $O(\Delta t)$ | Forward Euler |
| CFL number | $C = \dfrac{u \Delta t}{\Delta x}$ | — | Stability criterion |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Marine Engineering

- **Hull-form resistance prediction:** Solving the Laplace equation for the velocity potential $\phi$ around a 2D ship section using a body-fitted grid.
- **Sloshing analysis in LNG tanks:** Solving the 2D shallow-water equations on a staggered grid.
- **Boundary layer on a flat plate:** Solving the Blasius equation via finite differences with similarity transformation.
- **Cavitation modelling:** Solving the Rayleigh–Plesset equation coupled with a pressure Poisson solver.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Forward Difference Approximation

We start with the **Taylor series expansion** of $f(x+\Delta x)$ about $x$:
$$f(x+\Delta x) = f(x) + \Delta x \, f'(x) + \frac{\Delta x^2}{2!} f''(x) + \frac{\Delta x^3}{3!} f'''(x) + \cdots$$

Solving for $f'(x)$:
$$f'(x) = \frac{f(x+\Delta x) - f(x)}{\Delta x} - \frac{\Delta x}{2} f''(x) - \frac{\Delta x^2}{6} f'''(x) - \cdots$$

Dropping all terms beyond the first produces the **forward difference approximation**:
$$\boxed{\;f'(x) \approx \frac{f_{i+1} - f_i}{\Delta x}\;}$$

The leading neglected term is $-\frac{\Delta x}{2}f''(x)$, hence the **truncation error** is $O(\Delta x)$ — **first-order accurate**.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Central Difference Approximation

Write **two** Taylor expansions: one forward, one backward:
$$f(x+\Delta x) = f(x) + \Delta x \, f'(x) + \frac{\Delta x^2}{2} f''(x) + \frac{\Delta x^3}{6} f'''(x) + \cdots$$
$$f(x-\Delta x) = f(x) - \Delta x \, f'(x) + \frac{\Delta x^2}{2} f''(x) - \frac{\Delta x^3}{6} f'''(x) + \cdots$$

**Subtracting** the second equation from the first, the even-power terms cancel:
$$f(x+\Delta x) - f(x-\Delta x) = 2 \Delta x \, f'(x) + \frac{\Delta x^3}{3} f'''(x) + \cdots$$

Dividing by $2\Delta x$:
$$\boxed{\;f'(x) \approx \frac{f_{i+1} - f_{i-1}}{2\Delta x}\;}$$

The leading neglected term is $\frac{\Delta x^2}{6}f'''(x)$, so the truncation error is $O(\Delta x^2)$ — **second-order accurate**.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Second-Difference (Diffusion) Operator

**Add** the two Taylor expansions from §3.2:
$$f(x+\Delta x) + f(x-\Delta x) = 2 f(x) + \Delta x^2 \, f''(x) + \frac{\Delta x^4}{12} f^{(4)}(x) + \cdots$$

Rearranging for $f''(x)$:
$$f''(x) = \frac{f_{i+1} - 2 f_i + f_{i-1}}{\Delta x^2} - \frac{\Delta x^2}{12} f^{(4)}(x) + \cdots$$

Hence the **standard second-order central difference** is:
$$\boxed{\;f''(x) \approx \frac{f_{i+1} - 2 f_i + f_{i-1}}{\Delta x^2}\;}$$
with truncation error $O(\Delta x^2)$.

### 3.4 Worked Example — 1D Steady Heat Conduction (Marine Insulated Pipe Wall)

Consider a pipe wall of thickness $L = 0.05\;\text{m}$ with inner surface temperature $T(0) = 100^{\circ}\text{C}$ and outer surface $T(L) = 30^{\circ}\text{C}$. Solve the Laplace equation $\dfrac{d^2 T}{dx^2} = 0$ using FDM with $\Delta x = 0.01\;\text{m}$.

**Discretisation:** nodes $i = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5$ with $T_0 = 100$, $T_5 = 30$.

**FDM equation at interior nodes** $i = 1, 2, 3, 4$:
$$\frac{T_{i+1} - 2 T_i + T_{i-1}}{\Delta x^2} = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; T_{i+1} - 2T_i + T_{i-1} = 0$$

**Set up the linear system:**

For $i=1$: $-2T_1 + T_2 = -100$

For $i=2$: $T_1 - 2T_2 + T_3 = 0$

For $i=3$: $T_2 - 2T_3 + T_4 = 0$

For $i=4$: $T_3 - 2T_4 = -30$

**Matrix form:**
$$\begin{bmatrix} -2 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 1 & -2 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & -2 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & -2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} T_1 \\ T_2 \\ T_3 \\ T_4 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} -100 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ -30 \end{Bmatrix}$$

**Analytical solution** (linear): $T(x) = 100 - 1400 x$.

FDM yields $T = [85, 70, 55, 40]^{\circ}\text{C}$ — matching the analytical solution exactly (because the governing equation is linear and the discretisation is exact for a second-order polynomial).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 FDM Computational Workflow (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[StepA: Define Governing PDE and Domain] --> B0[StepB: Generate Discrete Grid]
    B0 --> C0[StepC: Apply Difference Operators from Taylor Series]
    C0 --> D0[StepD: Impose Boundary and Initial Conditions]
    D0 --> E0[StepE: Assemble Linear System A phi equals b]
    E0 --> F0{StepF: Is the System Linear?}
    F0 -- Yes --> G0[StepG: Solve using Thomas Algorithm or Gauss Elimination]
    F0 -- No --> H0[StepH: Iterate via Newton Raphson Method]
    G0 --> I0[StepI: Post Process and Visualise Results]
    H0 --> I0
    I0 --> J0{StepJ: Check Convergence and Grid Independence}
    J0 -- Not Converged --> B0
    J0 -- Converged --> K0[StepK: Final Marine Engineering Output]
```

### 4.2 2D Stencil Map — Central Difference Scheme

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N0[Node West at i minus 1, j] --- N1[Node Center at i, j]
    N1 --- N2[Node East at i plus 1, j]
    N3[Node South at i, j minus 1] --- N1
    N1 --- N4[Node North at i, j plus 1]
    N0 --> N1
    N2 --> N1
    N3 --> N1
    N4 --> N1
```

> [!NOTE]
> The "five-point stencil" above is the most commonly used pattern in KTU problems involving the **Laplace equation** $\nabla^2 \phi = 0$, which governs **inviscid, incompressible, irrotational flow** around ship hulls.

### 4.3 Stencil-to-Equation Mapping Table

| Operator | Continuous Form | Discrete 5-point Form |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Laplacian $\nabla^2 \phi$ | $\dfrac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial y^2}$ | $\dfrac{\phi_E - 2\phi_C + \phi_W}{\Delta x^2} + \dfrac{\phi_N - 2\phi_C + \phi_S}{\Delta y^2}$ |
| Biharmonic $\nabla^4 \psi$ | Stream-function vorticity form | 13-point or 9-point compact stencil |
| Gradient $\nabla \phi$ | $\partial_x \phi \hat{i} + \partial_y \phi \hat{j}$ | Central differences in both directions |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the Finite Difference Method. Mention any two advantages and one limitation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** FDM is a numerical technique that approximates the derivatives in a PDE by finite difference quotients derived from Taylor series expansion, converting the differential equation into a system of algebraic equations on a discrete grid.
- **Advantages (0.5 each):**
  1. Simple to formulate and implement on structured grids.
  2. Mature mathematical foundation with well-established stability theory (CFL condition).
- **Limitation (0.5):** Difficulty handling complex, curved marine geometries (e.g., bulbous bows, propeller blades) without body-fitted or unstructured grids.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*What is the order of accuracy of the central difference scheme for the first derivative? Justify your answer using a Taylor series.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The central difference scheme $\dfrac{f_{i+1} - f_{i-1}}{2\Delta x}$ has **second-order accuracy**, i.e., truncation error $O(\Delta x^2)$.
- **Justification (Taylor series):**
$$f_{i+1} = f_i + \Delta x f'_i + \tfrac{\Delta x^2}{2} f''_i + \tfrac{\Delta x^3}{6} f'''_i + \cdots$$
$$f_{i-1} = f_i - \Delta x f'_i + \tfrac{\Delta x^2}{2} f''_i - \tfrac{\Delta x^3}{6} f'''_i + \cdots$$
- Subtracting: $f_{i+1} - f_{i-1} = 2 \Delta x f'_i + \tfrac{\Delta x^3}{3} f'''_i + \cdots$
- Hence $f'_i = \dfrac{f_{i+1} - f_{i-1}}{2\Delta x} - \tfrac{\Delta x^2}{6} f'''_i + \cdots$, with leading error term $\tfrac{\Delta x^2}{6} f'''_i$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]*

**(a)** Derive the central difference approximation for the second derivative $\dfrac{d^2 f}{dx^2}$ using Taylor series. Show that the truncation error is of order $O(\Delta x^2)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the finite difference method, solve the boundary value problem $\dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} + y = 0$ with $y(0) = 0$ and $y(\pi/2) = 1$. Use $\Delta x = \pi/4$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**
Starting Taylor series expansion:
$$f(x+\Delta x) = f(x) + \Delta x f'(x) + \frac{\Delta x^2}{2} f''(x) + \frac{\Delta x^3}{6} f'''(x) + \frac{\Delta x^4}{24} f^{(4)}(x) + \cdots$$
$$f(x-\Delta x) = f(x) - \Delta x f'(x) + \frac{\Delta x^2}{2} f''(x) - \frac{\Delta x^3}{6} f'''(x) + \frac{\Delta x^4}{24} f^{(4)}(x) - \cdots$$

Adding both:
$$f_{i+1} + f_{i-1} = 2 f_i + \Delta x^2 f''_i + \frac{\Delta x^4}{12} f^{(4)}_i + \cdots$$

Solving for $f''_i$:
$$f''_i = \frac{f_{i+1} - 2 f_i + f_{i-1}}{\Delta x^2} - \frac{\Delta x^2}{12} f^{(4)}_i + \cdots$$

Hence the approximation $\dfrac{f_{i+1} - 2f_i + f_{i-1}}{\Delta x^2}$ has truncation error $O(\Delta x^2)$ — **second-order accurate**. ✓

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**
With $\Delta x = \pi/4$, nodes are at $x = 0,\; \pi/4,\; \pi/2$, so $i = 0, 1, 2$ with $y_0 = 0$, $y_2 = 1$.

FDM equation at $i = 1$:
$$\frac{y_2 - 2 y_1 + y_0}{\Delta x^2} + y_1 = 0$$
$$y_2 - 2 y_1 + y_0 + \Delta x^2 \, y_1 = 0$$
$$1 - 2 y_1 + 0 + \frac{\pi^2}{16} y_1 = 0$$
$$1 + y_1 \left(\frac{\pi^2}{16} - 2\right) = 0$$
$$y_1 = \frac{1}{2 - \pi^2/16} = \frac{16}{32 - \pi^2}$$

Numerically: $\pi^2 \approx 9.8696$, so $32 - \pi^2 \approx 22.1304$, giving $y_1 \approx 0.7230$.
**Exact solution** is $y = \sin(x)$, so $y_1 = \sin(\pi/4) = 0.7071$. The FDM solution is **within 2.2 %** of the exact value. ✓

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Explain with suitable diagrams the classification of finite difference schemes based on **forward, backward, and central** differences. Mention the order of accuracy of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Solve the Laplace equation $\nabla^2 \phi = 0$ over a square domain $0 \leq x, y \leq 1$ with boundary conditions $\phi = 0$ on three sides and $\phi = 100$ on the top side $y = 1$, using a $3 \times 3$ grid ($\Delta x = \Delta y = 0.5$). Use the standard 5-point stencil. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification (7 marks):**

| Scheme | Formula | Order | Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Forward | $\dfrac{f_{i+1}-f_i}{\Delta x}$ | $O(\Delta x)$ | Inlet, initial time |
| Backward | $\dfrac{f_i - f_{i-1}}{\Delta x}$ | $O(\Delta x)$ | Outlet, final time |
| Central | $\dfrac{f_{i+1}-f_{i-1}}{2\Delta x}$ | $O(\Delta x^2)$ | Interior nodes |

Diagrams (each scheme requires a 3-node stencil with arrows):
- Forward: arrow from $i$ to $i+1$.
- Backward: arrow from $i-1$ to $i$.
- Central: symmetric arrows $i-1 \leftrightarrow i \leftrightarrow i+1$.

The central scheme is **second-order accurate** because odd-order terms in the Taylor series cancel upon subtraction, while forward and backward retain a leading $O(\Delta x)$ term. Hence central differencing is preferred for interior marine CFD nodes. ✓

**(b) Solution of Laplace Equation (7 marks):**
With a $3 \times 3$ grid, interior node is only $(i,j) = (1,1)$ since the boundary occupies all other nodes.

Boundary values:
- $\phi_{0,0} = \phi_{1,0} = \phi_{2,0} = 0$ (bottom)
- $\phi_{0,1} = 0$, $\phi_{2,1} = 0$ (left and right)
- $\phi_{0,2} = \phi_{1,2} = \phi_{2,2} = 100$ (top)

Five-point stencil at interior node:
$$\frac{\phi_{i+1,j} - 2\phi_{i,j} + \phi_{i-1,j}}{\Delta x^2} + \frac{\phi_{i,j+1} - 2\phi_{i,j} + \phi_{i,j-1}}{\Delta y^2} = 0$$

With $\Delta x = \Delta y = 0.5$, the $0.5^2$ factors cancel:
$$\phi_{2,1} - 2\phi_{1,1} + \phi_{0,1} + \phi_{1,2} - 2\phi_{1,1} + \phi_{1,0} = 0$$
$$0 - 2\phi_{1,1} + 0 + 100 - 2\phi_{1,1} + 0 = 0$$
$$100 - 4\phi_{1,1} = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; \phi_{1,1} = 25$$

Thus the centre temperature/potential is **25**, which is the expected average between the cold sides (0) and the hot top (100). ✓

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to specify boundary conditions** when writing the difference equation — examiners deduct up to 2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up explicit vs implicit indices** ($n$ for time, $i$ for space). Always declare them clearly.
> 3. **Ignoring the truncation error order** in derivations — stating the formula without deriving $O(\Delta x)$ or $O(\Delta x^2)$ loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Not verifying the CFL condition** in time-dependent problems — required for full marks on stability questions.
> 5. **Confusing forward, backward, and central** schemes in classification questions — draw the stencil, do not just describe it.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for FDM (Module 2):**

- **Definition:** FDM replaces derivatives by algebraic difference quotients on a discrete grid.
- **Source of approximations:** Taylor series expansion about a node.
- **First derivative formulas:**
  - Forward: $(f_{i+1}-f_i)/\Delta x$ → $O(\Delta x)$
  - Backward: $(f_i - f_{i-1})/\Delta x$ → $O(\Delta x)$
  - Central: $(f_{i+1}-f_{i-1})/(2\Delta x)$ → $O(\Delta x^2)$ ✓ *preferred*
- **Second derivative (Laplacian 1D):** $(f_{i+1} - 2f_i + f_{i-1})/\Delta x^2$ → $O(\Delta x^2)$
- **5-point stencil** for 2D Laplacian combines $x$ and $y$ second-differences.
- **Boundary conditions** must be imposed explicitly (Dirichlet, Neumann, or Robin).
- **Truncation error** measures local accuracy; **round-off error** grows as $\Delta x$ shrinks.
- **CFL number** $C = u\Delta t/\Delta x \leq 1$ governs explicit scheme stability.
- **Explicit schemes** are simple but conditionally stable; **implicit schemes** are unconditionally stable but require matrix inversion.
- **Marine CFD applications:** ship-hull potential flow, viscous boundary layers, propeller wakes, LNG-sloshing, cavitation modelling.
- **Grid independence study** is mandatory before trusting any FDM result for ship design.
- **KTU-typical question pattern:** Derive formula (7 marks) + solve a small BVP/PDE (7 marks).
- Always quote the **order of accuracy** whenever you write a difference formula.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Finite Element Method and Finite Volume Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST635 - COMPUTATIONAL FLUID DYNAMICS IN MARINE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Finite Element Method and Finite Volume Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Two Pillars of CFD Discretization

Computational Fluid Dynamics, as applied in Marine Technology (ship resistance prediction, propeller flow, wave–structure interaction, sloshing, and cavitation), fundamentally relies on discretizing the governing partial differential equations (PDEs) — namely the **Navier–Stokes equations** and the **potential flow (Laplace) equation** — into a solvable algebraic system. The two most industrially relevant discretization philosophies are the **Finite Element Method (FEM)** and the **Finite Volume Method (FVM)**.

### 1.2 Finite Element Method (FEM) — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):**
> The Finite Element Method is a numerical technique for obtaining approximate solutions to boundary value problems. It divides the physical domain into a finite number of small sub-domains (elements), within which the solution is approximated by a linear combination of *shape functions* weighted by unknown nodal values. The PDE is converted into an equivalent **weak (variational) form** by multiplying the governing equation with a *test (weight) function* and integrating over the domain, thereby reducing the order of the highest derivative.

### 1.3 Finite Volume Method (FVM) — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):**
> The Finite Volume Method is a discretization technique that directly enforces the **conservation of physical quantities** (mass, momentum, energy) over discrete control volumes surrounding each computational node. The PDE, written in *conservation form*, is integrated over each control volume, and the divergence theorem is applied to convert volume integrals of fluxes into surface integrals. The result is an exact integral balance that is then approximated numerically.

### 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "Measuring a Bathtub of Water"**
>
> Imagine you want to know how much heat is leaving a hot bathtub into the cold bathroom.
>
> - **FEM Approach:** You take a fine fishing net and stretch it across the bathtub. Each mesh square (element) acts like a tiny independent "thermometer zone" with its own assumed temperature variation (a small tent-shaped curve — the shape function). The method *sews all these small tents together* and ensures they match at the seams (nodes), producing a global smooth temperature field. FEM is the choice for *irregular ship hull geometries, slender marine structures, and free-surface problems*.
>
> - **FVM Approach:** You draw invisible boxes around fixed points inside the water. For every box, you write down a strict balance: *What flows IN minus what flows OUT = What accumulates inside*. It doesn't care what shape the inside of the box looks like — the balance is exact. FVM is the workhorse of *commercial marine CFD codes (ANSYS Fluent, STAR-CCM+, OpenFOAM)* for solving RANS equations around ship hulls and propellers.

### 1.5 Why Both Methods Matter in Marine Technology

| Marine Application | Preferred Method | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Wave diffraction / radiation (potential flow)** | FEM | Handles complex hull geometry and radiation conditions naturally |
| **Viscous RANS around ship hull** | FVM | Strict local conservation of momentum, robust for turbulent flows |
| **Propeller cavitation analysis** | FVM | Captures shocks and steep gradients with conservative numerics |
| **Sloshing in LNG tanks** | FEM / FVM | Both used; FEM excels with moving meshes, FVM with VOF |
| **Structural deflection of hull plates** | FEM | Stress–strain analysis is variational by nature |

### 1.6 Key Metrics & Constants Used

- **Reynolds Number** for a ship: $\text{Re} = \dfrac{UL}{\nu} \approx 10^{7}$ to $10^{9}$ (full scale)
- **Froude Number**: $\text{Fr} = \dfrac{U}{\sqrt{gL}}$ ≈ 0.1 to 0.4
- **Mesh Density**: $10^{6}$ to $10^{8}$ cells for a full ship–propeller RANS simulation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 focuses on the *mathematical formulation* of FEM (weak form, Galerkin) and FVM (conservation form, control volume integration). Algorithm-level implementation details (e.g., SIMPLE algorithm) belong to Module 3.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Finite Element Method — Operational Logic Steps

The FEM procedure for a marine PDE problem (e.g., the Laplace equation for wave potential $\phi$ around a ship) proceeds in the following structured sequence:

1. **Strong Formulation:** Write the governing PDE in classical (pointwise) form over the domain $\Omega$ with boundary $\Gamma$.
2. **Discretization of Domain:** Subdivide $\Omega$ into $N_e$ non-overlapping elements (triangles/quadrilaterals in 2D, tetrahedra/prisms in 3D).
3. **Approximation:** Within each element, approximate the unknown $u$ as $\tilde{u} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} N_j(\mathbf{x}) u_j$, where $N_j$ are the **shape functions** and $u_j$ are the unknown nodal values.
4. **Weak Formulation:** Multiply the PDE by a weight function $w$ (chosen from the same space as $N_j$ in **Galerkin's method**) and integrate over $\Omega$.
5. **Integration by Parts (Green's Theorem):** Reduce the order of the derivatives, transferring them onto $w$. This produces a *symmetric bilinear form* — the cornerstone of FEM.
6. **Element-Level Assembly:** Compute the **element stiffness matrix** $[K]^e$ and **element load vector** $\{f\}^e$.
7. **Global Assembly:** Sum contributions from all elements sharing a node, producing the global system $[K]\{U\} = \{F\}$.
8. **Boundary Condition Application:** Impose **essential (Dirichlet)** BCs by row/column modification; **natural (Neumann)** BCs are *automatically satisfied* through the boundary integral.
9. **Solve & Post-process:** Solve the sparse symmetric system and visualize (pressure contours, wave elevation, streamlines).

### 2.2 Galerkin vs. Petrov–Galerkin

> [!NOTE]
> In the standard **Bubnov–Galerkin** method, the test function $w$ equals the trial function $N$. In **Petrov–Galerkin** (used in **SUPG** — Streamline-Upwind Petrov Galerkin for convection-dominated flows), the test function is *asymmetric* to add numerical diffusion along streamlines, stabilizing the solution for high-Reynolds marine flows.

### 2.3 Finite Volume Method — Operational Logic Steps

1. **Conservation Form:** Express the governing equation as $\dfrac{\partial \mathbf{U}}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot \mathbf{F} = \mathbf{S}$, where $\mathbf{U}$ = conserved variables, $\mathbf{F}$ = flux vector, $\mathbf{S}$ = source.
2. **Grid Generation:** Generate a computational mesh of nodes, with **control volumes (CVs)** defined around each node.
3. **CV Integration:** Integrate the conservation law over each CV and apply the **Divergence Theorem (Gauss's Theorem)** to convert divergence terms into surface (flux) integrals.
4. **Flux Approximation:** Approximate the surface integrals using numerical flux functions (e.g., central differencing, upwind, Roe, HLLC).
5. **Source Linearization:** Linearize source terms as $S = S_P \phi_P + S_C$ to enable implicit solution.
6. **Algebraic System:** Assemble the discrete equation $a_P \phi_P = \sum_{nb} a_{nb} \phi_{nb} + b$.
7. **Solution:** Solve iteratively (TDMA for 1D, conjugate gradient / multigrid for 2D/3D).
8. **Post-processing:** Compute derived quantities (Cp, $C_f$, wave resistance, $C_T$).

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master These Equations — They Appear Repeatedly in KTU Papers**

| # | Concept | Equation | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | General Transport Equation (FVM basis) | $\dfrac{\partial (\rho \phi)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{u} \phi) = \nabla \cdot (\Gamma \nabla \phi) + S$ | $\phi$ = transported scalar; $\Gamma$ = diffusivity; $S$ = source |
| 2 | FVM Integral Balance | $\displaystyle\int_{V} \dfrac{\partial (\rho \phi)}{\partial t} dV + \int_{A} \rho \mathbf{u} \phi \cdot d\mathbf{A} = \int_{A} \Gamma \nabla \phi \cdot d\mathbf{A} + \int_{V} S \, dV$ | Exact integral form over CV |
| 3 | Strong Form of Poisson PDE (1D) | $-\dfrac{d^2 u}{dx^2} = f(x), \quad x \in (0,L)$ | Model marine potential problem |
| 4 | Galerkin Weak Form (1D) | $\displaystyle\int_0^L \dfrac{dw}{dx} \dfrac{du}{dx} dx = \int_0^L w f \, dx + \left[w \dfrac{du}{dx}\right]_0^L$ | Reduced derivative order |
| 5 | Trial Function (Linear Element) | $\tilde{u}(x) = N_1 u_1 + N_2 u_2, \quad N_1 = \dfrac{x_2 - x}{h}, \; N_2 = \dfrac{x - x_1}{h}$ | Shape functions for 2-node element, $h = x_2 - x_1$ |
| 6 | 1D Element Stiffness Matrix | $\left[K\right]^e = \dfrac{1}{h}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Symmetric, positive definite |
| 7 | 1D Element Load Vector | $\{f\}^e = \dfrac{h}{6}\begin{Bmatrix} 2 f_1 + f_2 \\ f_1 + 2 f_2 \end{Bmatrix}$ | For linear variation of $f$ |
| 8 | FVM Discretized 1D Steady | $F_e \phi_e - F_w \phi_w = D_e (\phi_E - \phi_P) - D_w (\phi_P - \phi_W)$ | $F$ = convective, $D$ = diffusive conductance |
| 9 | Péclet Number (FVM stability) | $\text{Pe} = \dfrac{F}{D} = \dfrac{\rho u}{\Gamma / \delta x}$ | $\vert \text{Pe} \vert < 2$ ⇒ central scheme stable |
| 10 | Gauss Divergence Theorem | $\displaystyle\int_V \nabla \cdot \mathbf{F} \, dV = \int_A \mathbf{F} \cdot d\mathbf{A}$ | Converts volume flux integral to surface integral |
| 11 | Green's First Identity (FEM) | $\displaystyle\int_\Omega w(-\nabla^2 u) d\Omega = \int_\Omega \nabla w \cdot \nabla u \, d\Omega - \int_\Gamma w \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial n} d\Gamma$ | Derives weak form |
| 12 | Continuity Equation (Marine) | $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0$ | Incompressible RANS basis |

### 2.5 Engineering Utility of This System

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Marine Use-Cases**
>
> 1. **Hull-Form Optimization:** FVM-based RANS solvers predict viscous resistance, allowing iterative hull-shape modification. Reduction of 5–10% in $C_T$ saves millions of dollars in fuel over a ship's life.
> 2. **Wave-Structure Interaction (Wells Turret, Semi-Submersibles):** FEM solves the diffraction/radiation problem using the Laplace equation, predicting heave, pitch, and RAO (Response Amplitude Operator).
> 3. **Propeller Hydrodynamics:** FVM with sliding/deforming meshes computes open-water curves ($K_T$, $K_Q$, $\eta_0$) and cavitation inception.
> 4. **Structural Integrity of Stiffened Panels:** FEM predicts Von-Mises stress and buckling of ship plates under slamming loads.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Galerkin Weak Form of the 2D Laplace Equation

The 2D Laplace equation is the cornerstone of marine **potential flow theory** (wave diffraction, slender-body theory). The derivation below is exhaustive and follow KTU valuation key standards.

### Strong Form

Consider the boundary value problem governing the velocity potential $\phi$ around a ship hull in calm water:

$$
-\nabla^2 \phi = 0, \quad \text{in} \; \Omega
$$

with boundary conditions

$$
\phi = \bar{\phi} \quad \text{on} \; \Gamma_D \quad (\text{Dirichlet: hull surface})
$$

$$
\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial n} = q \quad \text{on} \; \Gamma_N \quad (\text{Newton: far-field / symmetry})
$$

where $\Gamma = \Gamma_D \cup \Gamma_N$ and $\Gamma_D \cap \Gamma_N = \emptyset$.

### Step 1 — Multiply by the Test Function

Choose a test function $w$ that vanishes on the Dirichlet boundary ($\,w = 0$ on $\Gamma_D$). Multiply the strong form by $w$:

$$
w \cdot (-\nabla^2 \phi) = 0
$$

### Step 2 — Integrate Over the Domain $\Omega$

$$
\int_{\Omega} w \, (-\nabla^2 \phi) \, d\Omega = 0
$$

### Step 3 — Apply Green's First Identity

The scalar form of Green's first identity is:

$$
\int_{\Omega} w \, (-\nabla^2 \phi) \, d\Omega = \int_{\Omega} \nabla w \cdot \nabla \phi \, d\Omega - \int_{\Gamma} w \, \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial n} \, d\Gamma
$$

Substituting:

$$
\int_{\Omega} \nabla w \cdot \nabla \phi \, d\Omega - \int_{\Gamma} w \, \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial n} \, d\Gamma = 0
$$

### Step 4 — Split the Boundary Integral

Since $w = 0$ on $\Gamma_D$, only $\Gamma_N$ contributes:

$$
\int_{\Omega} \nabla w \cdot \nabla \phi \, d\Omega = \int_{\Gamma_N} w \, \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial n} \, d\Gamma = \int_{\Gamma_N} w \, q \, d\Gamma
$$

### Step 5 — Final Weak Form

$$
\boxed{
\int_{\Omega} \nabla w \cdot \nabla \phi \, d\Omega = \int_{\Gamma_N} w \, q \, d\Gamma, \quad \forall \, w \in H^1_0(\Omega)
}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The weak form requires only *first derivatives* of $\phi$, whereas the strong form required *second derivatives*. This is why FEM handles complex geometries — the solution is sought in a *weaker (less smooth) function space*.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — 1D Element Stiffness Matrix & Load Vector for Poisson Equation

For the 1D Poisson equation $-\dfrac{d^2 u}{dx^2} = f(x)$ on $[0, L]$, with $u(0) = u(L) = 0$, derive the element matrices.

### Step 1 — Discretize Domain

Divide $[0, L]$ into $N$ elements of length $h$. Element $e$ has nodes $i$ and $j$ at $x_i$ and $x_{i+1}$.

### Step 2 — Approximate $u$ Using Linear Shape Functions

$$
u(x) \approx N_i(x) u_i + N_j(x) u_j
$$

where the linear shape functions are:

$$
N_i(x) = \dfrac{x_j - x}{h}, \quad N_j(x) = \dfrac{x - x_i}{h}
$$

### Step 3 — Their Derivatives

$$
\dfrac{dN_i}{dx} = -\dfrac{1}{h}, \quad \dfrac{dN_j}{dx} = \dfrac{1}{h}
$$

### Step 4 — Form the Element Stiffness Matrix Entry

By the Galerkin method, the entry $K_{ab}^e = \displaystyle\int_{x_i}^{x_j} \dfrac{dN_a}{dx} \dfrac{dN_b}{dx} dx$.

For $a = i, b = i$:

$$
K_{ii}^e = \int_{x_i}^{x_j} \left(-\dfrac{1}{h}\right) \left(-\dfrac{1}{h}\right) dx = \int_{x_i}^{x_j} \dfrac{1}{h^2} dx = \dfrac{h}{h^2} = \dfrac{1}{h}
$$

Similarly,

$$
K_{ij}^e = \int_{x_i}^{x_j} \left(-\dfrac{1}{h}\right) \left(\dfrac{1}{h}\right) dx = -\dfrac{1}{h}
$$

By symmetry, $K_{ji}^e = -\dfrac{1}{h}$ and $K_{jj}^e = \dfrac{1}{h}$. Thus:

$$
[K]^e = \dfrac{1}{h}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 5 — Form the Element Load Vector

The entry $f_a^e = \displaystyle\int_{x_i}^{x_j} N_a f \, dx$. For linearly varying $f(x) \approx N_i f_i + N_j f_j$:

$$
f_i^e = \int_{x_i}^{x_j} N_i (N_i f_i + N_j f_j) \, dx = f_i \int_{x_i}^{x_j} N_i^2 \, dx + f_j \int_{x_i}^{x_j} N_i N_j \, dx
$$

Using standard integrals:

$$
\int_{x_i}^{x_j} N_i^2 dx = \dfrac{h}{3}, \quad \int_{x_i}^{x_j} N_i N_j \, dx = \dfrac{h}{6}
$$

Therefore:

$$
f_i^e = f_i \cdot \dfrac{h}{3} + f_j \cdot \dfrac{h}{6} = \dfrac{h}{6}(2f_i + f_j)
$$

Similarly $f_j^e = \dfrac{h}{6}(f_i + 2f_j)$. Compact form:

$$
\{f\}^e = \dfrac{h}{6}\begin{Bmatrix} 2f_i + f_j \\ f_i + 2f_j \end{Bmatrix}
$$

### Step 6 — Final Element Equation

$$
\dfrac{1}{h}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{Bmatrix} u_i \\ u_j \end{Bmatrix} = \dfrac{h}{6}\begin{Bmatrix} 2f_i + f_j \\ f_i + 2f_j \end{Bmatrix}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — FVM Discretization of 1D Steady Convection-Diffusion

The 1D steady convection-diffusion equation is:

$$
\dfrac{d}{dx}(\rho u \phi) = \dfrac{d}{dx}\left(\Gamma \dfrac{d\phi}{dx}\right) + S
$$

### Step 1 — Define Control Volume

Let $P$ be the central node with west neighbor $W$ and east neighbor $E$. The control volume boundaries are $w$ and $e$, located mid-way between $P$–$W$ and $P$–$E$.

### Step 2 — Integrate the PDE Over the CV

$$
\int_{w}^{e} \dfrac{d}{dx}(\rho u \phi) \, dx = \int_{w}^{e} \dfrac{d}{dx}\left(\Gamma \dfrac{d\phi}{dx}\right) dx + \int_{w}^{e} S \, dx
$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate Volume Integrals

$$
(\rho u \phi)_e - (\rho u \phi)_w = \left(\Gamma \dfrac{d\phi}{dx}\right)_e - \left(\Gamma \dfrac{d\phi}{dx}\right)_w + \bar{S} \Delta x
$$

where $\Delta x = x_e - x_w$.

### Step 4 — Define Fluxes and Conductances

Define the convective mass flux:

$$
F = \rho u \quad \text{(kg/(m}^2 \text{·s))}
$$

and the diffusive conductance:

$$
D = \dfrac{\Gamma}{\delta x} \quad \text{(kg/(m·s))}
$$

### Step 5 — Discretize Surface Values (Central Differencing)

$$
F_e \phi_e - F_w \phi_w = D_e (\phi_E - \phi_P) - D_w (\phi_P - \phi_W) + S \Delta x
$$

### Step 6 — Rearrange to Standard Form

Group terms in $\phi_P$ on the LHS, all other nodal values on the RHS:

$$
\left( F_e - F_w + D_e + D_w \right) \phi_P = D_e \phi_E + D_w \phi_W + \left( F_e \phi_e - F_w \phi_w \right)_{\text{deferred}} + S \Delta x
$$

In the standard Patankar notation:

$$
a_P \phi_P = a_E \phi_E + a_W \phi_W + b
$$

where $a_E = D_e - F_e/2$, $a_W = D_w + F_w/2$, $a_P = a_E + a_W + (F_e - F_w)$, and $b = S_C \Delta x$.

### Step 7 — Péclet Number Stability Criterion

The Péclet number at face $e$ is:

$$
\text{Pe}_e = \dfrac{F_e}{D_e}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Result:** Central differencing is *stable* and physically consistent only if $\vert \text{Pe} \vert \le 2$. For high-Pe (convection-dominated) flows (e.g., boundary layer on a ship hull), an **upwind scheme** is mandatory: $\phi_e = \phi_P$ if $F_e > 0$.

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Diagrams:** All schematics below are rendered as **Mermaid block diagrams** to remain text-architectural, IDE-agnostic, and instantly readable for KTU revision.

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: FEM Workflow in Marine CFD

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Marine Geometry Input: Hull STL / IGES] --> B[Domain Definition: Water Region, Hull Surface, Far-field Boundary]
    B --> C[Mesh Generation: Triangular or Quadrilateral Elements]
    C --> D[Assign Material and Fluid Properties: Density, Viscosity, Gravity]
    D --> E[Formulate Strong Form of PDE: Laplace, Poisson, or Navier-Stokes]
    E --> F[Derive Weak Form: Multiply by Weight Function and Integrate]
    F --> G[Choose Shape Functions: Linear, Quadratic, or Cubic]
    G --> H[Compute Element Stiffness Matrix and Load Vector]
    H --> I[Global Assembly: Sum Element Contributions at Shared Nodes]
    I --> J[Apply Boundary Conditions: Hull Surface Dirichlet, Far-field Neumann or Radiation]
    J --> K[Solve Sparse Linear System: Direct or Iterative Solver]
    K --> L[Post-Processing: Pressure, Velocity, Wave Elevation, RAO]
    L --> M[Validation Against Experimental Data: ITTC Trials]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture: FVM Workflow in Marine CFD

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Marine Geometry: Ship Hull, Propeller, Rudder] --> B[Computational Domain: Bounding Box, Refinement Zones]
    B --> C[Volume Mesh Generation: Polyhedral or Hexahedral Cells]
    C --> D[Define Cell Centers and Face Centroids]
    D --> E[Select Physical Models: RANS, k-omega SST, VOF Free Surface]
    E --> F[Formulate Conservation Law: Mass, Momentum, Energy, Species]
    F --> G[Integrate Over Control Volume: Apply Gauss Divergence Theorem]
    G --> H[Approximate Surface Fluxes: Upwind, Central, Roe, HLLC]
    H --> I[Assemble Discrete Algebraic System: aP phiP equals Sum aNb phiNb plus b]
    I --> J[Pressure Velocity Coupling: SIMPLE or PISO Algorithm]
    J --> K[Solve Linear System: AMG or FGMRES]
    K --> L[Post-Processing: Cf, Cp, Wave Cut, Streamlines, Vorticity]
    L --> M[Force Integration: Total Resistance, Side Force, Propeller Thrust]
```

## 4.3 Control Volume Schematic (1D FVM)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Node W: phi_W] -->|west face w| B[Node P: phi_P]
    B -->|east face e| C[Node E: phi_E]
    W[West CV] -->|shares face w| P[Central CV: width delta x]
    P -->|shares face e| E[East CV]
    F1[Convective Flux Fw at w] -.-> P
    F2[Diffusive Flux Dw at w] -.-> P
    F3[Convective Flux Fe at e] -.-> P
    F4[Diffusive Flux De at e] -.-> P
    SRC[Source S in P] -.-> P
```

## 4.4 Comparison Block Diagram: FEM vs FVM in Marine CFD

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Marine CFD Discretization Choice] --> Q1{Domain Type?}
    Q1 -->|Irregular Hull Geometry| FEM[FEM Path]
    Q1 -->|Regular Hex Mesh| FVM[FVM Path]
    Q1 -->|Wave Diffraction, Radiation| FEM
    Q1 -->|Viscous Turbulent Flow| FVM

    FEM --> FEM1[Weak Form: Variational Principle]
    FEM1 --> FEM2[Shape Function Approximation]
    FEM2 --> FEM3[Sparse Symmetric System]
    FEM3 --> FEM4[Best for: Potential Flow, Structural Coupling]

    FVM --> FVM1[Conservation Form: Integral Balance]
    FVM1 --> FVM2[Surface Flux Discretization]
    FVM2 --> FVM3[Sparse Asymmetric System]
    FVM3 --> FVM4[Best for: RANS, LES, Cavitation, VOF]
```
