# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Adaptation]**
*Distinguish between the strong form and the weak form of a PDE. Why is the weak form preferred in the Finite Element Method?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The **strong form** is the pointwise statement of the PDE, requiring the solution to be differentiable up to the highest order appearing in the equation (e.g., second derivatives for the Laplace equation). (1 mark)
- The **weak form** is obtained by multiplying the PDE by a test (weight) function $w$ and integrating over the domain, then applying integration by parts to reduce the derivative order. (1 mark)
- The weak form is preferred in FEM because it allows the use of *piecewise polynomial* approximations (e.g., linear shape functions) that are only **first-order differentiable**, and it naturally incorporates the natural (Neumann) boundary conditions as a boundary integral term. (1 mark)

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2]**
*State the integral form of the general conservation law used as the starting point in the Finite Volume Method. Explain the role of the Divergence Theorem in FVM.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The integral conservation law over a control volume $V$ bounded by surface $A$ is:

$$
\dfrac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{V} \rho \phi \, dV + \int_{A} \rho \mathbf{u} \phi \cdot d\mathbf{A} = \int_{A} \Gamma \nabla \phi \cdot d\mathbf{A} + \int_{V} S \, dV
$$

(1 mark)
- The **Divergence Theorem** $\displaystyle\int_V \nabla \cdot \mathbf{F} \, dV = \int_A \mathbf{F} \cdot d\mathbf{A}$ converts the volume integral of the divergence of fluxes into a surface integral of fluxes crossing the CV boundary. (1 mark)
- This conversion is essential because the resulting *surface fluxes* can be approximated using neighboring cell values, yielding an exact local balance statement for every cell — a property called **local conservation**. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, with two sub-parts of 7 marks each, giving an internal choice between two full 14-mark questions.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
*For the 1D Poisson equation $-\dfrac{d^2 u}{dx^2} = f(x)$ on $[0,1]$ with $u(0) = u(1) = 0$, using a 2-node linear finite element:*
*(i) Derive the element stiffness matrix and load vector.*
*(ii) Assemble the global system for two equal elements of size $h = 0.5$.*
*(iii) Solve for the nodal values if $f(x) = x(1-x)$.*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step (i) — Element Matrices (3 marks):** From derivation in Section 3.2, with $f$ replaced by source $S = f(x) = x(1-x)$:

$$
[K]^e = \dfrac{1}{0.5}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & -2 \\ -2 & 2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

At the three nodes ($x = 0, 0.5, 1$), the source values are $f_1 = 0$, $f_2 = 0.5(0.5) = 0.25$, $f_3 = 0$. Element 1 (nodes 1,2):

$$
\{f\}^{(1)} = \dfrac{0.5}{6}\begin{Bmatrix} 2(0) + 0.25 \\ 0 + 2(0.25) \end{Bmatrix} = \dfrac{1}{12}\begin{Bmatrix} 0.25 \\ 0.50 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0.02083 \\ 0.04167 \end{Bmatrix}
$$

Element 2 (nodes 2,3): $f_2 = 0.25$, $f_3 = 0$:

$$
\{f\}^{(2)} = \dfrac{1}{12}\begin{Bmatrix} 2(0.25) + 0 \\ 0.25 + 0 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0.04167 \\ 0.02083 \end{Bmatrix}
$$

**Step (ii) — Global Assembly (2 marks):** Three nodes, global system $[K_G]\{U\} = \{F_G\}$:

$$
[K_G] = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & -2 & 0 \\ -2 & 4 & -2 \\ 0 & -2 & 2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \{F_G\} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0.02083 \\ 0.08333 \\ 0.02083 \end{Bmatrix}
$$

Apply BCs $u_1 = u_3 = 0$ ⇒ reduce to single unknown $u_2$:

$$
4 u_2 = 0.08333 \Rightarrow u_2 = 0.02083
$$

**Step (iii) — Final Solution (2 marks):**

$$
u_1 = 0, \quad u_2 = 0.02083, \quad u_3 = 0
$$

(Exact analytical solution: $u(x) = \dfrac{x^4}{12} - \dfrac{x^3}{6} + \dfrac{x}{12}$ gives $u(0.5) = 0.0208$.) ✓

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
*For a marine hydrodynamic problem, the governing equation is the 2D Laplace equation $\nabla^2 \phi = 0$ in a domain $\Omega$ bounded by the hull surface $\Gamma_H$ (Dirichlet: $\phi = \bar{\phi}$) and the far-field boundary $\Gamma_\infty$ (Neumann: $\partial \phi / \partial n = 0$). Using the Galerkin method, derive the weak form and identify the symmetric bilinear form and the linear functional.*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

Multiply the strong form $\nabla^2 \phi = 0$ by test function $w$ (with $w = 0$ on $\Gamma_H$):

$$
\int_\Omega w \nabla^2 \phi \, d\Omega = 0
$$

Apply Green's first identity (integration by parts):

$$
\int_\Omega \nabla w \cdot \nabla \phi \, d\Omega - \int_{\Gamma_H \cup \Gamma_\infty} w \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial n} d\Gamma = 0
$$

Since $w = 0$ on $\Gamma_H$ and $\partial \phi/\partial n = 0$ on $\Gamma_\infty$:

$$
\int_\Omega \nabla w \cdot \nabla \phi \, d\Omega = 0
$$

**Symmetric Bilinear Form (3 marks):**

$$
a(w, \phi) = \int_\Omega \nabla w \cdot \nabla \phi \, d\Omega
$$

This is **symmetric** ($a(w, \phi) = a(\phi, w)$), **positive definite** ($a(w, w) \ge 0$, equality only if $w = 0$), and **continuous**.

**Linear Functional (2 marks):**

$$
\ell(w) = 0
$$

**Final Weak Form (2 marks):**

$$
\boxed{
\text{Find } \phi \in H^1(\Omega) \text{ with } \phi = \bar{\phi} \text{ on } \Gamma_H \text{ such that } a(w, \phi) = \ell(w) \;\; \forall w \in H^1_0(\Omega)
}
$$

The symmetry and positive-definiteness of $a(\cdot, \cdot)$ guarantee a unique solution by the Lax–Milgram theorem.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice Alternative]
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper Adaptation]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
*For the 1D steady convection–diffusion equation $\dfrac{d}{dx}(\rho u \phi) = \dfrac{d}{dx}\left(\Gamma \dfrac{d\phi}{dx}\right)$, derive the FVM discretized equation for a control volume surrounding node $P$, with neighbors $W$ and $E$. Clearly define the convective flux $F$ and diffusive conductance $D$, and state the stability criterion for central differencing.*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — CV Integration (2 marks):** Integrate over CV $[x_w, x_e]$:

$$
(\rho u \phi)_e - (\rho u \phi)_w = \left( \Gamma \dfrac{d\phi}{dx} \right)_e - \left( \Gamma \dfrac{d\phi}{dx} \right)_w
$$

**Step 2 — Define Fluxes (1 mark):**

$$
F_e = (\rho u)_e, \quad F_w = (\rho u)_w, \quad D_e = \dfrac{\Gamma_e}{\delta x_e}, \quad D_w = \dfrac{\Gamma_w}{\delta x_w}
$$

**Step 3 — Central Differencing (2 marks):** Approximating face values and gradients:

$$
F_e \phi_e - F_w \phi_w = D_e(\phi_E - \phi_P) - D_w(\phi_P - \phi_W)
$$

with $\phi_e = \dfrac{\phi_E + \phi_P}{2}$, $\phi_w = \dfrac{\phi_P + \phi_W}{2}$.

**Step 4 — Rearrange to Standard Form (1 mark):**

$$
\left( D_e + D_w + \dfrac{F_e - F_w}{2} \right) \phi_P = \left( D_w + \dfrac{F_w}{2} \right) \phi_W + \left( D_e - \dfrac{F_e}{2} \right) \phi_E
$$

i.e., $a_P \phi_P = a_W \phi_W + a_E \phi_E$ with

$$
a_P = a_W + a_E + (F_e - F_w)
$$

**Step 5 — Stability Criterion (1 mark):** Central differencing is stable iff the coefficient $a_E = D_e - F_e/2 \ge 0$, which gives the **Péclet number condition**:

$$
\boxed{\text{Pe} = \dfrac{F_e}{D_e} \le 2}
$$

For $\text{Pe} > 2$ (convection-dominated, e.g., ship boundary layer), use the **upwind scheme**: $\phi_e = \phi_P$ if $F_e > 0$.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
*Compare the Finite Element Method and the Finite Volume Method with respect to:*
*(i) Mathematical foundation*
*(ii) Conservation property*
*(iii) Handling of complex marine geometries*
*(iv) Computational cost for steady-state RANS around a tanker hull*
*(v) Typical marine application areas.*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

| Aspect | FEM | FVM |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Mathematical Foundation (1.5 marks) | Variational / weak form; uses shape functions and Galerkin projection | Integral conservation form; uses Divergence Theorem on control volumes |
| (ii) Conservation Property (1.5 marks) | Satisfied globally (overall), not strictly cell-wise | Satisfied **locally and exactly** for every control volume — the defining advantage |
| (iii) Complex Marine Geometries (1.5 marks) | Excellent — handles curved hull surfaces, slender bodies, and irregular meshes naturally | Good — best with structured or trimmed hex meshes; polyhedral meshes improve flexibility |
| (iv) Computational Cost for Tanker RANS (1.5 marks) | Higher; sparse symmetric systems, but pressure-velocity coupling is non-trivial | Lower per-iteration; well-developed SIMPLE/PISO family; industrial standard |
| (v) Marine Application Areas (1 mark) | Wave diffraction, sloshing, structural FSI, mooring line analysis | Hull resistance, propeller flow, cavitation, free-surface VOF, LNG sloshing (commercial) |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks — Module 2**
>
> 1. **Confusing strong and weak forms:** Writing the weak form without the test function $w$ explicitly — KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for each missing $w$ (appearing 2–3 times per derivation).
> 2. **Forgetting the boundary integral in Green's identity:** The term $-\int_\Gamma w (\partial \phi / \partial n) d\Gamma$ is the most-skipped step. Examiners expect this *explicitly written*.
> 3. **Mixing up the convective and diffusive directions in FVM:** The east face $e$ separates $P$ from $E$, **not** from $W$. Mis-indexing costs up to 2 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting Dirichlet BCs in FEM:** Always state $u_i = \bar{u}_i$ on $\Gamma_D$ explicitly; modify the corresponding row/column of $[K]$ and set $f_i = \bar{u}_i$.
> 5. **Péclet number sign convention:** $\text{Pe} = F/D$ (positive if flow is from west to east). State the criterion $\vert \text{Pe} \vert \le 2$ with the absolute value to be safe.
> 6. **Shape function normalization:** Students often forget that $N_i(x_i) = 1$ and $N_i(x_j) = 0$ — this *defining property* is checked in short-answer questions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 2: FEM & FVM**
>
> - **FEM is variational; FVM is conservative.** This single sentence answers 50% of conceptual questions.
> - The **strong form** requires the solution to be $C^k$ continuous (where $k$ is the order of the highest derivative). The **weak form** requires only $C^{k-1}$.
> - **Galerkin's method** uses the same basis for trial and test functions: $w = N$. **Petrov–Galerkin** uses different ones (e.g., SUPG for convection-dominated ship flows).
> - The **element stiffness matrix** for 1D linear Poisson is $\dfrac{1}{h}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$; this is **symmetric, positive definite, and singular for a single element** (becomes non-singular after assembly with BCs).
> - **Global assembly** sums element contributions at shared nodes — the off-diagonal $-1/h$ entries from neighboring elements add up.
> - **Dirichlet (essential) BCs** are imposed strongly (modify $[K]$ and $\{F\}$). **Neumann (natural) BCs** appear *automatically* in the boundary integral and are imposed weakly.
> - The **FVM integral balance** is *exact* before discretization — only the surface flux approximation introduces error. This is why FVM is the gold standard for **RANS / LES** in marine CFD.
> - **Central differencing** in FVM is 2nd-order accurate but **unstable** for $\text{Pe} > 2$. **Upwind** is stable and 1st-order accurate. **QUICK / TVD schemes** are higher-order stable alternatives.
> - In marine practice: **potential flow (Laplace) → FEM** (Wamit, HydroStar); **viscous RANS → FVM** (Fluent, STAR-CCM+, OpenFOAM).
> - The **Péclet number** $\text{Pe} = F/D$ controls numerical stability of the FVM discretization, analogous to CFL for time-stepping.
> - The **Bilinear form** $a(w, \phi) = \int_\Omega \nabla w \cdot \nabla \phi \, d\Omega$ is symmetric and coercive — guarantees existence and uniqueness of the FEM solution by the **Lax–Milgram theorem**.
> - Always draw the **control volume** with clear labels $W, P, E$ and $w, e$ — KTU examiners reward clear schematics.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Basic Structure of a CFD Code
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST635 - COMPUTATIONAL FLUID DYNAMICS IN MARINE TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** The Basic Structure of a CFD Code ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD)**, a *CFD code* refers to a structured, modular software implementation that numerically solves the governing partial differential equations (PDEs) of fluid flow — namely the **Navier-Stokes equations**, the **continuity equation**, and auxiliary transport equations — over a discretized computational domain. The *basic structure* of a CFD code represents the canonical architecture consisting of three interconnected pillars: the **Pre-Processor**, the **Solver**, and the **Post-Processor**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A CFD code is essentially a numerical engine that converts the physical geometry of a marine system (e.g., hull, propeller, duct) into a discrete mathematical problem, solves it using iterative matrix algebra, and renders the resulting field variables for engineering interpretation. The governing conservation laws solved by any CFD code can be expressed in their general conservative form as: $$ \frac{\partial \mathbf{U}}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot \mathbf{F}(\mathbf{U}) = \mathbf{S}(\mathbf{U}) $$ where $\mathbf{U}$ is the conserved variable vector, $\mathbf{F}(\mathbf{U})$ is the convective-diffusive flux tensor, and $\mathbf{S}(\mathbf{U})$ is the source term vector. For marine applications, this typically encompasses **3 incompressible**, **3 momentum**, **1 energy**, and **1 turbulence** scalar transport equations. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy — The "Digital Wind Tunnel"** Imagine you have a model ship hull and you want to predict how water flows around it without ever putting it in a towing tank. A CFD code acts like a **"digital wind tunnel" for water** (or any fluid). Here is how the analogy works: - **Pre-Processor** → Like an architect drafting the *blueprints* and the *lego-grid* of the towing tank. You draw the hull, divide the surrounding water space into millions of tiny cells (mesh), and tell the system "water enters here, exits there, and the hull is a solid no-slip wall." - **Solver** → Like a super-fast accountant who, for each tiny cell, repeatedly applies Newton's second law (mass + momentum + energy conservation) until the flow field stabilizes. - **Post-Processor** → Like a film studio that takes the raw numerical results and produces colorful **streamlines**, **pressure contours**, and **vorticity ribbons** for visual inspection by the naval architect. > [!TIP] > Every commercial code (ANSYS Fluent, OpenFOAM, STAR-CCM+) and research code shares this same triadic backbone. Understanding the basic structure means understanding what each pillar *does*, *how it talks to the others*, and *where errors are most likely to creep in*. ### 1.3 Standard Engineering Constants and Metrics The following constants frequently appear inside the solver architecture of marine CFD codes: | Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value / Unit | |:------:|:---------|:---------------------:| | $\rho$ | Density of seawater | **1025 kg/m³** (typically **997 kg/m³** for freshwater) | | $\mu$ | Dynamic viscosity of water | **1.003 × 10⁻³ Pa·s** at 20 °C | | $\nu$ | Kinematic viscosity | **1.004 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s** | | $g$ | Gravitational acceleration | **9.81 m/s²** | | $Re_L$ | Reynolds number based on hull length | **10⁶ – 10⁹** for marine vessels | | $Fr$ | Froude number | **0.1 – 0.5** for displacement hulls | | $Ma$ | Mach number | **< 0.3** (incompressible regime for most marine flows) | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three-Pillar Architecture of a CFD Code The basic structure of a CFD code is logically partitioned into three principal modules. Each module contains sub-components that perform specific mathematical and logical tasks. #### Pillar I — The Pre-Processor The pre-processor is responsible for **problem definition** and **domain discretization**. Its tasks include: - **Geometry definition** — Import or CAD-construction of the marine body (hull, propeller blade, rudder, duct, sea-floor bathymetry). - **Domain generation** — Wrapping the body in a fluid envelope (control volume). - **Mesh generation** — Sub-dividing the domain into a finite number of cells or elements. - *Structured mesh* (e.g., O-grid around a propeller). - *Unstructured mesh* (e.g., tetrahedra around a complex bulbous bow). - *Hybrid mesh* (prism layers near walls + tetrahedra in the far field). - **Boundary condition specification** — Defining inlet, outlet, wall, symmetry, and free-surface conditions. - **Initial condition specification** — Providing a starting field for the iterative solver. > [!IMPORTANT] > The pre-processor essentially converts a **physical statement** ("water flows past this hull at 10 knots") into a **mathematical statement** ("solve ∇·u = 0 and ρ(∂u/∂t + u·∇u) = −∇p + μ∇²u + ρg in domain Ω with given boundary data on ∂Ω"). #### Pillar II — The Solver The solver is the **computational heart** of the CFD code. It performs the actual numerical solution and consists of: - **Discretization module** — Converts the governing PDEs into a system of algebraic equations. - **Finite Difference Method (FDM)** — Uses Taylor-series expansions. - **Finite Volume Method (FVM)** — The dominant method in marine CFD; enforces conservation at the cell level. - **Finite Element Method (FEM)** — Used for specialized free-surface and structural-fluid coupling problems. - **Spatial discretization schemes** — Selection of upwind, central, or high-resolution schemes for convective fluxes. - **Temporal discretization schemes** — Implicit, explicit, or Crank-Nicolson schemes for unsteady problems. - **Pressure-velocity coupling algorithms** — Required because pressure does not appear as an independent variable in the incompressible continuity equation. Standard algorithms include: - **SIMPLE** (Semi-Implicit Method for Pressure-Linked Equations) - **SIMPLEC** (SIMPLE-Consistent) - **PISO** (Pressure-Implicit with Splitting of Operators) - **Coupled solvers** (solve momentum and continuity simultaneously). - **Turbulence modeling module** — RANS (k-ε, k-ω SST), LES, DES, or DNS depending on the application. - **Linear algebra solver** — Conjugate Gradient, BiCGSTAB, or multigrid methods for solving the resulting sparse matrix systems. #### Pillar III — The Post-Processor The post-processor transforms raw numerical data into **engineering-meaningful visualizations** and quantitative reports: - **Vector and streamline plots** for velocity fields. - **Contour plots** for pressure, temperature, and turbulence intensity. - **Iso-surfaces** for vorticity magnitude (Q-criterion, λ₂). - **Force and moment integration** on hull surfaces (resistance, side force, yaw moment). - **Free-surface elevation** visualization using volume-of-fluid (VOF) iso-contours. - **Time-series monitoring** of drag, lift, and moment coefficients. ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet The table below summarizes every governing equation, discretization identity, and coupling algorithm formula required at the B.Tech level for Module 3 of PENST635. | # | Equation / Formula | Description / Physical Meaning | Domain | |:-:|:-------------------|:--------------------------------|:------:| | 1 | $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{u}) = 0$ | Continuity equation (mass conservation) | All flows | | 2 | $\rho \left( \dfrac{\partial \mathbf{u}}{\partial t} + \mathbf{u} \cdot \nabla \mathbf{u} \right) = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^{2} \mathbf{u} + \rho \mathbf{g}$ | Momentum (Navier-Stokes) equation | Viscous flow | | 3 | $\rho c_{p} \left( \dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t} + \mathbf{u} \cdot \nabla T \right) = k \nabla^{2} T + \Phi$ | Energy equation (thermal transport) | Heat transfer | | 4 | $\dfrac{\partial (\rho k)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho k \mathbf{u}) = \nabla \cdot \left[ \left( \mu + \dfrac{\mu_{t}}{\sigma_{k}} \right) \nabla k \right] + P_{k} - \rho \varepsilon$ | k-equation (turbulent kinetic energy) | RANS | | 5 | $\dfrac{\partial (\rho \varepsilon)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \varepsilon \mathbf{u}) = \nabla \cdot \left[ \left( \mu + \dfrac{\mu_{t}}{\sigma_{\varepsilon}} \right) \nabla \varepsilon \right] + C_{1\varepsilon} \dfrac{\varepsilon}{k} P_{k} - C_{2\varepsilon} \rho \dfrac{\varepsilon^{2}}{k}$ | ε-equation (dissipation rate) | RANS k-ε | | 6 | $u_{i, j+1} = u_{i, j}^{*} + \Delta t \cdot \mathrm{SU}_{i, j}$ | Generic iterative update inside solver loop | PISO/SIMPLE | | 7 | $a_{P} \phi_{P} = \sum_{nb} a_{nb} \phi_{nb} + b_{P}$ | Standard FVM algebraic equation | Discretization | | 8 | $p' = p^{*} - p$ | Pressure correction split (SIMPLE family) | Pressure coupling | | 9 | $\mathbf{u}_{f} = \mathbf{u}_{f}^{*} - \dfrac{\Delta t}{\rho \, V_{f}} \nabla_{f} p'$ | Rhie-Chow face velocity correction | Collocated grid | | 10 | $Re_{L} = \dfrac{\rho U L}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number (marine hull scale) | Flow regime | | 11 | $Fr = \dfrac{U}{\sqrt{g L}}$ | Froude number (free-surface effects) | Wave-making | | 12 | $\Delta t \le \dfrac{\Delta x}{U + c}$ | CFL stability condition (explicit schemes) | Time marching | | 13 | $C_{D} = \dfrac{2 F_{D}}{\rho U^{2} A}$ | Drag coefficient (post-processor output) | Resistance analysis | | 14 | $Y^{+} = \dfrac{\rho u_{\tau} y_{w}}{\mu}$ | Wall y⁺ (mesh-quality metric) | Turbulence modeling | > [!NOTE] > **Pipeline reminder:** Inside the solver, these equations are not solved directly. They are first integrated over each control volume (FVM) to form algebraic equations, then coupled through the pressure-correction algorithm, then solved by a linear algebra solver, then passed to the post-processor. ### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Marine Engineering The structured CFD pipeline is the **backbone of modern ship hydrodynamics**. Its applications include: - **Hull-form optimization** to reduce wave-making and viscous resistance. - **Propeller open-water analysis** for thrust and torque prediction. - **Self-propulsion simulation** combining hull + propeller + free-surface. - **Slender-body and squat effects** in shallow and confined waterways. - **Cavitation prediction** around propellers and high-speed appendages. - **Slamming and green-water loads** on offshore structures. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Generic Conservation Form Used Inside a CFD Solver Every CFD code, irrespective of its commercial or open-source pedigree, ultimately solves a *generic conservation equation* over each control volume. The mathematical pathway from the integral conservation statement to the discrete algebraic form is derived step-by-step below. **Step 1 — Starting Point: Integral Conservation Statement** For an arbitrary conserved scalar $\phi$ (representing mass, momentum component, energy, turbulence quantity, etc.) transported by a fluid, the integral conservation law over a fixed control volume $\mathcal{V}$ bounded by surface $\mathcal{S}$ with outward unit normal $\mathbf{n}$ reads: $$ \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{\mathcal{V}} \rho \phi \, dV + \int_{\mathcal{S}} \rho \phi (\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{n}) \, dS = \int_{\mathcal{S}} \Gamma \nabla \phi \cdot \mathbf{n} \, dS + \int_{\mathcal{V}} S_{\phi} \, dV $$ The first term represents the **transient (storage)** of $\phi$ inside the volume, the second is the **convective** flux across the boundary, the third is the **diffusive** flux across the boundary, and the fourth is the **source** of $\phi$ inside the volume. **Step 2 — Apply the Divergence Theorem** The surface integral of a dot product can be converted to a volume integral of a divergence. Applying the divergence theorem to the surface flux terms: $$ \int_{\mathcal{S}} \rho \phi (\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{n}) \, dS = \int_{\mathcal{V}} \nabla \cdot (\rho \phi \mathbf{u}) \, dV $$ $$ \int_{\mathcal{S}} \Gamma \nabla \phi \cdot \mathbf{n} \, dS = \int_{\mathcal{V}} \nabla \cdot (\Gamma \nabla \phi) \, dV $$ Substituting these back into the conservation statement: $$ \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{\mathcal{V}} \rho \phi \, dV + \int_{\mathcal{V}} \nabla \cdot (\rho \phi \mathbf{u}) \, dV = \int_{\mathcal{V}} \nabla \cdot (\Gamma \nabla \phi) \, dV + \int_{\mathcal{V}} S_{\phi} \, dV $$ **Step 3 — Collapse All Terms Into a Single Volume Integral** Since all four terms are now under the same volume integral over $\mathcal{V}$, they can be combined: $$ \int_{\mathcal{V}} \left[ \frac{\partial (\rho \phi)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \phi \mathbf{u}) - \nabla \cdot (\Gamma \nabla \phi) - S_{\phi} \right] dV = 0 $$ **Step 4 — Invoke the Local Conservation Argument** For the integral of an arbitrary integrand to be zero over an arbitrary volume, the integrand itself must vanish pointwise (Cauchy fundamental lemma). This yields the **differential conservation form**: $$ \frac{\partial (\rho \phi)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \phi \mathbf{u}) = \nabla \cdot (\Gamma \nabla \phi) + S_{\phi} $$ **Step 5 — Recover the Standard Navier-Stokes System** Setting $\phi = 1$ and $\Gamma = 0$ recovers the continuity equation (mass conservation): $$ \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{u}) = 0 $$ Setting $\phi = u_{i}$ (each velocity component) and $\Gamma = \mu$ yields the momentum equation: $$ \frac{\partial (\rho u_{i})}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho u_{i} \mathbf{u}) = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x_{i}} + \nabla \cdot (\mu \nabla u_{i}) + \rho g_{i} $$ **Step 6 — Discretize Over a Single Cell (FVM Heart of the CFD Code)** For a single finite volume $\mathcal{V}_{P}$ centered on node $P$ with neighbour faces $f$: $$ \frac{d}{dt}(\rho \phi)_{P} V_{P} + \sum_{f} (\rho \phi)_{f} (\mathbf{u}_{f} \cdot \mathbf{n}_{f}) S_{f} = \sum_{f} \Gamma_{f} (\nabla \phi)_{f} \cdot \mathbf{n}_{f} \, S_{f} + S_{\phi, P} V_{P} $$ **Step 7 — Linearize and Rearrange Into the Canonical Algebraic Form** Using explicit time discretization and gathering coefficients of $\phi_{P}$: $$ a_{P} \phi_{P} = \sum_{nb} a_{nb} \phi_{nb} + b_{P} $$ This is the canonical **discrete algebraic system** that the linear algebra solver inside the CFD code solves iteratively. ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Equations to CFD Code Modules The matrix below provides a one-shot mapping of every equation to the specific code module that *owns* it. This is the single most useful KTU revision artifact for Module 3. | Module Inside CFD Code | Equation(s) Handled | Algorithm / Method Used | |:----------------------:|:-------------------|:------------------------:| | Pre-Processor | Geometry, boundary & initial conditions | CAD kernel, surface mesher, volume mesher | | Discretization Module | Generic conservation form | Finite Volume Method (FVM) | | Convection Discretizer | Convective flux $(\rho \phi \mathbf{u}) \cdot \mathbf{n}$ | Upwind, central, TVD schemes | | Diffusion Discretizer | Diffusive flux $\Gamma \nabla \phi \cdot \mathbf{n}$ | Linear interpolation, gradient limiter | | Pressure-Velocity Coupler | Continuity + momentum coupling | SIMPLE / SIMPLEC / PISO | | Turbulence Module | k, ε, ω transport equations | k-ε, k-ω SST, LES | | Time Marcher | Transient term $\partial / \partial t$ | Implicit / explicit / Crank-Nicolson | | Linear Algebra Solver | $a_{P} \phi_{P} = \sum a_{nb} \phi_{nb} + b_{P}$ | AMG, BiCGSTAB, conjugate gradient | | Post-Processor | $C_{D}$, $C_{L}$, $Y^{+}$, free surface | Visualization & force integration | ### 3.3 Worked Example — Pressure Correction Inside the SIMPLE Algorithm To illustrate how the above modules *interact inside the solver*, we derive the SIMPLE pressure-correction step. Start with the discretized momentum equation at cell $P$: $$ u_{P} = H_{u}^{-1} \left[ -\nabla p + \mathbf{r}_{u} \right] $$ In the SIMPLE algorithm, the velocity field obtained from a guessed pressure $p^{*}$ does not satisfy continuity. We introduce a correction $p' = p - p^{*}$, and define corrected face velocity: $$ u_{f} = u_{f}^{*} - \frac{\Delta V}{\rho a_{P}^{u}} \nabla_{f} p' $$ Substituting this corrected velocity into the discrete continuity equation and rearranging gives the **pressure-correction Poisson equation** that the linear algebra solver solves each iteration: $$ \nabla \cdot \left( \frac{\Delta V}{\rho a_{P}^{u}} \nabla p' \right) = \nabla \cdot \mathbf{u}^{*} $$ Once $p'$ is solved, the pressure and velocity fields are corrected, and the loop is repeated until the residual of mass imbalance falls below a user-defined tolerance (typically $10^{-4}$ for marine RANS simulations). ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Top-Level Architecture of a CFD Code The Mermaid block below renders the canonical three-pillar architecture with all sub-modules, illustrating how information flows from the physical problem statement down to the engineering visualization. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Physical Problem Statement
Hull Propeller or Marine System] B[Pre Processor Module] C[Solver Module] D[Post Processor Module] E[Engineering Decisions
Design Iteration] A1[CAD Geometry Import] A2[Domain Envelope Creation] A3[Surface Mesh Generation] A4[Volume Mesh Generation] A5[Boundary Condition Setup] A6[Initial Condition Setup] C1[Discretization Module FVM] C2[Convection Diffusion Split] C3[Pressure Velocity Coupling SIMPLE PISO] C4[Turbulence Model RANS LES] C5[Time Marching Loop] C6[Linear Algebra Solver AMG BiCGSTAB] C7[Residual Monitor and Convergence Check] D1[Streamline and Contour Plots] D2[Force Moment Integration] D3[Free Surface Visualization] D4[Y plus Wall Resolution Check] A --> B B --> C C --> D D --> E E --> A B --> A1 B --> A2 B --> A3 B --> A4 B --> A5 B --> A6 C --> C1 C1 --> C2 C2 --> C3 C3 --> C4 C3 --> C5 C4 --> C6 C5 --> C6 C6 --> C7 C7 --> C3 ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Information Flow Inside the Solver ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeU[Conserved Variable Vector U] nodeF[Flux Tensor F of U] nodeS[Source Term Vector S of U] nodeDis[Discretization Operator D] nodeAlg[Algebraic System Matrix A phi equals b] nodeLin[Linear Algebra Solver] nodeRes[Residual Evaluator] nodeDec[Convergence Decision] nodeOut[Converged Field Solution Output] nodeU --> nodeDis nodeF --> nodeDis nodeS --> nodeDis nodeDis --> nodeAlg nodeAlg --> nodeLin nodeLin --> nodeRes nodeRes --> nodeDec nodeDec -->|Not Converged| nodeAlg nodeDec -->|Converged| nodeOut ``` ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Pressure-Velocity Coupling Loop ```mermaid flowchart TD stepA[Start Iteration k] stepB[Solve Momentum Equations
using pressure star] stepC[Compute Face Flux Imbalance] stepD[Solve Pressure Correction Equation] stepE[Correct Pressure and Velocity Fields] stepF[Update Turbulence Quantities] stepG[Check Mass and Momentum Residuals] stepH{Converged} stepI[Exit Loop and Hand Off to Post Processor] stepA --> stepB --> stepC --> stepD --> stepE --> stepF --> stepG --> stepH stepH -->|No| stepB stepH -->|Yes| stepI ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reading the diagrams:** Every commercial or open-source CFD code can be decomposed into these three nested loops. The **outer loop** is the user-driven design iteration, the **middle loop** is the SIMPLE/PISO inner iteration, and the **innermost loop** is the linear-algebra solver iteration. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PENST635 Module 3]` **List and briefly explain the three major modules of a typical CFD code.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The three major modules of a typical CFD code are: 1. **Pre-Processor (1 mark):** Converts the physical problem into a discretized mathematical form. It handles geometry import, meshing, and assignment of boundary and initial conditions. 2. **Solver (1 mark):** The computational core that discretizes the governing PDEs (Navier-Stokes, continuity, energy, turbulence) using FVM, couples pressure and velocity via SIMPLE/SIMPLEC/PISO, and solves the resulting sparse linear system using iterative methods such as AMG or BiCGSTAB. 3. **Post-Processor (1 mark):** Visualizes the converged field solution through streamlines, contours, force-moment reports, and free-surface iso-surfaces for engineering interpretation. --- **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, PENST635 Module 3]` **Why is the Finite Volume Method (FVM) the most widely used discretization technique in marine CFD codes?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** FVM is preferred in marine CFD because **(1)** it enforces exact conservation of mass, momentum, and energy at the discrete cell level, which is essential for hull-resistance and free-surface problems. **(2)** It accommodates complex, unstructured polyhedral meshes required around bulbous bows, propellers, and rudders. **(3)** Its algebraic formulation $a_{P}\phi_{P} = \sum a_{nb}\phi_{nb} + b_{P}$ is computationally efficient and naturally compatible with iterative pressure-coupling algorithms such as SIMPLE and PISO. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) #### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PENST635]` **With suitable block diagrams, describe the basic structure of a CFD code. List the major sub-components of the pre-processor and solver and explain their functions in the context of marine hydrodynamics.** **Part (a) — 7 Marks: Architecture and Pre-Processor** **Model Solution:** 1. **Architecture Overview (3 marks):** A CFD code is organized into three sequential modules: - **Pre-Processor** — Handles problem definition and discretization. - **Solver** — Performs numerical solution of governing equations. - **Post-Processor** — Generates engineering visualizations and quantitative outputs. 2. **Pre-Processor Sub-components (4 marks):** - *Geometry definition*: CAD import of hull, propeller, appendages. - *Domain creation*: Fluid envelope wrapping the body. - *Surface meshing*: Triangular or quadrilateral surface discretization. - *Volume meshing*: Tetrahedral, hexahedral, or prism layers. - *Boundary condition assignment*: Inlet, outlet, symmetry, wall, free-surface. - *Initial condition specification*: Starting field for the solver. **Part (b) — 7 Marks: Solver Sub-components and Marine Context** **Model Solution:** 1. **Discretization Module (2 marks):** Converts Navier-Stokes, continuity, energy, and turbulence PDEs into the algebraic form $a_{P}\phi_{P} = \sum a_{nb}\phi_{nb} + b_{P}$ using the Finite Volume Method. 2. **Pressure-Velocity Coupling (2 marks):** Marine incompressible flows require iterative algorithms such as SIMPLE, SIMPLEC, or PISO because pressure has no independent evolution equation. 3. **Turbulence Modeling (2 marks):** For full-scale ship Reynolds numbers ($Re_{L} \sim 10^{7}$–$10^{9}$), RANS models (k-ε, k-ω SST) or hybrid LES/DES methods are employed. 4. **Linear Algebra Solver (1 mark):** Iterative solvers (AMG, BiCGSTAB) handle the large sparse matrix systems arising from millions of cells in the marine mesh. #### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, PENST635]` **Derive the generic conservation equation solved by a CFD code. Show clearly how the Navier-Stokes and continuity equations are recovered as special cases. List the major roles played by the post-processor in marine CFD analysis.** **Part (a) — 7 Marks: Derivation of the Generic Conservation Form** **Model Solution:** 1. **Integral form over control volume $\mathcal{V}$ bounded by surface $\mathcal{S}$ (2 marks):** $$ \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{\mathcal{V}} \rho \phi \, dV + \int_{\mathcal{S}} \rho \phi (\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{n}) \, dS = \int_{\mathcal{S}} \Gamma \nabla \phi \cdot \mathbf{n} \, dS + \int_{\mathcal{V}} S_{\phi} \, dV $$ 2. **Apply divergence theorem and invoke local conservation (3 marks):** $$ \frac{\partial (\rho \phi)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \phi \mathbf{u}) = \nabla \cdot (\Gamma \nabla \phi) + S_{\phi} $$ 3. **Recover continuity and Navier-Stokes as special cases (2 marks):** - Setting $\phi = 1$, $\Gamma = 0$ yields $\partial \rho / \partial t + \nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{u}) = 0$. - Setting $\phi = u_{i}$, $\Gamma = \mu$ yields the Navier-Stokes momentum equation. **Part (b) — 7 Marks: Post-Processor Roles in Marine CFD** **Model Solution:** 1. **Qualitative Visualization (2 marks):** Streamlines around the hull, vorticity iso-surfaces behind the propeller, free-surface elevation contours. 2. **Force and Moment Integration (2 marks):** Total resistance $R_{T}$, frictional vs. wave-making decomposition, side force and yaw moment for maneuvering. 3. **Wall-Resolution Diagnostics (2 marks):** $Y^{+}$ distribution maps to verify turbulence mesh fidelity near the hull. 4. **Time-Series Monitoring and Validation (1 mark):** Comparison against towing-tank or CFD benchmark data (e.g., KCS, DTMB 5415 hull forms). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the divergence theorem step** — A direct jump from integral form to differential form without applying the divergence theorem loses 2 full marks. > 2. **Confusing "FVM" with "FEM"** — Writing that FVM minimizes a functional (which is FEM terminology) is a common error; FVM enforces cell-wise conservation. > 3. **Omitting boundary conditions in the pre-processor** — Boundary conditions are the *interface* between the physical problem and the solver; forgetting them costs 1 mark. > 4. **Ignoring the marine context** — Generic CFD answers without reference to hull resistance, free-surface, or propeller-Reynolds-number context lose application marks. ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Triadic backbone:** Every CFD code = Pre-Processor + Solver + Post-Processor. - **Governing equations:** Navier-Stokes + continuity + energy + turbulence scalar transport form the mathematical core. - **Generic conservation form:** $\partial(\rho \phi)/\partial t + \nabla \cdot (\rho \phi \mathbf{u}) = \nabla \cdot (\Gamma \nabla \phi) + S_{\phi}$. - **Discretization of choice in marine CFD:** Finite Volume Method (FVM), enforcing cell-level conservation. - **Canonical algebraic equation:** $a_{P}\phi_{P} = \sum a_{nb}\phi_{nb} + b_{P}$. - **Pressure-velocity coupling is mandatory for incompressible flows:** SIMPLE, SIMPLEC, or PISO. - **CFL stability:** $\Delta t \le \Delta x / (U + c)$ governs explicit time-stepping. - **Wall $Y^{+}$ metric:** $Y^{+} = \rho u_{\tau} y_{w} / \mu$ — must be $\le 1$ for low-Re RANS, $30 < Y^{+} < 100$ for wall functions. - **Marine Reynolds numbers** span $10^{6}$–$10^{9}$, mandating turbulence modeling (RANS or LES). - **Post-processor outputs** include $C_{D}$, $C_{L}$, side force, wave pattern, and $Y^{+}$ maps. - **Key constant:** Seawater density $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³; kinematic viscosity $\nu \approx 1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s. - **Exam tip:** Always explicitly state *which* module a given formula or step belongs to — examiners reward modular clarity.

topics.title: Pre-Processor
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST635 - COMPUTATIONAL FLUID DYNAMICS IN MARINE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pre-Processor

# 🛠️ PRE-PROCESSOR IN CFD: THE FOUNDATION OF ACCURATE MARINE FLOW SIMULATION

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 📘 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Pre-Processor (Definition):** The **pre-processor** is the first and most critical stage of the **CFD solution process**. It involves the construction of the **computational geometry**, definition of the **fluid domain**, generation of the **discretized grid/mesh**, and specification of **boundary conditions** and **fluid properties** that govern the marine flow problem.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, the pre-processor is recognized as the *geometry-to-grid pipeline* — the phase where the continuous physical world (e.g., a ship hull moving through ocean water) is converted into a discrete, mathematically tractable numerical domain.

### 🎯 The Three Pillars of CFD Workflow (KTU Standard)

| Stage | Function | CFD Software Tools |
|-------|----------|--------------------|
| **Pre-Processor** | Geometry → Mesh → BCs | ANSYS ICEM, GAMBIT, SpaceClaim, ICEM CFD |
| **Solver** | Numerically solves Navier-Stokes | ANSYS Fluent, OpenFOAM, CFX |
| **Post-Processor** | Visualization & analysis | ANSYS CFD-Post, ParaView, Tecplot |

### 🌊 Conceptual Analogy: The Marine Engineer's Lens

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain-English Analogy (Real-World):**
> Imagine you are an architect commissioned to design a luxury cruise ship. Before pouring a single drop of concrete or cutting a single sheet of steel, you must first:
> 1. **Draw a precise blueprint** of the hull (this is *geometry creation*)
> 2. **Mark out the exact zones** — engine room, cabins, ballast tanks (this is *domain zoning*)
> 3. **Carve the ship into millions of tiny bricks** so a computer can analyze each brick's stress (this is *mesh generation*)
> 4. **Stamp every brick's boundary** with a rule — "no water can exit here," "water flows in at 30 knots here" (this is *boundary conditions*)
>
> This entire preparatory choreography is what CFD engineers call the **Pre-Processor**. Without it, even the world's fastest supercomputer cannot solve a marine flow problem.

### 🔑 Key Physical & Numerical Constants (Must Memorize)

- **Reynolds Number** for marine vessels: $Re \approx 10^6$ to $10^9$ (turbulent)
- **Froude Number** for ships: $Fr \approx 0.1$ to $0.5$
- **Sea water density** $\rho = 1025 \, \text{kg/m}^3$
- **Kinematic viscosity of sea water** $\nu \approx 1.05 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}$
- **Standard gravity** $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 🧠 The Five Sequential Tasks of the Pre-Processor

1. **Geometry Definition**
   - Construction of the physical boundaries of the marine body (ship hull, propeller blade, submarine, offshore platform).
   - Performed using CAD tools (CATIA, SolidWorks, Rhino) or directly in the pre-processor.
   - The geometry must be **watertight**, with no gaps, overlaps, or non-manifold edges.

2. **Computational Domain (Flow Domain) Definition**
   - The fluid region surrounding the body where the governing equations (Navier-Stokes) will be solved.
   - For marine flows, the domain extends typically **5–10 ship lengths** upstream, **10–20 lengths** downstream, and **3–5 lengths** laterally.

3. **Mesh / Grid Generation**
   - Discretization of the continuous domain into millions of small control volumes or elements.
   - Determines the **resolution**, **accuracy**, and **computational cost** of the simulation.

4. **Boundary Condition Specification**
   - Mathematical prescriptions applied at each domain boundary:
     - Inlet (velocity inlet, pressure inlet)
     - Outlet (pressure outlet)
     - Wall (no-slip for viscous flow)
     - Symmetry plane
     - Interface (for multiphase flows — e.g., air-sea interface)

5. **Physical Property & Initial Condition Assignment**
   - Fluid properties: density $\rho$, viscosity $\mu$, specific heat $c_p$.
   - Initial conditions: Initial velocity field, pressure field, turbulence quantities.

### 📐 Mesh Classification (KTU High-Yield)

#### A. Structured Mesh
- Composed of regular, topologically rectangular (or cylindrical) cells arranged in an $i,j,k$ indexing pattern.
- **Pros:** Lower numerical diffusion, faster convergence, accurate for simple geometries.
- **Cons:** Difficult to apply to complex marine geometries (e.g., propeller blades, bulbous bows).

#### B. Unstructured Mesh
- Composed of irregular tetrahedral, hexahedral, prismatic, or pyramidal cells with no fixed indexing.
- **Pros:** Excellent for complex marine geometries.
- **Cons:** Higher memory consumption, slower convergence.

#### C. Hybrid Mesh
- Combines structured mesh near walls (where boundary layers form) and unstructured mesh in the far-field.
- **Most commonly used in marine CFD** (e.g., KCS container ship simulations).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Boundary layer meshing with $y^+ \approx 1$ is mandatory for resolving viscous sublayer in ship hydrodynamics.

### 📊 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Pre-Processor)

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---------|-------------|------|
| 1 | $y^+ = \dfrac{y \cdot u_\tau}{\nu}$ | Dimensionless wall distance (boundary layer) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $u_\tau = \sqrt{\dfrac{\tau_w}{\rho}}$ | Friction velocity | m/s |
| 3 | $\tau_w = \mu \left(\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right)_{y=0}$ | Wall shear stress | Pa |
| 4 | $y = y^+ \cdot \dfrac{\nu}{u_\tau}$ | First cell height | m |
| 5 | $h_{\text{wall}} = L \cdot \dfrac{y^+}{Re}$ | Normalized first cell height | dimensionless |
| 6 | $Re_L = \dfrac{U_\infty L}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number based on ship length | dimensionless |
| 7 | $Fr = \dfrac{U_\infty}{\sqrt{g L}}$ | Froude number (free-surface effect) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\text{Aspect Ratio} = \dfrac{\max(\Delta)}{\min(\Delta)}$ | Mesh quality metric | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\text{Skewness} = \dfrac{\text{optimal} - \text{cell size}}{\text{optimal}}$ | Mesh quality (0 = ideal, 1 = degenerate) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\text{Orthogonal Quality} = 1 - \dfrac{\theta}{90^\circ}$ | Mesh quality (1 = ideal, 0 = poor) | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\Delta t_{\max} \le \dfrac{\Delta x}{U_\infty + c}$ | CFL condition (solver-side constraint) | s |

### 🛥️ Real-World Marine Engineering Utility

- **Ship resistance prediction** (ITTC 1978 standard): Pre-processor must generate hull-attached mesh with wake-aligned grid.
- **Propeller hydrodynamics**: Hybrid mesh with prism layers around blade surfaces.
- **Wave-structure interaction**: Volume-of-Fluid (VOF) mesh for capturing free surface.
- **Cavitation analysis**: Fine mesh near blade tip where cavitation initiates.
- **Slamming loads**: Adaptive mesh refinement near water-entry zones.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 📐 Derivation 1: First Cell Height ($y$) for Marine Hull Boundary Layer

**Objective:** To derive the required first cell height $y$ such that $y^+ \approx 1$ for resolving the viscous sublayer in ship hull flows.

**Step 1:** Definition of the dimensionless wall distance

$$y^+ = \dfrac{y \cdot u_\tau}{\nu}$$

**Step 2:** Solve for the physical wall distance $y$

$$y = \dfrac{y^+ \cdot \nu}{u_\tau}$$

**Step 3:** Express the friction velocity $u_\tau$ in terms of wall shear stress

$$u_\tau = \sqrt{\dfrac{\tau_w}{\rho}}$$

**Step 4:** Use the ITTC friction line correlation for ship hulls (1978)

$$\tau_w = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot U_\infty^2 \cdot C_f$$

**Step 5:** Where the ITTC friction coefficient is given by

$$C_f = \dfrac{0.075}{(\log_{10} Re_L - 2)^2}$$

**Step 6:** Substitute back into the equation for $y$

$$\boxed{y = \dfrac{y^+ \cdot \nu}{\sqrt{\dfrac{C_f}{2}} \cdot U_\infty}}$$

### 📐 Derivation 2: Total Mesh Count Estimation for a Marine Vessel

**Objective:** To estimate the number of cells required for a mesh-independent solution.

**Step 1:** Define the volume of the computational domain

$$V_{\text{domain}} = L \times W \times H$$

where $L$, $W$, $H$ are domain extents in streamwise, transverse, and vertical directions.

**Step 2:** Define the average cell volume in the far field

$$\Delta V_{\text{far}} = (\Delta x_{\text{far}})^3$$

**Step 3:** Estimate near-wall cell volume from $y^+$

$$\Delta V_{\text{wall}} = y \cdot \Delta x_{\text{wall}} \cdot \Delta z_{\text{wall}}$$

**Step 4:** Total cell count approximation

$$N_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{domain}}}{\overline{\Delta V}}$$

**Step 5:** For a typical KCS container ship simulation (KTU standard case)

$$L_{\text{ship}} = 230 \, \text{m}, \quad U_\infty = 8.05 \, \text{m/s}, \quad Re_L \approx 2.2 \times 10^8$$

**Step 6:** Compute the ITTC friction coefficient

$$C_f = \dfrac{0.075}{(\log_{10}(2.2 \times 10^8) - 2)^2} = \dfrac{0.075}{(8.342 - 2)^2} \approx \dfrac{0.075}{40.18} \approx 1.866 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 7:** Compute the friction velocity

$$u_\tau = U_\infty \sqrt{\dfrac{C_f}{2}} = 8.05 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{1.866 \times 10^{-3}}{2}} \approx 8.05 \times 0.03056 \approx 0.246 \, \text{m/s}$$

**Step 8:** Compute the first cell height (target $y^+ = 1$)

$$y = \dfrac{1 \cdot 1.05 \times 10^{-6}}{0.246} \approx 4.27 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m} \approx 4.27 \, \mu\text{m}$$

**Step 9:** Result Interpretation
> [!IMPORTANT]
> For the KCS ship at $Re = 2.2 \times 10^8$, the first cell height must be approximately **4.27 micrometers** to resolve the viscous sublayer. This is why marine CFD uses **inflation layers** — they grow the mesh exponentially from this tiny near-wall value into the bulk flow region.

### 📐 Derivation 3: Grid Independence Study Metric (GCI)

**Objective:** To derive the Grid Convergence Index (GCI) for verifying mesh-independent solution.

**Step 1:** Define the apparent order of convergence $p$

$$p = \dfrac{\ln\left(\dfrac{f_3 - f_2}{f_2 - f_1}\right)}{\ln(r)}$$

where $r = h_2/h_1 = h_3/h_2$ is the grid refinement ratio (typically $r = 1.3$).

**Step 2:** Compute the extrapolated value

$$f_{\text{ext}} = f_1 + \dfrac{f_1 - f_2}{r^p - 1}$$

**Step 3:** Approximate relative error

$$e_a = \left \vert \dfrac{f_1 - f_2}{f_1} \right \vert \times 100\%$$

**Step 4:** GCI for fine grid

$$\text{GCI}_{\text{fine}} = \dfrac{1.25 \cdot e_a}{r^p - 1}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A GCI below **5%** is generally accepted as grid-independent for marine resistance predictions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 🔄 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Pre-Processing Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CAD Geometry Input: Ship Hull IGES STEP] --> B[Geometry Cleanup: Defeature Repair]
    B --> C[Surface Meshing: Curvature Size Function]
    C --> D[Volume Meshing Strategy Selection]
    D --> D1[Structured Hexahedral Mesh]
    D --> D2[Unstructured Tetrahedral Mesh]
    D --> D3[Hybrid Prism Boundary Layer]
    D1 --> E[Boundary Layer Inflation Layers]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Boundary Zone Specification]
    F --> F1[Inlet Velocity Patch]
    F --> F2[Outlet Pressure Patch]
    F --> F3[Hull Wall No Slip Condition]
    F --> F4[Symmetry Plane]
    F --> F5[Free Surface Interface]
    F1 --> G[Physical Property Assignment]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    F5 --> G
    G --> H[Mesh Quality Check]
    H --> H1[Skewness less than 0.85]
    H --> H2[Aspect Ratio less than 5]
    H --> H3[Orthogonal Quality greater than 0.15]
    H1 --> I[Export to Solver Fluent OpenFOAM]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    I --> J[Solver Stage Module 4]
```

### 🧩 Pre-Processor Sub-System Interaction Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Geometry Phase]
        A1[Hull CAD Model]
        A2[Propeller Geometry]
        A3[Domain Box]
    end
    
    subgraph MESH[Meshing Phase]
        B1[Surface Mesh]
        B2[Volume Mesh]
        B3[Boundary Layer]
    end
    
    subgraph BC[Boundary Setup]
        C1[Velocity Inlet]
        C2[Pressure Outlet]
        C3[Wall Functions]
    end
    
    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> C1
    B3 --> C2
    B3 --> C3
    C1 --> D[Solver Output]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
```

### 🌊 Structured Mesh Topology for a 2D Wigley Hull (KTU Standard Test Case)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N1[North Pole Inlet Boundary] --> N2[North Pole Free Surface]
    N2 --> N3[North Pole Symmetry Plane]
    N3 --> N4[North Pole Wall: Hull]
    N4 --> N5[South Pole Wall: Hull]
    N5 --> N6[South Pole Symmetry Plane]
    N6 --> N7[South Pole Free Surface]
    N7 --> N8[South Pole Outlet Boundary]
    N1 -. Structured O Grid .-> N4
    N4 -. Inflation Layers .-> N5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The Wigley hull uses a **C-type or O-type structured grid** with boundary layer inflation layers hugging the hull surface — this is the gold standard for ship hydrodynamics pre-processing.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: Define Pre-Processor. List its main functions.**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The **pre-processor** is the first stage of the CFD solution process where the physical problem is prepared for numerical computation. Its main functions are:

1. **Geometry creation/import** of the marine body (e.g., ship hull).
2. **Computational domain** definition surrounding the body.
3. **Mesh/grid generation** to discretize the domain.
4. **Boundary condition** specification at all domain faces.
5. **Physical property** and **initial condition** assignment.

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Tip:** Mentioning all 5 functions correctly fetches full 3 marks.

---

#### **Question 2: What is $y^+$ and why is it important in marine CFD pre-processing?**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The **dimensionless wall distance** $y^+$ is defined as:

$$y^+ = \dfrac{y \cdot u_\tau}{\nu}$$

where $y$ is the physical distance from the wall, $u_\tau$ is the friction velocity, and $\nu$ is the kinematic viscosity.

**Importance:** For ship hull boundary layer simulations, $y^+$ controls the near-wall mesh resolution. A target of $y^+ \approx 1$ ensures the viscous sublayer is resolved for accurate frictional resistance prediction. If $y^+ > 30$, wall functions must be used, which compromises accuracy.

---

### 📝 PART B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A: 14 Marks**
`[KTU University Exam - July 2024 - Module 3 Internal Choice]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the pre-processing stage of CFD for a typical ship hull resistance problem. List all boundary conditions used. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Introduction:**
Pre-processing converts the continuous ship hull geometry into a discretized numerical domain suitable for CFD solver application.

**Step 2 — Block Diagram:**

```
[Hull CAD] → [Domain Definition] → [Surface Mesh] → [Volume Mesh]
   → [Inflation Layer (y⁺ ≈ 1)] → [Boundary Setup] → [Property Assignment]
   → [Quality Check] → [Export to Solver]
```

**Step 3 — Boundary Conditions for Ship Resistance Simulation:**

| Boundary | Type | Value |
|----------|------|-------|
| Inlet (upstream) | Velocity inlet | $U_\infty$, $k$, $\varepsilon$ |
| Outlet (downstream) | Pressure outlet | $p = p_\infty$ (gauge) |
| Hull surface | No-slip wall | $u = v = w = 0$ |
| Symmetry plane (if half-hull) | Symmetry | $\partial \phi / \partial n = 0$ |
| Side and bottom walls | Slip or far-field | Pressure-based |
| Free surface (if modeled) | VOF interface | Air-sea interface |

**Step 4 — Justification:**
The no-slip condition on the hull is critical because it generates the viscous boundary layer that determines frictional resistance — typically 60–80% of total ship resistance.

---

**(b)** For a tanker of length $L = 300$ m moving at $U_\infty = 5$ m/s in seawater ($\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $\nu = 1.05 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s), compute the first cell height $y$ required for $y^+ = 1$. Use the ITTC 1978 friction line. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Reynolds number:**

$$Re_L = \dfrac{U_\infty L}{\nu} = \dfrac{5 \times 300}{1.05 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.4286 \times 10^9$$

**Step 2 — Compute ITTC friction coefficient:**

$$C_f = \dfrac{0.075}{(\log_{10} Re_L - 2)^2} = \dfrac{0.075}{(9.155 - 2)^2} = \dfrac{0.075}{51.16} \approx 1.466 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 3 — Compute friction velocity:**

$$u_\tau = U_\infty \sqrt{\dfrac{C_f}{2}} = 5 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{1.466 \times 10^{-3}}{2}} = 5 \times 0.02707 = 0.1354 \, \text{m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Compute first cell height:**

$$y = \dfrac{y^+ \cdot \nu}{u_\tau} = \dfrac{1 \times 1.05 \times 10^{-6}}{0.1354} \approx 7.756 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m} \approx 7.76 \, \mu\text{m}$$

**Step 5 — Result Interpretation:**
The required first cell height is approximately **7.76 μm**. This extremely fine mesh must be placed only in the first inflation layer near the hull; subsequent layers grow geometrically to reduce the total cell count.

---

#### **Question B: 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Compare structured, unstructured, and hybrid meshes. State when each is preferred in marine CFD applications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | Structured | Unstructured | Hybrid |
|---------|-----------|--------------|--------|
| Cell shape | Hexahedral | Tetrahedral | Mixed |
| Indexing | $i, j, k$ regular | Irregular | Mixed |
| Marine use | Simple hulls, propellers | Complex offshore platforms | Modern ship hulls |
| Accuracy | High, low diffusion | Moderate | High |
| Setup time | High | Low | Moderate |
| Memory | Low | High | Moderate |

**Recommendation for KTU syllabus:** Hybrid mesh with **prism boundary layers** near the hull and **tetrahedral far-field** is the modern industry standard (used by HSVA, MARIN, FORCE Technology).

---

**(b)** Define skewness and orthogonal quality. For a marine CFD mesh, what are the acceptable limits? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Skewness Definition:**
Skewness measures how far a cell deviates from its ideal shape (equilateral triangle, perfect cube):

$$\text{Skewness} = \dfrac{\text{Optimal Cell Size} - \text{Actual Cell Size}}{\text{Optimal Cell Size}}$$

Range: **0 (ideal)** to **1 (degenerate)**.

**Step 2 — Orthogonal Quality Definition:**

$$\text{OQ} = 1 - \dfrac{\theta}{90^\circ}$$

Range: **1 (ideal)** to **0 (poor)**, where $\theta$ is the angle between the cell face normal and the line connecting cell centroids.

**Step 3 — Acceptable Limits for Marine CFD:**

| Metric | Excellent | Acceptable | Unacceptable |
|--------|-----------|------------|--------------|
| Skewness | < 0.25 | < 0.85 | > 0.95 |
| Orthogonal Quality | > 0.70 | > 0.15 | < 0.10 |
| Aspect Ratio | ~ 1 | < 5 | > 10 |

**Step 4 — Industry Standard (Fluent Default):** Maximum skewness should be below **0.85** for accurate convergence.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to specify units** in $y$ calculation → loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Using $\log_{10}$ vs $\ln$ confusion** in ITTC formula → loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Not stating Reynolds number assumption** (turbulent vs laminar) → loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Confusing "structured" with "uniform" mesh** in answers → loses 2 marks.
> 5. **Skipping mesh quality metrics** in Part B answers → loses 3 marks.

---

### 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- ✅ Pre-processor is **stage 1** of CFD: Geometry → Domain → Mesh → BCs → Properties.
- ✅ Three mesh types: **Structured, Unstructured, Hybrid** (hybrid most common in marine).
- ✅ **$y^+ \approx 1$** for viscous sublayer resolution; $y^+ \approx 30-50$ for wall functions.
- ✅ **ITTC 1978 formula**: $C_f = \dfrac{0.075}{(\log_{10} Re - 2)^2}$ — must memorize.
- ✅ **First cell height** derivation: $y = \dfrac{y^+ \cdot \nu}{u_\tau}$.
- ✅ **Boundary conditions** for ship resistance: velocity inlet, pressure outlet, no-slip wall.
- ✅ **Mesh quality metrics**: skewness < 0.85, orthogonal quality > 0.15.
- ✅ **GCI < 5%** indicates grid-independent solution.
- ✅ Computational domain: typically **5–10 L** upstream, **10–20 L** downstream.
- ✅ Marine CFD best practice: **prism boundary layers** + tetrahedral far-field.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic:** **"G-D-M-B-P"** → **G**eometry, **D**omain, **M**esh, **B**oundary, **P**roperties — the 5 sequential tasks of the Pre-Processor.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solver and Postprocessor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST635 - COMPUTATIONAL FLUID DYNAMICS IN MARINE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Solver and Postprocessor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Solver: Numerical Engine of a CFD Code

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework of **Computational Fluid Dynamics in Marine Technology (PENST635)**, the **Solver** is formally defined as the *algorithmic kernel of the CFD software stack* that numerically integrates the discretized governing conservation equations (mass, momentum, energy, turbulence, species) over the computational domain using the finite volume method, arriving at a converged field of flow variables.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Verbatim Standard)**
> A solver is the numerical module that iteratively solves a system of algebraic equations derived from the spatial and temporal discretization of the RANS / Euler / Navier–Stokes equations, using pressure–velocity coupling algorithms and under-relaxation, until the residual of every conservation equation falls below a prescribed convergence tolerance (typically $\mathbf{1 \times 10^{-3}}$ for most variables and $\mathbf{1 \times 10^{-6}}$ for energy).

### 1.2 The Postprocessor: Visualizing the Invisible

The **Postprocessor** is the software layer responsible for the *qualitative and quantitative interrogation* of the solved flow field. It accepts raw numerical outputs (cell-centered scalars and node-based vectors) and converts them into physically interpretable graphics and engineering metrics.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition (Verbatim Standard)**
> A postprocessor is a computational tool that performs data extraction, graphical rendering, statistical analysis, and validation of the converged CFD solution through graphical objects (contours, vectors, streamlines, isosurfaces) and numerical reports (forces, moments, mass flow rates, integrals).

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Ship's Bridge Simulation"

Imagine you are simulating the wake of a fishing trawler operating off the Kerala coast:

- **The Solver is the engine room mechanic** — It runs the engine (governing equations), adjusts fuel injection (under-relaxation), pumps oil at the right pressure (pressure–velocity coupling), and watches the gauges (residuals) until the ship is cruising steadily (convergence).

- **The Postprocessor is the captain on the bridge** — Once the ship is steady, the captain looks at the navigation charts (contours), GPS track (streamlines), and the compass (vector plots) to *decide where to go*. Without the captain, all the engine's hard work produces no actionable intelligence.

> [!TIP]
> **Marine Tech Tip:** In ship hydrodynamics, you can run the solver for 5,000 iterations, but it is the postprocessor that tells you whether the *drag coefficient $C_D$* matches the model basin test. The solver produces numbers; the postprocessor produces *understanding*.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used in This Module

- **Convergence tolerance (default):** $\mathbf{1 \times 10^{-3}}$ (dimensionless residual)
- **Under-relaxation factor (pressure):** $\mathbf{0.3}$
- **Under-relaxation factor (momentum):** $\mathbf{0.7}$
- **Courant–Friedrichs–Lewy (CFL) Number:** $\mathbf{C \le 1}$ (transient flows)
- **Standard air kinematic viscosity:** $\nu = \mathbf{1.5 \times 10^{-5}\ \mathrm{m^2/s}}$
- **Seawater kinematic viscosity (at 20°C):** $\nu = \mathbf{1.05 \times 10^{-6}\ \mathrm{m^2/s}}$
- **Reference density (air at sea level):** $\rho = \mathbf{1.225\ kg/m^3}$
- **Marine Froude number criterion (ship resistance):** $\mathbf{Fr = V/\sqrt{gL} \approx 0.15 \text{ to } 0.35}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Decomposition of the Solver Workflow

The KTU 2024 scheme expects the student to articulate the solver pipeline as a *five-stage iterative protocol*:

**Step 1 — Initialization**
The flow field is seeded with initial values (often uniform freestream or a previous solution patched from a coarser mesh). This is the *zero-th guess* without which the iterative solver cannot begin.

**Step 2 — Discretization Assembly**
For each cell, the solver assembles the discretized algebraic form of every governing equation. The Finite Volume Method (FVM) ensures flux conservation across each cell face.

**Step 3 — Pressure–Velocity Coupling**
The continuity and momentum equations are *coupled* because pressure appears in the momentum equation and velocity divergence must vanish. Coupling algorithms decouple this coupling into a sequence of solvable steps:

- **SIMPLE** (Semi-Implicit Method for Pressure-Linked Equations)
- **SIMPLEC** (SIMPLE Consistent)
- **PISO** (Pressure-Implicit with Splitting of Operators)
- **COUPLED** (fully coupled solver)

**Step 4 — Under-Relaxation**
To prevent divergence of nonlinear iterations, all updated variables are damped:

$$\phi^{new} = \phi^{old} + \alpha \, \Delta \phi$$

where $\alpha$ is the under-relaxation factor ($0 < \alpha \le 1$).

**Step 5 — Convergence Monitoring**
The normalized residual $R^\phi$ of variable $\phi$ at iteration $k$ is:

$$R^\phi_k = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{\text{cells}} \left| \sum_{nb} a_{nb}\,\phi_{nb} - a_P\,\phi_P - b \right|}{\displaystyle\sum_{\text{cells}} \left| a_P\,\phi_P \right|}$$

Convergence is declared when $R^\phi \le \epsilon_{tol}$ for all $\phi$.

### 2.2 Pressure–Velocity Coupling Algorithms Compared

The choice of algorithm is a *high-frequency viva and 14-mark question* topic.

| Algorithm | Number of Pressure Corrections | Best Use Case | Marine Application | Speed |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| SIMPLE | 1 per iteration | Steady, incompressible | Ship hull resistance | Moderate |
| SIMPLEC | 1 per iteration | Higher under-relaxation permissible | Propeller open water | Moderate–Fast |
| PISO | 2 per iteration | Transient flows | Sloshing, free-surface | Fast (transient) |
| COUPLED | All equations solved together | Compressible flows | Supersonic marine rockets | Fast (compressible) |

### 2.3 Spatial Discretization Schemes (Convection Term)

The convection term discretization is *the single most important accuracy selector* in the solver.

| Scheme | Truncation Error | Stability | Best Suited For |
|---|---|---|---|
| First-Order Upwind | First order | Very stable | Initial iterations, coarse meshes |
| Second-Order Upwind | Second order | Stable | Production ship hydrodynamics |
| Central Differencing | Second order | Conditional (Pe $\le 2$) | Smooth flows, low Pe |
| QUICK | Third order | Mostly stable | Boundary layers, wakes |
| Power Law | Second order | Stable | Moderate Re flows |

### 2.4 The Postprocessor Pipeline

The postprocessor operates on the *solved field* through three sequential operations:

**Stage A — Field Interpolation**
Cell-centered values are interpolated to nodes or sample planes for rendering.

**Stage B — Derived Quantity Computation**
Turbulence quantities, vorticity $\omega$, Q-criterion, $\lambda_2$, wall shear stress $\tau_w$, pressure coefficient $C_p$, and force/moment coefficients are computed.

**Stage C — Visualization & Export**
Rendering as contours, vectors, streamlines, isosurfaces; export of XY plots, CSV, and reports.

### 2.5 Engineering Formulas Critical for Marine CFD Postprocessing

**Drag Coefficient:**

$$C_D = \frac{2 F_D}{\rho V^2 A}$$

where $F_D$ is drag force (N), $V$ is freestream velocity (m/s), $A$ is reference area (m²).

**Lift Coefficient:**

$$C_L = \frac{2 F_L}{\rho V^2 A}$$

**Pressure Coefficient:**

$$C_p = \frac{p - p_\infty}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2}$$

**Wall Shear Stress (viscous drag input):**

$$\tau_w = \mu \left. \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} \right\vert_{wall}$$

**Skin Friction Coefficient:**

$$C_f = \frac{2 \tau_w}{\rho V^2}$$

**Y+ (dimensionless wall distance — turbulence modelling sanity check):**

$$y^+ = \frac{y \, u_\tau}{\nu}$$

where $u_\tau = \sqrt{\tau_w / \rho}$.

**Volume Flow Rate (verification):**

$$Q = \int_S \vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}\, dA$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility (Marine Context)**
> In a KVLCC2 tanker or DTMB 5415 hull benchmark, the postprocessor extracts the *wave pattern*, *transom stern separation*, and *form drag vs. frictional drag split* — directly informing shipyard decisions on hull form optimization, fuel economy, and IMO EEDI/EEXI compliance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the SIMPLE Pressure-Correction Equation

The pressure-correction equation is the *backbone* of segregated solvers and is a KTU favourite for 14-mark derivations.

**Starting Point — Discretized Momentum Equation:**

For cell P, the discretized $u$-momentum is:

$$a_P u_P = \sum_{nb} a_{nb}\, u_{nb} + \left( p_{west} - p_{east} \right) A_x + b_P$$

where the source $b_P$ contains body forces, transient terms, and any linearized sources. In compact notation:

$$a_P u_P = \sum_{nb} a_{nb}\, u_{nb} + \left( p_{w} - p_{e} \right) A_x + b_P \quad \text{...(1)}$$

**Step 1 — Define Current Approximation and Correction**

Let $u^*$, $v^*$, $p^*$ be the current (incorrect) estimates. The corrected values are:

$$u = u^* + u', \quad v = v^* + v', \quad p = p^* + p'$$

Here $u'$, $v'$, $p'$ are the **corrections**.

**Step 2 — Substitute into Momentum Equation**

Substituting into Equation (1):

$$a_P \left( u^* + u' \right) = \sum_{nb} a_{nb} \left( u_{nb}^* + u_{nb}' \right) + \left( p_w^* + p_w' - p_e^* - p_e' \right) A_x + b_P \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**Step 3 — Subtract the Starred (Old) Momentum Equation**

Subtracting the momentum equation evaluated at starred quantities gives the **correction-only equation**:

$$a_P\, u' = \sum_{nb} a_{nb}\, u_{nb}' + \left( p_w' - p_e' \right) A_x$$

By Patankar's simplification, we **drop the neighbour correction terms** $\sum a_{nb} u_{nb}'$ to avoid implicit coupling:

$$u' \approx \frac{A_x}{a_P} \left( p_w' - p_e' \right) \quad \text{...(3)}$$

**Step 4 — Velocity Correction Formula**

In general, the velocity correction for velocity component $u_i$ in direction $i$ is:

$$u_i' = -\frac{1}{a_P} \frac{\partial p'}{\partial x_i} \, V_{cell} \approx -d_i \left( p_P' - p_{nb}' \right)$$

where $d_i = \dfrac{V_{cell}}{a_P\, \Delta x_i}$ is the **pressure-correction coefficient**.

**Step 5 — Pressure-Correction Poisson Equation**

Substitute the corrected velocities into the continuity equation $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$:

$$\frac{\partial \left( u^* + u' \right)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial \left( v^* + v' \right)}{\partial y} = 0$$

Since $u^*$, $v^*$ do not satisfy continuity, the divergence of starred velocity gives the *mass source*:

$$m^* = \frac{\partial u^*}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v^*}{\partial y}$$

Substituting the correction expressions and rearranging yields the **Poisson equation for pressure correction**:

$$\nabla^2 p' = -\frac{m^*}{\Delta t \, V_{cell}} \quad \text{(transient)}$$

or in steady form:

$$\sum_{nb} a_{nb}^p \, p_{nb}' - a_P^p \, p_P' = b_P^p$$

This linear system is solved iteratively using TDMA, AMG, or Gauss–Seidel.

**Step 6 — Pressure and Velocity Update**

$$p^{new} = p^* + \alpha_p\, p'$$
$$u^{new} = u^* + u'$$

with $\alpha_p$ being the **pressure under-relaxation** (typically 0.3).

### 3.2 Derivation of the Normalized Residual

**Starting from the discretized transport equation for $\phi$:**

$$a_P \phi_P = \sum_{nb} a_{nb}\,\phi_{nb} + b_P$$

**Rearrange to isolate the imbalance at iteration $k$:**

$$R_P^k = a_P \phi_P^k - \sum_{nb} a_{nb}\,\phi_{nb}^k - b_P$$

**The normalized residual (scaled residual) is defined as:**

$$R_{\text{norm}}^\phi = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{cells} \left\vert R_P^k \right\vert}{\displaystyle\sum_{cells} \left\vert a_P \phi_P^k \right\vert}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU evaluation, examiners often give full marks only when the student explicitly writes the **absolute value bars** and the **division by the sum of $a_P \phi_P$** (not by iteration count). Use $\vert \cdot \vert$ in written derivations to be safe.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Solver vs. Postprocessor at a Glance

| Aspect | Solver | Postprocessor |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Function** | Solves algebraic equations | Visualizes and analyzes solved field |
| **Input** | Mesh, BCs, fluid properties, schemes | Converged flow field (.dat, .vtk, .cgns) |
| **Output** | Updated flow field on mesh | Graphs, contours, force reports |
| **Governs** | Accuracy, convergence, stability | Engineering interpretation |
| **KTU Emphasis** | Algorithm choice, discretization | Validation, drag/wave extraction |
| **Failure Mode** | Divergence, oscillation | Misinterpretation, wrong variable plotted |
| **Convergence Indicator** | Residual < $10^{-3}$ | Comparison with experiment $<\ 5\%$ |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Pressure Correction in a 1-D Cell

Consider a single 1-D cell between nodes W (west) and E (east), with $u^* = 1.05\ \mathrm{m/s}$ but the required mass-conserving velocity should be $u^{exact} = 1.00\ \mathrm{m/s}$.

Given: $a_P = 10\ \mathrm{kg/(m^2\cdot s)}$, $A_x = 0.01\ \mathrm{m^2}$, $\rho = 1.225\ \mathrm{kg/m^3}$.

**Step 1 — Compute the velocity correction coefficient:**

$$d = \frac{\rho A_x}{a_P} = \frac{1.225 \times 0.01}{10} = 1.225 \times 10^{-3}\ \mathrm{m^3 \cdot s/kg}$$

**Step 2 — Compute required pressure correction:**

From $u' = u^{exact} - u^* = 1.00 - 1.05 = -0.05\ \mathrm{m/s}$:

$$p' = \frac{u'}{d} = \frac{-0.05}{1.225 \times 10^{-3}} = -40.82\ \mathrm{Pa}$$

**Step 3 — Apply under-relaxation ($\alpha_p = 0.3$):**

$$\Delta p_{\text{applied}} = \alpha_p \, p' = 0.3 \times (-40.82) = -12.25\ \mathrm{Pa}$$

**Step 4 — Updated pressure:**

$$p^{new} = p^* + \Delta p_{\text{applied}}$$

This corrected pressure propagates to neighbouring cells, reducing the continuity imbalance.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Solver Workflow in CFD

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["MESH AND CELL DATA INPUT"]
    nodeB["INITIALIZE FLOW FIELD"]
    nodeC["ASSEMBLE DISCRETIZED EQUATIONS"]
    nodeD["APPLY PRESSURE VELOCITY COUPLING"]
    nodeE["LINEAR SOLVER TDMA OR AMG"]
    nodeF["CHECK RESIDUAL CONVERGENCE"]
    nodeG["UNDER RELAXATION UPDATE"]
    nodeH["WRITE CONVERGED FIELD"]
    nodeI["POSTPROCESSOR INPUT"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeNo{"RESIDUAL LESS THAN TOLERANCE"}
    nodeNo -- NO --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeC
    nodeNo -- YES --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI
```

### 4.2 Architecture Diagram — Pressure–Velocity Coupling Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["PROBLEM CLASSIFICATION"]
    node2["FLOW TYPE"]
    node3["STEADY OR TRANSIENT"]
    node4["COMPRESSIBILITY"]
    node5["INCOMPRESSIBLE STEADY"]
    node6["INCOMPRESSIBLE TRANSIENT"]
    node7["COMPRESSIBLE TRANSIENT"]
    node8["ALGORITHM SELECTION"]
    node9["USE SIMPLE ALGORITHM"]
    node10["USE PISO ALGORITHM"]
    node11["USE COUPLED ALGORITHM"]
    node12["SECOND ORDER UPWIND SCHEME"]
    node13["SECOND ORDER UPWIND WITH PISO"]

    node1 --> node2
    node2 --> node3
    node2 --> node4
    node3 --> node5
    node3 --> node6
    node4 --> node7
    node5 --> node8
    node6 --> node8
    node7 --> node8
    node8 --> node9
    node8 --> node10
    node8 --> node11
    node9 --> node12
    node10 --> node13
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Postprocessor Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeData["CONVERGED FLOW FIELD"]
    nodeRead["FILE READER CGNS OR VTK"]
    nodeInterp["FIELD INTERPOLATION CELL TO NODE"]
    nodeDerived["DERIVED QUANTITY ENGINE"]
    nodeRend["RENDERING ENGINE"]
    nodeReport["REPORT GENERATOR"]
    nodeExport["DATA EXPORT CSV OR XLS"]

    nodeData --> nodeRead
    nodeRead --> nodeInterp
    nodeInterp --> nodeDerived
    nodeDerived --> nodeRend
    nodeDerived --> nodeReport
    nodeRend --> nodeExport
    nodeReport --> nodeExport
```

### 4.4 Sequence Diagram — Iterative Solver Convergence Loop

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant INIT as INITIALIZER
    participant ASSEMBLER as EQUATION ASSEMBLER
    participant COUPLER as PRESSURE VELOCITY COUPLER
    participant LINEAR as LINEAR SOLVER
    participant MONITOR as RESIDUAL MONITOR
    participant UPDATER as RELAXATION UPDATER

    INIT->>ASSEMBLER: PROVIDE INITIAL FIELD
    ASSEMBLER->>COUPLER: SEND DISCRETIZED COEFFICIENTS
    COUPLER->>LINEAR: SOLVE PRESSURE CORRECTION
    LINEAR->>COUPLER: RETURN PRESSURE CORRECTION
    COUPLER->>UPDATER: SEND CORRECTED VELOCITY
    UPDATER->>ASSEMBLER: UPDATE FLOW FIELD
    ASSEMBLER->>MONITOR: COMPUTE NEW RESIDUAL
    MONITOR-->>COUPLER: RESIDUAL BELOW TOLERANCE
    MONITOR-->>ASSEMBLER: CONTINUE ITERATION
```
