In the framework of the **Navier–Stokes equations**, turbulence manifests when the non-linear convective inertia term dominates the viscous diffusion term, quantified by a high **Reynolds Number** ($Re$).

Mathematically, for internal (pipe) flow:

$$Re = \frac{\rho \, U \, D}{\mu} > 4000$$

For external flow over a flat plate:

$$Re_x = \frac{\rho \, U \, x}{\mu} > 5 \times 10^5$$

The **critical Reynolds number** marks the transition threshold above which the laminar flow becomes unstable and turbulent structures (eddies) proliferate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> In Marine CFD, turbulent flow governs nearly every practical phenomenon — boundary layer development on ship hulls, wake structures behind propellers, cavitation bubble dynamics, and free-surface interactions. Accurate prediction of turbulence is the single largest source of uncertainty in marine hydrodynamics simulations.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

Imagine standing on the bank of a **slow, glassy river** — you can clearly see leaves drifting in straight, parallel paths. Now picture the **same river after heavy rain**: the surface becomes chaotic, water swirls in unpredictable circular motions, leaves tumble randomly, and a dye dropped into the flow instantly disperses in every direction.

The first case is **laminar flow** (orderly, layered); the second is **turbulent flow** (chaotic, mixed, multi-scale).

Another classic analogy: **A puff of cigarette smoke rising in still air**.
- Near the source → smooth, ribbon-like streamline (laminar).
- A few centimeters above → the smoke suddenly breaks into chaotic, swirling eddies (transition to turbulence).
- Further up → the smoke disperses rapidly in all directions due to intense molecular and turbulent mixing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition for CFD Students:**
> Turbulence is *not* noise, *not* random in the strict sense (it obeys deterministic Navier–Stokes equations), but practically *unpredictable* because of its extreme sensitivity to initial conditions (chaotic behavior — the "butterfly effect"). This is why we model turbulence statistically, not deterministically, in CFD.

---

### 1.3 The Reynolds Number as the Master Parameter

The single dimensionless group that determines flow regime is the **Reynolds Number (Re)**, named after **Osborne Reynolds (1883)**.

| Parameter | Symbol | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Fluid density | $\rho$ | kg/m³ |
| Characteristic velocity | $U$ | m/s |
| Characteristic length | $L$ | m |
| Dynamic viscosity | $\mu$ | Pa·s |
| Kinematic viscosity | $\nu = \mu/\rho$ | m²/s |

$$Re = \frac{\rho U L}{\mu} = \frac{UL}{\nu}$$

**Physical meaning of Re:**

$$Re = \frac{\text{Inertial (Convective) Forces}}{\text{Viscous Forces}}$$

- $Re \ll 1$ → Viscous forces dominate → **Stokes (creeping) flow**.
- $Re \sim 1$ → Inertia and viscosity comparable.
- $Re \gg 1$ → Inertia dominates → **Turbulent flow**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a typical cargo ship of length $L \approx 200$ m moving at $U = 10$ m/s in seawater ($\nu \approx 10^{-6}$ m²/s), the Reynolds number is of order **$Re \sim 2 \times 10^{9}$** — overwhelmingly turbulent. Marine flows are *always* turbulent.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Seven Defining Features of Turbulent Flow

Turbulent flow possesses a set of universal features that distinguish it from laminar flow. These are the bedrock concepts examined in KTU papers.

#### Feature 1 — **Irregularity (Randomness)**
Turbulence is inherently random and stochastic. The instantaneous velocity field $u_i(\mathbf{x},t)$ cannot be expressed as a closed-form function of space and time. We therefore resort to **statistical descriptions** (mean values, RMS fluctuations, probability density functions).

#### Feature 2 — **High Diffusivity (Enhanced Mixing)**
Turbulence dramatically increases the rates of:
- **Momentum transport** → effective (eddy) viscosity $\nu_t \gg \nu$.
- **Heat transfer** → convective heat transfer coefficients rise sharply.
- **Mass transfer** → species mixing accelerates.

This is why heat exchangers, mixers, and chemical reactors are designed to *induce* turbulence.

#### Feature 3 — **Rotationality (Vorticity)**
Turbulent flows are characterized by intense **vorticity fluctuations**. Stretching of vortex tubes by the velocity gradient tensor is the mechanism by which turbulence *amplifies itself* — a positive-feedback mechanism.

#### Feature 4 — **Three-Dimensionality**
Even when the mean flow is 2-D (e.g., flow over a flat plate at zero angle), turbulence in the boundary layer is intrinsically **3-D**. The fluctuating velocity components $\overline{u' v'}$ etc. are non-zero in all three spatial directions.

#### Feature 5 — **Unsteadiness**
Turbulence is fundamentally unsteady. The flow contains fluctuations over a **continuous spectrum** of frequencies, from very low (large eddies) to very high (small eddies where dissipation occurs).

#### Feature 6 — **Continuum of Scales (Energy Cascade)**
This is the **Kolmogorov (1941) cascade**:

$$L \gg \lambda \gg \eta$$

- **$L$** = integral length scale (energy-containing eddies, of the order of the flow domain size).
- **$\lambda$** = Taylor microscale (intermediate, where viscous effects begin to act).
- **$\eta$** = Kolmogorov length scale (smallest dissipative eddies).

Energy is injected at scale $L$ and cascades down (Richardson's poetic phrase: *"big whorls have little whorls which feed on their velocity, and little whorls have lesser whorls, and so on to viscosity"*) until dissipated at scale $\eta$.

#### Feature 7 — **Dissipative Nature**
Turbulence continuously dissipates kinetic energy into heat via viscous action. Without a continuous energy supply, turbulence decays rapidly (a key feature exploited in DNS initialization and sub-grid models).

> [!NOTE]
> **Intermittency** is an eighth, more subtle feature observed in boundary-layer turbulence: the turbulent/non-turbulent interface is not smooth but fractal, with patches of calm fluid embedded within turbulent regions.

---

### 2.2 Reynolds Decomposition

To make the Navier–Stokes equations tractable, **Osborne Reynolds (1895)** proposed splitting the instantaneous field into a mean and a fluctuating part:

$$u_i(\mathbf{x}, t) = \overline{u_i}(\mathbf{x}) + u_i'(\mathbf{x}, t)$$

With the **Reynolds averaging operators**:

$$\overline{u_i} = \frac{1}{T} \int_{t_0}^{t_0 + T} u_i \, dt \quad \text{(time average)}$$

$$\overline{u_i'}= 0, \quad \overline{\overline{u_i}\,v} = \overline{u_i}\,\overline{v}$$

Applying these rules to the incompressible Navier–Stokes equations yields the **Reynolds-Averaged Navier-Stokes (RANS)** equations, the cornerstone of industrial CFD.

---

### 2.3 Turbulent Kinetic Energy and Length Scales

**Turbulent kinetic energy per unit mass:**

$$k = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\overline{u_i' u_i'} = \tfrac{1}{2}\left(\overline{u'^2} + \overline{v'^2} + \overline{w'^2}\right)$$

**Turbulence intensity:**

$$I = \frac{\sqrt{\tfrac{2}{3}\,k}}{U_\infty} \times 100\,\%$$

**Kolmogorov length scale:**

$$\eta = \left(\frac{\nu^{3}}{\varepsilon}\right)^{1/4}$$

**Kolmogorov time scale:**

$$\tau_\eta = \left(\frac{\nu}{\varepsilon}\right)^{1/2}$$

**Kolmogorov velocity scale:**

$$v_\eta = (\nu \varepsilon)^{1/4}$$

**Taylor microscale (in isotropic turbulence):**

$$\lambda = \sqrt{\frac{15 \nu \overline{u'^2}}{\varepsilon}}$$

**Energy dissipation rate estimate:**

$$\varepsilon \sim \frac{U^3}{L}$$

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the one-stop reference for solving KTU problems on turbulent flow features.** All quantities, units, and typical magnitudes are summarized.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Expression | Typical Value / Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reynolds Number | $Re = UL/\nu$ | $10^5$ – $10^9$ (marine) | Inertia / Viscous ratio |
| 2 | Critical $Re$ (pipe) | $Re_{cr} \approx 2300$ | dimensionless | Laminar–turbulent transition |
| 3 | Reynolds Decomposition | $u_i = \overline{u_i} + u_i'$ | m/s | Mean + fluctuating split |
| 4 | Turbulent Kinetic Energy | $k = \tfrac{1}{2}\overline{u_i' u_i'}$ | m²/s² | KE per unit mass |
| 5 | Turbulence Intensity | $I = \sqrt{2k/3}\,/U$ | 1–10 % | Relative fluctuation strength |
| 6 | Energy Dissipation Rate | $\varepsilon \sim U^{3}/L$ | m²/s³ | Rate of KE → heat |
| 7 | Kolmogorov Length Scale | $\eta = (\nu^{3}/\varepsilon)^{1/4}$ | $\sim 10$–$100 \,\mu$m (marine BL) | Smallest eddy size |
| 8 | Kolmogorov Time Scale | $\tau_\eta = (\nu/\varepsilon)^{1/2}$ | s | Smallest eddy turnover time |
| 9 | Kolmogorov Velocity Scale | $v_\eta = (\nu\varepsilon)^{1/4}$ | m/s | Smallest eddy velocity |
| 10 | Taylor Microscale | $\lambda = \sqrt{15\nu\overline{u'^2}/\varepsilon}$ | m | Intermediate dissipative scale |
| 11 | Integral Length Scale | $L = U^{3}/\varepsilon$ | m | Energy-containing eddy size |
| 12 | Eddy Viscosity (Boussinesq) | $\nu_t = C_\mu k^{2}/\varepsilon$ | m²/s | Turbulent momentum diffusivity |
| 13 | Reynolds Stress Tensor | $\tau_{ij}^{R} = -\rho\overline{u_i' u_j'}$ | Pa | Apparent stress from fluctuations |
| 14 | $-5/3$ Kolmogorov Spectrum | $E(\kappa) = C_K \varepsilon^{2/3} \kappa^{-5/3}$ | m³/s² | Inertial subrange energy |
| 15 | RANS momentum equation | $\rho \overline{u_j}\partial_j \overline{u_i} = -\partial_i \overline{p} + \mu \nabla^{2}\overline{u_i} - \rho\partial_j\overline{u_i' u_j'}$ | N/m³ | Mean momentum balance |

> **Notation:** $\overline{u_i}$ denotes ensemble/time-averaged velocity, $u_i'$ is the fluctuating part, $\varepsilon$ is dissipation rate, $\nu$ is kinematic viscosity, $\kappa$ is wavenumber, and $C_K \approx 1.5$ is the Kolmogorov constant.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Reynolds Decomposition Applied to the Navier–Stokes Equations

We start with the **incompressible continuity and momentum equations**:

$$
\nabla \cdot \mathbf{u} = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathbf{u}}{\partial t} + (\mathbf{u}\cdot\nabla)\mathbf{u} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\nabla p + \nu \nabla^{2}\mathbf{u}
$$

#### Step 1 — Substitute the decomposition

Replace $\mathbf{u}$ with $\overline{\mathbf{u}} + \mathbf{u}'$ and $p$ with $\overline{p} + p'$:

$$
\frac{\partial (\overline{\mathbf{u}} + \mathbf{u}')}{\partial t} + [(\overline{\mathbf{u}} + \mathbf{u}')\cdot\nabla](\overline{\mathbf{u}} + \mathbf{u}') = -\frac{1}{\rho}\nabla(\overline{p}+p') + \nu\nabla^{2}(\overline{\mathbf{u}} + \mathbf{u}')
$$

#### Step 2 — Apply the time-average operator

Using $\overline{\overline{\mathbf{u}}} = \overline{\mathbf{u}}$ and $\overline{\mathbf{u}'} = 0$:

$$
\frac{\partial \overline{\mathbf{u}}}{\partial t} + \overline{(\overline{\mathbf{u}} + \mathbf{u}')\cdot\nabla(\overline{\mathbf{u}} + \mathbf{u}')} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\nabla\overline{p} + \nu \nabla^{2}\overline{\mathbf{u}}
$$

#### Step 3 — Expand the non-linear convective term

$$
(\overline{\mathbf{u}} + \mathbf{u}')\cdot\nabla(\overline{\mathbf{u}} + \mathbf{u}') = (\overline{\mathbf{u}}\cdot\nabla)\overline{\mathbf{u}} + (\overline{\mathbf{u}}\cdot\nabla)\mathbf{u}' + (\mathbf{u}'\cdot\nabla)\overline{\mathbf{u}} + (\mathbf{u}'\cdot\nabla)\mathbf{u}'
$$

Taking the average, $\overline{(\overline{\mathbf{u}}\cdot\nabla)\mathbf{u}'} = (\overline{\mathbf{u}}\cdot\nabla)\overline{\mathbf{u}'} = 0$ (mean gradient acting on zero-mean fluctuation). Similarly $\overline{(\mathbf{u}'\cdot\nabla)\overline{\mathbf{u}}} = 0$.

What remains:

$$
\overline{(\overline{\mathbf{u}}\cdot\nabla)\overline{\mathbf{u}}} + \overline{(\mathbf{u}'\cdot\nabla)\mathbf{u}'}
$$

#### Step 4 — Recognize the Reynolds stress term

Define the **Reynolds stress tensor**:

$$
\tau_{ij}^{R} \equiv -\rho\,\overline{u_i' u_j'}
$$

The averaged momentum equation becomes the **RANS equation**:

$$
\frac{\partial \overline{u_i}}{\partial t} + \overline{u_j}\frac{\partial \overline{u_i}}{\partial x_j} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial \overline{p}}{\partial x_i} + \nu \frac{\partial^{2}\overline{u_i}}{\partial x_j \partial x_j} - \frac{\partial}{\partial x_j}\overline{u_i' u_j'}
$$

**Reading the equation from left to right:**

$$
\text{Local inertia} + \text{Mean convection} = \text{Pressure force} + \text{Viscous diffusion} + \text{Reynolds stress divergence}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Reynolds stress term $\overline{u_i' u_j'}$ introduces **new unknowns**. This is the famous *closure problem* of turbulence: the RANS equations have more unknowns than equations. Resolving this is the central task of every turbulence model.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Turbulent Kinetic Energy (TKE) Equation

Multiply the RANS equation by $\overline{u_i}$ and contract with the fluctuating velocity equation to obtain the **TKE transport equation**:

$$
\frac{Dk}{Dt} = \underbrace{P_k}_{\text{Production}} - \underbrace{\varepsilon}_{\text{Dissipation}} + \underbrace{T_t}_{\text{Turbulent Transport}} + \underbrace{D_\nu}_{\text{Viscous Diffusion}}
$$

Where:

- **Production:** $P_k = -\overline{u_i' u_j'}\,\partial_j \overline{u_i}$ (energy extracted from mean shear).
- **Dissipation:** $\varepsilon = \nu \overline{\partial_j u_i'\, \partial_j u_i'}$ (always positive).
- **Turbulent transport:** $T_t = -\tfrac{1}{2}\partial_j \overline{u_i' u_i' u_j'}$ (redistribution in space).
- **Viscous diffusion:** $D_\nu = \nu \nabla^{2} k$.

In statistically steady, homogeneous shear flow, $T_t \approx 0$ and $D_\nu \approx 0$, leaving the **production–dissipation balance**:

$$
P_k \approx \varepsilon \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; -\overline{u'v'}\,\frac{\partial U}{\partial y} \approx \varepsilon
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Kolmogorov Length Scale from Dimensional Analysis

**Kolmogorov's hypothesis (K41, 1941):** at small scales, turbulence is statistically universal and characterized only by $\nu$ and $\varepsilon$.

We seek a length scale $\eta$ in terms of $\nu$ [m²/s] and $\varepsilon$ [m²/s³].

#### Step 1 — Identify dimensions

$$
[\eta] = L \quad \text{[m]}
$$
$$
[\nu] = L^{2} T^{-1} \quad \text{[m}^{2}\text{/s]}
$$
$$
[\varepsilon] = L^{2} T^{-3} \quad \text{[m}^{2}\text{/s}^{3}\text{]}
$$

#### Step 2 — Form dimensional combination

Assume $\eta = \nu^{a} \varepsilon^{b}$. Matching dimensions:

$$
L = (L^{2} T^{-1})^{a}(L^{2} T^{-3})^{b} = L^{2a+2b}\,T^{-a-3b}
$$

So:
- $2a + 2b = 1$
- $-a - 3b = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; a = -3b$

Substitute: $2(-3b) + 2b = 1 \;\Rightarrow\; -4b = 1 \;\Rightarrow\; b = -1/4$, then $a = 3/4$.

#### Step 3 — Final expression

$$
\eta = \nu^{3/4}\,\varepsilon^{-1/4} = \left(\frac{\nu^{3}}{\varepsilon}\right)^{1/4}
$$

Similarly:

$$
\tau_\eta = \left(\frac{\nu}{\varepsilon}\right)^{1/2}, \qquad v_\eta = (\nu\varepsilon)^{1/4}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Whenever asked to "derive Kolmogorov scales" — present the dimensional analysis argument explicitly. Examiners award full marks for stating the K41 hypothesis, listing dimensions, forming the ansatz, and solving the system — not for memorizing the result.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Feature-by-Feature Cause-and-Effect

| Feature | Physical Cause | Mathematical Signature | Marine Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Irregularity | Non-linear NS dynamics | Stochastic $u_i'(\mathbf{x},t)$ | Propeller wake turbulence |
| Diffusivity | Cross-stream mixing by eddies | High $\nu_t$ | Hull–boundary-layer skin friction |
| Rotationality | Vortex stretching | $\omega_i = \varepsilon_{ijk}\partial_j u_k'$ | Tip vortices behind propeller blades |
| 3-D Nature | Instability of 2-D base flow | All $\overline{u_i' u_j'} \neq 0$ | Cross-flow instabilities on bilge keels |
| Unsteadiness | Broadband frequency spectrum | $E(f)$ continuous | Slam-induced vibration |
| Multi-scale | Richardson cascade | $L/\eta \sim Re^{3/4}$ | Wakes at multiple scales |
| Dissipative | Viscous action at $\eta$ | $\varepsilon > 0$ | Drag heating of hull surface |
| Intermittency | Turbulent/non-turbulent interface | Non-Gaussian PDF | Free-surface entrainment of air |

**Useful scaling identity (KTU favorite):**

$$
\frac{L}{\eta} \sim Re^{3/4}
$$

This shows why high-Re marine flows demand enormous grid resolution in DNS: for $Re = 10^{9}$, $L/\eta \sim 10^{6.75}$ — utterly impractical without turbulence modeling.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Kolmogorov Energy Cascade (Turbulent Scales Hierarchy)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Energy Source: Mean Flow and Mean Shear] --> B[Integral Scale L, time T, velocity U]
    B --> C[Energy-Containing Eddies, anisotropic, O of L]
    C --> D[Inertial Subrange, universal, -5/3 law]
    D --> E[Kolmogorov Scale eta, isotropic, dissipative]
    E --> F[Viscous Dissipation into Heat]
    B -.production Pk.-> C
    C -.cascade.-> D
    D -.transfer.-> E
    E -.epsilon.-> F
    style A fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#b30000,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#ffffb3,stroke:#b3b300,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#cce0ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#e0ccff,stroke:#330099,stroke-width:2px
```

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Turbulence Modeling Hierarchy in CFD

```mermaid
graph TD
    NS[Incompressible Navier Stokes Equations] --> DNS[DNS: Direct Numerical Simulation]
    NS --> LES[LES: Large Eddy Simulation]
    NS --> RANS[RANS: Reynolds Averaged Navier Stokes]
    DNS --> DNSnote["Resolves ALL scales: grid N cubed ~ Re to power 9/4. Reference only, no model"]
    LES --> LESnote["Resolves large scales; models sub-grid via Smagorinsky, WALE, Dynamic"]
    RANS --> RANSnote["Models ALL turbulent scales; solves mean flow only"]
    RANS --> KE[k-epsilon: Standard, Realizable, RNG]
    RANS --> KW[k-omega: Standard, SST]
    RANS --> SPAL[Spalart Allmaras: 1 equation for nu-t]
    RANS --> RS[Reynolds Stress Models: 7 equation full closure]
    LES --> HYB[Hybrid RANS-LES: DES, DDES, SA-IDDES]
    KE --> APP1[Marine appendage flows, simple geometries]
    KW --> APP2[Marine boundary layers with adverse pressure gradient]
    SPAL --> APP3[Aerospace and external aerodynamics]
    RS --> APP4[Highly swirling and rotating flows, propeller flows]
    HYB --> APP5[Ship airwake, helicopter rotor flows]
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Reynolds Decomposition Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Instantaneous Field u_i of x,t] --> B[Time Averaging Operator]
    B --> C[Mean Field u-bar of x]
    B --> D[Fluctuating Field u-prime of x,t]
    C --> E[RANS Equation for Mean Flow]
    D --> F[New Unknown: Reynolds Stress -rho u-prime-i u-prime-j]
    F --> G[Turbulence Model Closure]
    G --> G1[Algebraic: Cebeci Smith, Baldwin Lomax]
    G --> G2[One Equation: Spalart Allmaras]
    G --> G3[Two Equation: k-epsilon, k-omega SST]
    G --> G4[Seven Equation: Reynolds Stress Transport Models]
    E --> H[Solved CFD Solution]
    G --> H
    style A fill:#ffcccc
    style F fill:#ffe066
    style G fill:#99ff99
    style H fill:#66ccff
```

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Feature Mapping of Turbulent Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    TF[Turbulent Flow Characteristics] --> F1[Irregularity and Randomness]
    TF --> F2[Enhanced Diffusivity]
    TF --> F3[Vortical Rotational Motion]
    TF --> F4[Intrinsic 3D Nature]
    TF --> F5[Unsteady Continuous Spectrum]
    TF --> F6[Multi-Scale Energy Cascade]
    TF --> F7[Energy Dissipation]
    TF --> F8[Intermittency]
    F1 --> F1a[Stochastic velocity field u-prime]
    F2 --> F2a[High eddy viscosity nu-t]
    F3 --> F3a[Vortex stretching and tilting]
    F4 --> F4a[All Reynolds stresses non-zero]
    F5 --> F5a[Energy spectrum E of kappa]
    F6 --> F6a[L greater than lambda greater than eta]
    F7 --> F7a[Dissipation epsilon positive]
    F8 --> F8a[Non-Gaussian PDF, turbulent-nonturbulent interface]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block Architecture Note:** The diagrams above emphasize the *computational* structure of how a CFD solver ingests and processes turbulent flow features — exactly the perspective demanded by the KTU 2024 Module 4 learning outcomes on turbulence modeling.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1**
**Define turbulent flow. List any four distinguishing features.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

Turbulent flow is a fluid motion regime characterized by chaotic, irregular, three-dimensional, unsteady fluctuations in velocity and pressure, occurring at high Reynolds numbers ($Re > Re_{cr}$).

Four distinguishing features:

1. **Irregularity** — the velocity field is stochastic and unpredictable in detail.
2. **High diffusivity** — turbulence greatly enhances momentum, heat, and mass transport compared to molecular action alone.
3. **Multi-scale energy cascade** — kinetic energy is transferred from large integral scales $L$ down to dissipative Kolmogorov scales $\eta$.
4. **Dissipative nature** — turbulence continuously converts kinetic energy into heat through viscous action at the smallest scales.

> **Examiner Tip:** Award full 3 marks only if the student explicitly defines the flow regime *and* lists at least four features with brief justification.

---

#### **Question 2**
**What is Reynolds decomposition? Why is it essential in turbulence modeling?** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Reynolds decomposition** expresses any instantaneous flow variable as the sum of a time-averaged (mean) component and a fluctuating component:

$$u_i(\mathbf{x}, t) = \overline{u_i}(\mathbf{x}) + u_i'(\mathbf{x}, t), \quad \text{with} \quad \overline{u_i'} = 0$$

**Why essential:**

When substituted into the Navier–Stokes equations, it yields the **RANS equations**, in which the convective non-linearity produces the unknown Reynolds stress tensor $-\rho\,\overline{u_i' u_j'}$. Because this tensor contains more unknowns than equations, the system is **unclosed**. Turbulence models (Boussinesq hypothesis, $k$-$\varepsilon$, $k$-$\omega$ SST, RSM) provide the closure relations necessary to obtain a solvable system.

Hence, Reynolds decomposition is the **mathematical bridge** between the deterministic Navier–Stokes equations and the statistical framework needed to model turbulent flows in CFD.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Regulation:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. The internal choice offers two alternatives of equal difficulty on the same module.

---

#### **Question A — Option 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]`

**(a)** With the aid of the **Reynolds decomposition**, derive the **Reynolds-Averaged Navier-Stokes (RANS) equation** for an incompressible flow. Clearly define all terms and state the closure problem. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Kolmogorov length, time, and velocity scales** using dimensional analysis. Comment on the practical implications for CFD grid resolution in marine boundary-layer simulations. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question A**

#### **(a) Derivation of RANS Equations (7 marks)**

**Starting equations** for incompressible flow:

$$
\nabla \cdot \mathbf{u} = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial u_i}{\partial t} + u_j \frac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x_i} + \nu \frac{\partial^{2} u_i}{\partial x_j \partial x_j}
$$

**Step 1 — Decomposition:** Substitute $u_i = \overline{u_i} + u_i'$ and $p = \overline{p} + p'$.

**Step 2 — Time average:** $\overline{\overline{u_i}} = \overline{u_i}$ and $\overline{u_i'} = 0$.

**Step 3 — Expand the convective term:**

$$
\overline{u_j \partial_j u_i} = \overline{(\overline{u_j}+u_j')\partial_j(\overline{u_i}+u_i')} = \overline{u_j}\partial_j \overline{u_i} + \overline{u_j' \partial_j u_i'}
$$

**Step 4 — Define Reynolds stress:** $\tau_{ij}^{R} = -\rho\,\overline{u_i' u_j'}$, so that $\rho\,\overline{u_j'\partial_j u_i'} = \partial_j \tau_{ij}^{R}$ (with sign adjustment).

**Step 5 — Final RANS form:**

$$
\boxed{\frac{\partial \overline{u_i}}{\partial t} + \overline{u_j}\frac{\partial \overline{u_i}}{\partial x_j} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial \overline{p}}{\partial x_i} + \nu\frac{\partial^{2}\overline{u_i}}{\partial x_j \partial x_j} - \frac{\partial}{\partial x_j}\overline{u_i' u_j'}}
$$

**Closure problem:** The term $\overline{u_i' u_j'}$ is a new, unknown symmetric tensor with 6 independent components. The RANS equations contain more unknowns than equations; therefore, **additional modeling assumptions are required to close the system**. The Boussinesq eddy-viscosity hypothesis or the more advanced $k$-$\varepsilon$, $k$-$\omega$ SST, and Reynolds Stress Transport Models are typical closure schemes.

#### **(b) Derivation of Kolmogorov Scales (7 marks)**

**Kolmogorov's First Similarity Hypothesis (K41, 1941):** at sufficiently high Reynolds numbers, the small-scale turbulent motion is *statistically stationary, universal, isotropic*, and determined uniquely by the two parameters $\nu$ and $\varepsilon$.

**Dimensional analysis** for the length scale $\eta$:

$$
[\eta] = L, \quad [\nu] = L^{2}T^{-1}, \quad [\varepsilon] = L^{2}T^{-3}
$$

Assume $\eta \sim \nu^{a} \varepsilon^{b}$:

- $L$-balance: $\;\;2a + 2b = 1$.
- $T$-balance: $\;\;-a - 3b = 0$.

Solving: $a = 3/4$, $b = -1/4$. Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\eta = \left(\frac{\nu^{3}}{\varepsilon}\right)^{1/4}}
$$

By the same procedure (with one additional dimension):

$$
\tau_\eta = \left(\frac{\nu}{\varepsilon}\right)^{1/2}, \qquad v_\eta = (\nu\varepsilon)^{1/4}
$$

**Practical CFD implication:**

Using the estimate $\varepsilon \sim U^{3}/L$, we obtain

$$
\frac{\eta}{L} \sim Re^{-3/4} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; N_{DNS} \sim Re^{9/4}
$$

For a marine boundary layer with $Re \sim 10^{9}$, the number of grid cells required for DNS is of order $10^{20}$ — far beyond any foreseeable computer. This is the central reason why **all practical marine CFD codes employ turbulence models** (RANS, LES, or hybrid DES) rather than direct numerical simulation.

---

#### **Question B — Option 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]`

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **seven defining features of turbulent flow** with appropriate physical interpretation for marine engineering applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Kolmogorov energy cascade hypothesis**. Derive the **$-5/3$ inertial-subrange spectrum** $E(\kappa) = C_K \varepsilon^{2/3} \kappa^{-5/3}$ using dimensional analysis. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B**

#### **(a) Seven Defining Features of Turbulent Flow (7 marks)**

| # | Feature | Marine Engineering Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Irregularity** | Wake behind a ship's propeller is statistically repeatable but instantaneously random — requires ensemble-averaged measurement techniques (PIV, LDA). |
| 2 | **Diffusivity** | High turbulent viscosity $\nu_t$ in the hull boundary layer drives skin friction, the largest component of ship resistance at high $Re$. |
| 3 | **Rotationality** | Tip vortices shed from propeller blades are the dominant source of cavitation inception and acoustic signature. |
| 4 | **Three-dimensionality** | Cross-flow over a yawed hull produces streamwise vortices (Görtler-like instabilities) that govern manoeuvring forces. |
| 5 | **Unsteadiness** | Slam loads during ship–wave interaction contain a broad frequency spectrum that may excite resonant structural modes. |
| 6 | **Multi-scale cascade** | From ship-length scales (~100 m) down to sub-millimetre Kolmogorov eddies, the turbulence contains ~10⁶ decades of scale. |
| 7 | **Dissipative nature** | Without sustained mean-flow input, turbulence decays in $O(L/U)$ time; viscous dissipation contributes to thermal boundary-layer heating on high-speed craft. |

A logical conclusion: marine CFD must employ **scale-resolving or scale-modeling techniques** that correctly account for these features, with the choice of model dictated by the Reynolds number, geometry complexity, and computational budget.

#### **(b) Kolmogorov Energy Cascade and the $-5/3$ Spectrum (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Richardson's qualitative picture (1922):** Energy is injected at the largest scales, transferred without loss through a cascade of progressively smaller eddies, and finally dissipated by viscosity at the smallest scales.

**Step 2 — Kolmogorov's hypotheses (1941):**
- **H1 (First Similarity):** Small-scale turbulence is statistically universal, determined by $\nu$ and $\varepsilon$.
- **H2 (Second Similarity):** In the inertial subrange (where neither injection nor dissipation matters), the statistics depend only on $\varepsilon$ and the scale size.

**Step 3 — Dimensional analysis for the energy spectrum $E(\kappa)$:**

In the inertial subrange, $E(\kappa)$ depends only on $\varepsilon$ and $\kappa$:
- $[E(\kappa)] = L^{3} T^{-2}$
- $[\varepsilon] = L^{2} T^{-3}$
- $[\kappa] = L^{-1}$

Assume $E(\kappa) = C_K \varepsilon^{\alpha} \kappa^{\beta}$:

- $T$-balance: $\;-3\alpha = -2 \;\Rightarrow\; \alpha = 2/3$.
- $L$-balance: $\;\;3 - 2\alpha - \beta = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \beta = -5/3$.

**Step 4 — Result:**

$$
\boxed{E(\kappa) = C_K \,\varepsilon^{2/3} \,\kappa^{-5/3}, \qquad C_K \approx 1.5}
$$

**Physical interpretation:** The energy density per unit wavenumber decreases as the inverse $5/3$ power. This universal law has been confirmed experimentally in grid turbulence, atmospheric boundary layers, and oceanic flows, and is the cornerstone upon which sub-grid-scale models for LES are built.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on Turbulence Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "irregularity" with "noise."** Turbulence is *deterministic chaos* governed by Navier–Stokes, not random noise. (–1 mark)
> 2. **Forgetting the closure statement.** When deriving RANS, students often omit to explicitly state "the system is now unclosed and requires modeling of $\overline{u_i' u_j'}$." (–2 marks)
> 3. **Mixing up Reynolds stress sign convention.** Some texts define $\tau_{ij}^{R} = +\rho\,\overline{u_i' u_j'}$; KTU 2024 convention is the negative form appearing on the *RHS* of RANS. (–1 mark)
> 4. **Skipping dimensional-analysis steps.** Examiners expect explicit dimensions and the simultaneous equation solving, not just the final Kolmogorov result. (–2 marks)
> 5. **Writing $\eta = (\nu/\varepsilon)^{1/4}$ — a common power-of-three error.** Verify exponents from dimension matching before writing the answer. (–2 marks)
> 6. **Omitting units and physical interpretation** in formula sheets. KTU examiners reward explanations, not just equations. (–1 mark)

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **Pin this checklist on your wall before the exam.**

- [x] **Definition:** Turbulent flow = chaotic, 3-D, unsteady, multi-scale, dissipative fluid motion at high $Re$.
- [x] **Reynolds Number:** $Re = UL/\nu$; transition threshold ~2300 (pipe), ~$5 \times 10^{5}$ (flat plate).
- [x] **Reynolds Decomposition:** $u_i = \overline{u_i} + u_i'$ — the cornerstone of all RANS modeling.
- [x] **RANS Equation:** Mean momentum balance with Reynolds stress term $\overline{u_i' u_j'}$ on the RHS.
- [x] **Closure Problem:** RANS has more unknowns than equations; closure requires turbulence models.
- [x] **Seven Features:** Irregularity, Diffusivity, Rotationality, 3-D, Unsteadiness, Multi-scale, Dissipative (+ Intermittency).
- [x] **Kolmogorov Scales:** $\eta = (\nu^{3}/\varepsilon)^{1/4}$, $\tau_\eta = (\nu/\varepsilon)^{1/2}$, $v_\eta = (\nu\varepsilon)^{1/4}$.
- [x] **Energy Cascade:** $L \to \lambda \to \eta$ — large eddies transfer energy down to dissipative scales.
- [x] **$-5/3$ Spectrum:** $E(\kappa) = C_K \varepsilon^{2/3} \kappa^{-5/3}$ — universal in inertial subrange.
- [x] **DNS cost scaling:** $N_{DNS} \sim Re^{9/4}$ — impractically large for marine flows ($\sim 10^{20}$ cells at $Re = 10^{9}$).
- [x] **Marine Relevance:** Boundary layers on hulls, propeller wakes, cavitation, slam loads, ship airwakes — all turbulent.
- [x] **Model Hierarchy:** DNS > LES > DES/DDES > RSM > $k$-$\omega$ SST > $k$-$\varepsilon$ > Algebraic (in decreasing fidelity and cost).
- [x] **Turbulent Kinetic Energy:** $k = \tfrac{1}{2}\overline{u_i' u_i'}$.
- [x] **Turbulence Intensity:** $I = \sqrt{2k/3}\,/U_\infty$.
- [x] **Dimensional Analysis Trick:** Always form $[Ansatz] = [parameters]^{powers}$ and solve the linear system for the powers.

---

**End of Notes — Features of Turbulent Flow | PENST635 Module 4 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vorticity Transport Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST635 - COMPUTATIONAL FLUID DYNAMICS IN MARINE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Vorticity Transport Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Vorticity** is a vector field defined as the curl of the velocity field, characterizing the local rotational motion of a fluid element. For a velocity field $\vec{V}$, the vorticity is mathematically expressed as:

$$\vec{\omega} = \nabla \times \vec{V}$$

The **Vorticity Transport Equation (VTE)** is the fundamental governing differential equation that describes the temporal and spatial evolution of the vorticity field in a viscous, Newtonian fluid. It is derived directly from the Navier–Stokes equations through a curl operation and governs the convection, stretching, tilting, and diffusion of vorticity throughout the flow domain.

For a **Newtonian, incompressible fluid** with constant viscosity, the general Vorticity Transport Equation takes the form:

$$\frac{\partial \vec{\omega}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{\omega} = (\vec{\omega} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V} + \nu \nabla^2 \vec{\omega} + \nabla \times \vec{f}$$

where:
- $\frac{\partial \vec{\omega}}{\partial t}$ is the local (unsteady) rate of change of vorticity.
- $(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{\omega}$ is the **convective transport** of vorticity by the velocity field.
- $(\vec{\omega} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}$ is the **vortex stretching and tilting** term.
- $\nu \nabla^2 \vec{\omega}$ is the **viscous diffusion** of vorticity.
- $\nabla \times \vec{f}$ is the curl of body forces per unit mass (e.g., gravity has zero curl).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The VTE is the **cornerstone of the Stream Function–Vorticity formulation**, the most widely taught incompressible viscous flow solver in CFD. It eliminates the pressure term (a major headache in primitive-variable formulations) and decouples velocity components through the stream function.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **tea cup with a spinning tea leaf**: as you stir tea, the leaf rotates because the fluid around it rotates. The local "spin" of the fluid is what we call **vorticity**. 

Now, the VTE is the rulebook describing **how that spin changes**:
- **Convection term** ($\vec{V} \cdot \nabla \vec{\omega}$): Spin is carried along by the flow — like a leaf being carried downstream in a river.
- **Stretching term** ($\vec{\omega} \cdot \nabla \vec{V}$): When a vortex tube is stretched (e.g., a tornado narrowing), it spins faster, just as a figure skater spins faster when pulling their arms in.
- **Diffusion term** ($\nu \nabla^2 \vec{\omega}$): Viscous friction causes spin to spread outward and decay — like honey stirring slowing down when you stop.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world marine analogy:** Behind a moving ship, the helical wake consists of **trailing vortices** shed from the hull. The strength and downstream decay of these vortices are governed entirely by the Vorticity Transport Equation.

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities (Bold Constants & Metrics)

| Symbol | Quantity | SI Unit | Marine Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\vec{\omega}$ | Vorticity vector | $\text{s}^{-1}$ | Wake and propeller vortex intensity |
| $\nu$ | Kinematic viscosity | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ | $\approx 1 \times 10^{-6}$ for seawater |
| $\rho$ | Density | $\text{kg}/\text{m}^3$ | $\approx 1025$ for seawater |
| $\psi$ | Stream function | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ | Used to reconstruct velocity field |
| $\Gamma$ | Circulation | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ | Total enclosed vorticity (Kelvin) |
| $Re$ | Reynolds number | dimensionless | $\frac{UL}{\nu}$, classifies viscous vs. inertial flow |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic: Breaking Down the VTE

The VTE is a balance among four physical mechanisms. Each term is analyzed below.

### Term 1 — Local Unsteadiness: $\dfrac{\partial \vec{\omega}}{\partial t}$

- **Why it exists:** Captures how vorticity changes at a *fixed* spatial location over time.
- **How it works:** In **steady** (time-invariant) flows, this term is identically zero, simplifying the VTE to a purely convective-diffusive balance.
- **Marine Example:** A cavitating propeller produces strongly unsteady sheet cavitation, requiring this term to be retained.

### Term 2 — Convection: $(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{\omega}$

- **Why it exists:** Vorticity is a *material property* of a fluid element (Kelvin's theorem) and is therefore *advected* by the velocity.
- **How it works:** Written in conservative form as $\nabla \cdot (\vec{V} \vec{\omega})$, it represents the bulk transport of vorticity by the mean flow.
- **Marine Example:** Tip vortices from ship propellers are convected far downstream before dissipating.

### Term 3 — Vortex Stretching & Tilting: $(\vec{\omega} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}$

- **Why it exists:** Rate-of-strain tensor couples with existing vorticity to amplify it (stretching) or rotate it (tilting).
- **How it works:** In 3D, a vortex tube elongated along the velocity gradient produces a *stronger* vortex (angular momentum conservation). In 2D, $\vec{\omega}$ is purely in the $z$-direction and this term vanishes identically.
- **Marine Example:** Tip vortex intensification along a propeller blade (3D stretching).

### Term 4 — Viscous Diffusion: $\nu \nabla^2 \vec{\omega}$

- **Why it exists:** Vorticity gradients drive a flux of $\vec{\omega}$ from high to low regions, just as heat diffuses down a temperature gradient.
- **How it works:** The Laplacian operator $\nabla^2$ is elliptic; it guarantees **smooth, infinitely differentiable** vorticity fields in viscous flow.
- **Marine Example:** Boundary layer vorticity is generated at the hull, diffused outward, and eventually shed into the wake.

---

## 2.2 Special Forms of the VTE

| Flow Condition | Simplified VTE | Engineering Context |
|---|---|---|
| **Inviscid, irrotational** | $\dfrac{D\vec{\omega}}{Dt} = 0$ | Kelvin's theorem; potential flow around a hull |
| **Inviscid, barotropic, conservative body force** | $\dfrac{D\vec{\omega}}{Dt} = \dfrac{\nabla \rho \times \nabla p}{\rho^2}$ | Stratified ocean flows |
| **2D incompressible viscous** | $\dfrac{D\omega_z}{Dt} = \nu \nabla^2 \omega_z$ | Most CFD cavity/lid-driven tutorials |
| **2D, steady, no body force** | $(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\omega_z = \nu \nabla^2 \omega_z$ | Marine wake steady-state solvers |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** All formula tables below use the symbols directly required for KTU 2024 Scheme questions on the VTE.

| # | Equation | Description | SI Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| F1 | $\vec{\omega} = \nabla \times \vec{V}$ | Definition of vorticity vector | $\text{s}^{-1}$ |
| F2 | $\omega_z = \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y}$ | 2D vorticity scalar | $\text{s}^{-1}$ |
| F3 | $\Gamma = \oint_C \vec{V} \cdot d\vec{l} = \int_A \omega_z \, dA$ | Circulation–vorticity link | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ |
| F4 | $\dfrac{\partial \vec{\omega}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{\omega} = (\vec{\omega} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V} + \nu \nabla^2 \vec{\omega}$ | Full VTE (3D, incompressible) | $\text{m}/\text{s}^3$ |
| F5 | $\dfrac{D\omega_z}{Dt} = \nu \nabla^2 \omega_z$ | VTE for 2D incompressible viscous flow | $\text{m}/\text{s}^3$ |
| F6 | $u = \dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}, \quad v = -\dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$ | Velocity from stream function | $\text{m}/\text{s}$ |
| F7 | $\nabla^2 \psi = -\omega_z$ | Poisson: stream function to vorticity | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ |
| F8 | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | Kinematic viscosity | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ |
| F9 | $\dfrac{D\vec{\omega}}{Dt} = 0$ | Inviscid VTE (Kelvin's theorem) | $\text{m}/\text{s}^3$ |
| F10 | $Re = \dfrac{UL}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number | dimensionless |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The VTE is the workhorse equation for the **stream function–vorticity ($\psi$-$\omega$)** formulation, the most popular incompressible viscous flow solver in CFD textbooks (Anderson, Tannehill, Pletcher, Versteeg). Its advantages are:

1. **Pressure is eliminated** — no Poisson equation for $p$ is required.
2. **Continuity is automatically satisfied** — by definition of $\psi$.
3. **Boundary conditions are well-defined** — no-slip in terms of $\psi$ and $\omega$ is straightforward.
4. **Compact discretization** — each grid point carries only two unknowns ($\psi$ and $\omega$).

In marine CFD, this formulation is used in simulating:
- Flow around **submerged bodies** (submarines, AUVs).
- **Cavity flows** (ducted propellers, moonpools).
- **Stratified** and **rotating** oceanic flows.
- **Wake mixing zones** behind ship hulls.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From Navier–Stokes to the VTE

### Starting Point — Incompressible Navier–Stokes (Vector Form)

The momentum equation for a Newtonian, incompressible fluid with constant viscosity and body force $\vec{f}$ is:

$$\rho \left[\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}\right] = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^2 \vec{V} + \rho \vec{f}$$

### Step 1 — Divide by $\rho$ to obtain the per-unit-mass form

We divide throughout by $\rho$ to obtain:

$$\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\nabla p + \nu \nabla^2 \vec{V} + \vec{f}$$

where $\nu = \mu / \rho$ is the **kinematic viscosity**.

### Step 2 — Take the curl of both sides

Apply the curl operator $\nabla \times$ to each term. Since $\nabla \times$ is a linear operator, the curl of the left-hand side equals the curl of the right-hand side:

$$\nabla \times \left[\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t}\right] + \nabla \times \left[(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}\right] = -\frac{1}{\rho}\nabla \times \nabla p + \nu \nabla \times \nabla^2 \vec{V} + \nabla \times \vec{f}$$

### Step 3 — Simplify each curl term

**Term-by-term evaluation:**

- $\nabla \times \left[\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t}\right] = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}(\nabla \times \vec{V}) = \frac{\partial \vec{\omega}}{\partial t}$ (curl and partial time derivative commute for steady operators).

- $\nabla \times \nabla p = \vec{0}$ (curl of a gradient is identically zero). Thus, the **pressure term vanishes** — a key feature of the VTE.

- $\nu \nabla \times \nabla^2 \vec{V} = \nu \nabla^2 (\nabla \times \vec{V}) = \nu \nabla^2 \vec{\omega}$ (vector Laplacian commutes with curl for constant-coefficient operators).

- The convective term requires the **vector identity** (shown in the next derivation).

### Step 4 — Apply the convective-term identity

We use the identity:

$$\nabla \times \left[(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}\right] = (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{\omega} - (\vec{\omega} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V} + \vec{\omega}(\nabla \cdot \vec{V})$$

For **incompressible flow**, $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$, so the third term vanishes:

$$\nabla \times \left[(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}\right] = (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{\omega} - (\vec{\omega} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}$$

### Step 5 — Assemble the Final VTE

Substituting the simplified terms back into the curl-applied N–S equation:

$$\frac{\partial \vec{\omega}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{\omega} - (\vec{\omega} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V} + \nu \nabla^2 \vec{\omega} + \nabla \times \vec{f} = \vec{0}$$

Rearranging into standard conservation form:

$$\frac{\partial \vec{\omega}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{\omega} = (\vec{\omega} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V} + \nu \nabla^2 \vec{\omega} + \nabla \times \vec{f}$$

### Step 6 — Convective (Material) Form

Recognising the material derivative $D/Dt = \partial/\partial t + \vec{V}\cdot\nabla$, we obtain the most compact form:

$$\frac{D\vec{\omega}}{Dt} = (\vec{\omega} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V} + \nu \nabla^2 \vec{\omega} + \nabla \times \vec{f}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For conservative body forces** (e.g., gravity $\vec{g} = -g\hat{k}$), $\nabla \times \vec{g} = \vec{0}$, so the body force term drops out completely.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Vector Identity for the Convective Term

The convective-term identity is essential. Here is its step-by-step derivation using index notation (Einstein summation).

### Starting Point — Convective acceleration in tensor form

We begin with the identity:

$$(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V} = \nabla\left(\frac{\vec{V} \cdot \vec{V}}{2}\right) - \vec{V} \times (\nabla \times \vec{V})$$

Taking the curl of both sides:

$$\nabla \times \left[(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}\right] = \nabla \times \nabla\left(\frac{\vec{V} \cdot \vec{V}}{2}\right) - \nabla \times \left[\vec{V} \times \vec{\omega}\right]$$

### Step 1 — First term on RHS vanishes

Since $\nabla \times \nabla \phi = 0$ for any scalar $\phi$, we have:

$$\nabla \times \nabla\left(\frac{\vec{V} \cdot \vec{V}}{2}\right) = \vec{0}$$

### Step 2 — Use BAC–CAB for the second term

The vector identity $\nabla \times (\vec{A} \times \vec{B}) = \vec{A}(\nabla \cdot \vec{B}) - \vec{B}(\nabla \cdot \vec{A}) + (\vec{B} \cdot \nabla)\vec{A} - (\vec{A} \cdot \nabla)\vec{B}$ applied to $\vec{A} = \vec{V}$ and $\vec{B} = \vec{\omega}$:

$$\nabla \times (\vec{V} \times \vec{\omega}) = \vec{V}(\nabla \cdot \vec{\omega}) - \vec{\omega}(\nabla \cdot \vec{V}) + (\vec{\omega} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V} - (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{\omega}$$

### Step 3 — Apply two known identities

- $\nabla \cdot \vec{\omega} = \nabla \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{V}) = 0$ (divergence of a curl is always zero).
- $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$ (incompressibility).

Both vanish, so:

$$\nabla \times (\vec{V} \times \vec{\omega}) = (\vec{\omega} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V} - (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{\omega}$$

### Step 4 — Assemble the result

Substituting back:

$$\nabla \times \left[(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}\right] = -\left[(\vec{\omega} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V} - (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{\omega}\right] = (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{\omega} - (\vec{\omega} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}$$

This matches the identity used in Step 4 of Derivation 1. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Reduction to 2D Incompressible VTE

For 2D flow in the $x$-$y$ plane, the velocity field is:

$$\vec{V} = u(x, y, t)\hat{i} + v(x, y, t)\hat{j}, \quad w = 0$$

The vorticity vector reduces to a single scalar:

$$\vec{\omega} = \omega_z \hat{k} = \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right) \hat{k}$$

### Step 1 — Vortex-stretching term vanishes

Since $\vec{\omega}$ has only a $z$-component and $\vec{V}$ has no $z$-component:

$$(\vec{\omega} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V} = \omega_z \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial z} = 0$$

The vortex-stretching term is identically zero in 2D.

### Step 2 — Final 2D VTE

The 2D VTE becomes:

$$\frac{\partial \omega_z}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial \omega_z}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial \omega_z}{\partial y} = \nu \left(\frac{\partial^2 \omega_z}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \omega_z}{\partial y^2}\right)$$

Or in vector compact notation:

$$\frac{D\omega_z}{Dt} = \nu \nabla^2 \omega_z$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the canonical equation solved in almost every introductory CFD course when teaching the **stream function–vorticity** method for lid-driven cavity flow.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Stream Function–Vorticity Coupled System

### Step 1 — Why a stream function?

For 2D incompressible flow, define a scalar stream function $\psi(x, y, t)$ such that:

$$u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}, \quad v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$$

By construction, this automatically satisfies the continuity equation:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x \partial y} - \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial y \partial x} = 0$$

### Step 2 — Relate $\psi$ to $\omega_z$

Substituting the velocity definitions into the 2D vorticity:

$$\omega_z = \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(-\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}\right) - \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}\right) = -\left(\frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial y^2}\right)$$

This yields the **Poisson equation for $\psi$**:

$$\nabla^2 \psi = -\omega_z$$

### Step 3 — The coupled system

The final closed system is the pair:

$$\frac{\partial \omega_z}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial \omega_z}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial \omega_z}{\partial y} = \nu \nabla^2 \omega_z$$

$$\nabla^2 \psi = -\omega_z$$

with $u, v$ recovered from $\psi$ via Step 1. This decouples **pressure** entirely and is the **standard CFD test case** formulation for incompressible viscous flow.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Term-by-Term Summary

| Term | Physical Meaning | 3D Magnitude | 2D Behaviour | Numerical Character |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\partial \vec{\omega}/\partial t$ | Unsteady accumulation | Active when flow is time-dependent | Active in start-up/lid-driven | Hyperbolic in time |
| $(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{\omega}$ | Advection by flow | Always present | Reduces to $u \partial_x \omega + v \partial_y \omega$ | Nonlinear hyperbolic |
| $(\vec{\omega} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}$ | Stretching + tilting | Active in 3D | **Vanishes** | Source of 3D turbulence |
| $\nu \nabla^2 \vec{\omega}$ | Viscous diffusion | Always present | Reduces to $\nu(\omega_{xx} + \omega_{yy})$ | Elliptic smoothing |
| $\nabla \times \vec{f}$ | Body force curl | $\vec{0}$ for conservative forces | $\vec{0}$ | Often neglected |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Physical Meaning of VTE Terms

The figure below maps the four physical mechanisms within the VTE to their marine-flow manifestations.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vorticity Transport Equation] --> B[Local Unsteadiness]
    A --> C[Convective Transport]
    A --> D[Vortex Stretching and Tilting]
    A --> E[Viscous Diffusion]

    B --> B1[Time varying wakes]
    B --> B2[Sheet cavitation collapse]
    B1 --> B1A[Unsteady propeller inflow]
    B2 --> B2A[Transient cavity collapse]

    C --> C1[Wake advection downstream]
    C --> C2[Tip vortex convection]
    C1 --> C1A[Stern wake trailing]
    C2 --> C2A[Propeller slipstream]

    D --> D1[3D vortex intensification]
    D --> D2[Tip vortex stretching]
    D1 --> D1A[Tornado like marine eddies]
    D2 --> D2A[Hub to tip blade gradient]

    E --> E1[Boundary layer diffusion]
    E --> E2[Vortex decay downstream]
    E1 --> E1A[Hull surface vorticity]
    E2 --> E2A[Far wake dissipation]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of the $\psi$–$\omega$ Solver

The flow chart below shows how the stream function–vorticity formulation is implemented as a numerical solver.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Init[Initialize omega and psi field at t equals 0] --> BC1[Apply boundary conditions on omega walls]
    BC1 --> SolvePsi[Solve Poisson for psi using current omega]
    SolvePsi --> VelRec[Recover u and v from psi gradients]
    VelRec --> Convect[Compute convective term u dwdx plus v dwdy]
    Convect --> Diffuse[Compute viscous diffusion nu Laplacian omega]
    Diffuse --> TimeAdv[Advance omega to new time level]
    TimeAdv --> CheckConverge{Converged or t equals t_final}
    CheckConverge -- No --> BC1
    CheckConverge -- Yes --> Output[Post process streamline vorticity field]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Derivation Roadmap from N–S to VTE

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    NS[Navier Stokes equation] --> Div[Divide by rho]
    Div --> Curl[Take curl of entire equation]
    Curl --> P1[Curl of dp dx is zero]
    Curl --> P2[Laplacian commutes with curl]
    Curl --> P3[Vector identity for convective term]
    P1 --> ElimP[Pressure term eliminated]
    P2 --> Diff[nu Laplacian omega]
    P3 --> Conv[V dot grad omega minus omega dot grad V]
    ElimP --> Assemble[Assemble final VTE]
    Diff --> Assemble
    Conv --> Assemble
    Assemble --> Incomp[Impose divergence of V equals zero]
    Incomp --> FinalVTE[Final VTE: D omega Dt equals omega dot grad V plus nu Laplacian omega plus curl f]
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Marine Application Map of the VTE

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    VTE[Vorticity Transport Equation] --> Hull[Hull boundary layer analysis]
    VTE --> Prop[Propeller wake and tip vortex]
    VTE --> Cav[Cavitation and cavity collapse]
    VTE --> Strat[Stratified ocean flow]
    VTE --> Rot[Rotating frame ship maneuvering]

    Hull --> HullA[Hull generated vorticity shed into wake]
    Prop --> PropA[Blade tip vortex stretching and convection]
    Cav --> CavA[Unsteady vorticity from cavity shedding]
    Strat --> StratA[Baroclinic torque term nonzero]
    Rot --> RotA[Coriolis adds body force curl]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: Definition of Vorticity
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]`**

> Define vorticity. Write the vorticity transport equation for a 2D incompressible viscous flow with no body forces and explain the physical meaning of each term.

**Model Answer:**

Vorticity is defined as the curl of the velocity vector field: $\vec{\omega} = \nabla \times \vec{V}$. For a 2D incompressible viscous flow with no body forces, the VTE is:

$$\frac{\partial \omega_z}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial \omega_z}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial \omega_z}{\partial y} = \nu \left(\frac{\partial^2 \omega_z}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \omega_z}{\partial y^2}\right)$$

- **Local term** $\partial \omega_z/\partial t$: rate of change of vorticity at a point.
- **Convective term** $u\,\partial_x \omega_z + v\,\partial_y \omega_z$: transport of vorticity by the velocity field.
- **Viscous diffusion** $\nu \nabla^2 \omega_z$: viscous spreading and decay of vorticity.

The vortex-stretching term is zero in 2D.

---

### Question A2: Pressure Elimination
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]`**

> Why does the pressure term vanish in the Vorticity Transport Equation, and what is its CFD significance?

**Model Answer:**

The pressure gradient $-\nabla p$ in the Navier–Stokes equation is an *irrotational* vector field. Because the curl of any gradient is identically zero: $\nabla \times (\nabla p) = \vec{0}$, the pressure term is eliminated when the curl operator is applied to the Navier–Stokes equation.

**CFD Significance:** This elimination is the key motivation for the VTE formulation. It removes the elliptic Poisson equation for pressure that otherwise couples all grid points in incompressible flow, allowing the stream function–vorticity method to solve the system efficiently.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Common mistakes students make on VTE questions include (1) **forgetting to invoke $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$** for incompressible flow, leading to a spurious $\omega(\nabla \cdot \vec{V})$ term, (2) **dropping the body-force curl term incorrectly** when the force is non-conservative (e.g., electromagnetic or Coriolis), and (3) **sign errors in the convective vector identity** when applying BAC–CAB. Each of these will cost 2 to 3 marks in a 7-mark sub-question.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — **Derivation and 2D Reduction**

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4, Model]`**

**(a)** Derive the Vorticity Transport Equation from the incompressible Navier–Stokes equation for a Newtonian fluid with body force $\vec{f}$. Clearly state all vector identities used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Hence specialize the equation to (i) 2D incompressible flow in the $x$-$y$ plane with no body force, and (ii) 3D inviscid, barotropic flow with conservative body force. Comment on the physical implications. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Write incompressible N–S in per-unit-mass form:**

$$\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\nabla p + \nu \nabla^2 \vec{V} + \vec{f}$$

**Step 2 — Take the curl of both sides:**

$$\nabla \times \left[\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}\right] = -\frac{1}{\rho}\nabla \times \nabla p + \nu \nabla \times \nabla^2 \vec{V} + \nabla \times \vec{f}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify each term:**

- $\nabla \times \partial_t \vec{V} = \partial_t (\nabla \times \vec{V}) = \partial_t \vec{\omega}$.
- $\nabla \times \nabla p = \vec{0}$ → pressure term drops.
- $\nu \nabla \times \nabla^2 \vec{V} = \nu \nabla^2 \vec{\omega}$.
- Apply the **vector identity**: $\nabla \times [(\vec{V}\cdot\nabla)\vec{V}] = (\vec{V}\cdot\nabla)\vec{\omega} - (\vec{\omega}\cdot\nabla)\vec{V} + \vec{\omega}(\nabla \cdot \vec{V})$.
- For incompressible flow $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$ → the third term vanishes.

**Step 4 — Assemble the VTE:**

$$\frac{\partial \vec{\omega}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{\omega} = (\vec{\omega} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V} + \nu \nabla^2 \vec{\omega} + \nabla \times \vec{f}$$

which can be compactly written as:

$$\boxed{\frac{D\vec{\omega}}{Dt} = (\vec{\omega} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V} + \nu \nabla^2 \vec{\omega} + \nabla \times \vec{f}}$$

**(b) Specializations (7 marks):**

**(i) 2D Incompressible, no body force:**

In 2D, $\vec{\omega} = \omega_z \hat{k}$ and $\vec{V}$ has no $z$-component, so $(\vec{\omega} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V} = \omega_z \partial \vec{V}/\partial z = \vec{0}$. Also $\nabla \times \vec{f} = \vec{0}$. The VTE reduces to:

$$\frac{\partial \omega_z}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial \omega_z}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial \omega_z}{\partial y} = \nu \left(\frac{\partial^2 \omega_z}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \omega_z}{\partial y^2}\right)$$

**Physical implication:** Vorticity in 2D cannot be created or intensified by the flow itself — it can only be *transported* and *diffused*. The vortex-stretching mechanism, which is the source of 3D turbulence, is absent.

**(ii) 3D Inviscid, Barotropic, Conservative Body Force:**

Set $\nu = 0$, $\nabla \times \vec{f} = \vec{0}$ (for conservative $\vec{f}$), and barotropic ($p = p(\rho)$) so $\nabla \rho \times \nabla p = 0$. The VTE reduces to:

$$\frac{D\vec{\omega}}{Dt} = 0$$

**Physical implication:** This is **Kelvin's Circulation Theorem** in differential form — vorticity is conserved following a fluid particle. This underlies potential-flow theory used in classical ship-hydrodynamics panel methods.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **Stream Function–Vorticity Formulation**

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4, Model]`**

**(a)** For 2D incompressible viscous flow, define the stream function $\psi$ and show that the continuity equation is identically satisfied. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the coupled $\psi$–$\omega$ system governing 2D viscous flow. State one marine application where this formulation is preferred over the primitive-variable form. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Stream Function and Continuity (7 marks):**

For 2D incompressible flow in the $x$-$y$ plane, the stream function $\psi(x, y, t)$ is defined such that:

$$u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}, \quad v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$$

**Continuity check:** The 2D continuity equation is:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0$$

Substituting the stream-function definitions:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(-\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}\right) = \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x \partial y} - \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial y \partial x} = 0$$

(Equality of mixed partial derivatives guarantees this is zero.) Thus, by *construction*, $\psi$ automatically satisfies continuity.

**(b) Coupled $\psi$–$\omega$ System (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Vorticity from stream function:**

The 2D vorticity scalar is:

$$\omega_z = \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}$$

Substituting:

$$\omega_z = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(-\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}\right) - \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}\right) = -\left(\frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial y^2}\right)$$

This yields the **Poisson equation for $\psi$**:

$$\nabla^2 \psi = -\omega_z$$

**Step 2 — Vorticity transport in 2D:**

From the specialized 2D VTE (with no body force):

$$\frac{\partial \omega_z}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial \omega_z}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial \omega_z}{\partial y} = \nu \nabla^2 \omega_z$$

**Step 3 — Coupled system:**

$$\boxed{\begin{aligned} \frac{\partial \omega}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial \omega}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial \omega}{\partial y} &= \nu \nabla^2 \omega \\[4pt] \nabla^2 \psi &= -\omega \end{aligned}}$$

with velocity components $u = \partial \psi/\partial y$ and $v = -\partial \psi/\partial x$.

**Marine Application:** This formulation is preferred in simulating the **flow inside a moonpool or ducted propeller** — a closed 2D domain where pressure boundary conditions are difficult to prescribe, but the no-slip condition is naturally expressed in terms of $\psi$ and $\omega$ along the wall.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Vorticity Transport Equation (Module 4)**

- **Definition:** $\vec{\omega} = \nabla \times \vec{V}$; 2D scalar: $\omega_z = \partial v/\partial x - \partial u/\partial y$.
- **Full 3D Incompressible VTE:** $\dfrac{D\vec{\omega}}{Dt} = (\vec{\omega} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V} + \nu \nabla^2 \vec{\omega} + \nabla \times \vec{f}$.
- **Pressure term vanishes** because $\nabla \times \nabla p = 0$ — key motivation for the formulation.
- **Convective-term identity** (essential for derivations): $\nabla \times [(\vec{V}\cdot\nabla)\vec{V}] = (\vec{V}\cdot\nabla)\vec{\omega} - (\vec{\omega}\cdot\nabla)\vec{V} + \vec{\omega}(\nabla \cdot \vec{V})$.
- **Incompressibility** $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$ is used to drop the $\vec{\omega}(\nabla \cdot \vec{V})$ term.
- **2D reduction:** vortex stretching $\equiv 0$; VTE becomes $\dfrac{D\omega_z}{Dt} = \nu \nabla^2 \omega_z$.
- **Inviscid, conservative:** $\dfrac{D\vec{\omega}}{Dt} = 0$ → Kelvin's theorem.
- **Stream function definition:** $u = \partial \psi/\partial y$, $v = -\partial \psi/\partial x$ — automatically satisfies continuity.
- **Poisson coupling:** $\nabla^2 \psi = -\omega_z$.
- **Advantages of $\psi$-$\omega$ formulation:** no pressure unknown, no continuity equation, no Poisson-for-$p$ solve.
- **Marine uses:** ship hull wakes, propeller tip vortices, moonpool flows, cavity flows, rotating-frame manoeuvring.
- **Common pitfalls:** forgetting $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$, sign errors in the vector identity, ignoring the body-force curl for non-conservative forces, treating $(\vec{\omega} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}$ as nonzero in 2D.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: General Properties of Turbulent Quantities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST635 - COMPUTATIONAL FLUID DYNAMICS IN MARINE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** General Properties of Turbulent Quantities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a "Turbulent Quantity"?

In **Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD)** and marine hydrodynamics, any instantaneous flow variable (velocity component, pressure, temperature, concentration, etc.) in a **turbulent flow field** is called a **turbulent quantity**. Because turbulence is inherently unsteady and chaotic, every turbulent quantity fluctuates rapidly about a well-defined mean value.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> A *turbulent quantity* is an instantaneous flow variable $u_i(\mathbf{x},t)$, $p(\mathbf{x},t)$ or $T(\mathbf{x},t)$ in a turbulent flow whose value at any point can be expressed as the sum of a time-averaged (mean) component and a fluctuating component, in accordance with **Reynolds Decomposition**.

The instantaneous velocity field $\mathbf{u}(\mathbf{x},t)$ is written as:

$$\mathbf{u}(\mathbf{x},t) = \overline{\mathbf{u}}(\mathbf{x}) + \mathbf{u}'(\mathbf{x},t)$$

where:
- $\overline{\mathbf{u}}(\mathbf{x})$ = **time-averaged (mean) velocity** — a steady, deterministic function of position.
- $\mathbf{u}'(\mathbf{x},t)$ = **turbulent fluctuation** — a zero-mean, stochastic function of position and time.

## 1.2 Reynolds Decomposition — The Foundation Stone

Introduced by **Osborne Reynolds (1895)**, this decomposition is the mathematical gateway to all turbulence modelling. For any scalar $\phi$:

$$\phi(\mathbf{x},t) = \overline{\phi}(\mathbf{x}) + \phi'(\mathbf{x},t)$$

For a vector quantity, the decomposition applies **component-wise**:

$$u(\mathbf{x},t) = \overline{u}(\mathbf{x}) + u'(\mathbf{x},t)$$
$$v(\mathbf{x},t) = \overline{v}(\mathbf{x}) + v'(\mathbf{x},t)$$
$$w(\mathbf{x},t) = \overline{w}(\mathbf{x}) + w'(\mathbf{x},t)$$

## 1.3 The Time-Average Operator

For a **statistically stationary (ergodic)** turbulent flow, the time average over a sufficiently long interval $T$ is:

$$\overline{\phi}(\mathbf{x}) = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{T} \int_{t_0}^{t_0+T} \phi(\mathbf{x},t)\, dt$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stationarity assumption:** The mean $\overline{\phi}$ is **independent of the time origin $t_0$**. This is the *ergodic hypothesis* and is mandatory before Reynolds averaging rules can be applied. Marine flows around ships, propellers, and offshore structures are often *non-stationary*, requiring **ensemble averaging** instead — but KTU Module 4 focuses on stationary turbulence.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Boat on Choppy Sea"

Imagine a small fishing boat moving on the **choppy Arabian Sea**. If you record its instantaneous vertical displacement (height above mean sea level), you get a wildly oscillating signal — large crests and troughs. But if you average the signal over **10 minutes**, you essentially get zero (the calm mean sea level). The oscillations are the **fluctuations** $u'$, the calm sea is the **mean** $\overline{u}$.

Key parallels:
- The **boat bobbing** = instantaneous turbulent quantity $u$.
- The **calm sea level** = mean component $\overline{u}$.
- The **bobbing motion** = fluctuation $u'$.
- The **average bobbing** (over a long time) = **0**.

## 1.5 Why Study "General Properties"?

Before any turbulence model ($k$-$\varepsilon$, $k$-$\omega$, RSM, LES) can be implemented, the student must master the **algebraic and statistical properties** of turbulent quantities. These properties dictate:
1. How non-linear convective terms (e.g., $u_i u_j$) simplify under averaging.
2. The emergence of **Reynolds stresses** — extra unknowns that close the mean-flow equations.
3. The mathematical definition of **turbulent kinetic energy**, intensity, and dissipation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU Module 4 explicitly tests the *averaging rules*, the *properties of correlations* ($\overline{u_i' u_j'}$), and the *tensor nature of Reynolds stresses*. Marks are heavily awarded for deriving these properties step-by-step.

## 1.6 Statistical Quantities of Interest

| Quantity | Symbol | Engineering Significance |
|----------|--------|--------------------------|
| Turbulent Kinetic Energy | $k$ | Energy reservoir of fluctuations |
| Turbulence Intensity | $I$ | Measure of unsteadiness |
| Reynolds Stress | $\tau^{Re}_{ij}$ | Apparent stress due to fluctuations |
| Integral Length Scale | $L$ | Size of energy-containing eddies |
| Energy Dissipation Rate | $\varepsilon$ | Rate of $k$ loss to heat |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Fundamental Reynolds Averaging Rules

For any two turbulent quantities $\phi$ and $\psi$ and a constant $c$, Reynolds averaging obeys four algebraic identities — these are the **most heavily tested theorems** in KTU Module 4.

### Rule 1 — Linearity

$$\overline{\phi + \psi} = \overline{\phi} + \overline{\psi}$$
$$\overline{c\,\phi} = c\,\overline{\phi}$$

> **Why:** Because integration is a linear operator, and time averaging is just a definite integral normalised by $T$.

### Rule 2 — Mean of a Mean is the Mean

$$\overline{\overline{\phi}} = \overline{\phi}$$

> **Why:** $\overline{\phi}$ is independent of time (stationary), so its time average equals itself.

### Rule 3 — Mean of a Fluctuation is Zero

$$\overline{\phi'} = 0$$

> **Why:** By definition, $\phi' = \phi - \overline{\phi}$. Taking the mean of both sides: $\overline{\phi'} = \overline{\phi - \overline{\phi}} = \overline{\phi} - \overline{\phi} = 0$.

### Rule 4 — Mean of a Product of a Mean and a Fluctuation is Zero

$$\overline{\overline{\phi}\,\psi'} = \overline{\phi}\,\overline{\psi'} = 0$$

> **Why:** $\overline{\phi}$ is a constant in time, so it factors out; then by Rule 3, $\overline{\psi'} = 0$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pitfall:** Rule 4 does **NOT** mean $\overline{\phi' \psi'} = 0$. The product of two *fluctuations* has a non-zero mean — this is precisely the origin of **Reynolds stresses**. KTU examiners frequently set this as a trick question.

## 2.2 Product Rule for Two Fluctuating Quantities

When applying Reynolds decomposition to non-linear terms like $u\,v$ in the Navier–Stokes equations, we get:

$$u\,v = (\overline{u} + u')(\overline{v} + v') = \overline{u}\,\overline{v} + \overline{u}\,v' + u'\,\overline{v} + u'v'$$

Time-averaging each term:

$$\overline{u\,v} = \overline{u}\,\overline{v} + \overline{u}\,\overline{v'} + \overline{u'\,\overline{v}} + \overline{u'v'} = \overline{u}\,\overline{v} + \overline{u'v'}$$

This gives the **most important identity** in turbulence theory:

$$\boxed{\overline{u\,v} = \overline{u}\,\overline{v} + \overline{u'v'}}$$

> **Physical meaning:** The mean of a product is *not* the product of means; the extra term $\overline{u'v'}$ is the **turbulent (Reynolds) flux**.

## 2.3 Generalised Product Rule for Three or More Quantities

For three fluctuating scalars:

$$\overline{\phi\,\psi\,\chi} = \overline{\phi}\,\overline{\psi}\,\overline{\chi} + \overline{\phi}\,\overline{\psi'\chi'} + \overline{\psi}\,\overline{\phi'\chi'} + \overline{\chi}\,\overline{\phi'\psi'} + \overline{\phi'\psi'\chi'}$$

> **Derivation logic:** Expand $(\overline{\phi}+\phi')(\overline{\psi}+\psi')(\overline{\chi}+\chi')$ into 8 terms; mean each; apply Rules 1–4.

## 2.4 Higher-Order Correlations and Velocity Derivatives

Beyond $\overline{u'v'}$, marine CFD also needs correlations involving:
- **Velocity–pressure gradients:** $\overline{u_i' \frac{\partial p'}{\partial x_i}}$
- **Velocity–velocity derivatives:** $\overline{u_i' \frac{\partial u_j'}{\partial x_k}}$
- **Triple products:** $\overline{u_i' u_j' u_k'}$

These appear in the **Reynolds Stress Transport Equations** (RSTE), which are derived in later modules.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Property / Identity | Mathematical Statement | Physical Meaning |
|---|---------------------|------------------------|------------------|
| 1 | Reynolds decomposition | $u = \overline{u} + u'$ | Instantaneous = Mean + Fluctuation |
| 2 | Time-average operator | $\overline{\phi} = \lim_{T\to\infty}\frac{1}{T}\int_{t_0}^{t_0+T}\phi\,dt$ | Statistical mean over long time |
| 3 | Linearity | $\overline{\phi+\psi}=\overline{\phi}+\overline{\psi}$ | Averaging is linear |
| 4 | Mean of mean | $\overline{\overline{\phi}}=\overline{\phi}$ | Idempotent operator |
| 5 | Mean of fluctuation | $\overline{u'}=0$ | Fluctuation has zero mean |
| 6 | Mean × Fluctuation = 0 | $\overline{\overline{u}\,u'}=0$ | Constants factor out |
| 7 | Product rule (key) | $\overline{uv}=\overline{u}\,\overline{v}+\overline{u'v'}$ | Origin of Reynolds stress |
| 8 | Variance of $u$ | $\overline{u'^2} = \overline{u^2} - \overline{u}^2$ | Turbulent intensity squared |
| 9 | Reynolds stress tensor | $\tau^{Re}_{ij} = -\rho\,\overline{u_i'u_j'}$ | Apparent stress in mean flow |
| 10 | Turbulent kinetic energy | $k = \tfrac{1}{2}\overline{u_i'u_i'}=\tfrac{1}{2}(\overline{u'^2}+\overline{v'^2}+\overline{w'^2})$ | Energy of fluctuations |
| 11 | Turbulence intensity | $I = \frac{\sqrt{\tfrac{2}{3}k}}{U_\infty}$ | Relative unsteadiness |
| 12 | Continuity (mean, incompressible) | $\partial \overline{u}_i/\partial x_i = 0$ | Mean flow is divergence-free |
| 13 | Continuity (fluctuation) | $\partial u_i'/\partial x_i = 0$ | Fluctuation is divergence-free |
| 14 | Symmetry of Reynolds stress | $\tau^{Re}_{ij}=\tau^{Re}_{ji}$ | Tensor is symmetric |
| 15 | Identity: $\overline{u'^2}\ge 0$ | Always non-negative | Variance is non-negative |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Memorise items 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, and 10 — they appear in nearly every Part A (3-mark) question on this topic. Items 14 and 15 are favourites for **"Prove that..."** Part B sub-questions.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In marine technology, these properties directly enable:
- **Hull-form optimisation:** Predicting frictional resistance from Reynolds stresses at the viscous sublayer.
- **Propeller cavitation analysis:** Pressure fluctuations $p'$ determine cavitation inception.
- **Slamming & green-water loads:** Triple correlations $\overline{u_i'u_j'u_k'}$ feed into second-order wave-load models.
- **Stern-flow & wake prediction:** Reynolds stresses dictate the **wake defect** behind a ship's stern, critical for propeller inflow.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: $\overline{u'} = 0$ (Rule 3)

Starting from the Reynolds decomposition:

$$u(\mathbf{x},t) = \overline{u}(\mathbf{x}) + u'(\mathbf{x},t)$$

Apply the time-average operator to both sides:

$$\overline{u(\mathbf{x},t)} = \overline{\overline{u}(\mathbf{x}) + u'(\mathbf{x},t)}$$

Apply **Linearity (Rule 1)** on the right-hand side:

$$\overline{u} = \overline{\overline{u}} + \overline{u'}$$

But $\overline{u}$ is the LHS, and by **Rule 2** ($\overline{\overline{u}} = \overline{u}$), we substitute:

$$\overline{u} = \overline{u} + \overline{u'}$$

Subtract $\overline{u}$ from both sides:

$$\boxed{\overline{u'} = 0} \qquad \blacksquare$$

> **Engineering interpretation:** The average of a turbulent fluctuation vanishes by definition — otherwise the mean would be mis-defined.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: $\overline{\overline{u}\,v'} = \overline{u}\,\overline{v'} = 0$

Since $\overline{u}(\mathbf{x})$ is independent of time, it factors out of the time integral:

$$\overline{\overline{u}\,v'} = \overline{u}\cdot\overline{v'}$$

By Rule 3, $\overline{v'} = 0$, hence:

$$\boxed{\overline{\overline{u}\,v'} = 0} \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: The Reynolds Stress Identity $\overline{uv} = \overline{u}\,\overline{v} + \overline{u'v'}$

This is the **most important derivation** in KTU Module 4 — it is the gateway to the **Reynolds-Averaged Navier–Stokes (RANS)** equations.

**Step 1.** Decompose each velocity into mean + fluctuation:

$$u = \overline{u} + u', \quad v = \overline{v} + v'$$

**Step 2.** Form the product $u\,v$:

$$u\,v = (\overline{u} + u')(\overline{v} + v')$$

**Step 3.** Expand (FOIL multiplication):

$$u\,v = \overline{u}\,\overline{v} + \overline{u}\,v' + u'\,\overline{v} + u'v'$$

**Step 4.** Apply the time-average operator:

$$\overline{u\,v} = \overline{\overline{u}\,\overline{v}} + \overline{\overline{u}\,v'} + \overline{u'\,\overline{v}} + \overline{u'v'}$$

**Step 5.** Evaluate each term:
- $\overline{\overline{u}\,\overline{v}} = \overline{u}\,\overline{v}$ (both factors are means → Rule 2)
- $\overline{\overline{u}\,v'} = \overline{u}\,\overline{v'} = \overline{u}\cdot 0 = 0$ (Rule 4 + Rule 3)
- $\overline{u'\,\overline{v}} = \overline{v}\,\overline{u'} = \overline{v}\cdot 0 = 0$ (Rule 4 + Rule 3)
- $\overline{u'v'}$ remains as a non-zero correlation.

**Step 6.** Assemble the result:

$$\boxed{\overline{u\,v} = \overline{u}\,\overline{v} + \overline{u'v'}} \qquad \blacksquare$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The term $\overline{u'v'}$ is the **only survivor** of the non-linear convection that remains after averaging — it represents the **turbulent transport of momentum** and is the foundation of every RANS closure model.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Turbulent Kinetic Energy $k = \frac{1}{2}\overline{u_i'u_i'}$

**Step 1.** By analogy with the kinetic energy per unit mass of a fluctuating velocity field:

$$\text{Instantaneous kinetic energy} = \tfrac{1}{2}(u^2 + v^2 + w^2) = \tfrac{1}{2}u_i u_i$$

**Step 2.** Decompose each component:

$$u_i u_i = (\overline{u}_i + u_i')(\overline{u}_i + u_i') = \overline{u}_i\overline{u}_i + 2\overline{u}_i u_i' + u_i' u_i'$$

**Step 3.** Time-average:

$$\overline{u_i u_i} = \overline{u}_i\overline{u}_i + 2\overline{u}_i\overline{u_i'} + \overline{u_i' u_i'} = \overline{u}_i\overline{u}_i + 0 + \overline{u_i' u_i'}$$

**Step 4.** Define $k$ as the energy **purely in the fluctuations** (subtract mean-flow energy):

$$k = \tfrac{1}{2}\overline{u_i' u_i'}$$

**Step 5.** Expand in Cartesian form:

$$k = \tfrac{1}{2}\left(\overline{u'^2} + \overline{v'^2} + \overline{w'^2}\right) \qquad \blacksquare$$

> **Engineering interpretation:** $k$ has units of m²/s² (energy per unit mass). It is the key transported variable in the $k$-$\varepsilon$ and $k$-$\omega$ SST models.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Variance Identity $\overline{u'^2} = \overline{u^2} - \overline{u}^2$

**Step 1.** Square the decomposition:

$$u^2 = (\overline{u} + u')^2 = \overline{u}^2 + 2\overline{u}\,u' + u'^2$$

**Step 2.** Time-average:

$$\overline{u^2} = \overline{u}^2 + 2\overline{u}\,\overline{u'} + \overline{u'^2}$$

**Step 3.** Using $\overline{u'}=0$:

$$\overline{u^2} = \overline{u}^2 + \overline{u'^2}$$

**Step 4.** Rearrange:

$$\boxed{\overline{u'^2} = \overline{u^2} - \overline{u}^2} \qquad \blacksquare$$

> This is the **classical statistical definition of variance** applied to a turbulent flow signal.

---

## 3.6 Derivation: Symmetry of the Reynolds Stress Tensor

For a turbulent flow field, the Reynolds stress tensor is defined as:

$$\tau^{Re}_{ij} \equiv -\rho\,\overline{u_i' u_j'}$$

**Step 1.** Check the order of indices. By definition of the product $u_i' u_j'$, swapping indices gives:

$$\tau^{Re}_{ji} = -\rho\,\overline{u_j' u_i'}$$

**Step 2.** Multiplication of real scalars is **commutative**: $u_i' u_j' = u_j' u_i'$.

**Step 3.** Therefore:

$$\overline{u_i' u_j'} = \overline{u_j' u_i'}$$

**Step 4.** Hence:
