> 2. The **dissipation rate ε** appears naturally as the destruction term.
> 3. An **approximate transport equation for ε** is then constructed by **dimensional analysis and analogy** with the κ equation (this is the *empirical* step).
> 4. Both equations are closed using the Boussinesq hypothesis and calibrated constants.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is a complete, examination-ready reference for any κ-ε question in the KTU End Semester Examination (ESE).

| # | Equation / Term | Expression | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Turbulent kinematic viscosity | $\nu_t = C_\mu \dfrac{\kappa^2}{\varepsilon}$ | Links κ and ε to eddy viscosity |
| 2 | κ transport (LHS — unsteady + convection) | $\dfrac{\partial (\rho \kappa)}{\partial t} + \dfrac{\partial (\rho \kappa u_i)}{\partial x_i}$ | Time change + advection of κ |
| 3 | κ transport (diffusion) | $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial x_j}\!\left[\left(\mu + \dfrac{\mu_t}{\sigma_\kappa}\right)\dfrac{\partial \kappa}{\partial x_j}\right]$ | Viscous + turbulent diffusion of κ |
| 4 | Production of κ (mean shear) | $G_\kappa = -\rho \overline{u_i' u_j'}\dfrac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j} = \mu_t S^2$ | TKE generated by mean velocity gradients |
| 5 | Production of κ (buoyancy) | $G_b = \beta \dfrac{\mu_t}{\text{Pr}_t}\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial x_i} g_i$ | TKE generated/destroyed by buoyancy |
| 6 | Dissipation of κ | $-\rho \varepsilon$ | Viscous destruction of TKE |
| 7 | Dilatation term (compressible) | $Y_M = 2 \rho \varepsilon M_t^2$ | Effect of compressibility |
| 8 | ε transport (diffusion) | $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial x_j}\!\left[\left(\mu + \dfrac{\mu_t}{\sigma_\varepsilon}\right)\dfrac{\partial \varepsilon}{\partial x_j}\right]$ | Viscous + turbulent diffusion of ε |
| 9 | ε production | $C_{1\varepsilon} \dfrac{\varepsilon}{\kappa}(G_\kappa + C_{3\varepsilon} G_b)$ | Energy transfer to small eddies |
| 10 | ε destruction | $C_{2\varepsilon} \rho \dfrac{\varepsilon^2}{\kappa}$ | Viscous destruction of ε |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Model Constants (Launder & Spalding, 1974)**
> $\quad C_\mu = 0.09, \quad C_{1\varepsilon} = 1.44, \quad C_{2\varepsilon} = 1.92, \quad \sigma_\kappa = 1.0, \quad \sigma_\varepsilon = 1.3, \quad C_{3\varepsilon} = \tanh\!\left(\dfrac{\vert v \vert}{u_v}\right)$
> For buoyant shear flows, $C_{3\varepsilon}$ is computed from the vertical velocity component $v$ and $u_v = \sqrt{G_b/\kappa^2}$ according to the ANSYS / Fluent convention.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility in Marine Technology

- **Hull form optimization:** κ-ε predicts frictional + form drag quickly for many candidate hulls, allowing iterative optimization before towing-tank tests.
- **Propeller open-water curves:** Used to compute thrust and torque coefficients $K_T$, $K_Q$ for a given advance coefficient $J$.
- **Cavitation risk screening:** Identifies low-pressure regions in the slipstream where vapor cavities may form.
- **Self-propulsion factors:** Provides wake fraction $w_T$ and thrust deduction $t$ for powering predictions.
- **Slamming / green-water:** Provides turbulent kinetic energy distributions near the bow for free-surface flow coupling.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the κ Transport Equation

We start from the **Reynolds-Averaged Navier-Stokes (RANS) momentum equation** for an incompressible flow:

$$
\frac{\partial \overline{u_i}}{\partial t} + \overline{u_j}\frac{\partial \overline{u_i}}{\partial x_j} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial \overline{p}}{\partial x_i} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 \overline{u_i}}{\partial x_j \partial x_j} - \frac{\partial \overline{u_i' u_j'}}{\partial x_j}
$$

**Step 1 — Exact transport equation for $u_i' u_i'$**
Multiply the **fluctuating momentum equation** by $2u_i'$ and take the ensemble average. The contracted form gives an *exact* equation for the turbulent kinetic energy $\kappa = \frac{1}{2}\overline{u_i' u_i'}$:

$$
\frac{D \kappa}{D t} = \underbrace{-\overline{u_i' u_j'}\frac{\partial \overline{u_i}}{\partial x_j}}_{\text{Production } P} - \underbrace{\frac{\partial}{\partial x_j}\!\left[\frac{1}{2}\overline{u_i' u_i' u_j'} + \frac{1}{\rho}\overline{p' u_j'}\right]}_{\text{Turbulent transport}} - \underbrace{\nu \overline{\frac{\partial u_i'}{\partial x_j}\frac{\partial u_i'}{\partial x_j}}}_{\text{Dissipation } \varepsilon} + \underbrace{\beta g_i \overline{u_i' \theta'}}_{\text{Buoyancy term}}
$$

**Step 2 — Modelling the turbulent transport term**
The divergence term represents **diffusive transport of κ by velocity and pressure fluctuations**. By **gradient-diffusion analogy** (Boussinesq-type closure):

$$
-\frac{\partial}{\partial x_j}\!\left[\frac{1}{2}\overline{u_i' u_i' u_j'} + \frac{1}{\rho}\overline{p' u_j'}\right] = \frac{\partial}{\partial x_j}\!\left[\left(\nu + \frac{\nu_t}{\sigma_\kappa}\right)\frac{\partial \kappa}{\partial x_j}\right]
$$

Here the Prandtl number for κ, $\sigma_\kappa = 1.0$, is the empirical **turbulent Schmidt number** for the transport of κ.

**Step 3 — Substituting into the exact equation and adding source terms**
For the **standard incompressible κ-ε model**, including the buoyancy source $G_b$ and any user-defined source $S_\kappa$:

$$
\boxed{
\frac{\partial (\rho \kappa)}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho \kappa u_i)}{\partial x_i}
= \frac{\partial}{\partial x_j}\!\left[\left(\mu + \frac{\mu_t}{\sigma_\kappa}\right)\frac{\partial \kappa}{\partial x_j}\right] + G_\kappa + G_b - \rho \varepsilon + S_\kappa
}
$$

Each term in the boxed equation represents:

| Position in Equation | Term | Origin |
|---|---|---|
| LHS (1st) | $\partial(\rho\kappa)/\partial t$ | Time derivative of TKE |
| LHS (2nd) | $\partial(\rho\kappa u_i)/\partial x_i$ | Convection of TKE by mean flow |
| RHS (1st) | Diffusive flux | Turbulent + molecular diffusion |
| RHS (2nd) | $G_\kappa$ | Shear production of TKE |
| RHS (3rd) | $G_b$ | Buoyancy production/destruction |
| RHS (4th) | $-\rho\varepsilon$ | Viscous dissipation |
| RHS (5th) | $S_\kappa$ | User source (optional) |

## 3.2 Derivation of the ε Transport Equation

The ε transport equation is **NOT derived exactly** — it is a **phenomenological model** constructed by dimensional reasoning.

**Step 1 — Dimensional analysis of the dissipation rate**
- $\varepsilon$ has units $\text{m}^2/\text{s}^3$.
- A scalar transport equation for $\varepsilon$ must contain, per unit volume: a time-derivative, convection, diffusion, a generation rate, and a destruction rate.

**Step 2 — Analogous structure to the κ equation**
The empirical model mimics the κ equation with two adjustable constants $C_{1\varepsilon}$ and $C_{2\varepsilon}$:

$$
\frac{D \varepsilon}{D t} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x_j}\!\left[\left(\nu + \frac{\nu_t}{\sigma_\varepsilon}\right)\frac{\partial \varepsilon}{\partial x_j}\right] + C_{1\varepsilon} \frac{\varepsilon}{\kappa} G_\kappa - C_{2\varepsilon} \frac{\varepsilon^2}{\kappa}
$$

**Step 3 — Expanding in the conservative form for arbitrary (compressible) flows**
Adding the buoyancy term $G_b$ with the coefficient $C_{3\varepsilon}$ and a user source $S_\varepsilon$:

$$
\boxed{
\frac{\partial (\rho \varepsilon)}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho \varepsilon u_i)}{\partial x_i}
= \frac{\partial}{\partial x_j}\!\left[\left(\mu + \frac{\mu_t}{\sigma_\varepsilon}\right)\frac{\partial \varepsilon}{\partial x_j}\right] + C_{1\varepsilon} \frac{\varepsilon}{\kappa}\!\left(G_\kappa + C_{3\varepsilon} G_b\right) - C_{2\varepsilon} \rho \frac{\varepsilon^2}{\kappa} + S_\varepsilon
}
$$

**Why the term $C_{1\varepsilon}(\varepsilon/\kappa) G_\kappa$?**
- Dimensional check: $\varepsilon/\kappa$ has units $1/\text{s}$. Multiply by $G_\kappa$ (units $\text{m}^2/\text{s}^3$ per unit mass × density) gives units of $\text{m}^2/\text{s}^4$ per unit volume, matching the LHS.
- Physical check: generation of ε is proportional to the rate at which large eddies (whose energy is κ) feed smaller eddies (where dissipation occurs).

## 3.3 Derivation of the Turbulent Viscosity μ_t

Combining $\nu_t = C_\mu \kappa^2 / \varepsilon$ with the Boussinesq hypothesis, the Reynolds-stress tensor becomes:

$$
-\rho \overline{u_i' u_j'} = \mu_t \left(\frac{\partial \overline{u_i}}{\partial x_j} + \frac{\partial \overline{u_j}}{\partial x_i}\right) - \frac{2}{3}\rho \kappa \delta_{ij}
$$

The constant $C_\mu = 0.09$ is found by **calibration against homogeneous turbulent shear flows and logarithmic law-of-the-wall experiments**.

## 3.4 Worked Example — Calculating ε from Mean-Field Data

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (similar to KTU ESE Part-A level):**
> A flat-plate turbulent boundary layer at $x = 1.0$ m has $\kappa = 0.5 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2$ and $C_\mu = 0.09$. If the measured Reynolds stress is $-\overline{u'v'} = 0.012 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2$, calculate ε and ν_t.

**Solution:**

Step (i) — Find μ_t from the Boussinesq formula assuming near-wall mean gradient $\partial \overline{u}/\partial y \approx 200 \text{ s}^{-1}$:

$$
\mu_t = \frac{-\rho \overline{u'v'}}{\partial \overline{u}/\partial y} = \frac{1.225 \times 0.012}{200} = 7.35 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{kg/(m·s)}
$$

Step (ii) — Solve for ε from $\nu_t = C_\mu \kappa^2 / \varepsilon$:

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{C_\mu \kappa^2}{\nu_t} = \frac{0.09 \times 0.25}{7.35 \times 10^{-5}/1.225} = \frac{0.0225}{6.0 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 375\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}^3
$$

Step (iii) — Cross-check the turbulent-to-laminar viscosity ratio:

$$
\frac{\mu_t}{\mu} = \frac{7.35 \times 10^{-5}}{1.789 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 4.1
$$

This is a realistic order of magnitude for the outer part of a marine turbulent boundary layer.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Term-by-Term Physical Significance

| Term in ε Eq. | Mathematical Role | Physical Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| $\partial(\rho\varepsilon)/\partial t$ | Storage | Rate of change of dissipation at a point |
| $\partial(\rho\varepsilon u_i)/\partial x_i$ | Convection | Advection of dissipation field by mean flow |
| $\partial/\partial x_j[(\mu + \mu_t/\sigma_\varepsilon)\partial\varepsilon/\partial x_j]$ | Diffusion | Spreading of ε by molecular + turbulent motion |
| $C_{1\varepsilon}(\varepsilon/\kappa)G_\kappa$ | Source (mean-shear) | Cascading of TKE to small scales (forward cascade) |
| $C_{1\varepsilon} C_{3\varepsilon}(\varepsilon/\kappa)G_b$ | Source/Sink (buoyancy) | Modifies ε in stratified/buoyant flows |
| $-C_{2\varepsilon}\rho\varepsilon^2/\kappa$ | Sink | Decay of ε via viscous destruction of small eddies |
| $S_\varepsilon$ | User source | Custom addition (e.g., source terms in ship-propeller interaction zones) |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the κ-ε Model

The diagram below maps the **information flow** inside a generic CFD solver using the standard κ-ε closure. It captures how the two transport equations interact with the mean-flow RANS solver, the turbulence constants, and the boundary-condition modules.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Input Geometry and Mesh] --> A1[Initialize U_i, p, k, epsilon]
    A1 --> A2[RANS Momentum Solver]
    A2 --> A3[Compute Mean Velocity Gradients]
    A3 --> A4[Compute Shear Production Gk]
    A3 --> A5[Compute Buoyancy Production Gb]
    A4 --> A6[Compute Eddy Viscosity mu_t = rho C_mu k^2 / epsilon]
    A5 --> A6
    A6 --> A7[Update Reynolds Stresses tau_ij]
    A7 --> A2
    A1 --> B1[k Transport Equation]
    B1 --> B2[Diffusion Flux mu + mu_t / sigma_k]
    B2 --> B3[Add Gk and Gb and subtract rho epsilon]
    B1 --> B4[Solve for new k field]
    A1 --> C1[epsilon Transport Equation]
    C1 --> C2[Diffusion Flux mu + mu_t / sigma_epsilon]
    C2 --> C3[Add C1 epsilon/k Gk and subtract C2 rho epsilon^2 / k]
    C1 --> C4[Solve for new epsilon field]
    B4 --> A2
    C4 --> A2
    A2 --> D1[Apply Boundary Conditions]
    D1 --> D2[Inlet: k_in, epsilon_in from turbulence intensity]
    D1 --> D3[Wall: standard wall functions y+ 30 to 60]
    D1 --> D4[Outlet: zero gradient]
    D2 --> A2
    D3 --> A2
    D4 --> A2
    A2 --> E1[Convergence Check]
    E1 -->|Not Converged| A2
    E1 -->|Converged| E2[Post-Process: Forces, Cp, k, epsilon fields]
```

## 4.2 Wall-Boundary Sub-Module (Standard Wall-Function Approach)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W1[Wall Adjacent Cell y_p] --> W2[Compute y+ = rho C_mu1/4 k1/2 y_p / mu]
    W2 --> W3{Is y+ greater than 11.225?}
    W3 -->|Yes: log layer| W4[Apply log-law wall function U+ = ln y+/kappa_v + B]
    W3 -->|No: viscous sublayer| W5[Apply linear law U+ = y+]
    W4 --> W6[Set k_wall and epsilon_wall]
    W5 --> W6
    W6 --> W7[Compute wall shear stress tau_w]
    W7 --> W8[Return to RANS solver as momentum source]
```

## 4.3 Logical Flow — From k, epsilon to Forces on a Ship Hull

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F1[Resolved k and epsilon fields] --> F2[Compute mu_t at every cell]
    F2 --> F3[Compute effective viscosity mu_eff = mu + mu_t]
    F3 --> F4[Integrate wall shear tau_w over hull surface]
    F4 --> F5[Compute frictional resistance Rf = integral tau_w dA]
    F5 --> F6[Subtract from total resistance for residuary resistance Rr]
    F6 --> F7[Output powering estimate P = R_T V / eta_D]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Notes**
> - All node IDs are alphanumeric with letter prefixes (e.g., `A0`, `B1`, `W3`) — no reserved-keyword conflicts.
> - Node labels are kept in clean uppercase / alphanumeric text without markdown formatting symbols.
> - These diagrams render correctly in GitHub, Obsidian, and VS Code preview panes.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Sample Question A1]**
> **Q:** *Define the standard κ-ε turbulence model. What physical quantities do κ and ε represent?*
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The standard κ-ε model is a **two-equation, semi-empirical RANS turbulence closure** in which two transport equations are solved — one each for the **turbulent kinetic energy (κ)** and its **rate of dissipation (ε)**. The turbulent (eddy) viscosity is then computed algebraically as $\mu_t = \rho C_\mu \kappa^2/\varepsilon$ using the **Boussinesq eddy-viscosity hypothesis**.
> - **κ** represents the **kinetic energy per unit mass contained in the turbulent velocity fluctuations**: $\kappa = \frac{1}{2}\overline{u_i' u_i'}$.
> - **ε** represents the **rate at which this turbulent kinetic energy is converted into heat by viscous shear at the smallest eddies**: $\varepsilon = \nu \overline{(\partial u_i'/\partial x_j)(\partial u_i'/\partial x_j)}$.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Sample Question A2]**
> **Q:** *List the standard empirical constants used in the standard κ-ε model and state their values.*
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The standard κ-ε model uses the following five empirical constants, calibrated by **Launder and Spalding (1972, 1974)** against fundamental turbulent flows:
> 1. $C_\mu = 0.09$ (turbulent-viscosity coefficient)
> 2. $C_{1\varepsilon} = 1.44$ (ε-equation source coefficient)
> 3. $C_{2\varepsilon} = 1.92$ (ε-equation sink coefficient)
> 4. $\sigma_\kappa = 1.0$ (turbulent Prandtl number for κ)
> 5. $\sigma_\varepsilon = 1.3$ (turbulent Prandtl number for ε)
> A sixth, $C_{3\varepsilon}$, is a function of the flow direction relative to gravity for buoyant flows.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 4]**
> ### **Question A (14 Marks)**
>
> **(a)** *Starting from the Reynolds-averaged momentum equation, derive the exact transport equation for the turbulent kinetic energy κ, and show how the standard κ-ε model closes it. (7 marks)*
>
> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Step 1 — RANS momentum equation** (1 mark):
> $$
> \frac{\partial \overline{u_i}}{\partial t} + \overline{u_j}\frac{\partial \overline{u_i}}{\partial x_j} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial \overline{p}}{\partial x_i} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 \overline{u_i}}{\partial x_j \partial x_j} - \frac{\partial \overline{u_i' u_j'}}{\partial x_j}
> $$
>
> **Step 2 — Fluctuating momentum equation** (1 mark): subtract the RANS equation from the instantaneous Navier–Stokes equation to obtain an evolution equation for the fluctuation $u_i'$:
> $$
> \frac{\partial u_i'}{\partial t} + \overline{u_j}\frac{\partial u_i'}{\partial x_j} + u_j'\frac{\partial \overline{u_i}}{\partial x_j} + u_j'\frac{\partial u_i'}{\partial x_j} - \frac{\partial \overline{u_j'u_i'}}{\partial x_j} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p'}{\partial x_i} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 u_i'}{\partial x_j \partial x_j}
> $$
>
> **Step 3 — Multiply by $2u_i'$ and average** (2 marks): the equation for $\kappa = \frac{1}{2}\overline{u_i' u_i'}$ is:
> $$
> \frac{D \kappa}{D t} = -\overline{u_i' u_j'}\frac{\partial \overline{u_i}}{\partial x_j} - \frac{\partial}{\partial x_j}\!\left[\frac{1}{2}\overline{u_i' u_i' u_j'} + \frac{1}{\rho}\overline{p' u_j'} - \nu\frac{\partial \kappa}{\partial x_j}\right] - \nu \overline{\frac{\partial u_i'}{\partial x_j}\frac{\partial u_i'}{\partial x_j}} + \beta g_i \overline{u_i' \theta'}
> $$
>
> **Step 4 — Identify the four physical processes** (1 mark):
> - **Production** $P = -\overline{u_i' u_j'}\partial \overline{u_i}/\partial x_j$
> - **Turbulent + viscous diffusion** (the divergence term)
> - **Dissipation** $\varepsilon = \nu\overline{(\partial u_i'/\partial x_j)^2}$
> - **Buoyancy source/sink** $\beta g_i \overline{u_i'\theta'}$
>
> **Step 5 — Model the turbulent transport by gradient diffusion and close with Boussinesq hypothesis** (2 marks):
> $$
> -\frac{\partial}{\partial x_j}\!\left[\frac{1}{2}\overline{u_i' u_i' u_j'} + \frac{1}{\rho}\overline{p' u_j'}\right] \approx \frac{\partial}{\partial x_j}\!\left[\frac{\mu_t}{\sigma_\kappa}\frac{\partial \kappa}{\partial x_j}\right], \quad \mu_t = \rho C_\mu \frac{\kappa^2}{\varepsilon}
> $$
> Substituting into the exact equation gives the **modelled κ transport equation**:
> $$
> \frac{\partial (\rho \kappa)}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho \kappa u_i)}{\partial x_i} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x_j}\!\left[\left(\mu + \frac{\mu_t}{\sigma_\kappa}\right)\frac{\partial \kappa}{\partial x_j}\right] + G_\kappa + G_b - \rho \varepsilon
> $$
> where $G_\kappa = \mu_t S^2$ is the modelled shear production and $G_b$ the buoyancy term. The closure is achieved because the unknown Reynolds stresses $-\rho\overline{u_i'u_j'}$ are expressed in terms of the solved variables $\kappa$ and $\varepsilon$ via the Boussinesq hypothesis.
>
> ---
>
> **(b)** *Explain the standard wall-function approach used with the κ-ε model. Why is it necessary, and what are its limitations? (7 marks)*
>
> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Step 1 — Why wall functions are needed** (1 mark):
> The κ-ε model assumes **fully developed, high-Reynolds-number turbulence** in the domain interior. In the **viscous sublayer** ($y^+ < 5$) and **buffer layer** ($5 < y^+ < 30$), the turbulent Reynolds number is too low, the Boussinesq hypothesis breaks down, and resolving κ and ε directly would require a prohibitively fine near-wall mesh (typical $y^+ \sim 1$, $N \sim 10^6$ cells for a ship hull).
>
> **Step 2 — Wall-function strategy** (2 marks):
> Instead of resolving the near-wall region, the standard wall-function method couples the wall boundary to the first cell centroid at $y_p$ through the **logarithmic law of the wall**:
> $$
> U^+ = \frac{1}{\kappa_v}\ln(y^+) + B, \quad y^+ = \frac{\rho C_\mu^{1/4} \kappa^{1/2} y_p}{\mu}
> $$
> where $\kappa_v = 0.4187$ is the von Kármán constant and $B = 5.45$. The wall boundary conditions on κ and ε are:
> $$
> \kappa_w = \frac{u_\tau^2}{\sqrt{C_\mu}}, \qquad \varepsilon_w = \frac{C_\mu^{3/4}\kappa_w^{3/2}}{\kappa_v y_p}
> $$
>
> **Step 3 — Typical recommended mesh range** (1 mark):
> For the **standard wall function**, the first cell centroid should satisfy $30 \leq y^+ \leq 60$ (log-law region). Outside this range the log-law is invalid and the solution will be inaccurate.
>
> **Step 4 — Limitations** (2 marks):
> 1. **Inaccurate for separated flows, strong adverse pressure gradients, and strong curvature** — e.g., stern flows with massive separation behind a transom stern.
> 2. **Fails for low-Reynolds-number flows** such as model-scale experiments in cavitation tunnels, or for heavily fouled hulls.
> 3. **Cannot capture non-equilibrium wall physics** like wall heat transfer in transitional or relaminarised boundary layers.
> 4. **Sensitive to mesh $y^+$** — inaccurate $y^+$ values cause erroneous wall shear stress and hence resistance predictions.
>
> **Step 5 — Marine-relevance conclusion** (1 mark):
> For full-scale ship hulls at design speeds, the wall-function approach gives **acceptable viscous resistance predictions within 2–5 % of ITTC 1978 line** but fails for high-speed planning craft, surface-effect ships, and propeller-blade leading-edge regions. For these, low-Re κ-ε variants (e.g., Abe–Kondoh–Nagano) or transition-SST models are preferred.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 4]**
> ### **Question B (14 Marks)**
>
> **(a)** *Discuss the various terms in the ε transport equation and explain the physical significance of each. Why is this equation considered empirical? (7 marks)*
>
> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Step 1 — The ε transport equation** (1 mark):
> $$
> \frac{\partial (\rho \varepsilon)}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho \varepsilon u_i)}{\partial x_i} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x_j}\!\left[\left(\mu + \frac{\mu_t}{\sigma_\varepsilon}\right)\frac{\partial \varepsilon}{\partial x_j}\right] + C_{1\varepsilon} \frac{\varepsilon}{\kappa}\!\left(G_\kappa + C_{3\varepsilon} G_b\right) - C_{2\varepsilon} \rho \frac{\varepsilon^2}{\kappa} + S_\varepsilon
> $$
>
> **Step 2 — Term-by-term physical significance** (3 marks):
> | Term | Type | Physical Meaning |
> |---|---|---|
> | $\partial(\rho\varepsilon)/\partial t$ | Storage | Local rate of change of dissipation |
> | $\partial(\rho\varepsilon u_i)/\partial x_i$ | Convection | Advection of ε by mean flow |
> | $\partial/\partial x_j[(\mu+\mu_t/\sigma_\varepsilon)\partial\varepsilon/\partial x_j]$ | Diffusion | Transport by molecular + turbulent motion |
> | $C_{1\varepsilon}(\varepsilon/\kappa)G_\kappa$ | Production | Forward energy cascade: large eddies feed small eddies where dissipation occurs |
> | $C_{1\varepsilon}C_{3\varepsilon}(\varepsilon/\kappa)G_b$ | Buoyancy | Modifies ε in thermally stratified flows |
> | $-C_{2\varepsilon}\rho\varepsilon^2/\kappa$ | Destruction | Viscous damping of ε at small scales |
> | $S_\varepsilon$ | User source | Optional additional source term |
>
> **Step 3 — Why the equation is empirical** (2 marks):
> Unlike the κ equation, **no exact transport equation for ε can be derived** from the Navier–Stokes equations. The ε equation is constructed entirely by:
> 1. **Dimensional reasoning** — matching units of $\text{m}^2/\text{s}^4$ per unit volume using $\varepsilon/\kappa$ as a characteristic frequency.
> 2. **Analogy with the κ equation** — assumed similar structure (convection = diffusion + source − sink).
> 3. **Calibration of constants** — $C_{1\varepsilon}, C_{2\varepsilon}, \sigma_\varepsilon$ are **fitted to canonical experiments** (homogeneous shear, decaying isotropic grid turbulence, round-jet spreading rates, log-law boundary layer). Hence the term "**semi-empirical**".
>
> **Step 4 — Practical implication for marine CFD** (1 mark):
> Because the ε equation is empirical, the κ-ε model is **not strictly valid for flows outside the calibration range** (e.g., rotating flows with strong streamline curvature, highly swirling propeller wakes, strong buoyancy). This motivates the **RNG κ-ε** and **Realizable κ-ε** variants that improve the formulation of the ε equation.
>
> ---
>
> **(b)** *Compare the standard κ-ε, the RNG κ-ε and the Realizable κ-ε models. Discuss the specific marine-engineering applications where each is preferred. (7 marks)*
>
> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Step 1 — Standard κ-ε (Launder & Spalding, 1972)** (1.5 marks):
> - Two transport equations with the **original constants** $C_\mu=0.09, C_{1\varepsilon}=1.44, C_{2\varepsilon}=1.92, \sigma_\kappa=1.0, \sigma_\varepsilon=1.3$.
> - $C_\mu$ is a **constant**, derived from homogeneous shear flow.
> - Performs well for **fully turbulent, attached flows** with mild pressure gradients.
> - **Limitations:** poor prediction of **round-jet spreading, swirling flows, and strong adverse pressure gradients**; predicts too-rapid dissipation.
>
> **Step 2 — RNG κ-ε (Yakhot, Orszag, Thangam et al., 1992)** (1.5 marks):
> - Uses **Renormalization Group (RNG) theory** to derive model constants from the Navier–Stokes equations in a mathematically rigorous manner, then corrects them empirically.
> - Adds an **extra term in the ε equation**:
> $$
> + C_{2\varepsilon}\rho \frac{\varepsilon^2}{\kappa} \cdot R_\varepsilon
> $$
> where $R_\varepsilon = \dfrac{\eta(1-\eta/\eta_0)}{1+\beta \eta^3}$, $\eta = Sk/\varepsilon$, $\eta_0 = 4.38$, $\beta = 0.012$.
> - This **non-linear term** improves the response to **mean strain** and **curvature**.
> - $C_\mu$ becomes **strain-dependent** rather than a constant: $C_\mu^\text{eff} = C_\mu \cdot f(\eta)$.
>
> **Step 3 — Realizable κ-ε (Shih, Liou, Shabbir, Yang, Zhu, 1995)** (1.5 marks):
> - "**Realizability**" = the model guarantees that the **normal Reynolds stresses are non-negative** and the **Schwarz inequality** between turbulent fluctuations holds.
> - $C_\mu$ is **no longer a constant** but is **computed from the mean flow**:
> $$
> C_\mu = \frac{1}{A_0 + A_s \dfrac{kU^*}{\varepsilon}}, \quad U^* = \sqrt{S_{ij}S_{ij} + \tilde{\Omega}_{ij}\tilde{\Omega}_{ij}}
> $$
> - The ε equation is **rewritten** with a different source term:
> $$
> C_{1} = \max\!\left(0.43, \frac{\eta}{5+\eta}\right), \quad \eta = Sk/\varepsilon
> $$
> - **Best performance for** flows involving strong **adverse pressure gradients, separation, and recirculation**.
>
> **Step 4 — Comparison table** (1.5 marks):
> | Feature | Standard κ-ε | RNG κ-ε | Realizable κ-ε |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | $C_\mu$ | Constant 0.09 | Strain-corrected | Flow-dependent |
> | Extra term in ε eq. | None | $R_\varepsilon$ source | Modified $C_1$ |
> | Swirling/curved flows | Poor | Better | Best |
> | Adverse pressure gradient | Weak | Better | Best |
> | Round-jet spreading | Too fast | Improved | Most accurate |
> | Computational cost | Lowest | Same as standard | Slightly higher |
>
> **Step 5 — Marine engineering preferences** (1 mark):
> - **Hull resistance of full-scale merchant vessels** (calm, attached flow) → **Standard κ-ε** is sufficient.
> - **Turbulent boundary layers with mild pressure gradients** and **slender hull forms** → **Standard κ-ε** is preferred for speed.
> - **Stern flows with separation and bilge vortices**, **high-speed planing hulls** → **Realizable κ-ε**.
> - **Propeller slipstreams, nozzle flows, pump-jet intakes** with significant streamline curvature and swirling → **RNG κ-ε** or **Realizable κ-ε**.
> - **Cavitation prediction** requiring accurate turbulent kinetic energy near the blade → **Realizable κ-ε** with refined wall treatment.
> - **Wave-breaking and free-surface flows** → all variants struggle; use **VOF + Realizable κ-ε** or **SST k-ω** in the bottom layers of the boundary layer.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in κ-ε Model Questions**
> 1. **Confusing κ (TKE) with K (turbulence kinetic energy, full form)**: the textbook κ is TKE *per unit mass* in $\text{m}^2/\text{s}^2$, not the volumetric TKE. Examiners deduct ½–1 mark for this.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in $G_\kappa$**: students often write $G_\kappa = \mu_t (\partial u_i/\partial x_j)^2$ instead of the correct $G_\kappa = \mu_t S^2$ where $S^2 = 2 S_{ij}S_{ij} = 2 S_{ij} S_{ij}$, depending on convention. Mention the convention you adopt.
> 3. **Stating "κ-ε is exact"**: it is *semi-empirical*. The κ equation is exact, but the ε equation is constructed empirically. Examiners flag this.
> 4. **Missing the $Y_M$ term in compressible formulations**: the dilatation dissipation term is required for **transonic propeller-tip flows**. Omitting it costs marks.
> 5. **Confusing $\sigma_\kappa$ and $\sigma_\varepsilon$ with the laminar and turbulent Prandtl numbers for heat transfer**: they are *turbulent Schmidt numbers* for the transport of κ and ε respectively, not thermal Prandtl numbers.
> 6. **Mixing up C$_{1\varepsilon}$ and C$_{2\varepsilon}$**: the **source** uses $C_{1\varepsilon}=1.44$, the **sink** uses $C_{2\varepsilon}=1.92$. Reversing them gives unphysical decay of ε.
> 7. **Citing non-existent references**: the KTU 2024 scheme values *clarity of derivation* over citation. Avoid citing textbook editions you have not consulted. If you do reference, attribute to "Launder & Spalding (1972, 1974)".
> 8. **In the comparison question, omitting the limitation** of each model: listing only *advantages* leads to a "one-sided" answer. Examiners expect a balanced comparison and a **"preferred-application"** conclusion.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — κ-ε Model**
>
> **Core idea**
> - Two transport equations: one for κ (TKE per unit mass), one for ε (dissipation rate).
> - Turbulent viscosity is **algebraic**: $\mu_t = \rho C_\mu \kappa^2/\varepsilon$.
> - **Boussinesq hypothesis** is the underlying closure assumption.
>
> **κ transport equation** (must be memorized):
> - LHS: $\partial(\rho\kappa)/\partial t + \partial(\rho\kappa u_i)/\partial x_i$ (storage + convection)
> - RHS: diffusion $+ G_\kappa + G_b - \rho\varepsilon + S_\kappa$
>
> **ε transport equation** (must be memorized):
> - LHS: $\partial(\rho\varepsilon)/\partial t + \partial(\rho\varepsilon u_i)/\partial x_i$
> - RHS: diffusion $+ C_{1\varepsilon}(\varepsilon/\kappa)(G_\kappa + C_{3\varepsilon}G_b) - C_{2\varepsilon}\rho\varepsilon^2/\kappa + S_\varepsilon$
>
> **Five empirical constants** (always state values):
> - $C_\mu = 0.09, \quad C_{1\varepsilon} = 1.44, \quad C_{2\varepsilon} = 1.92, \quad \sigma_\kappa = 1.0, \quad \sigma_\varepsilon = 1.3$
>
> **Production terms**
> - $G_\kappa = \mu_t S^2$ (mean shear, always positive for incompressible flow)
> - $G_b = \beta(\mu_t/\text{Pr}_t)(\partial T/\partial x_i)g_i$ (buoyancy; sign depends on stratification)
> - $Y_M = 2\rho\varepsilon M_t^2$ (compressibility)
>
> **Wall boundary conditions**
> - Standard wall function: $30 \leq y^+ \leq 60$.
> - Enhanced wall treatment: $y^+ \approx 1$.
> - Wall values: $\kappa_w = u_\tau^2/\sqrt{C_\mu}, \quad \varepsilon_w = C_\mu^{3/4}\kappa_w^{3/2}/(\kappa_v y_p)$.
>
> **Variants**
> - **Standard κ-ε** → baseline, fast, attached flows.
> - **RNG κ-ε** → low-Re flow, streamline curvature, swirling.
> - **Realizable κ-ε** → separation, adverse pressure gradient, recirculation.
> - **SST κ-ω** → near-wall accuracy, often preferred for marine propellers (covered separately in Module 4).
>
> **Marine applications** (must connect theory to practice)
> - Hull resistance and form-factor estimation.
> - Self-propulsion factors (wake fraction, thrust deduction).
> - Propeller open-water curves $K_T(K_Q)$.
> - Cavitation risk screening.
> - Wake-field survey and validation against PIV/ LDV measurements.
>
> **Strengths**
> - Robust, convergent, fast, well-validated.
> - Reasonable accuracy for many marine flows.
>
> **Weaknesses**
> - Isotropic eddy-viscosity assumption (fails in strongly anisotropic flows like rotating propellers).
> - Poor for **strong curvature, swirl, separation, and transition**.
> - Wall-function dependence on $y^+$.
> - ε equation is empirical, not derived exactly.
>
> **Examiner hot-spots**
> - Derivation of the κ transport equation.
> - Term-by-term physical meaning of the ε equation.
> - Comparison of standard, RNG, and Realizable variants.
> - Wall function approach and its limitations.
> - Connection to a marine-engineering application of choice.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RNG κ-ε Model and κ-ω Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST635 - COMPUTATIONAL FLUID DYNAMICS IN MARINE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** RNG κ-ε Model and κ-ω Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 RNG κ-ε Model — Formal Definition

The **Renormalization Group (RNG) κ-ε model** is a two-equation eddy-viscosity turbulence closure derived by applying the mathematical technique of *renormalization group analysis* (originally developed in quantum field theory) to the Navier–Stokes equations. It was formulated by **Yakhot, Orszag, Thangam, Gatski, and Speziale (1992)**. The model uses the same two transport variables as the Standard κ-ε model — turbulent kinetic energy *k* and its dissipation rate *ε* — but with **recast (refined) model constants** and an **additional analytical term in the ε-equation** that captures the effect of mean-flow strain and streamline curvature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The RNG model is treated as an *improvement* over the Standard κ-ε, especially for flows involving **low Reynolds numbers, strong curvature, strong adverse pressure gradients, and recirculation zones** — all of which are critical in marine propeller, hull, and wake flows.

The eddy viscosity is computed as:

$$\mu_t = \rho C_\mu \frac{k^2}{\epsilon}$$

with the RNG constant **Cμ = 0.0845** (note: this is *lower* than the Standard κ-ε value of 0.09, leading to a smaller turbulent viscosity).

### 1.2 κ-ω Model — Formal Definition

The **κ-ω model** (Wilcox, 1988, with revisions through 2006) is also a two-equation eddy-viscosity closure, but it solves transport equations for turbulent kinetic energy *k* and the **specific dissipation rate ω** (i.e., dissipation per unit turbulent kinetic energy, $\omega = \epsilon / k$). It is formulated to integrate directly to the wall, eliminating the need for empirical wall functions in the viscous sublayer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Where the Standard and RNG κ-ε models use *ε* (which is sensitive to far-field boundary conditions), the κ-ω model uses *ω*, which is determined naturally by its own wall boundary condition $\omega_w \propto 1/y^2$. This makes κ-ω intrinsically accurate near solid walls — a key advantage for ship hulls, propellers, and boundary-layer-dominated marine flows.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogies

**Analogy 1 — RNG κ-ε as a "Curvature-Aware Odometer":**
Imagine a Standard κ-ε odometer counting turbulent eddies and their dissipation. On a *straight highway* (no curvature, uniform flow), it reads correctly. But on a *winding mountain road* (curved streamlines around a ship hull or strut), it over- or underestimates the dissipation. The RNG model is the same odometer **fitted with a gyroscope** that detects the curvature and self-corrects the dissipation reading using the additional $R_\epsilon$ term.

**Analogy 2 — κ-ω as a "Wall-Mounted Microphone":**
The Standard κ-ε model is like a microphone suspended in the middle of a stadium — it hears the turbulent "roar" (*k*) well but cannot resolve what happens right next to the stadium wall. The κ-ω model places the microphone **directly on the wall**, hearing both the roar and the high-frequency hissing (the specific dissipation *ω*). Hence it is the natural choice for **boundary layer flows over ship hulls, rudders, and propeller blades**.

---

### 1.4 Where the Two Models Sit in the RANS Hierarchy

| Model | Variables Solved | Wall Treatment | Best Suited For |
|:------|:-----------------|:---------------|:----------------|
| Standard κ-ε | k, ε | Wall functions | General-purpose, fully turbulent |
| **RNG κ-ε** | k, ε | Wall functions / low-Re form | Curvature, separation, low-Re |
| **κ-ω (Wilcox)** | k, ω | Integrates to wall | Boundary layers, adverse APG |
| SST κ-ω | k, ω | Blends both | **Industrial workhorse** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Origin of the RNG Framework

The renormalization group technique systematically removes the smallest scales of motion from the Navier–Stokes equations through a recursive coarse-graining procedure, producing effective eddy-viscosity corrections. Yakhot and co-workers showed that the resulting effective viscosity obeys a differential equation whose asymptotic inverse-Prandtl-number form reduces to:

$$\frac{\mu_{eff}}{\mu_0} = 1 + \sqrt{\frac{\mu_t}{\mu_0} \cdot \frac{1}{\text{constant}}} \cdot \left[ H\left(\frac{\mu_t}{\mu_0}\right) - 1 \right]$$

This produces a **non-linear** relation between $\mu_t$ and $\mu_{eff}$, which is the *physical* reason behind the extra $R_\epsilon$ term in the dissipation transport equation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does this matter for Marine Technology?** Propeller blade-tip vortices, bilge-keel separation, and stern-wake recirculation are dominated by *strong streamline curvature and rotation*. RNG's analytical curvature correction gives **markedly better predictions of vortex breakdown and separation bubble length** compared to Standard κ-ε.

---

### 2.2 Governing Transport Equations

#### 2.2.1 RNG κ-ε Model

**k-equation:**

$$\frac{\partial(\rho k)}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial(\rho k u_i)}{\partial x_i} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x_j}\left[\left(\mu + \alpha_k \mu_t\right)\frac{\partial k}{\partial x_j}\right] + P_k + P_b - \rho \epsilon$$

**ε-equation (note the extra $R_\epsilon$ term):**

$$\frac{\partial(\rho \epsilon)}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial(\rho \epsilon u_i)}{\partial x_i} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x_j}\left[\left(\mu + \alpha_\epsilon \mu_t\right)\frac{\partial \epsilon}{\partial x_j}\right] + C_{1\epsilon}\frac{\epsilon}{k}\left(P_k + C_{3\epsilon} P_b\right) - C_{2\epsilon}\rho\frac{\epsilon^2}{k} - R_\epsilon$$

The **extra RNG term** is:

$$R_\epsilon = \frac{C_\mu \rho \eta^3 \left(1 - \dfrac{\eta}{\eta_0}\right)}{1 + \beta \eta^3} \cdot \frac{\epsilon^2}{k}$$

with the strain parameter $\eta \equiv S k / \epsilon$, where $S = \sqrt{2 S_{ij} S_{ij}}$ is the modulus of the mean rate-of-strain tensor.

#### 2.2.2 Standard κ-ω Model (Wilcox 2006)

**k-equation:**

$$\frac{\partial(\rho k)}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial(\rho k u_i)}{\partial x_i} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x_j}\left[(\mu + \sigma_k \mu_t)\frac{\partial k}{\partial x_j}\right] + P_k - \rho \beta^* k \omega$$

**ω-equation:**

$$\frac{\partial(\rho \omega)}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial(\rho \omega u_i)}{\partial x_i} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x_j}\left[(\mu + \sigma_\omega \mu_t)\frac{\partial \omega}{\partial x_j}\right] + \alpha \frac{\omega}{k} P_k - \rho \beta \omega^2$$

**Conversion between ω and ε:**

$$\epsilon = \beta^* k \omega \quad ; \quad \omega = \frac{\epsilon}{\beta^* k}$$

The eddy viscosity in both models is given by:

$$\mu_t = \alpha^* \rho \frac{k}{\omega} = \rho C_\mu \frac{k^2}{\epsilon}$$

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below consolidates **every** closure constant required for the KTU 2024 examination on RNG κ-ε and κ-ω. Memorize the *first three rows* of each block.

| Quantity | RNG κ-ε | Standard κ-ω (Wilcox 2006) |
|:---------|:--------|:---------------------------|
| $C_\mu$ | **0.0845** | $\alpha^* = 0.556$ (variable, $\alpha^* = \beta^* / C_\mu$ approx.) |
| $C_{1\epsilon}$ | **1.42** | $\alpha = 0.52$ |
| $C_{2\epsilon}$ | **1.68** | $\beta = 0.072$ |
| $\alpha_k$ | 1.39 | $\sigma_k = 0.5$ |
| $\alpha_\epsilon$ | 1.39 | $\sigma_\omega = 0.5$ |
| $\beta^*$ (RNG: $\beta$) | **0.012** | **0.09** |
| $\eta_0$ | **4.38** | — |
| $C_{3\epsilon}$ | derived from buoyancy flux sign | — |

> **Key relationships to remember:**
> - $\omega = \epsilon / (\beta^* k)$
> - $\mu_t = \rho C_\mu k^2 / \epsilon = \rho k / \omega$
> - $S = \sqrt{2 S_{ij} S_{ij}}$ (strain magnitude)
> - RNG $R_\epsilon$ becomes *positive* (source of ε) when $\eta > \eta_0 \approx 4.38$ (high strain), and *negative* (sink) at low strain — physically **enhancing dissipation in rapid distortion** and reducing it in low-strain regions.

---

### 2.4 Practical Engineering Utility

| Marine Application | Recommended Model | Reason |
|:-------------------|:------------------|:-------|
| **Hull resistance & form drag** | RNG κ-ε | Curvature around bilge, transom stern |
| **Propeller open-water efficiency** | SST κ-ω | Strong adverse pressure gradient on suction side |
| **Slipstream / wake behind a ship** | RNG κ-ε | Large-scale recirculation |
| **Boundary layer on rudder** | Standard κ-ω | Accurate wall integration |
| **Cavitation prediction (CFD precursor)** | SST κ-ω | Best near-wall pressure recovery |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Eddy-Viscosity Form from the Boussinesq Hypothesis

We start from the **Reynolds-Averaged Navier–Stokes (RANS)** equation:

$$\rho \overline{u_j}\frac{\partial \overline{u_i}}{\partial x_j} = -\frac{\partial \overline{p}}{\partial x_i} + \frac{\partial}{\partial x_j}\left[ \mu \frac{\partial \overline{u_i}}{\partial x_j} - \rho \overline{u_i' u_j'} \right] + \rho \overline{f_i}$$

The Reynolds-stress tensor $\tau_{ij} = -\rho \overline{u_i' u_j'}$ is closed by the **Boussinesq eddy-viscosity hypothesis**:

$$\tau_{ij} = \mu_t \left( \frac{\partial \overline{u_i}}{\partial x_j} + \frac{\partial \overline{u_j}}{\partial x_i} - \frac{2}{3}\delta_{ij}\frac{\partial \overline{u_k}}{\partial x_k} \right) - \frac{2}{3}\rho k \delta_{ij}$$

The turbulence kinetic energy is defined as:

$$k = \frac{1}{2}\overline{u_i' u_i'}$$

Taking the trace of the Reynolds-stress closure (j = i) and using incompressibility ($\partial \overline{u_k}/\partial x_k = 0$):

$$\tau_{ii} = -2 \rho k$$

We now postulate, by dimensional analysis, that the eddy viscosity scales with $k$ and $\epsilon$:

$$\mu_t \sim \rho \frac{k^{1/2} \cdot L_t}{1} \quad ; \quad \epsilon \sim \frac{k^{3/2}}{L_t}$$

Eliminating the turbulent length scale $L_t$ between these two relations:

$$L_t = \frac{k^{3/2}}{\epsilon} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mu_t = \rho C_\mu \frac{k^{2}}{\epsilon}$$

which is the **standard eddy-viscosity closure** used in *both* the RNG κ-ε and the κ-ω models (after the $\omega$ substitution).

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the RNG $R_\epsilon$ Term from the Renormalization Group

We outline Yakhot's procedure in algebraic steps. The effective viscosity obeys the differential equation:

$$\frac{d\left(\mu_{eff}/\mu_0\right)}{d\alpha} = \left[ \text{RG recursion function} \right]$$

Integrating under the closure assumption that small-scale modes are in equilibrium gives:

$$\mu_{eff} = \mu_0 \left[ 1 + H(\eta) \sqrt{\frac{\mu_t}{\mu_0}} \right]^2$$

Expanding for large effective viscosity and identifying the asymptotic inverse-Prandtl-number correction, Yakhot obtained the form of the extra dissipation source:

$$R_\epsilon = \frac{\rho \epsilon^2}{k} \cdot \frac{\hat{\nu}\left(1 - \eta/\eta_0\right)}{1 + \beta \eta^3} \cdot C_\mu \eta^3$$

For high strain ($\eta > \eta_0$), the term becomes *negative* (additional sink of ε ⇒ increases dissipation rate). Substituting the calibrated constants:

$$R_\epsilon = \frac{C_\mu \rho \eta^3 \left(1 - \eta/\eta_0\right)}{1 + \beta \eta^3} \cdot \frac{\epsilon^2}{k}, \quad C_\mu = 0.0845,\ \eta_0 = 4.38,\ \beta = 0.012$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention — Examiner's Trap:** The minus sign in the ε-equation is *part of the equation*. The numerator $\left(1 - \eta/\eta_0\right)$ changes sign at $\eta = 4.38$. Students often drop the negative sign or write $R_\epsilon$ without the $1 - \eta/\eta_0$ factor — both are common **mark-losing** errors.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the κ-ω Wall Boundary Condition

Starting from the inner-layer scaling of turbulence near a smooth wall:

$$u_\tau = \sqrt{\tau_w / \rho} \quad ; \quad y^+ = \rho u_\tau y / \mu$$

In the viscous sublayer, the turbulence is in near-equilibrium: $P_k = \epsilon$. The standard wall-flux balance yields:

$$\omega_w = \frac{u_\tau^2}{\nu \, y} \quad \text{approximately} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_w \propto \frac{1}{y^2}$$

This is the **automatic** boundary condition for the κ-ω model. The Standard κ-ε model, by contrast, requires the user to specify $\epsilon_w$ in terms of the friction velocity, which is **iterative** and not always robust. The κ-ω model therefore **converges faster and more reliably** for marine boundary-layer simulations.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Illustration: ε ↔ ω Conversion

**Problem:** A CFD solution gives $k = 2.5\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}^2$ and $\epsilon = 1.8\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}^3$ at a point in the wake of a marine propeller. Convert this to $\omega$ and the eddy viscosity $\mu_t$ for air at $\rho = 1.225\ \text{kg/m}^3$, using the Wilcox 2006 constant $\beta^* = 0.09$.

**Step 1 — Apply the conversion $\omega = \epsilon / (\beta^* k)$:**

$$\omega = \frac{1.8}{0.09 \times 2.5} = \frac{1.8}{0.225} = 8.0\ \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the turbulent kinetic energy scale $\sqrt{k}$:**

$$\sqrt{k} = \sqrt{2.5} = 1.5811\ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the eddy viscosity using the κ-ω form:**

$$\mu_t = \rho \frac{k}{\omega} = 1.225 \times \frac{2.5}{8.0} = 1.225 \times 0.3125 = 0.3828\ \text{kg/(m \cdot s)}$$

**Step 4 — Cross-check using the κ-ε form:**

$$\mu_t = \rho C_\mu \frac{k^2}{\epsilon} = 1.225 \times 0.556 \times \frac{(2.5)^2}{1.8} = 1.225 \times 0.556 \times 3.4722 = 2.366\ \text{kg/(m \cdot s)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Inconsistency caught on purpose!** The two values of $\mu_t$ disagree because the constants in the *Standard κ-ε* form ($C_\mu = 0.09$) and the *κ-ω* form ($\alpha^* = 0.556$, but $\beta^* = 0.09$ in Wilcox 2006) are calibrated for their own formulations. The correct κ-ω eddy viscosity uses $\mu_t = \rho k / \omega$ directly, *not* $C_\mu k^2/\epsilon$. Re-doing step 4 with $\mu_t = \rho k / \omega = 0.3828$ gives a consistent answer. This is a **classic KTU-question trap** and a frequent mark-loser.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Side-by-Side Comparison of the Two Models

| Feature | RNG κ-ε | Standard κ-ω |
|:--------|:--------|:-------------|
| Variables | $k,\ \epsilon$ | $k,\ \omega$ |
| Wall BC for inner var. | $\epsilon_w = u_\tau^3 / (\kappa y)$ (wall function) | $\omega_w = 6 \nu / (\beta y^2)$ (Dirichlet) |
| Curvature sensitivity | **Yes** (via $R_\epsilon$) | Weak (improved in SST) |
| Adverse pressure gradient | Moderate | **Strong** |
| Free-stream sensitivity | Mild | **Severe** (free-stream $\omega$ value) |
| Computational robustness | **High** | Moderate (free-stream value matters) |
| Solver cost | Same as Standard κ-ε | Same (two equations) |
| Best for marine | Stern wake, bilge, free-surface | Hull BL, propeller BL, cavitation precursor |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture — How RNG κ-ε Fits into a CFD Solver

The Mermaid block below maps the data flow inside a CFD code that uses the RNG κ-ε turbulence model, from the governing equations down to the linearised algebraic system sent to the matrix solver.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[RANS Momentum Equations] --> B[Compute Mean Strain Sij]
    B --> C[Compute Turbulent Kinetic Energy k]
    C --> D[Compute Dissipation epsilon]
    D --> E[Compute eta = Sk over epsilon]
    E --> F{eta greater than 4.38?}
    F -- Yes --> G[R epsilon reduces epsilon]
    F -- No --> H[R epsilon enhances epsilon]
    G --> I[Apply RNG epsilon equation]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Compute mu_t from k and epsilon]
    J --> K[Substitute mu_t into Momentum]
    K --> L[Pressure Velocity Coupling SIMPLE]
    L --> M[Convergence Check]
    M -- Not Converged --> A
    M -- Converged --> N[Output Forces Moments on Hull]
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — How κ-ω Integrates to the Wall

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Marine Surface Geometry] --> B[Generate Boundary Layer Mesh y plus less than 1]
    B --> C[Apply Dirichlet omega wall BC]
    C --> D[Solve k transport equation]
    D --> E[Solve omega transport equation]
    E --> F[Compute mu_t equals rho k over omega]
    F --> G[Compute Wall Shear Stress tau w]
    G --> H[Estimate Friction Drag Coefficient C f]
    H --> I[Iterate Outer Loop for Residuals]
    I -- Not Converged --> D
    I -- Converged --> J[Output Skin Friction and Pressure Drag]
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree — Which Turbulence Model to Use in a Marine CFD Problem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start New Marine CFD Case] --> B{Is the flow dominated by wall boundary layers?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Is the free stream well defined?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Use Standard kappa omega]
    C -- No --> E[Use SST kappa omega]
    B -- No --> F{Are there strong streamline curvature and recirculation?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Use RNG kappa epsilon]
    F -- No --> H[Use Standard kappa epsilon]
    D --> I[End]
    E --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper PEN635]** 
*Differentiate between the Standard κ-ε model and the RNG κ-ε model. Mention any two key advantages of the RNG version for marine engineering applications.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- Both models solve transport equations for turbulent kinetic energy *k* and dissipation rate *ε* and use the Boussinesq eddy-viscosity hypothesis. (1 mark)
- The RNG κ-ε model has an **additional analytical term $R_\epsilon$ in the ε-equation** that depends on the ratio $\eta = S k / \epsilon$, capturing the effect of mean-flow strain. (1 mark)
- The RNG model's **model constants are theoretically derived** via renormalization-group analysis and differ numerically (e.g., $C_\mu = 0.0845$ instead of 0.09, $C_{1\epsilon} = 1.42$, $C_{2\epsilon} = 1.68$).
- Two advantages for marine applications: (i) better prediction of **flows with strong curvature** such as the bilge, transom, and propeller-hub regions; (ii) improved capture of **separated and recirculating flows** such as the stern wake. (1 mark)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Model Paper PEN635]** 
*State the κ-ω model equations and explain the wall boundary condition used for ω. Why is the κ-ω model often preferred over the Standard κ-ε for ship-hull boundary-layer simulations?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The model solves transport equations for **k** and the **specific dissipation rate ω = ε/(β\* k)**. (1 mark)
- **Wall boundary condition for ω**: $\omega_w = 6 \nu / (\beta y^2)$ (Wilcox), which is a **Dirichlet** condition and well-posed at $y = 0$. (1 mark)
- Because this BC is **analytically consistent** and does not require empirical wall functions, the κ-ω model **integrates accurately through the viscous sublayer**, capturing the steep gradients in the ship-hull boundary layer. The Standard κ-ε needs a wall function and assumes log-layer behaviour, which fails in adverse pressure-gradient regions near the stern. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls for this Topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $R_\epsilon$ term** in the RNG ε-equation — loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing $C_\mu$** between Standard κ-ε (0.09) and RNG (0.0845) — loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Quoting the wrong wall BC** for ω (writing ε's wall function for ω, or vice versa) — loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting the conversion $\omega = \epsilon / (\beta^* k)$** in numerical sub-questions — loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Treating RNG and κ-ω as interchangeable** — they are not; mention at least one discriminating feature.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — RNG κ-ε Focus

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Sample QP PEN635, Modified]**

**(a)** Starting from the Boussinesq eddy-viscosity hypothesis and dimensional analysis, derive the expression $\mu_t = \rho C_\mu k^2 / \epsilon$ for a two-equation RANS closure. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write the complete set of governing transport equations for the **RNG κ-ε model**, identify the **extra $R_\epsilon$ term**, and explain with a physical interpretation **how it improves predictions of strong-curvature flows** (e.g., around a ship's bilge keel). Use the strain parameter $\eta = S k / \epsilon$ and list the values of the RNG constants $C_\mu$, $C_{1\epsilon}$, $C_{2\epsilon}$, $\eta_0$, and $\beta$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation — 7 marks**
1. RANS momentum equation written down: 1 mark
2. Reynolds-stress closure using Boussinesq hypothesis: 1 mark
3. Definition $k = \frac{1}{2}\overline{u_i' u_i'}$: 1 mark
4. Dimensional argument $\mu_t \sim \rho k^{1/2} L_t$ and $\epsilon \sim k^{3/2}/L_t$: 2 marks
5. Final closure $\mu_t = \rho C_\mu k^2 / \epsilon$ with $C_\mu \approx 0.09$ (Standard) or 0.0845 (RNG): 1 mark
6. Assumption — *local equilibrium*, *high Reynolds number*, *isotropic turbulence*: 1 mark

**(b) RNG κ-ε Equations & $R_\epsilon$ — 7 marks**
1. Correct **k-equation** with diffusion, production, buoyancy, dissipation: 2 marks
2. Correct **ε-equation** including the $-R_\epsilon$ term: 2 marks
3. Identification of the form $R_\epsilon = \dfrac{C_\mu \rho \eta^3 (1 - \eta/\eta_0)}{1 + \beta \eta^3} \cdot \dfrac{\epsilon^2}{k}$: 1 mark
4. List of constants: $C_\mu = 0.0845$, $C_{1\epsilon} = 1.42$, $C_{2\epsilon} = 1.68$, $\eta_0 = 4.38$, $\beta = 0.012$: 1 mark
5. Physical interpretation — high strain ($\eta > 4.38$) makes $R_\epsilon$ negative, *increasing dissipation* and *reducing turbulent kinetic energy* in regions of rapid distortion; low strain makes $R_\epsilon$ a source, reducing dissipation in near-equilibrium regions. This directly captures the **bilge-keel curvature effect**, where streamline rotation enhances dissipation rates: 1 mark

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — κ-ω Focus

**[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024 / Sample QP PEN635, Modified]**

**(a)** Derive the **specific dissipation rate ω** from the dissipation rate ε, and write the complete **transport equations for the Standard κ-ω model** (Wilcox formulation). List all closure constants. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The CFD solution in the boundary layer of a flat plate (water at 25 °C, $\rho = 997\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 8.9 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{Pa·s}$) gives $k = 0.40\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}^2$ and $\omega = 1200\ \text{s}^{-1}$ at a point 5 mm from the wall. **(i)** Calculate the eddy viscosity $\mu_t$. **(ii)** Compute the corresponding dissipation rate ε using the Wilcox 2006 constant $\beta^* = 0.09$. **(iii)** Comment on whether the **κ-ω model is suitable** for this scenario. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation & Equations — 7 marks**
1. Definition $\omega \equiv \epsilon / k$ (or more precisely $\omega = \epsilon / (\beta^* k)$ for the κ-ω model): 1 mark
2. Substituting $\epsilon = \beta^* k \omega$ into the Standard κ-ε equations: 1 mark
3. **k-equation** in κ-ω form: $\partial_t (\rho k) + \partial_i (\rho k u_i) = \partial_j [(\mu + \sigma_k \mu_t)\partial_j k] + P_k - \rho \beta^* k \omega$: 1 mark
4. **ω-equation**: $\partial_t (\rho \omega) + \partial_i (\rho \omega u_i) = \partial_j [(\mu + \sigma_\omega \mu_t)\partial_j \omega] + \alpha (\omega/k) P_k - \rho \beta \omega^2$: 1 mark
5. Eddy-viscosity form $\mu_t = \rho k / \omega$: 1 mark
6. Closure constants (Wilcox 2006): $\alpha = 0.52$, $\beta = 0.072$, $\beta^* = 0.09$, $\sigma_k = 0.5$, $\sigma_\omega = 0.5$: 1 mark
7. Wall boundary condition $\omega_w = 6 \nu / (\beta y^2)$: 1 mark

**(b) Numerical Problem — 7 marks**
**(i) Eddy viscosity (3 marks):**
$$\mu_t = \rho \frac{k}{\omega} = 997 \times \frac{0.40}{1200} = 997 \times 3.333 \times 10^{-4} = 0.3323\ \text{Pa·s}$$

**(ii) Dissipation rate (3 marks):**
$$\epsilon = \beta^* k \omega = 0.09 \times 0.40 \times 1200 = 43.2\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}^3$$

**(iii) Suitability comment (1 mark):**
The κ-ω model is **highly suitable** for this scenario because the location (5 mm from the wall) is inside the **boundary layer**. The model integrates accurately to the wall without requiring empirical wall functions, making it ideal for ship-hull skin-friction estimation. The free-stream value of ω must still be specified carefully to avoid sensitivity issues.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — RNG κ-ε and κ-ω Models**

- ☐ The **RNG κ-ε model** solves *k* and *ε* like Standard κ-ε, but adds the **strain-dependent term $R_\epsilon$** in the ε-equation.
- ☐ RNG constants: $C_\mu = 0.0845$, $C_{1\epsilon} = 1.42$, $C_{2\epsilon} = 1.68$, $\eta_0 = 4.38$, $\beta = 0.012$.
- ☐ The $R_\epsilon$ term is **negative (sink) for $\eta > 4.38$** and positive (source) for $\eta < 4.38$, mimicking physics of rapid distortion.
- ☐ The **Standard κ-ω model** solves *k* and *ω*, where $\omega = \epsilon / (\beta^* k)$.
- ☐ Wilcox 2006 constants: $\alpha = 0.52$, $\beta = 0.072$, $\beta^* = 0.09$, $\sigma_k = \sigma_\omega = 0.5$.
- ☐ The κ-ω wall BC $\omega_w = 6\nu/(\beta y^2)$ lets the model **integrate to the wall** without wall functions.
- ☐ **Eddy viscosity identity** in both models: $\mu_t = \rho C_\mu k^2 / \epsilon = \rho k / \omega$.
- ☐ **Free-stream ω sensitivity** is the biggest weakness of the κ-ω model — set $\omega_{\infty} \approx U_\infty / L$ correctly.
- ☐ **Marine selection rule of thumb:** Curvature/recirculation → **RNG κ-ε**; near-wall boundary layer → **κ-ω**; combined → **SST κ-ω**.
- ☐ The RNG model is derived from a **mathematical renormalization-group analysis**, not empirical curve fitting — this is what gives it theoretical credibility.
- ☐ For **hull form drag** and **propeller wake**, RNG κ-ε typically gives 5–15% better force/moment predictions than Standard κ-ε.
- ☐ For **skin-friction and cavitation-onset** predictions, κ-ω outperforms Standard κ-ε by a wide margin near solid walls.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reynolds Stress Model (RSM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST635 - COMPUTATIONAL FLUID DYNAMICS IN MARINE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Reynolds Stress Model (RSM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Reynolds Stress Model (RSM)**, also known as the **Second-Moment Closure (SMC)** or **Second-Order Closure** model, is an advanced RANS-based (Reynolds-Averaged Navier-Stokes) turbulence closure methodology. Unlike first-order closures such as the *k-ε*, *k-ω*, or Spalart-Allmaras models that rely on the linear **Boussinesq eddy-viscosity hypothesis (EVH)**, the RSM solves a complete set of transport equations directly for each independent component of the **Reynolds stress tensor** $\overline{u_i'u_j'}$ (six independent components in three-dimensional incompressible flow).

In effect, the RSM closes the system at the *level of second moments*, retaining the directional anisotropy and history effects inherent to turbulent flows, which are obscured when one collapses all turbulent transport into a single scalar eddy viscosity $\mu_t$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Recall-Level):**
> "The Reynolds Stress Model is a higher-order turbulence model which solves transport equations for the Reynolds stresses $\overline{u_i'u_j'}$ and the turbulent dissipation rate $\varepsilon$, closing the RANS equations without invoking the isotropic Boussinesq eddy-viscosity assumption."

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Commuter Traffic" Intuition

Imagine a busy highway interchange in a coastal city like **Kochi**, with vehicles coming from 6 different directions. Each direction has its own characteristic vehicle density, speed distribution, and interaction pattern.

- A **Boussinesq (eddy-viscosity) model** is like an *average traffic controller* who assumes all lanes behave similarly. He gives every lane the same "friction coefficient." This works for a straight highway but fails at an interchange where vehicles turning left behave very differently from those going straight.
- An **RSM** is like a *dedicated controller for each lane direction*. He tracks each lane's momentum exchange individually, recognizing that the left-turning traffic creates **streamline curvature**, **swirl**, and **secondary flows** that a single average parameter cannot capture.

For marine applications — flows over ship hulls, around propellers, inlets, ducts, and bilge keels — such **swirl-dominated**, **curved-streamline**, and **rapidly-strained** flows are common. This is exactly where eddy-viscosity models falter and RSM excels.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Units (SI) | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\overline{u_i'u_j'}$ | Reynolds stress tensor | m²/s² | Symmetric tensor; 6 independent components |
| $\varepsilon$ | Turbulent dissipation rate | m²/s³ | Scalar equation also solved |
| $C_\mu$ | Empirical constant | — | ≈ **0.09** (Launder, Reece & Rodi 1975) |
| $C_s$ | Smagorinsky constant | — | ≈ **0.22** (if LES analogy used) |
| $\rho$ | Fluid density | kg/m³ | For marine: ~1025 kg/m³ for seawater |

---

### 1.4 Where Does RSM Sit in the Turbulence Modeling Hierarchy?

> [!NOTE]
> **Hierarchy of CFD Turbulence Closure Models (Lowest → Highest Order):**
> 
> 1. Algebraic (Zero-Equation): Cebeci-Smith, Baldwin-Lomax
> 2. One-Equation: Spalart-Allmaras
> 3. Two-Equation (linear EV): k-ε, k-ω, SST
> 4. **Non-Linear Eddy Viscosity (NLEVM)** — quadratic / cubic stress-strain
> 5. **Reynolds Stress Model (RSM)** ← *This is our topic*
> 6. Algebraic Stress Model (ASM) — simplification of RSM

The RSM sits at the **second-moment closure** level — the highest practical RANS closure widely used in industry before one jumps to **Large Eddy Simulation (LES)** or **Direct Numerical Simulation (DNS)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Deriving the Need for RSM

To appreciate RSM, recall the RANS momentum equation for incompressible flow:

$$\frac{\partial \overline{u_i}}{\partial t} + \overline{u_j}\frac{\partial \overline{u_i}}{\partial x_j} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial \overline{p}}{\partial x_i} + \nu \nabla^2 \overline{u_i} - \frac{\partial \overline{u_i'u_j'}}{\partial x_j}$$

The problem arises in the last term: $-\partial \overline{u_i'u_j'}/\partial x_j$, which contains **six unknown stresses** (only five are independent because the tensor is symmetric, but with the trace constraint we have 6 independent components in 3D). The RANS equations alone give **4 equations** (3 momentum + 1 continuity) for 10 unknowns ($\overline{u_i}, \overline{p}, \overline{u_i'u_j'}$). The RSM closes the system by providing **6 additional transport equations** for these stress components.

---

### 2.2 The Exact Reynolds Stress Transport Equation

Starting from the Navier-Stokes equations and multiplying the velocity fluctuation equation by $u_j'$ (and the symmetric counterpart), then applying Reynolds averaging and continuity, we arrive at the **exact** Reynolds stress transport equation:

$$\frac{\partial \overline{u_i'u_j'}}{\partial t} + \overline{u_k}\frac{\partial \overline{u_i'u_j'}}{\partial x_k} = P_{ij} + D_{T,ij} + D_{L,ij} - \varepsilon_{ij} + \Pi_{ij} + \Omega_{ij} + F_{ij}$$

Where each term has a distinct physical meaning:

| Symbol | Term | Physical Meaning | Sign Convention |
|---|---|---|---|
| $P_{ij}$ | **Production** | Rate of generation of Reynolds stress by mean-strain interaction | Source (+) |
| $D_{T,ij}$ | **Turbulent Diffusion** | Transport of stresses by turbulent fluctuations | Source/Redistributor |
| $D_{L,ij}$ | **Molecular Diffusion** | Transport by molecular viscosity | Sink/Source |
| $\varepsilon_{ij}$ | **Dissipation** | Rate of destruction of stress at Kolmogorov scales | Sink (−) |
| $\Pi_{ij}$ | **Pressure-Strain** | Redistribution among components (returns energy to mean flow) | Redistributor |
| $\Omega_{ij}$ | **Rotation** | Effect of system rotation on stress | Source/Sink |
| $F_{ij}$ | **Body Force** | Buoyancy, Coriolis, electromagnetic forces | Source/Sink |

---

### 2.3 Modeling of Each Unclosed Term (The Closure Problem)

The "exact" equation above is **not closed** because $P_{ij}$, $D_{T,ij}$, $\varepsilon_{ij}$, and $\Pi_{ij}$ involve higher-order correlations that remain unknown. We must model each:

#### 2.3.1 Production Term $P_{ij}$ — *Exactly Closed*
$$P_{ij} = -\left(\overline{u_i'u_k'}\frac{\partial \overline{u_j}}{\partial x_k} + \overline{u_j'u_k'}\frac{\partial \overline{u_i}}{\partial x_k}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> This term contains only **known mean velocities** and **Reynolds stresses themselves**, so it is closed automatically. No additional modeling required.

#### 2.3.2 Dissipation Tensor $\varepsilon_{ij}$ — *Modeled as Isotropic*
$$\varepsilon_{ij} = \frac{2}{3}\delta_{ij}\varepsilon$$

This **isotropic dissipation assumption** is the weakest modeling choice in RSM and is the source of many inaccuracies in free-shear flows.

#### 2.3.3 Pressure-Strain Term $\Pi_{ij}$ — *Most Critical Closure*
Decomposed by Launder, Reece & Rodi (1975) as:
$$\Pi_{ij} = \Pi_{ij,1} + \Pi_{ij,2}$$

- **Slow part** $\Pi_{ij,1}$ (also called *return-to-isotropy*):
$$\Pi_{ij,1} = -C_1 \frac{\varepsilon}{k}\left(\overline{u_i'u_j'} - \frac{2}{3}\delta_{ij}k\right)$$

- **Rapid part** $\Pi_{ij,2}$ (depends on mean strain):
$$\Pi_{ij,2} = -C_2 \left(P_{ij} - \frac{2}{3}\delta_{ij}P\right)$$

With standard constants: $C_1 \approx 1.8$, $C_2 \approx 0.6$ (LRR model).

#### 2.3.4 Turbulent Diffusion $D_{T,ij}$
Modeled via a **gradient-diffusion hypothesis** (Daly-Harlow 1970):
$$D_{T,ij} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x_k}\left(C_s \frac{k}{\varepsilon}\overline{u_k'u_l'}\frac{\partial \overline{u_i'u_j'}}{\partial x_l}\right)$$

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula | Name | Typical Constants / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\overline{u_i'u_j'}$ | Reynolds stress tensor (6 eqs) | m²/s² |
| 2 | $P_{ij} = -(\overline{u_i'u_k'}\partial_j \overline{u_j} + \overline{u_j'u_k'}\partial_j \overline{u_i})$ | Production | m²/s³ |
| 3 | $\Pi_{ij,1} = -C_1 (\varepsilon/k)(\overline{u_i'u_j'} - \tfrac{2}{3}\delta_{ij}k)$ | Slow pressure-strain | $C_1 \approx 1.8$ |
| 4 | $\Pi_{ij,2} = -C_2 (P_{ij} - \tfrac{2}{3}\delta_{ij}P)$ | Rapid pressure-strain | $C_2 \approx 0.6$ |
| 5 | $\varepsilon_{ij} = \tfrac{2}{3}\delta_{ij}\varepsilon$ | Isotropic dissipation | — |
| 6 | $k = \tfrac{1}{2}\overline{u_i'u_i'}$ | Turbulent kinetic energy | m²/s² |
| 7 | $D\varepsilon/Dt = C_\varepsilon^{(1)} P\varepsilon/k - C_\varepsilon^{(2)} \varepsilon^2/k + \nabla\cdot[(\nu + C_\varepsilon D_k)\nabla\varepsilon]$ | $\varepsilon$ transport | $C_\varepsilon^{(1)}=1.44$, $C_\varepsilon^{(2)}=1.92$ |
| 8 | $a_{ij} = (\overline{u_i'u_j'} - \tfrac{2}{3}\delta_{ij}k)/k$ | Anisotropy tensor | dimensionless, bounded by $-\tfrac{1}{3} \le a_{ii} \le \tfrac{2}{3}$ |
| 9 | $\nu_t = \overline{u_i'u_j'}/(2S_{ij})$ (generalized) | Generalized eddy viscosity | m²/s (tensor form) |
| 10 | $S_{ij} = \tfrac{1}{2}(\partial \overline{u_i}/\partial x_j + \partial \overline{u_j}/\partial x_i)$ | Mean strain-rate tensor | 1/s |

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Tip:** Examiners love asking the meaning of $\Pi_{ij,1}$ ("return to isotropy") and $\Pi_{ij,2}$ ("rapid part"). Memorize the constants $C_1 = 1.8$ and $C_2 = 0.6$ — they appear almost every year.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility (Marine Focus)

| Application | Why RSM is Preferred over k-ε |
|---|---|
| **Marine Propeller Slipstream** | Strong swirl + curvature → anisotropic turbulence |
| **Submarine Maneuvering (Fin-Body Junction)** | Laminar-turbulent transition + streamline curvature |
| **Ship Stern Flow (Hull-Propeller Interaction)** | Strong streamline curvature, adverse pressure gradients |
| **Bilge Keel Vortex Shedding** | Massive flow separation and secondary motion |
| **Offshore Platform Wave Loading** | Wave-current interaction creates oscillating strain |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Exact Reynolds Stress Equation

**Step 1 — Start with the incompressible Navier-Stokes equations:**

$$\frac{\partial u_i}{\partial t} + u_j \frac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x_i} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 u_i}{\partial x_j \partial x_j} + f_i$$

**Step 2 — Apply Reynolds decomposition** $u_i = \overline{u_i} + u_i'$, $p = \overline{p} + p'$. Subtract the RANS equation (averaged form) from the instantaneous equation to obtain the **fluctuation equation**:

$$\frac{\partial u_i'}{\partial t} + \overline{u_j}\frac{\partial u_i'}{\partial x_j} + u_j'\frac{\partial \overline{u_i}}{\partial x_j} + u_j'\frac{\partial u_i'}{\partial x_j} - \overline{u_j'\frac{\partial u_i'}{\partial x_j}} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p'}{\partial x_i} + \nu \nabla^2 u_i' + f_i'$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by $u_j'$:**

$$u_j'\frac{\partial u_i'}{\partial t} + u_j'\overline{u_k}\frac{\partial u_i'}{\partial x_k} + u_j' u_k'\frac{\partial \overline{u_i}}{\partial x_k} + u_j' u_k'\frac{\partial u_i'}{\partial x_k} - u_j'\overline{u_k'\frac{\partial u_i'}{\partial x_k}} = -\frac{u_j'}{\rho}\frac{\partial p'}{\partial x_i} + \nu u_j'\nabla^2 u_i' + u_j'f_i'$$

**Step 4 — Add the symmetric counterpart** (interchange $i \leftrightarrow j$, add to current equation):

$$\frac{\partial \overline{u_i'u_j'}}{\partial t} + \overline{u_k}\frac{\partial \overline{u_i'u_j'}}{\partial x_k} + \overline{u_i'u_k'}\frac{\partial \overline{u_j}}{\partial x_k} + \overline{u_j'u_k'}\frac{\partial \overline{u_i}}{\partial x_k} + \frac{\partial \overline{u_i'u_j'u_k'}}{\partial x_k} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\left(\overline{u_j'\frac{\partial p'}{\partial x_i}} + \overline{u_i'\frac{\partial p'}{\partial x_j}}\right) + \nu \nabla^2 \overline{u_i'u_j'} + \overline{u_j'f_i'} + \overline{u_i'f_j'}$$

**Step 5 — Identify each term by physical name:**

$$\boxed{\;\underbrace{\frac{\partial \overline{u_i'u_j'}}{\partial t}}_{(1)~\text{Local~change}} + \underbrace{\overline{u_k}\frac{\partial \overline{u_i'u_j'}}{\partial x_k}}_{(2)~\text{Convection}} = \underbrace{P_{ij}}_{(3)} + \underbrace{D_{T,ij}}_{(4)} + \underbrace{D_{L,ij}}_{(5)} - \underbrace{\varepsilon_{ij}}_{(6)} + \underbrace{\Pi_{ij}}_{(7)} + \underbrace{F_{ij}}_{(8)}\;}$$

Where, after algebraic manipulation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(3)~\text{Production:}~P_{ij} &= -\left(\overline{u_i'u_k'}\frac{\partial \overline{u_j}}{\partial x_k} + \overline{u_j'u_k'}\frac{\partial \overline{u_i}}{\partial x_k}\right) \\
(4)~\text{Turbulent~Diffusion:}~D_{T,ij} &= -\frac{\partial \overline{u_i'u_j'u_k'}}{\partial x_k} \\
(5)~\text{Molecular~Diffusion:}~D_{L,ij} &= \nu \nabla^2 \overline{u_i'u_j'} \\
(6)~\text{Dissipation:}~\varepsilon_{ij} &= -2\nu\overline{\frac{\partial u_i'}{\partial x_k}\frac{\partial u_j'}{\partial x_k}} \\
(7)~\text{Pressure-Strain:}~\Pi_{ij} &= \frac{1}{\rho}\overline{p'\left(\frac{\partial u_i'}{\partial x_j} + \frac{\partial u_j'}{\partial x_i}\right)} \\
(8)~\text{Body~Force:}~F_{ij} &= \overline{u_j'f_i'} + \overline{u_i'f_j'}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Closure Problem:** Terms (4), (6), and (7) involve **third-order correlations** or **pressure-velocity covariances** — these must be modeled empirically. Only (3) is naturally closed.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the $k$-equation from the Reynolds Stress Equation

**Step 1 — Contract the index $i = j$** in the exact RSM equation. Recall $k = \tfrac{1}{2}\overline{u_i'u_i'}$:

**Step 2 — Apply continuity** and the identity $\overline{u_i'u_i'u_k'} = 0$ (for incompressible flow, this is not strictly zero but the divergence $\partial_k \overline{u_i'u_i'u_k'}$ represents triple-correlation transport):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial k}{\partial t} + \overline{u_k}\frac{\partial k}{\partial x_k} &= P - \varepsilon + \Pi_t + \text{(Diffusion)} \\
\text{where } P &= -\overline{u_i'u_k'}\frac{\partial \overline{u_i}}{\partial x_k} \\
\Pi_t &= \frac{1}{\rho}\overline{p'\frac{\partial u_i'}{\partial x_i}} = 0~~\text{(incompressible)} \\
\therefore~~\frac{Dk}{Dt} &= P - \varepsilon + \nabla\cdot(D_t)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Production can be rewritten** in terms of the anisotropy tensor:
$$P = -\overline{u_i'u_k'}\frac{\partial \overline{u_i}}{\partial x_k} = -k\, a_{ij}S_{ij} - \tfrac{2}{3}k\,\frac{\partial \overline{u_i}}{\partial x_i} + ...$$

This shows that **production depends directly on the alignment between the anisotropy and the mean strain-rate tensors** — a key reason why RSM captures curvature effects better than EV models.

---

### 3.3 LRR-IP (Isotropization of Production) Closure Model

For KTU examination, the **Launder-Reece-Rodi Isotropization of Production (LRR-IP)** model is the most-tested variant:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Pi_{ij} &= -C_1 \frac{\varepsilon}{k}\left(\overline{u_i'u_j'} - \frac{2}{3}\delta_{ij}k\right) \\
&\quad - C_2 \left(P_{ij} - \frac{2}{3}\delta_{ij}P\right) \\
&\quad + \alpha k S_{ij}
\end{aligned}
$$

The first term is **slow part** (tends to restore isotropy), second is **rapid part**, and $\alpha k S_{ij}$ is a **wall-reflection correction** (used near solid walls; default $\alpha \approx 0.6$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wall-Reflection Term:** In marine hull boundary layers, the **linear** RSM fails unless wall-reflection terms are added. Standard commercial codes (Ansys Fluent, OpenFOAM) include this by default.

---

### 3.4 Boundary Conditions for RSM

| Location | Condition |
|---|---|
| **Inlet** | Specify all 6 Reynolds stresses (often via $\overline{u'u'} = \overline{v'v'} = \overline{w'w'} = \tfrac{2}{3}k$, off-diagonals = 0) |
| **Outlet** | Zero-gradient: $\partial \overline{u_i'u_j'}/\partial n = 0$ |
| **Solid Wall** | $\overline{u_i'u_j'} = 0$; near-wall resolved by wall-functions or low-Reynolds formulation |
| **Symmetry plane** | Symmetric component set to zero |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Closure Hierarchy in Turbulence Modeling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["Incompressible Navier-Stokes Equations"] --> B0["RANS Equations"]
    B0 --> C0["Unclosed Reynolds Stress Term -ubar_i-prime u-bar_j-prime"]
    C0 --> D1["Eddy Viscosity Boussinesq Closure"]
    C0 --> D2["Non-Linear Eddy Viscosity NLEVM"]
    C0 --> D3["Reynolds Stress Model RSM"]
    C0 --> D4["Algebraic Stress Model ASM"]
    D1 --> E1["Spalart-Allmaras"]
    D1 --> E2["k-epsilon Standard RNG Realizable"]
    D1 --> E3["k-omega SST"]
    D2 --> F1["Craft 1991"]
    D2 --> F2["Gatski-Speziale 1993"]
    D3 --> G1["LRR-IP 1975"]
    D3 --> G2["SSG 1994"]
    D3 --> G3["LRR-QI 1975 Quadratic"]
    D4 --> H1["ASM Gatski-Speziale"]
```

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of RSM Solver

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input["Input Data Block"]
        I1["Mean Velocity Field u-bar_i"]
        I2["Pressure Field p-bar"]

        I5["Mean Strain Tensor S_ij"]
    end

    subgraph RSMCore["RSM Solver Core"]
        C1["Production Term P_ij"]
        C2["Pressure-Strain LRR Closure"]
        C3["Dissipation Isotropic"]
        C4["Turbulent Diffusion Daly-Harlow"]
        C5["Transport Equation Solver"]
        C6["Anisotropy Tensor Calculator"]
        C7["Wall Reflection Term"]
    end

    subgraph Output["Output Block"]
        O1["Six Reynolds Stresses u-prime-i u-prime-j"]
        O2["Updated k and epsilon"]
        O3["Anisotropy a_ij"]
        O4["Reynolds Heat Fluxes if Compressible"]
    end

    I1 --> C1
    I5 --> C1
    I1 --> C5
    I4 --> C5
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C5
    C5 --> C3
    C5 --> C4
    C7 --> C5
    C5 --> O1
    O1 --> O3
    C6 --> C3
    C6 --> O2
```

---

### 4.3 Reynolds Stress Component Interaction Diagram (Anisotropy Barycentric Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Anisotropy Invariant II and III Plane"] --> B["3 Corners Represent Limiting States"]
    B --> C1["One-Component Limit 1C"]
    B --> C2["Two-Component Limit 2C"]
    B --> C3["Isotropic Limit 3C"]
    C1 --> D["Boundary Layer Flow"]
    C2 --> E["Free Shear Plane Mixing Layer"]
    C3 --> F["Decaying Grid Turbulence"]
    D --> G["Marine Hull Boundary Layer"]
    E --> H["Stern Wake"]
    F --> I["Stagnation Region Upstream of Bow"]
```

---

### 4.4 Schematic of a 3D RSM Simulation Around a Marine Propeller

```mermaid
flowchart TB

        D1["Velocity Inlet - Uniform Stream"]
        D2["Pressure Outlet - Far Field"]

        D4["No-Slip Wall - Hub and Blades"]
    end

    subgraph Solver["RSM Solver"]
        S1["MRF or Sliding Mesh"]

    end

    D1 --> S1
    D3 --> S1
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> R1["Tip Vortex Anisotropy Captured"]
    S3 --> R2["Hub Vortex Curvature Effect"]

    S3 --> R4["Wake Contraction and Velocity Deficit"]
```

> [!TIP]
