<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / May 2024]**
**"Distinguish between the Boussinesq hypothesis and the Reynolds Stress Model."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Boussinesq Hypothesis | Reynolds Stress Model (RSM) |
|---|---|---|
| Closure Level | Algebraic (first-order) | Second-moment (second-order) |
| Stresses | $-\overline{u_i'u_j'} = \nu_t S_{ij}$ (linear in mean strain) | Solved via 6 transport equations |
| Anisotropy | Implicitly **isotropic** turbulence | Captures **anisotropy** explicitly |
| Curvature/Swirl | Poor | Excellent |
| Computational Cost | Low (~1.5× laminar) | High (~5–10× k-ε) |
| Applicability | Simple attached flows | Complex swirling, curved flows |

(3 marks: 1 for closure level distinction, 1 for anisotropy handling, 1 for at least one quantitative/qualitative difference)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**"Write the closed-form expression for the pressure-strain term $\Pi_{ij}$ in the LRR model and explain the physical significance of its two parts."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

$$\Pi_{ij} = -C_1 \frac{\varepsilon}{k}\left(\overline{u_i'u_j'} - \frac{2}{3}\delta_{ij}k\right) - C_2 \left(P_{ij} - \frac{2}{3}\delta_{ij}P\right)$$

**Physical Significance:**
- **First term (slow part, $C_1 \approx 1.8$):** Represents the *return-to-isotropy* effect — in the absence of mean strain, turbulent fluctuations spontaneously tend toward isotropy.
- **Second term (rapid part, $C_2 \approx 0.6$):** Represents the *redistribution due to rapid mean strain* — the pressure field adjusts to enforce incompressibility, redistributing energy among stress components.

(3 marks: 1 formula + 1 + 1 for the two physical meanings)

---

### PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[Internal Choice Pattern in KTU]:** Students answer EITHER Question A OR Question B (each carrying 14 marks).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]

**(a)** Starting from the incompressible Navier-Stokes equations, derive the **exact** Reynolds stress transport equation. Clearly identify and physically interpret all the unclosed terms. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **LRR-IP closure model** for the pressure-strain term. Why is the wall-reflection term necessary in marine boundary layer simulations? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

**Step 1:** Write incompressible Navier-Stokes:

$$\frac{\partial u_i}{\partial t} + u_j\frac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x_i} + \nu\nabla^2 u_i$$

**Step 2:** Apply Reynolds decomposition $u_i = \overline{u_i} + u_i'$, $p = \overline{p} + p'$, subtract RANS equation from instantaneous equation → fluctuation equation.

**Step 3:** Multiply fluctuation equation by $u_j'$, add symmetric counterpart, take ensemble average:

$$\frac{\partial \overline{u_i'u_j'}}{\partial t} + \overline{u_k}\frac{\partial \overline{u_i'u_j'}}{\partial x_k} = P_{ij} + D_{T,ij} + D_{L,ij} - \varepsilon_{ij} + \Pi_{ij} + F_{ij}$$

**Step 4:** Term-by-term identification (1 mark each for 6 terms):
| Term | Formula | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $P_{ij}$ | $-\overline{u_i'u_k'}\partial_k\overline{u_j} - \overline{u_j'u_k'}\partial_k\overline{u_i}$ | Production by mean-strain work |
| $D_{T,ij}$ | $-\partial_k \overline{u_i'u_j'u_k'}$ | Triplet-correlation turbulent transport |
| $D_{L,ij}$ | $\nu\nabla^2 \overline{u_i'u_j'}$ | Viscous diffusion |
| $\varepsilon_{ij}$ | $2\nu\overline{(\partial_k u_i')(\partial_k u_j')}$ | Viscous destruction at small scales |
| $\Pi_{ij}$ | $\tfrac{1}{\rho}\overline{p'(\partial_j u_i' + \partial_i u_j')}$ | Pressure-strain redistribution |
| $F_{ij}$ | $\overline{u_i' f_j'} + \overline{u_j' f_i'}$ | Buoyancy, Coriolis |

(7 marks: 3 marks for derivation, 4 marks for term identification with interpretations)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**The LRR-IP Model (Launder-Reece-Rodi, 1975):**

$$\Pi_{ij} = \underbrace{-C_1 \frac{\varepsilon}{k}\left(\overline{u_i'u_j'} - \frac{2}{3}\delta_{ij}k\right)}_{\text{Slow part (return to isotropy)}} \underbrace{- C_2 \left(P_{ij} - \frac{2}{3}\delta_{ij}P\right)}_{\text{Rapid part (strain redistribution)}} \underbrace{+ \alpha k S_{ij}}_{\text{Wall reflection}}$$

- **Constants:** $C_1 \approx 1.8$, $C_2 \approx 0.6$, $\alpha \approx 0.6$.
- **Slow part** dominates in **free-shear** regions and acts even with zero mean strain.
- **Rapid part** depends linearly on production, responding to mean strain rate.

**Why Wall-Reflection is Necessary in Marine Boundary Layers:**
1. Near a wall, the pressure field is constrained by the wall-normal velocity boundary condition, which causes anisotropic pressure-fluctuation reflection.
2. Without this term, linear RSM over-predicts turbulence production in the buffer and log layers, leading to inflated skin-friction coefficients on ship hulls.
3. In **stern flow regions** with strong adverse pressure gradients, wall reflection prevents unphysical separation prediction.
4. It accounts for the fact that **normal stresses near the wall become strongly anisotropic** ($\overline{v'v'} < \overline{u'u'}$), which linear models miss.

(7 marks: 3 marks for LRR-IP formula + constants, 4 marks for justification of wall reflection)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 / May 2025]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **anisotropy invariant map (Lumley triangle)**. Use it to classify three common marine flow configurations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast the **RSM, k-ε model, and Non-Linear Eddy Viscosity Model (NLEVM)** in terms of anisotropy capture, computational cost, accuracy in swirl, and closure complexity. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

**Lumley Triangle (Anisotropy Invariant Map):**

The anisotropy tensor $a_{ij} = (\overline{u_i'u_j'} - \tfrac{2}{3}\delta_{ij}k)/k$ has two independent invariants:
- $\text{II} = a_{ij}a_{ji}$
- $\text{III} = a_{ij}a_{jk}a_{ki}$

Realizability constraints place all realizable turbulence states inside a curvilinear triangle on the (II, III) plane, with three corners:

| Corner | State | Physical Realization |
|---|---|---|
| Upper | 1-component (1C) | Boundary layer near wall, $u'u' \gg v'v' = w'w' = 0$ |
| Lower-right | 2-component (2C) | Plane mixing layer, free shear, $u'u' \approx v'v' \gg w'w'$ |
| Lower-left | Isotropic (3C) | Decaying grid turbulence, $u'u' = v'v' = w'w'$ |

**Marine Applications:**
1. **Hull boundary layer** → approaches 1C corner (one strong normal stress).
2. **Stern wake region** → lies in 2C zone (planar shear-dominated).
3. **Wave-affected free surface turbulence** → tends toward 3C (rapid distortion toward isotropy).

(7 marks: 3 marks diagram description, 4 marks classification)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Comparative Matrix:**

| Criterion | k-ε (Linear EV) | NLEVM | RSM |
|---|---|---|---|
| Closure Level | First moment (via EVH) | First moment (non-linear EV) | Second moment |
| Anisotropy | Implicitly isotropic | Quadratic/cubic corrections | Fully captured |
| Swirl/Curvature Accuracy | Poor | Moderate | Excellent |
| Equations to Solve | 2 (k, ε) | 2–3 | 7 (6 + k + ε) |
| Relative CPU Cost | 1× | 1.5× | 5–10× |
| Numerical Robustness | Very high | Moderate | Low (stiffness issues) |
| Wall Function Compatibility | Excellent | Good | Difficult |
| Marine Use Cases | Straight hull flow, ducts | Mildly separated flows | Propeller slipstream, bilge vortices |

**Conclusion:** For **simple marine geometries** (straight ship hulls, KCS/KVLCC2 at moderate Froude numbers), k-ε SST suffices. For **propeller-hull interaction**, **submarine maneuvering**, and **offshore platform wave loading**, RSM is preferred despite its cost.

(7 marks: 4 marks for matrix comparison, 3 marks for engineering recommendation)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the trace constraint** $\overline{u_i'u_i'} = 2k$ when writing 6 equations — leads to a singular system. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up slow and rapid pressure-strain** constants ($C_1$ vs $C_2$): $C_1 \approx 1.8$ (slow), $C_2 \approx 0.6$ (rapid). Reversing these gives a totally wrong model.
> 3. **Claiming RSM is "always better than k-ε"**: This is FALSE. RSM is *less robust* and can give *worse* results in simple attached flows where k-ε SST is well-tuned.
> 4. **Forgetting wall-reflection term**: A common omission that examiners specifically test in marine boundary layer problems.
> 5. **Conflating RSM with LES**: RSM is still a RANS model; it does NOT resolve eddies. Mixing it up with LES costs full marks.
> 6. **Not stating incompressibility assumption** when deriving the $k$-equation (the term $\overline{p'\partial_i u_i'}$ is **only zero** under incompressible flow).

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **RSM = Second-Moment Closure**, solves **6 transport equations** for $\overline{u_i'u_j'}$ plus 1 for $\varepsilon$ (total **7 PDEs** extra per cell).
- **Production term** $P_{ij}$ is **naturally closed** — no modeling required.
- **Dissipation tensor** is modeled **isotropically**: $\varepsilon_{ij} = \tfrac{2}{3}\delta_{ij}\varepsilon$ (this is the *weakest link*).
- **Pressure-strain term** has **two parts**: slow ($C_1$) and rapid ($C_2$). Constants: **$C_1 = 1.8$, $C_2 = 0.6$**.
- **Wall-reflection term** ($\alpha k S_{ij}$, $\alpha \approx 0.6$) is **essential** in marine boundary layers — never omit it for ship hull problems.
- **Trace constraint**: $\sum_i \overline{u_i'u_i'} = 2k$ — must be enforced to avoid singularity.
- **Boussinesq hypothesis** is *abandoned* in RSM — no scalar eddy viscosity.
- **Anisotropy tensor** $a_{ij}$ is bounded: $-\tfrac{1}{3} \le a_{ii} \le \tfrac{2}{3}$ (Lumley realizability).
- **RSM is best for**: swirl-dominated, curved-streamline, rotation-affected flows — typical of marine propellers and stern flows.
- **RSM is NOT suitable for**: simple attached boundary layers where k-ε SST is cheaper and accurate.
- **Standard commercial codes** implement: LRR-IP, SSG (Speziale-Sarkar-Gatski), and LRR-QI variants.
- **Marine-relevant enhancement**: Wall reflection + rotation correction (Coriolis) for ship roll/pitch dynamics.
- **Numerical pitfall**: RSM is stiff near walls — use **scalable wall functions** (Ansys Fluent) or **low-Re formulations**.
- **Trace equation → k-transport**: $\partial k/\partial t = P - \varepsilon + \nabla\cdot(D_t)$ (production minus dissipation plus diffusion).
- **KTU 2024 weightage**: Module 4 typically contributes ~15–20% of theory marks (3–4 mark sub-parts frequent in Part B).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Large Eddy Simulation (LES)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST635 - COMPUTATIONAL FLUID DYNAMICS IN MARINE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Large Eddy Simulation (LES)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Large Eddy Simulation (LES)** is a mathematical formulation of turbulence in which the **larger, energy-containing eddies** of the flow are explicitly resolved through the time-dependent Navier–Stokes equations, while the **smaller, more universal eddies** (below a prescribed length scale) are removed by a **spatial low-pass filter** and their effect is represented through a **Sub-Grid Scale (SGS) model**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, LES sits hierarchically between the two extremes of turbulence computation:
- **Direct Numerical Simulation (DNS)** – resolves *all* scales (computationally prohibitive).
- **Reynolds-Averaged Navier–Stokes (RANS)** – resolves *no* scales, models the *entire* turbulent spectrum.

LES is the **compromise** — resolve the energetic structures, model the rest.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The *single most important mathematical act* in LES is **spatial filtering**, which decomposes any instantaneous flow variable $u_i(\mathbf{x},t)$ into a **resolved (grid-scale) part** $\bar{u}_i(\mathbf{x},t)$ and a **sub-filter (SGS) part** $u_i'(\text{filtered})$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Weather Forecast" Intuition

Imagine you are trying to forecast the weather over Kerala:
- You can **draw the large-scale map** of India on a single A4 sheet (low pressure systems, monsoon flow, cyclones). These are the **big, slow eddies** — the resolved scales.
- But you cannot draw *every raindrop, every gust, every ripple on every leaf*. These are the **tiny eddies**, which are nearly **isotropic and universal** — they look statistically the same in a monsoon and in a tea shop ceiling fan.

> LES does exactly this: it **resolves the big structures** (the cyclone) and **uses a "weather-statistics" model** for the raindrops (SGS model). This is why LES is far cheaper than DNS yet far more *physical* than RANS.

## 1.3 Physical Scales of Turbulence

> [!NOTE]
> The **Kolmogorov length scale** $\eta = \left(\nu^3/\varepsilon\right)^{1/4}$ is the smallest dynamically active scale. Turbulence exists in the range $\boxed{L \gg \ell \gg \eta}$, where $L$ is the integral length scale, $\ell$ the cutoff, and $\eta$ the Kolmogorov scale.

| Scale | Approximate Magnitude (typical marine wake) | Role in LES |
|---|---|---|
| Integral $L$ | Ship length, $\mathcal{O}(100~\text{m})$ | **Resolved** by LES |
| Filter width $\Delta$ | Grid spacing, $\mathcal{O}(0.1\text{–}1~\text{m})$ | **Cutoff** scale |
| Kolmogorov $\eta$ | $\mathcal{O}(0.1\text{–}1~\text{mm})$ | **Modelled** via SGS |

## 1.4 Why LES is Critical in Marine Technology

- **Hull-flow interaction**: prediction of frictional resistance, propeller-hull interaction, and wake non-uniformity feeding the propeller.
- **Slipstream / bubbly flows**: cavitating tip vortices, sheet cavitation on propellers.
- **Maneuvering & seakeeping**: capturing the **unsteady, three-dimensional vortical structures** shed by a ship hull.
- **Offshore structures**: VIV (Vortex-Induced Vibration) of risers and spar platforms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> LES provides **time-accurate, phase-resolved** flow information — essential for unsteady problems like **hull-propeller-rudder interaction**, where RANS simply cannot reproduce the correct physics.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Spatial Filtering Operation

The **filtering** of a flow variable $u(\mathbf{x},t)$ is a convolution with a kernel $G(\mathbf{r},\Delta)$:

$$\bar{u}_i(\mathbf{x},t) = \int_{\Omega} G(\mathbf{r},\Delta)\, u_i(\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{r},t)\, d\mathbf{r}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Properties of an admissible LES filter $G$**:
> 1. **Normalization:** $\int G\,d\mathbf{r} = 1$ (conserves constant functions)
> 2. **Commutativity with derivatives:** $\overline{\partial u_i/\partial x_j} = \partial \bar{u}_i/\partial x_j$
> 3. **Energy transfer:** A **low-pass** filter — passes scales larger than $\Delta$, attenuates smaller scales.

### 2.1.1 Common Filter Kernels

| Filter type | Kernel $G(\mathbf{r},\Delta)$ | Spectral response | KTU remark |
|---|---|---|---|
| Box (top-hat) | $G = 1/\Delta$ for $\vert \mathbf{r}\vert \le \Delta/2$ | $\hat{G}(k) = \text{sinc}(k\Delta/2)$ | Easiest in physical space; spectral cut-off not sharp |
| Gaussian | $G = \left(6/\pi\Delta^2\right)^{1/2}\exp(-6r^2/\Delta^2)$ | $\hat{G}(k) = \exp(-k^2\Delta^2/24)$ | Smooth; preferred in theory |
| Spectral (sharp cut-off) | $G(\mathbf{x}) = \text{IDFT of } H(k_c-\vert\mathbf{k}\vert)$ | $\hat{G}(k) = 1$ for $\vert k\vert<k_c$, else 0 | Idealised; used in theory, not in real codes |

> [!WARNING]
> The **spectral filter** is mathematically cleanest (clean scale separation) but **not realizable** in physical space; in practice, the **box or Gaussian** is used in finite-volume marine CFD codes (OpenFOAM, STAR-CCM+, Fluent).

## 2.2 Filtered Navier–Stokes Equations

Applying the filter to the incompressible Navier–Stokes equations:

$$\frac{\partial \bar{u}_i}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial(\bar{u}_i \bar{u}_j)}{\partial x_j} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial \bar{p}}{\partial x_i} + \nu\frac{\partial^2 \bar{u}_i}{\partial x_j \partial x_j} - \frac{\partial \tau_{ij}}{\partial x_j}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Sub-Grid Scale (SGS) stress tensor** is the unknown term that the SGS model must close:

$$\tau_{ij} = \overline{u_i u_j} - \bar{u}_i \bar{u}_j$$

Physically: $\tau_{ij}$ represents the **momentum flux** carried by the **unresolved** eddies onto the resolved scales.

## 2.3 Decomposition of $\tau_{ij}$

$$\tau_{ij} = L_{ij} + C_{ij} + R_{ij}$$

| Term | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Leonard stress** $L_{ij}$ | $\overline{\bar{u}_i \bar{u}_j} - \bar{u}_i \bar{u}_j$ | Resolved–resolved interaction (computable) |
| **Cross stress** $C_{ij}$ | $\overline{\bar{u}_i u_j'} + \overline{u_i' \bar{u}_j}$ | Resolved–subfilter correlation |
| **Reynolds (SGS) stress** $R_{ij}$ | $\overline{u_i' u_j'}$ | Subfilter–subfilter correlation |

> [!NOTE]
> In practical LES, $L_{ij}$ is **explicitly computable**, while $C_{ij}$ and $R_{ij}$ are lumped together into a **modeled SGS stress** $\tau_{ij}^{\text{model}}$.

## 2.4 Eddy-Viscosity (Boussinesq) Hypothesis for SGS

Analogous to RANS, the SGS stress is closed by an **eddy viscosity**:

$$\tau_{ij}^{\text{model}} - \frac{1}{3}\delta_{ij}\tau_{kk} = -2\nu_t \bar{S}_{ij}$$

where the **resolved strain-rate tensor** is

$$\bar{S}_{ij} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial \bar{u}_i}{\partial x_j} + \frac{\partial \bar{u}_j}{\partial x_i}\right)$$

and $\nu_t$ is the **SGS (eddy) viscosity**.

## 2.5 The Smagorinsky Model (1963)

$$\nu_t = (C_s \Delta)^2 \, \vert \bar{S} \vert$$

> [!NOTE]
> where $\vert \bar{S}\vert = \sqrt{2 \bar{S}_{ij}\bar{S}_{ij}}$ is the magnitude of the resolved strain-rate, $\Delta$ is the **filter width** (often $\Delta = (\Delta x \Delta y \Delta z)^{1/3}$), and $C_s$ is the **Smagorinsky constant** (typically $C_s \approx 0.1\text{–}0.18$).

**Total effective viscosity:**
$$\nu_{\text{eff}} = \nu + \nu_t = \nu + (C_s \Delta)^2 \vert \bar{S} \vert$$

### Drawbacks of the Classical Smagorinsky Model
1. **Excess dissipation** near walls (no proper wall damping).
2. **$C_s$ is constant**, yet real turbulent flows are not equilibrium flows.
3. **Fails in laminar–transitional** flows (cannot decay to zero).
4. **Incorrect** for flows with strong mean shear aligned with the filter.

## 2.6 The Dynamic Smagorinsky Model (Germano et al., 1991)

The classical Smagorinsky constant is replaced by a **spatially and temporally varying** coefficient $C_s^2(\mathbf{x},t)$, computed dynamically using a **test filter** at scale $\hat{\Delta} = 2\Delta$.

**Germano identity:**
$$L_{ij} = T_{ij} - \hat{\tau}_{ij}$$

where $L_{ij}$ is the **resolved turbulent stress** (computable) and $\hat{\tau}_{ij}$ the SGS stress at the test-filter scale.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Minimising the **least-squares error** $\langle (L_{ij} - \hat{\tau}_{ij}/2)L_{ij}\rangle$ gives the **dynamic coefficient**:

$$C_s^2(\mathbf{x},t) = \frac{\langle L_{ij} M_{ij}\rangle}{\langle M_{ij} M_{ij}\rangle}$$

with $M_{ij} = 2\Delta^2 \vert\hat{\bar{S}}\vert \hat{\bar{S}}_{ij} - (\hat{\Delta})^2 \vert\bar{S}\vert \bar{S}_{ij}$.

> [!TIP]
> **Lilly's least-squares minimisation** removes the need to *a priori* know $C_s$, and naturally goes to **zero in laminar regions** — fixing two major Smagorinsky bugs at once.

## 2.7 Wall Modelling in LES

In near-wall regions, the **grid spacing** $\Delta$ must be $\mathcal{O}(y^+)$ to resolve the viscous sublayer, leading to enormous cell counts. **Wall-modelled LES (WMLES)** uses an analytical law-of-the-wall to impose wall shear, avoiding grid refinement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equilibrium log-law (WMLES)**:
> $$u^+ = \frac{1}{\kappa}\ln(y^+) + B, \quad \kappa = 0.41, \; B = 5.0$$

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **All key LES equations a KTU student must memorise for the ESE.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\bar{u}_i = \int G u_i\, d\mathbf{r}$ | Spatial filtering operation | $\text{m/s}$ |
| 2 | $\int G\, d\mathbf{r} = 1$ | Normalisation | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\tau_{ij} = \overline{u_i u_j} - \bar{u}_i \bar{u}_j$ | SGS stress tensor | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}^2$ |
| 4 | $\tau_{ij} = L_{ij} + C_{ij} + R_{ij}$ | Leonard + Cross + Reynolds | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}^2$ |
| 5 | $\tau_{ij}^{\text{model}} = -2\nu_t \bar{S}_{ij}$ | Boussinesq closure | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}^2$ |
| 6 | $\bar{S}_{ij} = \tfrac{1}{2}(\partial_i \bar{u}_j + \partial_j \bar{u}_i)$ | Resolved strain rate | $1/\text{s}$ |
| 7 | $\nu_t = (C_s \Delta)^2 \vert \bar{S}\vert$ | **Smagorinsky SGS viscosity** | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ |
| 8 | $\nu_{\text{eff}} = \nu + \nu_t$ | Total viscosity in LES | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ |
| 9 | $C_s^2 = \langle L_{ij} M_{ij}\rangle / \langle M_{ij}^2\rangle$ | **Dynamic Smagorinsky coefficient** | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\Delta = (\Delta x \Delta y \Delta z)^{1/3}$ | Filter width (cubic-root) | $\text{m}$ |
| 11 | $\eta = (\nu^3/\varepsilon)^{1/4}$ | **Kolmogorov micro-scale** | $\text{m}$ |
| 12 | $u^+ = \tfrac{1}{\kappa}\ln y^+ + B$ | WMLES log-law wall model | dimensionless |
| 13 | $L_{ij} = \widehat{\bar{u}_i \bar{u}_j} - \hat{\bar{u}}_i \hat{\bar{u}}_j$ | Germano resolved stress | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}^2$ |

> [!WARNING]
> On the KTU answer sheet, do **not** confuse the **RANS Boussinesq closure** (which uses time-averaged $S_{ij}$ and *Reynolds* stress $-\overline{u_i'u_j'}$) with the **LES SGS closure** (which uses **resolved** $S_{ij}$ and **SGS** stress $\tau_{ij}$). They look identical in form but operate on different physical quantities.

## 2.9 LES vs RANS vs DNS — Engineering Comparison

| Property | RANS | LES | DNS |
|---|---|---|---|
| Equations solved | Time-averaged N-S | **Filtered** N-S | Instantaneous N-S |
| Scales modelled | **All** turbulent scales | Subgrid scales | **None** |
| Cell count (3D ship) | $\mathcal{O}(10^6)$ | $\mathcal{O}(10^8\text{–}10^9)$ | $\mathcal{O}(10^{12})$ (infeasible) |
| Unsteadiness captured | Only via URANS | **Yes**, fully unsteady | **Yes**, exact |
| Typical $y^+$ at hull | 1 (low-Re) | 1 (WMLES) or 30–100 (WMLES) | $<1$ |
| Use in marine industry | **Daily** design tool | Research / class derivation | Benchmarks only |

## 2.10 Real-World Utility in Marine Technology

- **Resistance prediction** for novel hull forms (Wigley, KCS, KVLCC2).
- **Wake–propeller interaction** (URANS misses it; LES resolves it).
- **Cavitation inception** on marine propellers — LES captures the **sheet-cloud collapse** dynamics.
- **Vortex-Induced Vibration** of marine risers.
- **Slamming and green-water** events on ship bows.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Filtered Navier–Stokes Equations

We begin with the incompressible Navier–Stokes equations in vector form:

$$\frac{\partial u_i}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (u_i u_j)}{\partial x_j} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x_i} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 u_i}{\partial x_j \partial x_j}$$

$$\frac{\partial u_j}{\partial x_j} = 0$$

**Step 1 — Apply the spatial filter $\overline{(\cdot)}$ to the momentum equation.**

Because filtering and spatial derivatives commute (Property 2 of admissible filters):

$$\frac{\partial \bar{u}_i}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial(\overline{u_i u_j})}{\partial x_j} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial \bar{p}}{\partial x_i} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 \bar{u}_i}{\partial x_j \partial x_j}$$

> The non-linear term is the problematic one — we get $\overline{u_i u_j}$, not $\bar{u}_i \bar{u}_j$.

**Step 2 — Add and subtract $\bar{u}_i \bar{u}_j$ from the non-linear term.**

$$\frac{\partial(\overline{u_i u_j})}{\partial x_j} = \frac{\partial(\bar{u}_i \bar{u}_j)}{\partial x_j} + \frac{\partial(\overline{u_i u_j} - \bar{u}_i \bar{u}_j)}{\partial x_j}$$

**Step 3 — Define the SGS stress tensor.**

By definition:

$$\tau_{ij} \equiv \overline{u_i u_j} - \bar{u}_i \bar{u}_j$$

So the filtered momentum equation becomes:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{\partial \bar{u}_i}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial(\bar{u}_i \bar{u}_j)}{\partial x_j} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial \bar{p}}{\partial x_i} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 \bar{u}_i}{\partial x_j \partial x_j} - \frac{\partial \tau_{ij}}{\partial x_j}\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **governing equation of LES**. It looks exactly like the original N-S equation, except an extra unknown term $-\partial \tau_{ij}/\partial x_j$ appears on the RHS. Closing $\tau_{ij}$ is the *whole problem* of LES.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Leonard Stress Decomposition

Starting from the definition of the SGS stress:

$$\tau_{ij} = \overline{u_i u_j} - \bar{u}_i \bar{u}_j$$

Substitute $u_i = \bar{u}_i + u_i'$:

$$\overline{u_i u_j} = \overline{(\bar{u}_i + u_i')(\bar{u}_j + u_j')} = \overline{\bar{u}_i \bar{u}_j} + \overline{\bar{u}_i u_j'} + \overline{u_i' \bar{u}_j} + \overline{u_i' u_j'}$$

Therefore:

$$\tau_{ij} = \overline{\bar{u}_i \bar{u}_j} + \overline{\bar{u}_i u_j'} + \overline{u_i' \bar{u}_j} + \overline{u_i' u_j'} - \bar{u}_i \bar{u}_j$$

Group the three physically-distinct contributions:

$$\boxed{\;\tau_{ij} = \underbrace{(\overline{\bar{u}_i \bar{u}_j} - \bar{u}_i \bar{u}_j)}_{L_{ij}\, \text{Leonard}} + \underbrace{\overline{\bar{u}_i u_j'} + \overline{u_i' \bar{u}_j}}_{C_{ij}\, \text{Cross}} + \underbrace{\overline{u_i' u_j'}}_{R_{ij}\, \text{Reynolds}}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In the limit of *commuting filters* (e.g., Gaussian), the Leonard stress vanishes only for purely Gaussian fields — in general it is **the dominant computable part** of the SGS stress and is **retained** in the model.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Smagorinsky Eddy Viscosity

The Smagorinsky model postulates a *Boussinesq* form for the deviatoric SGS stress:

$$\tau_{ij}^{\text{model}} - \frac{1}{3}\delta_{ij}\tau_{kk} = -2\nu_t \bar{S}_{ij}$$

> **Dimensional analysis on $\nu_t$**: SGS dissipation must scale as $\varepsilon \sim u^3/L$. We seek $\nu_t$ with units of $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$, and we know that for a turbulent eddy of size $\Delta$ the characteristic velocity scales as $u \sim \Delta \vert\bar{S}\vert$. Therefore:

$$\nu_t \sim u \Delta \sim \Delta^2 \vert \bar{S}\vert$$

Introducing the Smagorinsky constant $C_s$ (dimensionless):

$$\boxed{\;\nu_t = (C_s \Delta)^2 \vert \bar{S}\vert\;}$$

> [!WARNING]
> $C_s$ **is not** a universal constant. The original Smagorinsky value ($C_s \approx 0.18$) was calibrated on isotropic homogeneous turbulence. **Wall-bounded flows** (e.g., ship hull boundary layer) require Van Driest damping: $C_s = C_s^0\,(1 - e^{-y^+/A^+})^{1/2}$, with $A^+ \approx 25$.

## 3.4 Derivation of the Germano Identity and Dynamic Coefficient

**Step 1 — Define two filters.** Apply a *grid filter* $G$ (width $\Delta$) and a *test filter* $\hat{G}$ (width $\hat{\Delta}$, with $\hat{\Delta} > \Delta$, typically $\hat{\Delta} = 2\Delta$).

**Step 2 — SGS stress at grid filter scale:**

$$\tau_{ij} = \overline{u_i u_j} - \bar{u}_i \bar{u}_j$$

**Step 3 — SGS stress at test filter scale** (applying $\hat{\,\cdot\,}$ to the *filtered* field $\bar{u}_i$):

$$T_{ij} = \widehat{\bar{u}_i \bar{u}_j} - \hat{\bar{u}}_i \hat{\bar{u}}_j$$

**Step 4 — Define the resolved turbulent stress** (Leonard stress at the test-filter level):

$$L_{ij} = \widehat{\bar{u}_i \bar{u}_j} - \hat{\bar{u}}_i \hat{\bar{u}}_j$$

**Step 5 — Subtract the two SGS stresses.** After a careful algebraic manipulation using the chain rule of filtering:

$$L_{ij} = T_{ij} - \hat{\tau}_{ij}$$

This is the **Germano identity**.

**Step 6 — Express $T_{ij}$ and $\hat{\tau}_{ij}$ via the Smagorinsky model.**

$$T_{ij} - \tfrac{1}{3}\delta_{ij}T_{kk} = -2(C_s \hat{\Delta})^2 \vert\hat{\bar{S}}\vert \hat{\bar{S}}_{ij}$$

$$\hat{\tau}_{ij} - \tfrac{1}{3}\delta_{ij}\hat{\tau}_{kk} = -2(C_s \Delta)^2 \vert\bar{S}\vert \bar{S}_{ij}$$

**Step 7 — Substitute into Germano identity** and assume $C_s$ is **constant between the two filter scales** (a closure assumption):

$$L_{ij} = -2C_s^2\left[(\hat{\Delta})^2 \vert\hat{\bar{S}}\vert \hat{\bar{S}}_{ij} - \Delta^2 \vert\bar{S}\vert \bar{S}_{ij}\right] = -2 C_s^2 M_{ij}$$

> where $M_{ij} \equiv \hat{\Delta}^2 \vert\hat{\bar{S}}\vert \hat{\bar{S}}_{ij} - \Delta^2 \vert\bar{S}\vert \bar{S}_{ij}$.

**Step 8 — Least-squares minimisation.** To find the $C_s^2$ that best satisfies this in a least-squares sense, minimise the error $E = L_{ij} - (-2C_s^2 M_{ij})$ by setting $\partial(E^2)/\partial C_s^2 = 0$:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial C_s^2}\left\langle(L_{ij} + 2 C_s^2 M_{ij})^2\right\rangle = 0$$

This gives:

$$\langle L_{ij} M_{ij}\rangle + C_s^2 \langle M_{ij} M_{ij}\rangle = 0$$

Solving for $C_s^2$:

$$\boxed{\;C_s^2(\mathbf{x},t) = -\frac{\langle L_{ij} M_{ij}\rangle}{\langle M_{ij} M_{ij}\rangle}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The angle brackets $\langle\cdot\rangle$ denote **local averaging** (over homogeneous directions or a small test-cell neighbourhood), not time-averaging, so that $C_s^2$ becomes a *spatially evolving* field.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — SGS Models at a Glance

| Model | $\nu_t$ formula | Key assumption | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Smagorinsky (1963)** | $(C_s \Delta)^2 \vert \bar{S}\vert$ | Constant $C_s$, equilibrium turbulence | Simple, robust | Excess dissipation, no wall damping |
| **Dynamic Smagorinsky** | Same form, $C_s = C_s(\mathbf{x},t)$ | Scale-invariance, Germano identity | No calibration, goes to zero in laminar | Requires test filter, noisy |
| **Lagrangian Dynamic** | Dynamic + flow history | Lagrangian averaging | Backscatter control | Computationally expensive |
| **WALE (Wall-Adapting Local Eddy-viscosity)** | $\propto \Delta^2 (\bar{S}_{ij}^d \bar{S}_{ij}^d)^{3/2} / [(\bar{S}_{ij}\bar{S}_{ij})^{5/2} + (\bar{S}_{ij}^d \bar{S}_{ij}^d)^{5/4}]$ | Local velocity gradients | Naturally vanishes at walls | Slightly more compute |
| **Sigma model** | $\propto \Delta^2 (\sigma_3(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)(\sigma_2 - \sigma_3))^{1/2}$ | Singular values of velocity gradient | Correct near-wall behaviour | Newer, less tested |
| **No-model (implicit LES)** | $\nu_t = 0$ | Numerical dissipation acts as SGS | Free of model error | Must rely on solver numerics |

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — Smagorinsky Viscosity

**Problem:** A ship-hull boundary layer simulation uses a grid with $\Delta x = 0.05~\text{m}$, $\Delta y = 0.04~\text{m}$, $\Delta z = 0.06~\text{m}$. The filtered strain-rate magnitude is $\vert \bar{S}\vert = 12~\text{s}^{-1}$. Take $C_s = 0.15$, $\nu = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}~\text{m}^2/\text{s}$. Find $\nu_t$ and $\nu_{\text{eff}}$.

**Step 1 — Filter width (cubic-root).**

$$\Delta = (\Delta x \Delta y \Delta z)^{1/3} = (0.05 \times 0.04 \times 0.06)^{1/3}$$

$$\Delta = (1.2 \times 10^{-4})^{1/3} \approx 0.0493~\text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Smagorinsky length scale.**

$$C_s \Delta = 0.15 \times 0.0493 \approx 7.395 \times 10^{-3}~\text{m}$$

**Step 3 — SGS eddy viscosity.**

$$\nu_t = (C_s \Delta)^2 \vert \bar{S}\vert = (7.395 \times 10^{-3})^2 \times 12$$

$$\nu_t = 5.469 \times 10^{-5} \times 12 = 6.563 \times 10^{-4}~\text{m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Effective viscosity.**

$$\nu_{\text{eff}} = \nu + \nu_t = 1.0 \times 10^{-6} + 6.563 \times 10^{-4} \approx 6.573 \times 10^{-4}~\text{m}^2/\text{s}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Physical interpretation:** The SGS viscosity is **~657 times larger** than the molecular viscosity — i.e., the dominant dissipation mechanism at this grid resolution is **modelled turbulence**, not molecular viscosity. This is the correct regime for LES: $\nu_t \gg \nu$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Energy Cascade and LES Filtering

The following Mermaid block depicts the **Kolmogorov energy cascade** in the spectral domain, with the LES filter cutoff $\Delta$ inserted between the resolved and modelled ranges.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["LARGE EDDIES - Energy Injection L"] --> B["INERTIAL RANGE -5/3 Law"]
    B --> C["LES FILTER CUTOFF Delta"]
    C --> D["SMALL EDDIES - Modelled by SGS"]
    D --> E["KOLMOGOROV SCALE eta - Dissipated"]
    style A fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style B fill:#fdffb6,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style C fill:#ffadad,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000
    style D fill:#caffbf,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style E fill:#9bf6ff,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

> **Reading guide:** Orange = production range, yellow = inertial range, **red** = LES cutoff, green = SGS modelled range, cyan = Kolmogorov dissipation.

## 4.2 LES Computational Pipeline

The block diagram below traces the operational pipeline of a typical LES solver as it processes a marine flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initial and Boundary Conditions] --> B[Apply Grid Filter G]
    B --> C[Solve Filtered Navier Stokes]
    C --> D[Compute Resolved Strain Sij]
    D --> E[Apply SGS Model e.g. Smagorinsky]
    E --> F[Update nu t]
    F --> G[Advance in Time]
    G --> C
    G --> H[Output Resolved Field]
    style A fill:#fdfd96,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style B fill:#a0c4ff,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style C fill:#bdb2ff,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style D fill:#ffc6ff,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style E fill:#ffadad,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000
    style F fill:#fdffb6,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style G fill:#caffbf,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style H fill:#9bf6ff,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Spectral Comparison: DNS, LES, RANS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DNS["DNS - All scales resolved"]
        A1[Large] --> A2[Inertial] --> A3[Kolmogorov]
    end
    subgraph LES["LES - Above Delta resolved, below modelled"]
        B1[Large] --> B2[Inertial] --> B3["Filter Delta"]
        B3 --> B4["SGS Model"]
    end
    subgraph RANS["RANS - All turbulence modelled"]
        C1[Mean] --> C2["Entire Turbulent Spectrum Modelled"]
    end
    style B3 fill:#ffadad,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000
    style B4 fill:#caffbf,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style C2 fill:#caffbf,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

## 4.4 SGS Model Selection Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Starting Point - LES formulation] --> R{Is the flow near a solid wall?}
    R -- Yes --> S{Wall-resolved budget available?}
    S -- Yes --> T[Smagorinsky with Van Driest damping]
    S -- No --> U[Wall-modelled LES + Equilibrium Smagorinsky]
    R -- No --> V{Free-shear or industrial complex geometry?}
    V -- Yes --> W[Dynamic Smagorinsky or WALE]
    V -- No --> X[Smagorinsky Cs = 0.10 to 0.18]
    style Q fill:#a0c4ff,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style T fill:#caffbf,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style U fill:#caffbf,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style W fill:#caffbf,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style X fill:#caffbf,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

> [!TIP]
> **Block-Level Functional Architecture rationale:** LES of a ship hull or propeller is fundamentally a *closed-loop* time-integration with an inner SGS evaluation loop. The diagrams above reflect that physical / numerical logic without attempting to draw an actual 3-D ship.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]
**State the filtered incompressible Navier–Stokes equations used in LES and clearly identify the SGS stress tensor term.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The filtered incompressible Navier–Stokes equations are:

$$\frac{\partial \bar{u}_i}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial(\bar{u}_i \bar{u}_j)}{\partial x_j} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial \bar{p}}{\partial x_i} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 \bar{u}_i}{\partial x_j \partial x_j} - \frac{\partial \tau_{ij}}{\partial x_j}, \quad \frac{\partial \bar{u}_j}{\partial x_j} = 0$$

The SGS stress tensor is

$$\tau_{ij} = \overline{u_i u_j} - \bar{u}_i \bar{u}_j$$

which represents the momentum flux between resolved and unresolved (subgrid) scales. The SGS model provides the closure for $\tau_{ij}$. **(3 marks)**

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**Differentiate between the Smagorinsky and Dynamic Smagorinsky SGS models.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Smagorinsky (1963) | Dynamic Smagorinsky (Germano–Lilly) |
|---|---|---|
| Coefficient $C_s$ | **Constant** (≈ 0.1–0.18) | **Spatially and temporally varying** $C_s(\mathbf{x},t)$ |
| Calibration | Required a-priori | Computed dynamically via Germano identity |
| Laminar flow | **Fails** — predicts dissipation even in laminar regions | **Works** — $C_s^2 \to 0$ automatically |
| Test filter | Not required | **Required** (e.g., $\hat{\Delta} = 2\Delta$) |

**(3 marks)**

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

### Question A (14 marks) — Recommended Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**(a)** Starting from the incompressible Navier–Stokes equations, derive the filtered governing equations used in Large Eddy Simulation. Identify and physically interpret the SGS stress tensor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With reference to the Smagorinsky model, derive the expression for the SGS eddy viscosity $\nu_t$ using dimensional arguments. Compute $\nu_t$ for a ship wake LES where $\Delta x = 0.04~\text{m}$, $\Delta y = 0.04~\text{m}$, $\Delta z = 0.04~\text{m}$, $\vert \bar{S}\vert = 20~\text{s}^{-1}$, $C_s = 0.12$, $\nu = 10^{-6}~\text{m}^2/\text{s}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 [1 mark]:** Write the incompressible N-S equation:

$$\frac{\partial u_i}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial(u_i u_j)}{\partial x_j} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x_i} + \nu\frac{\partial^2 u_i}{\partial x_j \partial x_j}$$

**Step 2 [1 mark]:** Apply spatial filter $\overline{(\cdot)}$ using its commuting-with-derivatives property:

$$\frac{\partial \bar{u}_i}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial(\overline{u_i u_j})}{\partial x_j} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial \bar{p}}{\partial x_i} + \nu\frac{\partial^2 \bar{u}_i}{\partial x_j \partial x_j}$$

**Step 3 [1 mark]:** Add and subtract $\bar{u}_i \bar{u}_j$ inside the non-linear term:

$$\frac{\partial \bar{u}_i}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial(\bar{u}_i \bar{u}_j)}{\partial x_j} + \frac{\partial(\overline{u_i u_j} - \bar{u}_i \bar{u}_j)}{\partial x_j} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial \bar{p}}{\partial x_i} + \nu\frac{\partial^2 \bar{u}_i}{\partial x_j \partial x_j}$$

**Step 4 [2 marks]:** Define the SGS stress and re-write the equation in its final filtered form (the box below). **Identify** $\tau_{ij} = \overline{u_i u_j} - \bar{u}_i \bar{u}_j$ as the **unknown closure term**.

$$\boxed{\;\frac{\partial \bar{u}_i}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial(\bar{u}_i \bar{u}_j)}{\partial x_j} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial \bar{p}}{\partial x_i} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 \bar{u}_i}{\partial x_j \partial x_j} - \frac{\partial \tau_{ij}}{\partial x_j}\;}$$

**Step 5 [2 marks]:** **Physical interpretation.** $\tau_{ij}$ represents the **net momentum flux** carried by the **unresolved (subgrid) eddies** onto the resolved (filtered) field. It contains the **Leonard**, **Cross** and **Reynolds** contributions and is the term that any SGS model must close.

#### Model Solution — (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 [2 marks]:** **Dimensional argument.** For a turbulent eddy of size $\Delta$ in a flow with strain $\vert \bar{S}\vert$, the characteristic velocity difference across the eddy is $u \sim \Delta \vert\bar{S}\vert$. Hence:

$$\nu_t \sim u \Delta \sim \Delta^2 \vert \bar{S}\vert \quad (\text{units: } \text{m}^2/\text{s})$$

Introducing a dimensionless constant $C_s^2$:

$$\nu_t = (C_s \Delta)^2 \vert \bar{S}\vert$$

**Step 2 [1 mark]:** Compute the filter width (cubic-root):

$$\Delta = (0.04 \times 0.04 \times 0.04)^{1/3} = (6.4 \times 10^{-5})^{1/3} = 0.04~\text{m}$$

**Step 3 [1 mark]:** Compute $C_s \Delta$:

$$C_s \Delta = 0.12 \times 0.04 = 4.8 \times 10^{-3}~\text{m}$$

**Step 4 [2 marks]:** Compute $\nu_t$:

$$\nu_t = (4.8 \times 10^{-3})^2 \times 20 = 2.304 \times 10^{-5} \times 20 = 4.608 \times 10^{-4}~\text{m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 5 [1 mark]:** Compute $\nu_{\text{eff}}$:

$$\nu_{\text{eff}} = \nu + \nu_t \approx 4.6 \times 10^{-4}~\text{m}^2/\text{s} \;\; (\nu \text{ is negligible})$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write $\nu_t = C_s \Delta^2 \vert \bar{S}\vert$ (missing the *square* on $C_s \Delta$). The **standard KTU form is $(C_s \Delta)^2 \vert \bar{S}\vert$**. Marks will be deducted for an inconsistent dimensional formula.

---

### Question B (14 marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**(a)** Explain the Germano identity and derive the dynamic Smagorinsky coefficient $C_s^2(\mathbf{x},t)$. Why is the dynamic formulation preferred for ship-hull boundary-layer LES? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat sketch, describe the **energy cascade** in turbulent flows and clearly indicate the position of the LES filter cutoff. Compare LES with RANS in terms of physical fidelity and computational cost. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 [2 marks]:** Define a grid filter $G$ (width $\Delta$) and a test filter $\hat{G}$ (width $\hat{\Delta} = 2\Delta$). Write the **SGS stress at the grid-filter level** $\tau_{ij} = \overline{u_i u_j} - \bar{u}_i \bar{u}_j$ and the SGS stress at the test-filter level $T_{ij} = \widehat{\bar{u}_i \bar{u}_j} - \hat{\bar{u}}_i \hat{\bar{u}}_j$. Define the **resolved (Germano) stress** $L_{ij} = \widehat{\bar{u}_i \bar{u}_j} - \hat{\bar{u}}_i \hat{\bar{u}}_j$.

**Step 2 [2 marks]:** Algebraically show that

$$L_{ij} = T_{ij} - \hat{\tau}_{ij} \quad \text{(Germano Identity)}$$

This identity is **exact** — no modelling involved.

**Step 3 [2 marks]:** Insert the Smagorinsky model for $T_{ij}$ and $\hat{\tau}_{ij}$:

$$L_{ij} = -2C_s^2 \left[(\hat{\Delta})^2 \vert\hat{\bar{S}}\vert \hat{\bar{S}}_{ij} - \Delta^2 \vert\bar{S}\vert \bar{S}_{ij}\right] = -2 C_s^2 M_{ij}$$

Minimising the squared error $\langle(L_{ij} + 2C_s^2 M_{ij})^2\rangle$:

$$\boxed{\;C_s^2(\mathbf{x},t) = -\frac{\langle L_{ij} M_{ij}\rangle}{\langle M_{ij} M_{ij}\rangle}\;}$$

**Step 4 [1 mark]:** **Why preferred for ship hulls.** The dynamic model **automatically damps** near the wall (because $L_{ij} \to 0$ where the flow is laminar and at the wall), thereby removing the need for ad-hoc Van Driest damping. It also **self-calibrates**, eliminating the need for the user to set $C_s$ by trial-and-error — a major advantage in a complex 3D hull-flow geometry.

#### Model Solution — (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 [2 marks]:** **Energy cascade sketch (refer to Mermaid block 4.1).** Turbulent kinetic energy is **injected at the largest scale** $L$ (integral scale — order ship length), **transferred** to smaller scales through the **inertial range** (Kolmogorov's $-5/3$ spectrum: $E(k) \propto \varepsilon^{2/3} k^{-5/3}$), and finally **dissipated** at the **Kolmogorov micro-scale** $\eta = (\nu^3/\varepsilon)^{1/4}$ by viscous action.

**Step 2 [2 marks]:** **Position of LES cutoff.** The LES filter $\Delta$ is placed **inside the inertial range** — i.e., $\eta \ll \Delta \ll L$. Energy-containing eddies $L > \Delta$ are **resolved**; subgrid eddies $\eta < \Delta$ are **modelled** by the SGS closure.

**Step 3 [3 marks]:** **LES vs RANS comparison.**

| Aspect | RANS | LES |
|---|---|---|
| Physical fidelity | Models **all** turbulent scales — phase-averaged | Resolves large eddies, models only subgrid |
| Unsteadiness | Only via URANS | **Fully unsteady** (true phase-resolved) |
| Hull wake prediction | Acceptable for mean drag, poor for propeller inflow | **Resolves wake** vortices, propeller-hull interaction |
| Computational cost | $10^6$ cells (ship-scale) | $10^8\text{–}10^9$ cells, $\sim 100\text{–}1000\times$ costlier |
| CFD turnaround for industry | Hours | Days to weeks |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** In (b) part 2, students often **confuse RANS and LES filter operations** — the RANS operator is a **time-averaging** operator producing $\langle u\rangle$, whereas the LES filter is a **spatial low-pass** operator producing $\bar{u}$. They are **not** mathematically equivalent.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density revision checklist — must-know bullet points for KTU ESE on LES:**

- **LES is a spatial-filtering technique**, NOT a time-averaging technique like RANS.
- The **single governing equation** is the **filtered Navier–Stokes** equation with an additional $-\partial \tau_{ij}/\partial x_j$ term on the RHS.
- The **SGS stress tensor** is $\tau_{ij} = \overline{u_i u_j} - \bar{u}_i \bar{u}_j$ — this is what every SGS model tries to close.
- The **decomposition** is $\tau_{ij} = L_{ij} + C_{ij} + R_{ij}$ — Leonard, Cross, Reynolds sub-stress terms.
- The **Smagorinsky model** is the simplest closure: $\nu_t = (C_s \Delta)^2 \vert \bar{S}\vert$ with $C_s \approx 0.1\text{–}0.18$.
- The **Dynamic Smagorinsky** model uses the **Germano identity** to make $C_s$ a *function of space and time*, computed as $C_s^2 = -\langle L_{ij} M_{ij}\rangle / \langle M_{ij}^2\rangle$.
- **Filter width** in a finite-volume code: $\Delta = (\Delta x \Delta y \Delta z)^{1/3}$.
- **LES cutoff** must lie in the **inertial range**: $\eta \ll \Delta \ll L$.
- **Wall-modelled LES (WMLES)** uses the **log-law** $u^+ = (1/\kappa)\ln y^+ + B$ to avoid refining the boundary-layer mesh.
- **DNS > LES > RANS** in physical fidelity, but **RANS < LES < DNS** in computational cost.
- For **marine applications**, LES is critical for **propeller-hull wake interaction, cavitation, and vortex-induced vibration**.
- **WALE model** is a popular modern alternative to Smagorinsky that **naturally vanishes at walls** without explicit damping.
- **Implicit LES (ILES)** uses the **numerical dissipation of the scheme** (e.g., 2nd-order upwind with adaptive flux limiting) as the SGS model, with no explicit $\nu_t$ term.
- **Test filter** in dynamic LES is typically at $\hat{\Delta} = 2\Delta$.
- **Germano identity** is **exact**; only the subsequent *substitution* of the Smagorinsky form introduces modelling error.
- For KTU numerical problems, remember: **filter width** uses cubic-root formula and **$\nu_{\text{eff}} = \nu + \nu_t$**.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic — "F-L-S-D-W" for the LES foundation blocks:**
> **F**iltering → **L**eonard stress → **S**magorinsky (constant $C_s$) → **D**ynamic (variable $C_s$) → **W**all modelling (log-law).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Direct Numerical Simulation (DNS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST635 - COMPUTATIONAL FLUID DYNAMICS IN MARINE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Direct Numerical Simulation (DNS)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — DIRECT NUMERICAL SIMULATION (DNS)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Direct Numerical Simulation (DNS)** is a branch of Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) in which the **complete Navier–Stokes equations** are solved numerically, **without resorting to any turbulence model**, sub-grid closure, or averaged formulation. The grid and time step are chosen fine enough to **resolve every relevant length and time scale** of the turbulent flow, from the largest energy-containing eddies all the way down to the smallest **Kolmogorov dissipative scales**.

In DNS, the continuity equation and the full, time-dependent, three-dimensional Navier–Stokes momentum equations are integrated forward in time:

$$\frac{\partial u_i}{\partial t} + u_j \frac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x_i} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 u_i}{\partial x_j \partial x_j} + f_i$$

The entire spectrum of turbulent fluctuations, $u'(\vec{x},t)$, is explicitly computed as part of the solution — nothing is modelled.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** For the course *Computational Fluid Dynamics in Marine Technology*, DNS is treated as the **gold-standard reference method** against which all engineering turbulence models (RANS, LES, DES) are validated, especially in canonical marine flows such as boundary layers over ship hulls, propulsive jets, and wave–structure interaction problems.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are observing a **shoal of a million fish** moving in the ocean.

- **RANS** is like a marine biologist who only records the *average heading* and *average speed* of the shoal every hour. The motion of every individual fish is hidden.
- **LES** is like watching the shoal from a helicopter. You see the large swirling clusters of fish (big eddies), but the small darting motions of individual fish inside each cluster are smeared out by a "model" you assume.
- **DNS** is like following the shoal with an underwater drone and a high-speed camera that **tracks every single fish, every single second**. You see the tiniest flick of every tail, the smallest vortex shed by a single fin — and nothing is assumed or guessed.

That is exactly what DNS does in CFD: it captures **every eddy**, from ship-scale (metres) down to millimetre or smaller scales, without inventing any artificial "average" behaviour.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Metrics

The following quantities govern the feasibility of a DNS calculation and must be committed to memory:

- **Reynolds number** $Re_L = \dfrac{U L}{\nu}$ — the governing non-dimensional parameter that sets the **scale separation** between large and small eddies.
- **Kolmogorov length scale** $\eta = \left(\dfrac{\nu^{3}}{\varepsilon}\right)^{1/4}$ — the size of the smallest eddies.
- **Kolmogorov time scale** $\tau_{\eta} = \left(\dfrac{\nu}{\varepsilon}\right)^{1/2}$ — the lifetime of the smallest eddies.
- **Kolmogorov velocity scale** $u_{\eta} = \left(\nu \varepsilon\right)^{1/4}$ — the velocity fluctuation of the smallest eddies.
- **Energy dissipation rate per unit mass** $\varepsilon$ — the rate at which turbulent kinetic energy cascades from large to small scales.
- **Kinematic viscosity of seawater** $\nu \approx 1.0 \times 10^{-6}\ \mathrm{m^2/s}$ (at 20 °C).

> [!TIP]
> **Memory hook for exams:** the three Kolmogorov scales are often memorised as the *η – τ – u* triple, linked through the dissipation rate $\varepsilon$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Philosophy of DNS

DNS rests on a single, uncompromising idea: **solve the Navier–Stokes equations exactly (numerically) and let turbulence emerge from the solution itself.** There is no:

- Reynolds decomposition into mean + fluctuation;
- turbulence model such as $k$–$\varepsilon$, $k$–$\omega$, or Spalart–Allmaras;
- wall function;
- sub-grid scale (SGS) model such as Smagorinsky;
- statistical assumption about the turbulence structure.

The price for this purity is **enormous computational cost**, because the grid must be fine enough to resolve the smallest eddies and the time step small enough to capture the fastest fluctuations.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Performing a DNS

1. **Identify the largest length scale** $L$ (e.g., ship hull length, propeller diameter, boundary-layer thickness) and the characteristic velocity $U$.
2. **Compute the global Reynolds number** $Re_L = UL/\nu$.
3. **Estimate the dissipation rate** $\varepsilon$. For free shear flows and fully developed turbulence, the classical estimate is $\varepsilon \sim U^{3}/L$. For boundary layers it is $\varepsilon \sim u_{\tau}^{3}/\kappa y$ near the wall.
4. **Compute the Kolmogorov scales** $\eta$, $\tau_{\eta}$, $u_{\eta}$ from the dissipation rate.
5. **Choose the grid spacing** $\Delta x \leq \eta$ (and usually $\Delta x \approx 2\eta$ in the bulk and $\Delta x^{+} \approx 1$ in wall units for wall-bounded flows).
6. **Choose the time step** $\Delta t$ from the **CFL (Courant–Friedrichs–Lewy) condition** for the smallest eddies.
7. **Integrate the Navier–Stokes equations** in time using a stable, accurate, and preferably high-order scheme (e.g., spectral, pseudo-spectral, high-order finite difference, or discontinuous Galerkin).
8. **Store and post-process** the full time-resolved 3-D field for analysis — velocity, pressure, vorticity, and all relevant turbulent statistics.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Governing equations | Full, unsteady 3-D Navier–Stokes + continuity | — No averaging, no modelling |
| 2 | Reynolds number | $Re_L = \dfrac{U L}{\nu}$ | Dimensionless |
| 3 | Integral (large-eddy) length scale | $L$ (input geometry) | metres |
| 4 | Taylor micro-scale | $\lambda = \sqrt{15 \nu u'^2 / \varepsilon}$ | metres |
| 5 | Kolmogorov length scale | $\eta = (\nu^{3}/\varepsilon)^{1/4}$ | metres |
| 6 | Kolmogorov time scale | $\tau_{\eta} = (\nu/\varepsilon)^{1/2}$ | seconds |
| 7 | Kolmogorov velocity scale | $u_{\eta} = (\nu \varepsilon)^{1/4}$ | m/s |
| 8 | Scale ratio $L/\eta$ | $\sim Re_L^{3/4}$ | Number of decades of eddies |
| 9 | Required grid points (3-D, isotropic) | $N \sim Re_L^{9/4}$ | Total cells |
| 10 | Required time steps | $M \sim Re_L^{3/4}$ | Per integral time $L/U$ |
| 11 | Computational operations | $O \sim Re_L^{3}$ (often quoted $Re^3$ to $Re^4$ depending on algorithm) | FLOPs per integral time |
| 12 | Memory requirement | $\sim Re_L^{9/4}$ words | Storage scaling |
| 13 | Grid spacing (bulk) | $\Delta x \le 2\eta$ | metres |
| 14 | Wall-normal grid (wall units) | $\Delta y^+ \le 1$ | $y^+ = y u_{\tau}/\nu$ |
| 15 | Time step (CFL) | $\Delta t \le \dfrac{\Delta x}{U + c}$ (advective CFL) | seconds |
| 16 | Dissipation estimate (free flow) | $\varepsilon \sim U^{3}/L$ | $\mathrm{m^2/s^3}$ |
| 17 | Dissipation estimate (boundary layer) | $\varepsilon \sim u_{\tau}^{3}/(\kappa y)$ | $\mathrm{m^2/s^3}$ |
| 18 | Friction velocity | $u_{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_w / \rho}$ | m/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the formula table above, the symbol $\sim$ means "scales as". For example, $N \sim Re^{9/4}$ means that if you double $Re$, the required number of grid cells grows by a factor of $2^{9/4} \approx 4.76$.

### 2.4 Why DNS is Computationally Demanding — The "Why" Explained

In a turbulent flow at $Re_L = 10^{5}$:

- Scale separation: $L/\eta \sim Re^{3/4} \approx 10^{3.75} \approx 5600$.
- Grid points: $N \sim Re^{9/4} \approx 5 \times 10^{9}$ — that is **5 billion cells** even in a cube.
- Time steps: $M \sim Re^{3/4} \approx 5600$ per large-eddy turnover.
- For a marine application such as the turbulent boundary layer along a 100 m ship hull at cruising speed, $Re_L$ easily exceeds $10^{8}$–$10^{9}$, making DNS **physically impossible** on current hardware.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering reality for marine flows:** Full DNS is therefore used only for **fundamental, canonical, low-$Re$ problems** (channel flow, pipe flow, cylinder wake at $Re \le 10^{4}$, laminar separation bubbles) or for generating **reference data** to validate RANS/LES models used on full ship geometries.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Marine Engineering

- **Validation of RANS / LES models** used in ship-hydrodynamic codes (e.g., CFD around DTMB 5415, KCS, KVLCC2 hull forms).
- **Investigation of fundamental turbulent boundary layer physics** over flat plates at sea-state relevant Reynolds numbers.
- **Propeller tip-vortex cavitation and noise** studies at low Reynolds numbers where every eddy must be resolved.
- **Wave–boundary-layer interaction** in the air–sea interface, where small-scale turbulence controls air–sea momentum and gas exchange.
- **Benchmarking of high-order numerical schemes** (spectral, discontinuous Galerkin) intended for next-generation marine CFD solvers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Kolmogorov Length Scale $\eta$

The derivation begins with the **Kolmogorov hypothesis (K41)**: at sufficiently high Reynolds number, the small-scale turbulent motion is **statistically universal** and is uniquely determined by the kinematic viscosity $\nu$ and the mean energy dissipation rate per unit mass $\varepsilon$.

**Step 1 — Dimensional argument.**
The only physical quantities available to characterise the smallest eddies are $\nu$ and $\varepsilon$.

- $\nu$ has dimensions $[L^{2} T^{-1}]$.
- $\varepsilon$ has dimensions $[L^{2} T^{-3}]$.

**Step 2 — Form a length scale.**
Construct a length scale $\eta$ as a product of powers of $\nu$ and $\varepsilon$:

$$\eta \sim \nu^{a}\, \varepsilon^{b}$$

Match dimensions:

$$[L] = [L^{2} T^{-1}]^{a}\,[L^{2} T^{-3}]^{b} = [L^{2a+2b}\, T^{-a-3b}]$$

This gives two equations:

$$2a + 2b = 1 \quad\text{(length exponent)}$$
$$-a - 3b = 0 \quad\text{(time exponent)}$$

**Step 3 — Solve the system.**
From the time equation: $a = -3b$. Substitute into the length equation:

$$2(-3b) + 2b = 1 \;\Rightarrow\; -6b + 2b = 1 \;\Rightarrow\; -4b = 1 \;\Rightarrow\; b = -\tfrac{1}{4}$$

Then $a = -3b = -3(-\tfrac{1}{4}) = \tfrac{3}{4}$.

**Step 4 — Substitute back.**

$$\eta \sim \nu^{3/4}\, \varepsilon^{-1/4} = \left(\frac{\nu^{3}}{\varepsilon}\right)^{1/4}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the canonical Kolmogorov length scale. The same dimensional logic applied to time and velocity yields $\tau_{\eta} = (\nu/\varepsilon)^{1/2}$ and $u_{\eta} = (\nu \varepsilon)^{1/4}$ respectively.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Scale Ratio $L/\eta$

Assume a free shear flow of size $L$ and velocity $U$ at high $Re$. By the well-known turbulent scaling,

$$\varepsilon \sim \frac{U^{3}}{L}$$

Now form the ratio:

$$\frac{L}{\eta} = \frac{L}{(\nu^{3}/\varepsilon)^{1/4}} = \frac{L}{(\nu^{3} L / U^{3})^{1/4}} = \frac{L \cdot U^{3/4}}{\nu^{3/4}\, L^{1/4}} = \frac{L^{3/4} U^{3/4}}{\nu^{3/4}}$$

Re-writing using the Reynolds number $Re = UL/\nu$, i.e. $\nu = UL/Re$:

$$\frac{L}{\eta} = \left(\frac{UL}{\nu}\right)^{3/4} = Re_L^{3/4}$$

Hence the **dynamic range** of length scales that must be resolved in DNS is:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{L}{\eta} \sim Re_L^{3/4}\;}$$

### 3.3 Derivation of the Grid-Point Requirement

If we choose a uniform cubic grid of spacing $\Delta x \sim \eta$ spanning a domain of side $L$, then the number of cells along one direction is:

$$n \sim \frac{L}{\eta} \sim Re_L^{3/4}$$

For a 3-D box the total number of cells is:

$$N \sim n^{3} \sim \left(Re_L^{3/4}\right)^{3} = Re_L^{9/4}$$

Hence:

$$\boxed{\;N_{3D} \sim Re_L^{9/4}\;}$$

### 3.4 Derivation of the Time-Step Requirement

The smallest eddies evolve on the Kolmogorov time scale $\tau_{\eta}$. To resolve them, the time step must satisfy:

$$\Delta t \le \tau_{\eta} = \left(\frac{\nu}{\varepsilon}\right)^{1/2} \sim \left(\frac{\nu L}{U^{3}}\right)^{1/2} = \frac{L}{U} \, Re_L^{-1/2}$$

The integral (large-eddy turnover) time is $T = L/U$. The number of time steps required to advance through one such integral time is:

$$M = \frac{T}{\Delta t} \sim \frac{L/U}{L/(U\,Re^{1/2})} = Re_L^{1/2}$$

Combining grid and time-step requirements, the total number of operations scales as:

$$O \sim N \cdot M \sim Re_L^{9/4} \cdot Re_L^{1/2} = Re_L^{11/4}$$

The classical engineering rule of thumb (and the one most commonly adopted in the KTU 2024 syllabus and Pope's textbook) is:

$$\boxed{\;O_{DNS} \sim Re_L^{3}\;}$$

The numerical factor depends on the algorithm (spectral methods push the constant down by a large factor; finite-difference methods need more operations), but the $Re^{3}$ scaling is the canonical order-of-magnitude statement.

### 3.5 Worked Example — DNS of Channel Flow at $Re_\tau = 590$

This is a classical benchmark used heavily in marine hydrodynamics research. Let $u_\tau$ be the friction velocity and $\delta$ the channel half-height.

Given:
- $Re_\tau = u_\tau \delta / \nu = 590$.
- Domain size: $L_x \times L_y \times L_z = 2\pi\delta \times 2\delta \times \pi\delta$.

**Step 1 — Required grid resolution in wall units.**

The conventional resolution is $\Delta x^+ \approx 12$, $\Delta y^+ \approx 0.05$ near the wall to $\Delta y^+ \approx 8$ in the centre, and $\Delta z^+ \approx 6$.

Number of streamwise points:

$$N_x = \frac{L_x}{\Delta x} = \frac{2\pi\delta \times u_\tau/\nu}{12} = \frac{2\pi \times 590}{12} \approx 308$$

Number of wall-normal points (typical): $N_y \approx 256$.

Number of spanwise points:

$$N_z = \frac{\pi \times 590}{6} \approx 309$$

**Step 2 — Total number of grid points.**

$$N = N_x N_y N_z \approx 308 \times 256 \times 309 \approx 2.4 \times 10^{7}$$

So roughly **24 million grid points** are needed — feasible on modern supercomputers.

**Step 3 — Time step.**

$\Delta t^+ = \Delta t \, u_\tau^{2}/\nu \approx 0.05$, hence $\Delta t \approx 0.05 \nu / u_\tau^{2}$. With $u_\tau/\nu = 590/\delta$, the time step in physical units is of order $10^{-4}$ s for $\delta = 1$ m, requiring millions of time steps to capture the large-eddy turnover $T \sim \delta/u_\tau$.

**Step 4 — Estimate of computational cost.**

Assuming $\sim 100$ floating-point operations per grid point per time step, the total work is:

$$O \approx 100 \times N \times M \approx 100 \times 2.4 \times 10^{7} \times 5 \times 10^{5} \approx 10^{15}\ \text{FLOPs}$$

This is on the **peta-flop scale** — typical of a single workstation-class simulation using spectral or high-order finite-difference methods.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 DNS Workflow Flowchart

The diagram below maps the complete workflow of a Direct Numerical Simulation, from problem specification to turbulent statistics extraction.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Problem Definition: Geometry, U, L, fluid] --> B[Compute Re_L = UL / nu]
    B --> C[Estimate dissipation rate epsilon]
    C --> D[Compute Kolmogorov scales: eta, tau_eta, u_eta]
    D --> E[Design Computational Grid: Delta x <= eta]
    E --> F[Choose Time Step: CFL condition using tau_eta]
    F --> G[Discretise Navier-Stokes: spectral / high-order FDM / DG]
    G --> H[Integrate in Time: RK3 / RK4 / Crank-Nicolson]
    H --> I[Store Full 3D Time-Resolved Field]
    I --> J[Compute Statistics: mean, RMS, Reynolds stresses]
    J --> K[Validate against reference data / experiments]
    K --> L{Validation OK?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Use as benchmark for RANS and LES]
    L -- No --> G
```

### 4.2 Comparison of Turbulence Modelling Approaches

The block diagram below positions DNS relative to other turbulence modelling strategies. Notice that DNS sits at the extreme end of "physical fidelity" and is the only method that does **not** invoke any closure model.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[NS Equations] --> B{Which Scales Resolved?}
    B -- All scales -- C[DNS: No Model]
    B -- Large scales -- D[LES with SGS model]
    B -- None, only mean -- E[RANS with closure]
    subgraph Comparison
    C --> C1[Fidelity: Highest]
    C --> C2[Cost: Prohibitive for ship-scale Re]
    D --> D1[Fidelity: High]
    D --> D2[Cost: Moderate]
    E --> E1[Fidelity: Engineering]
    E --> E2[Cost: Lowest]
    end
```

### 4.3 Eddy Cascade View of DNS

DNS resolves the entire **Richardson–Kolmogorov energy cascade**: kinetic energy is injected at the integral scale $L$, cascades through the inertial range, and is dissipated at the Kolmogorov scale $\eta$. The diagram below shows the spectral and physical-space interpretation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Integral Scale L] -- injection --> B[Inertial Range k^-5/3]
    B -- cascade --> C[Kolmogorov Scale eta]
    C -- dissipation --> D[Heat via nu]
    A -->|u ~ U| A
    B -->|u ~ epsilon^1/3 k^-1/3| B
    C -->|u_eta| C
```

### 4.4 Marine Engineering Application Map

The following block diagram shows where DNS data are typically used in the marine CFD workflow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[DNS of canonical marine flow: flat plate, channel, cylinder] --> B[Reference database]
    B --> C[Calibrate RANS models: k-omega SST, etc]
    B --> D[Calibrate LES SGS: Smagorinsky, WALE]
    C --> E[Full-ship RANS: hull form optimisation]
    D --> F[Full-ship LES: cavitation, manoeuvring]
    E --> G[Resistance, powering, seakeeping]
    F --> G
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, PENST635]**
*Define Direct Numerical Simulation (DNS). State any two reasons why DNS is rarely used for full-scale ship hydrodynamics problems.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

DNS is a CFD approach in which the full, unsteady, three-dimensional Navier–Stokes equations are solved numerically on a grid fine enough to resolve **all** turbulent length and time scales — from the integral scale $L$ down to the Kolmogorov scale $\eta$ — **without** using any turbulence closure model.

Two reasons why DNS is rarely used in full-scale ship hydrodynamics:

1. **Prohibitive computational cost** — for a full-scale ship at $Re_L \sim 10^{9}$, the number of grid cells scales as $N \sim Re^{9/4} \sim 10^{20}$, far beyond the memory and FLOP capacity of any existing supercomputer.
2. **Lack of suitable boundary conditions for complex hull geometries** — the finest scales must be resolved right down to the hull surface, requiring $\Delta y^+ \le 1$, which combined with the large ship length yields an unmanageable number of wall-normal cells.

*(1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for the first reason, 1 mark for the second reason.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, PENST635]**
*State the expressions for the Kolmogorov length, time, and velocity scales. What is the physical significance of each?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three Kolmogorov scales are:

$$\eta = \left(\frac{\nu^{3}}{\varepsilon}\right)^{1/4}, \qquad \tau_{\eta} = \left(\frac{\nu}{\varepsilon}\right)^{1/2}, \qquad u_{\eta} = \left(\nu \varepsilon\right)^{1/4}$$

**Physical significance:**

- **$\eta$ — Kolmogorov length scale:** represents the size of the **smallest eddies** in the flow, at which viscous dissipation becomes dominant. In DNS, the grid spacing must satisfy $\Delta x \le \eta$ to resolve all dissipative structures.
- **$\tau_{\eta}$ — Kolmogorov time scale:** represents the **lifetime** of the smallest eddies. The integration time step must be a small fraction of $\tau_{\eta}$ to capture their evolution.
- **$u_{\eta}$ — Kolmogorov velocity scale:** represents the **velocity fluctuation** of the smallest eddies. It sets the lower bound on the dynamic range of velocity fluctuations that must be represented in a DNS.

*(1 mark for the three expressions, 1 mark for $\eta$, 1 mark for the remaining two scales.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall**
> Examiners frequently deduct 2–3 marks when students (a) confuse DNS with LES and claim DNS uses a sub-grid model, (b) forget to write the **scaling exponent** on $Re$ (writing $N \sim Re^{9/4}$ instead of just $N \sim Re$), and (c) give numerical values without showing the **dimensional / scaling derivation**. Always show: assumption $\rightarrow$ dimensional analysis $\rightarrow$ final scaling. Avoid writing "DNS solves RANS equations" — that is a guaranteed zero for that part.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Full Derivation of DNS Resolution and Cost Scaling

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PENST635, Module 4 Choice A]**

**(a)** Starting from the Kolmogorov hypotheses and the dimensional definition of the dissipation rate $\varepsilon$, derive the Kolmogorov length, time, and velocity scales in terms of $\nu$ and $\varepsilon$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Show, by dimensional and order-of-magnitude arguments, that the number of grid points required in a 3-D DNS scales as $N \sim Re_L^{9/4}$ and the number of time steps per integral time as $M \sim Re_L^{1/2}$. Hence estimate the number of grid points needed for a DNS of turbulent flow past a 5 m model ship hull at 5 m/s in seawater, taking $\nu = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}\ \mathrm{m^2/s}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of the Kolmogorov scales (7 marks):**

The Kolmogorov hypothesis (K41) states that at sufficiently high Reynolds number, the small-scale turbulent motion depends only on $\nu$ and $\varepsilon$.

*Length scale:* form $\eta \sim \nu^{a}\varepsilon^{b}$. Equating dimensions $[L^{2}T^{-1}]^{a}[L^{2}T^{-3}]^{b} = [L]$ gives $2a+2b=1$ and $-a-3b=0$, hence $a=3/4$, $b=-1/4$, and:

$$\eta = \left(\frac{\nu^{3}}{\varepsilon}\right)^{1/4}$$

*Time scale:* form $\tau_{\eta} \sim \nu^{a}\varepsilon^{b}$ with dimension $[T]$. This gives $2a+2b=0$ and $-a-3b=1$, hence $a=1/2$, $b=-1/2$, and:

$$\tau_{\eta} = \left(\frac{\nu}{\varepsilon}\right)^{1/2}$$

*Velocity scale:* form $u_{\eta} \sim \nu^{a}\varepsilon^{b}$ with dimension $[LT^{-1}]$. This gives $2a+2b=1$ and $-a-3b=-1$, hence $a=1/4$, $b=1/4$, and:

$$u_{\eta} = (\nu \varepsilon)^{1/4}$$

These are the three Kolmogorov microscales that define the smallest eddies to be resolved in DNS. *(Full marks: 2 for length, 2 for time, 2 for velocity, 1 for the K41 assumption.)*

**(b) Grid and time-step scaling (7 marks):**

For a free shear flow of size $L$ and velocity $U$, the dissipation rate scales as $\varepsilon \sim U^{3}/L$.

The ratio of large to small length scales is:

$$\frac{L}{\eta} = \frac{L}{(\nu^{3}/\varepsilon)^{1/4}} = \frac{L \cdot U^{3/4}}{\nu^{3/4} L^{1/4}} = \left(\frac{UL}{\nu}\right)^{3/4} = Re_L^{3/4}$$

If the grid spacing in each direction is of order $\eta$, then the number of cells in one direction is $n \sim Re^{3/4}$, and the **3-D total** is:

$$N \sim \left(Re_L^{3/4}\right)^{3} = Re_L^{9/4}$$

The integral time is $T \sim L/U$. The CFL-limited time step is of order $\tau_{\eta} = (\nu/\varepsilon)^{1/2} \sim (L/U) Re^{-1/2}$. Hence the number of time steps per integral time is:

$$M = \frac{T}{\tau_{\eta}} \sim Re_L^{1/2}$$

*Numerical estimate for the model ship:*

- $L = 5$ m, $U = 5$ m/s, $\nu = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}\ \mathrm{m^2/s}$.
- $Re_L = \dfrac{U L}{\nu} = \dfrac{5 \times 5}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 2.5 \times 10^{7}$.
- $N \sim Re^{9/4} = (2.5 \times 10^{7})^{2.25} \approx 2.5^{2.25} \times 10^{15.75} \approx 8.5 \times 10^{16}$.

This is **about $8.5 \times 10^{16}$ cells** — clearly infeasible. Hence DNS is not used at full ship scale, but the scaling tells us why a 1:100 model at $1/100$ the Reynolds number is still only marginally within reach.

*(Marks: 2 for grid scaling, 2 for time scaling, 1 for $Re$ computation, 2 for the numerical estimate and physical interpretation.)*

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — DNS vs LES vs RANS, with Marine Application

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PENST635, Module 4 Choice B]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast Direct Numerical Simulation (DNS), Large-Eddy Simulation (LES), and Reynolds-Averaged Navier–Stokes (RANS) approaches in terms of (i) equations solved, (ii) turbulence modelling, (iii) grid requirement, and (iv) typical computational cost. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A research laboratory wishes to use DNS to study the **turbulent boundary layer developing along a flat plate** at free-stream velocity $U_\infty = 1\ \mathrm{m/s}$, with characteristic length $L = 0.1\ \mathrm{m}$ and water as the working fluid ($\nu = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}\ \mathrm{m^2/s}$). Estimate the Kolmogorov scales, the required number of grid points, the time step, and comment on the feasibility of this DNS. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison table (7 marks):**

| Aspect | DNS | LES | RANS |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Equations solved | Full, unsteady 3-D Navier–Stokes | Filtered, unsteady 3-D Navier–Stokes | Time-averaged (or ensemble-averaged) Navier–Stokes |
| (ii) Turbulence modelling | **None** — every scale resolved | Sub-grid scale (SGS) model for unresolved scales (e.g., Smagorinsky) | Full turbulence closure model (e.g., $k$–$\varepsilon$, $k$–$\omega$ SST) |
| (iii) Grid requirement | $\Delta x \le \eta$ — finest possible | $\Delta$ in inertial range | Coarse; wall functions often used |
| (iv) Cost | $\sim Re^{3}$ — prohibitive for ships | $\sim Re^{2}$ — feasible on clusters | $\sim Re^{1}$ — fast, industrial |

DNS is the **reference method** against which LES and RANS are validated; LES captures the energetic large scales and models the small ones; RANS captures only the mean field and models everything else.

*(1 mark each for the four aspects, 3 marks for quality of explanation.)*

**(b) Numerical DNS feasibility study (7 marks):**

Step 1 — Reynolds number:

$$Re_L = \frac{U_\infty L}{\nu} = \frac{1 \times 0.1}{10^{-6}} = 10^{5}$$

Step 2 — Estimate the dissipation rate. For a turbulent boundary layer we can use $\varepsilon \sim U^{3}/L$ as a rough estimate:

$$\varepsilon \sim \frac{1^{3}}{0.1} = 10\ \mathrm{m^{2}/s^{3}}$$

Step 3 — Kolmogorov microscales:

$$\eta = \left(\frac{\nu^{3}}{\varepsilon}\right)^{1/4} = \left(\frac{10^{-18}}{10}\right)^{1/4} = (10^{-19})^{1/4} \approx 1.78 \times 10^{-5}\ \mathrm{m} \approx 17.8\ \mu\mathrm{m}$$

$$\tau_{\eta} = \left(\frac{\nu}{\varepsilon}\right)^{1/2} = \left(\frac{10^{-6}}{10}\right)^{1/2} = 3.16 \times 10^{-4}\ \mathrm{s}$$

$$u_{\eta} = (\nu \varepsilon)^{1/4} = (10^{-6} \times 10)^{1/4} = (10^{-5})^{1/4} \approx 5.6 \times 10^{-2}\ \mathrm{m/s}$$

Step 4 — Grid points. Scale ratio $L/\eta = Re^{3/4} = (10^{5})^{0.75} \approx 1780$, so $N \sim 1780^{3} \approx 5.6 \times 10^{9}$ cells — about **5.6 billion grid cells**.

Step 5 — Time step. $M \sim Re^{1/2} = 316$ time steps per integral time $T = L/U = 0.1$ s, so $\Delta t \sim 0.1/316 \approx 3.2 \times 10^{-4}$ s, consistent with $\tau_{\eta}$.

Step 6 — Feasibility. Approximately $5.6 \times 10^{9}$ cells is **at the edge of modern supercomputer capability** (sub-tera-cell memory × time step integration). The DNS is therefore **technically possible on a leadership-class HPC facility**, but still infeasible on a typical workstation — illustrating why DNS is reserved for fundamental studies of small canonical marine flows, not full ship hulls.

*(Marks: 1 for $Re$, 2 for Kolmogorov scales, 2 for $N$ and $\Delta t$, 2 for feasibility comment.)*

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- DNS solves the **complete, time-dependent Navier–Stokes equations with no turbulence model at all**.
- DNS **must** resolve the **Kolmogorov microscales**: $\eta = (\nu^{3}/\varepsilon)^{1/4}$, $\tau_{\eta} = (\nu/\varepsilon)^{1/2}$, $u_{\eta} = (\nu\varepsilon)^{1/4}$.
- The dynamic range to be resolved is $L/\eta \sim Re_L^{3/4}$.
- 3-D grid-point requirement: $N \sim Re_L^{9/4}$.
- Time-step requirement per integral time: $M \sim Re_L^{1/2}$.
- Overall computational cost: $O \sim Re_L^{3}$ (canonical rule of thumb for KTU 2024).
- For wall-bounded flows, **wall resolution** must satisfy $\Delta y^{+} \le 1$ and $\Delta x^{+}, \Delta z^{+} \le O(10)$.
- The grid must satisfy a **CFL condition** based on the smallest eddy time scale.
- DNS is the **gold standard** for validating RANS and LES models in marine CFD.
- Full-scale ship $Re \sim 10^{8}$–$10^{9}$ ⇒ DNS is **infeasible**; DNS is restricted to canonical, low-to-moderate $Re$ marine flows.
- Common student errors: confusing DNS with LES (DNS has **no** SGS model), dropping the exponent when writing scaling laws, and using $Re = U L / \mu$ instead of $Re = U L / \nu$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
