> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST635 - COMPUTATIONAL FLUID DYNAMICS IN MARINE TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** A Minor CFD Project in Ship Hydrodynamics using any Commercial or Open-Source CFD Package ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **A Minor CFD Project in Ship Hydrodynamics** is a structured, end-to-end virtual experimentation exercise in which a student applies the **Navier–Stokes equations** (typically Reynolds-Averaged, i.e., **RANS**) over a discretised ship hull domain to predict resistance, wave pattern, sinkage, trim, and local flow features, using a commercial package (e.g., **ANSYS Fluent, Star-CCM+, NUMECA**) or an open-source package (e.g., **OpenFOAM, Code Saturne, SU2**). According to the KTU 2024 syllabus for **PENST635**, the minor project must demonstrate the complete CFD chain — **Geometry → Meshing → Set-up → Solution → Post-processing → Validation** — on a benchmark or self-chosen hull form. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: "The Wind Tunnel That Lives in Your Computer"** > Imagine you are an architect designing a sleek new ship. Before spending crores building a physical model and towing it through a 100-metre test basin, you build a *digital twin* of the basin on your laptop. You "blow" virtual water past the digital hull, and the computer tells you how much resistance the ship will face, how the bow wave will look, and whether the stern will squat. **A Minor CFD Project is exactly that — a virtual towing tank.** ### 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Marine Engineering - It is the **capstone synthesis** of Modules 1–4 (governing equations, discretisation, turbulence, free-surface). - It mirrors **industry practice** — almost all classification societies (DNV, Lloyd's, BV) now accept CFD-derived resistance as supplementary evidence. - It carries **direct employability value** for marine CFD roles at companies such as **TTS, Ulstein, Kongsberg, Siemens Energy, Mazagon Dock, Cochin Shipyard**. ### 1.4 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics | Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit | |---|---|---| | Density of seawater | $\rho$ | **1025 kg/m³** | | Kinematic viscosity (20 °C) | $\nu$ | **1.004 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s** | | Dynamic viscosity | $\mu$ | **1.002 × 10⁻³ Pa·s** | | Gravitational acceleration | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** | | Reference length (Lpp) | $L$ | **m** | | Ship speed | $U$ | **m/s** or **knots** | | Froude number | $F_n$ | $\dfrac{U}{\sqrt{gL}}$ (dimensionless) | | Reynolds number | $Re$ | $\dfrac{UL}{\nu}$ (dimensionless) | | Block coefficient | $C_B$ | **0.40 – 0.85** (typical merchant hulls) | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always quote the **Froude number** alongside the speed for any free-surface ship problem — it is the dominant similarity parameter for resistance. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Six Phases of a Minor Ship-CFD Project > [!IMPORTANT] > A KTU-evaluated minor project is judged on **methodology, not just results.** A "good" project with a wrong setup scores lower than a "mediocre" project with rigorous methodology. #### Phase 1 — Problem Definition & Case Selection - **Why first?** A clear engineering question (e.g., *"What is the calm-water resistance of the KCS at $F_n = 0.26$?"*) prevents the student from chasing meaningless contours. - **Good practice:** Use a **benchmark hull** (KCS, KVLCC2, DTMB 5415, Wigley hull) for validation; only use a custom hull once you can defend the choice. - **Deliverable:** A one-page **Project Charter** stating geometry, speed, $F_n$, $Re$, expected output, and software. #### Phase 2 — Geometry Preparation - **Hull forms** are typically supplied as **IGES/STEP** from a naval architect's lines plan, or built parametrically (e.g., in Rhino + **Orca3D**). - **Critical operations:** 1. **Hull closure** at the transom (for full forms) or use a **dummy stern plane**. 2. **Waterline definition** at design draft. 3. **Domain extraction** — create a rectangular (or half, exploiting symmetry) fluid domain around the hull. 4. **Boolean subtraction** — hull cuts out of the domain. - **Domain sizing rules (ITTC 7.5-03-01-02):** - **Upstream:** $\ge 1L$ ahead of the FP - **Downstream:** $\ge 2.5L$ behind the AP - **Lateral:** $\ge 1L$ from the centreline - **Vertical (bottom):** $\ge 0.5L$ below WL - **Vertical (top):** $\ge 0.5L$ above WL #### Phase 3 — Meshing (The Heart of CFD) - **Strategy choice:** - **Structured hex** mesh → best for simple Wigley-type hulls (use **ICEM CFD**, **Pointwise**). - **Unstructured polyhedral** mesh → preferred for full ship forms (use **Star-CCM+**, **ANSYS Meshing**, **snappyHexMesh**). - **Overset / Chimera** mesh → for self-propulsion with rotating propeller. - **Refinement zones:** 1. **Near-wall prism layer** — first cell height such that $y^+ \approx 30$–$60$ for $k$-$\omega$ SST with wall functions, or $y^+ \le 1$ for low-Re. 2. **Free-surface refinement** — cells of size $\le 0.02L$ around the WL ($\pm 0.1L$). 3. **Stern wake region** — finer cells to capture vortices. #### Phase 4 — Physics Set-up (Boundary Conditions & Models) This is the **conceptual core** of the project. - **Inlet (velocity-inlet):** $U = U_\infty$ uniform or logarithmic profile. - **Outlet (pressure-outlet):** $p = 0$ gauge, free-stream level. - **Top / bottom / sides:** Symmetry or slip wall (if half-domain) or far-field. - **Hull surface:** No-slip wall, smooth or with prescribed $k_s$ (roughness) for full-scale. - **Free surface (VOF):** Air + water with $\rho_{air}=1.225\,\text{kg/m}^3$ and $\rho_{water}=1025\,\text{kg/m}^3$. - **Turbulence model (recommended start):** $k$-$\omega$ **SST** (Menter 1994) — best compromise for adverse pressure gradients on a stern. - **Discretisation:** Second-order upwind for momentum, **PRESTO!** or second-order for pressure, **SIMPLE** or **Coupled** pressure–velocity coupling. - **Transient or steady?** - **Steady RANS** is the **mandatory starting point** for a minor project. - Move to **unsteady RANS (URANS)** or **DDES/IDDES** only if $F_n$ is high and you are studying wave breaking or vortex shedding. #### Phase 5 — Solution & Convergence - **Monitor:** Total resistance $R_T$, residuals, mass-flow imbalance, and free-surface elevation at fixed probes. - **Stop when:** Residuals $<10^{-5}$, $R_T$ plateaued over 500+ iterations, mass imbalance $<0.5\%$. - **Time-step (if transient):** $\Delta t \approx L/100U$ or smaller for free-surface accuracy (Courant number $C \le 1$ at the interface). #### Phase 6 — Post-Processing & Validation - **Force coefficients:** Total resistance $R_T$, frictional $R_F$, viscous pressure $R_{VP}$, wave $R_W$. - **Wave pattern:** Side-view and bird's-eye contour of water-air interface elevation $\eta(x,y)$. - **Sinkage & trim:** Integrate pressure over the wetted hull. - **Validation:** Compare $R_T$ and $\lambda / L$ (wave length) against **ITTC benchmark** data or published **SIMMAN 2008/2014** results. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Hot-Sheet** — reproduce this table verbatim in the answer booklet if asked to "list the formulae used". | # | Quantity | Formula | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Total resistance coefficient** | $C_T = \dfrac{R_T}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^2 S}$ | $S$ = wetted surface area | | 2 | **Frictional coefficient (ITTC-57)** | $C_F = \dfrac{0.075}{(\log Re - 2)^2}$ | Form drag: $C_{VP} = C_T - C_F$ | | 3 | **Wave-resistance coefficient** | $C_W = C_T - C_F(1+k)$ | $k$ = form factor (Prohaska method) | | 4 | **Frictional resistance** | $R_F = C_F \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^2 S$ | $S \approx 1.7 L \nabla^{0.5} + T/\nabla \cdot LBP$ for tankers | | 5 | **Form factor (Prohraska)** | $k = 0.125 + 0.175\,\tfrac{B}{T}\,(C_B - 0.6)$ | $B$=beam, $T$=draft | | 6 | **Reynolds number** | $Re = \dfrac{UL}{\nu}$ | Use model or full-scale $L$ | | 7 | **Froude number** | $F_n = \dfrac{U}{\sqrt{gL}}$ | Free-surface similarity | | 8 | **Effective power** | $P_E = R_T \cdot U$ | Watts | | 9 | **Viscous sublayer $y^+$** | $y^+ = \dfrac{u_\tau y}{\nu} = \dfrac{y}{\nu}\sqrt{\dfrac{\tau_w}{\rho}}$ | $u_\tau$ = friction velocity | | 10 | **Courant number** | $C = \dfrac{U \Delta t}{\Delta x}$ | $\le 1$ for explicit free-surface | ### 2.3 The Three RANS Equations (Syllabus Recall) The incompressible steady **RANS** equations for ship hydrodynamics are: $$\frac{\partial \bar{u}_i}{\partial x_i} = 0$$ $$\rho \bar{u}_j \frac{\partial \bar{u}_i}{\partial x_j} = -\frac{\partial \bar{p}}{\partial x_i} + \frac{\partial}{\partial x_j}\!\left[\mu\!\left(\frac{\partial \bar{u}_i}{\partial x_j} + \frac{\partial \bar{u}_j}{\partial x_i}\right) - \rho \overline{u_i' u_j'}\right] + \rho g_i$$ where the **Reynolds stress tensor** is closed by the **Boussinesq hypothesis**: $$-\rho \overline{u_i' u_j'} = \mu_t \left(\frac{\partial \bar{u}_i}{\partial x_j} + \frac{\partial \bar{u}_j}{\partial x_i}\right) - \tfrac{2}{3}\rho k \delta_{ij}$$ and $\mu_t$ is provided by the turbulence model (e.g., $k$-$\omega$ SST). ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Hull-form optimisation** at the design stage — cheaper than building 20 models. - **Hull-propeller interaction** (when extended to overset meshes). - **Slender-body vs full-form selection** for new ship contracts. - **Eco-design compliance** with **EEDI / EEXI / CII** by the IMO — CFD helps predict $C_W$ reduction through hull form. - **Damage stability** — flooding scenarios can be simulated with VOF. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: From Towing-Tank Resistance to CFD Force Coefficient > [!NOTE] > **Pedagogical goal:** Show the student the *exact* link between a real tank measurement ($R_T$ in Newtons) and a CFD-derived coefficient. **Step 1 — The towing-tank measurement.** A model is towed at constant speed $U$ in a towing tank. The dynamometer reads a force $R_T$ (Newtons). The wetted surface area of the model is measured as $S_m$ (m²). **Step 2 — Non-dimensionalise.** To compare the model with the ship, we divide force by dynamic pressure × area: $$C_T \equiv \frac{R_T}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^2 S}$$ The dynamic pressure term $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^2$ has units of Pascal, and $S$ has units of m², so the denominator is N — units match. **Step 3 — Split $C_T$ into physical components.** Following the **ITTC 1978 / 2014 procedure**, total resistance is split into frictional and residual (form + wave): $$R_T = R_F + R_R = R_F + (R_{VP} + R_W)$$ Dividing through by the same denominator: $$C_T = C_F + C_{VP} + C_W$$ **Step 4 — Express the form factor $k$.** Empirically, viscous-pressure resistance scales with frictional resistance through the **form factor $k$**: $$C_{VP} = k \cdot C_F$$ So: $$C_T = (1 + k) C_F + C_W$$ **Step 5 — Apply the ITTC-57 line.** For a turbulent flat plate at the same $Re$: $$C_F = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} Re - 2)^2}$$ **Step 6 — Extract the wave component.** Rearrange to isolate the wave-making part, which is what CFD primarily predicts: $$C_W = C_T - (1 + k) C_F$$ **Step 7 — Convert back to total resistance in Newtons.** For a full-scale ship with wetted area $S_s$: $$R_T = C_T \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^2 S_s$$ --- ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Setup | Decision Point | Option A (Beginner) | Option B (Advanced) | KTU-Project Recommendation | |---|---|---|---| | Hull | **Wigley** (analytic) | **KCS** (benchmark) | Start with **Wigley**, then upgrade | | Domain | **Half** (symmetry) | **Full** (no symmetry, captures heel) | Half is acceptable for minor project | | Mesh type | **Tetrahedral + prism** | **Polyhedral** or **structured hex** | Trimmed hex / polyhedral preferred | | $y^+$ target | **30–60** (wall functions) | **$\le 1$** (low-Re resolved) | Wall functions are sufficient | | Turbulence | **$k$-$\varepsilon$ standard** | **$k$-$\omega$ SST** or **$k$-$\omega$ SST with low-Re corr.** | **$k$-$\omega$ SST** strongly recommended | | Free surface | **Rigid-lid** (single phase) | **VOF** (two-phase) | Rigid-lid first; VOF if time permits | | Solver | **Steady** | **Transient with VOF** | Steady is acceptable for minor project | | Discretisation | First-order upwind | **Second-order upwind** | **Second-order** mandatory | | Code | **OpenFOAM** (free) | **Star-CCM+** (academic) | Either; OpenFOAM recommended for KTU lab access | --- ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** A **Wigley hull** of length $L = 3.0$ m, beam $B = 0.3$ m, draft $T = 0.1875$ m is towed at $U = 1.0$ m/s. CFD gives $C_T = 4.86 \times 10^{-3}$ at $Re = 2.99 \times 10^6$. Compute $R_T$, $R_F$, and $R_W$. (Take $k = 0.18$ from literature, $S \approx 1.5 \times \nabla^{1/3} \cdot L \cdot T \approx 0.97$ m², $\rho = 998$ kg/m³, $\nu = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s.) **Step 1 — Compute ITTC-57 frictional coefficient:** $$C_F = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10}(2.99\times 10^6) - 2)^2}$$ $$\log_{10}(2.99\times 10^6) = 6.476$$ $$C_F = \frac{0.075}{(6.476 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{20.02} = 3.747 \times 10^{-3}$$ **Step 2 — Compute $R_F$:** $$R_F = C_F \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^2 S = 3.747 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.5 \cdot 998 \cdot 1^2 \cdot 0.97$$ $$R_F = 3.747 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 484.03 = 1.813 \text{ N}$$ **Step 3 — Compute $R_T$:** $$R_T = C_T \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^2 S = 4.86 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 484.03 = 2.352 \text{ N}$$ **Step 4 — Compute $C_W$:** $$C_W = C_T - (1+k)C_F = 4.86\times 10^{-3} - 1.18 \cdot 3.747\times 10^{-3}$$ $$C_W = 4.86 \times 10^{-3} - 4.421 \times 10^{-3} = 4.39 \times 10^{-4}$$ **Step 5 — Compute $R_W$:** $$R_W = C_W \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^2 S = 4.39 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 484.03 = 0.2125 \text{ N}$$ **Step 6 — Sanity check:** $$R_F + R_W = 1.813 + 0.2125 = 2.025 \text{ N}$$ The small residual $\approx 0.33$ N is the **viscous pressure resistance $R_{VP} = k \cdot R_F$** which we already absorbed into $C_{VP} = kC_F$. This matches the ITTC decomposition. | Quantity | Value | % of $R_T$ | |---|---|---| | $R_F$ | 1.813 N | 77.1 % | | $R_{VP}$ | 0.326 N | 13.9 % | | $R_W$ | 0.213 N | 9.0 % | | $R_T$ | 2.352 N | 100 % | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 The CFD Project Workflow — Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A0[Project Charter:
Hull choice, Fn, Re, outputs] --> B0[Geometry: IGES or
parametric lines plan] B0 --> C0[Domain Extraction:
ITTC 1L/2.5L/1L/0.5L] C0 --> D0[Surface Cleanup:
Watertight STL, edges] D0 --> E0[Surface Mesh:
target size 0.01L] E0 --> F0[Volume Mesh:
Prism layer + polyhedral] F0 --> G0[Setup: BCs, k-omega SST,
VOF if free-surface] G0 --> H0[Solve: Steady RANS,
monitor R-T and residuals] H0 --> I0[Post-process: R-T,
wave pattern, sinkage] I0 --> J0[Validate vs ITTC /
literature benchmark] J0 --> K0[Report and
presentation] ``` ### 4.2 Domain Sizing & Boundary Zone Map ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph doma["Fluid Domain - Half Breadth View"] A1[Inlet Velocity
U=1.0 m/s] -->|1L upstream| B1[Hull Surface
No-slip wall] B1 -->|Bow region| C1[Wave Refinement
zone + or - 0.1L] C1 --> D1[Midship region] D1 --> E1[Stern wake
refinement zone] E1 --> F1[Outlet Pressure
p=0 gauge] A1 --- G1[Top far-field
0.5L above WL] G1 --- H1[Bottom far-field
0.5L below WL] H1 --- F1 end ``` ### 4.3 Free-Surface VOF Refinement Concept ```mermaid flowchart TD A2[Global Mesh
dx approx 0.05L] --> B2[Identify WL plane
y=0 at design draft] B2 --> C2[Refinement Box
plus or minus 0.1L vertical] C2 --> D2[Local Cell Size
dx less than 0.02L] D2 --> E2[Match air and water
mesh density at interface] E2 --> F2[2-phase VOF
alpha water and alpha air] ``` ### 4.4 Turbulence-Model Decision Tree for Hull Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A3[Is the flow strongly
separated at stern?] -->|No| B3[Use k-omega SST
wall functions] A3 -->|Yes| C3[Use k-omega SST
with low-Re correction] C3 -->|Still poor| D3[Upgrade to
DDES or IDDES] D3 -->|Captures unsteady
vortices| E3[Capture mean and
fluctuating loads] B3 -->|Baseline| F3[Compare with
ITTC 1978 benchmark] ``` ### 4.5 Convergence-Monitoring Dashboard ```mermaid flowchart LR A4[Iteration Loop] --> B4[Residuals
mass, momentum, k, omega] A4 --> C4[Total Resistance
R-T probe] A4 --> D4[Mass Inflow equals
Mass Outflow check] B4 --> E4{All below 1e-5
and stable?} C4 --> E4 D4 --> E4 E4 -->|Yes| F4[Converged Solution] E4 -->|No| G4[Adjust under-relaxation
or check mesh quality] G4 --> A4 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **[Q1] [KTU University Exam – Model Question, PENST635]** — *(3 marks)* *State the **ITTC recommended minimum domain dimensions** for a ship-CFD simulation in terms of ship length $L$. Why is each dimension specified?* **Model Answer:** | Direction | Min. Distance from Hull | Reason | |---|---|---| | Upstream of bow | $\ge 1L$ | Avoid inlet boundary affecting bow wave | | Downstream of stern | $\ge 2.5L$ | Stern wake and wave field must decay | | Lateral (from C/L) | $\ge 1L$ | Avoid blockage > 1 % (ITTC) | | Below keel | $\ge 0.5L$ | Bilge vortex development | | Above WL | $\ge 0.5L$ | Spray and wave crest region | Each dimension isolates the hull from artificial boundary effects, ensuring that the simulated resistance is purely due to hull–fluid interaction and not domain constraints. **(3 marks)** --- **[Q2] [KTU University Exam – Model Question, PENST635]** — *(3 marks)* *List the **six stages** of a minor CFD project in ship hydrodynamics and identify the **most critical stage** for the final accuracy of resistance prediction.* **Model Answer:** The six stages are: 1. Problem definition & case selection 2. Geometry preparation 3. **Meshing** (most critical — grid resolution, $y^+$, and free-surface refinement directly control $R_T$ accuracy) 4. Physics set-up (BCs, turbulence model, free-surface model) 5. Solution (solver settings, convergence) 6. Post-processing & validation The most critical stage is **Meshing** — an inadequate mesh leads to wrong forces, no matter how good the solver is. **(3 marks)** --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) --- #### ⭐ QUESTION A — *14 marks* [KTU University Exam – Model Question, PENST635] **A ship-CFD project is being planned for the KCS hull at $F_n = 0.26$ and $Re = 5.4 \times 10^6$ (model scale).** **(a)** Describe in detail the **geometry preparation, meshing strategy, and boundary conditions** you would specify for this project, justifying each choice. **— 7 marks** **(b)** A steady RANS solution yields a total resistance coefficient $C_T = 3.85 \times 10^{-3}$ at this speed. Given $S = 9.51$ m² (model), $\rho = 998$ kg/m³, $k = 0.18$, compute $R_T$, $R_F$, and $R_W$ in Newtons, and comment on whether the wave component is realistic for $F_n = 0.26$. **— 7 marks** --- ### 🅰️ Model Solution — Question A **Part (a) — 7 marks** **Geometry preparation (2 marks):** - Obtain the KCS hull as an **IGES** or **STEP** file from the **SIMMAN 2008** benchmark distribution. - Apply **hull-closure** to seal any open transom for a single-phase run, or model the transom if doing VOF. - Extract a **rectangular fluid domain** sized at $1L$ upstream, $2.5L$ downstream, $1L$ laterally, $0.5L$ vertically, per ITTC 7.5-03-01-02. - Subtract the hull from the domain using a Boolean operation, leaving a watertight fluid cavity. - **Justification:** The KCS has a fine forebody and a flat transom, demanding strict domain sizing to avoid contaminating the bow-wave and wake regions. **Meshing strategy (3 marks):** - Use a **polyhedral / trimmed-hex** mesh for a balance of accuracy and speed. - Generate a **prism layer** on the hull with **5–10 layers**, growth ratio 1.2, first-cell height targeting $y^+ \approx 50$ (i.e., $y \approx 5 \times 10^{-5}$ m at this $Re$). - Place a **free-surface refinement box** of $\pm 0.1L$ vertically around WL with cell size $\le 0.02L \approx 0.13$ m. - Add a **stern wake refinement** box of $0.5L \times 0.3L \times 0.3L$ behind the transom. - Target **2–5 million cells** for the half-domain — adequate for a minor project. - **Justification:** Polyhedral cells offer superior convergence behaviour, prism layers resolve the viscous sublayer, and free-surface refinement captures the sharp water–air interface in VOF. **Boundary conditions (2 marks):** - **Inlet** → velocity-inlet with $U = U_\infty$, turbulence intensity 1 %, $L_t = 0.07L$. - **Outlet** → pressure-outlet, $p = 0$ gauge, target mass flow specified. - **Hull** → no-slip smooth wall. - **Top, bottom, sides** → symmetry (if half-domain) or slip walls. - **Turbulence model:** $k$-$\omega$ **SST** (Menter 1994), to handle adverse pressure gradients on the run. - **Solver:** Simple algorithm, second-order upwind for momentum, PRESTO for pressure. - **Justification:** $k$-$\omega$ SST is the industry default for ship hydrodynamics; second-order schemes eliminate numerical diffusion; symmetry reduces compute time by half. --- **Part (b) — 7 marks** Given: $C_T = 3.85 \times 10^{-3}$, $S = 9.51$ m², $U = F_n \sqrt{gL}$. Assume $L = 7.278$ m (KCS model), so: $$U = 0.26 \cdot \sqrt{9.81 \cdot 7.278} = 0.26 \cdot 8.450 = 2.197 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 1 — Compute $R_T$:** $$R_T = C_T \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^2 S = 3.85 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.5 \cdot 998 \cdot (2.197)^2 \cdot 9.51$$ $$(2.197)^2 = 4.827$$ $$R_T = 3.85 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.5 \cdot 998 \cdot 4.827 \cdot 9.51$$ $$= 3.85 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 22{,}917 = 88.23 \text{ N}$$ **Step 2 — Compute $C_F$ (ITTC-57):** $$Re = \frac{U L}{\nu} = \frac{2.197 \cdot 7.278}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.599 \times 10^7$$ $$\log_{10}(1.599 \times 10^7) = 7.204$$ $$C_F = \frac{0.075}{(7.204 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{27.10} = 2.768 \times 10^{-3}$$ **Step 3 — Compute $R_F$:** $$R_F = 2.768 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.5 \cdot 998 \cdot 4.827 \cdot 9.51 = 63.43 \text{ N}$$ **Step 4 — Compute $C_W$ and $R_W$:** $$C_W = C_T - (1+k) C_F = 3.85 \times 10^{-3} - 1.18 \cdot 2.768 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$= 3.85 \times 10^{-3} - 3.266 \times 10^{-3} = 5.84 \times 10^{-4}$$ $$R_W = C_W \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^2 S = 5.84 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 22{,}917 = 13.38 \text{ N}$$ **Step 5 — Comment on physical realism:** | Component | Value | % of $R_T$ | |---|---|---| | $R_F$ | 63.43 N | 71.9 % | | $R_{VP}$ | 11.42 N | 12.9 % | | $R_W$ | 13.38 N | 15.2 % | | $R_T$ | 88.23 N | 100 % | The wave component is **15.2 % of $R_T$**, which is realistic for a $F_n = 0.26$ full-form container-ship hull. Literature (SIMMAN 2008) reports wave contributions in the 12–18 % range at this $F_n$, so the result is **physically consistent and acceptable**. --- #### ⭐ QUESTION B — Alternative Choice — *14 marks* [KTU University Exam – Model Question, PENST635] **A Wigley parabolic hull ($L = 3.0$ m, $B = 0.3$ m, $T = 0.1875$ m) is to be analysed using OpenFOAM at $U = 1.0$ m/s.** **(a)** Outline the complete **OpenFOAM case set-up** you would build — listing the **key directories** (`0/`, `constant/`, `system/`), the **essential files** in each, and the **solver choice** with justification. **— 7 marks** **(b)** After solving with `simpleFoam` (steady, $k$-$\omega$ SST), the resistance components are $R_F = 1.81$ N, $R_{VP} = 0.33$ N, $R_W = 0.21$ N. The ITTC correlation predicts $C_F = 3.747 \times 10^{-3}$. Compute the **form factor $k$** from the viscous-pressure component, the **percentage error** of the CFD-predicted $C_T$ versus the ITTC estimate, and recommend **two mesh improvements** to reduce the error. **— 7 marks** --- ### 🅱️ Model Solution — Question B **Part (a) — 7 marks** **OpenFOAM case layout (3 marks):** ``` wigley/ ├── 0/ │ ├── U (velocity field) │ ├── p (pressure field) │ ├── k (turbulent kinetic energy) │ ├── omega (specific dissipation rate) │ ├── nut (eddy viscosity) │ └── alpha.water (if VOF; for free-surface) ├── constant/ │ ├── transportProperties │ ├── turbulenceProperties │ ├── RASProperties (kOmegaSST) │ └── polyMesh/ (blockMeshDict, snappyHexMeshDict) └── system/ ├── controlDict ├── fvSchemes ├── fvSolution ├── blockMeshDict └── snappyHexMeshDict ``` **Solver choice (2 marks):** `simpleFoam` — steady-state, incompressible, SIMPLE algorithm. **Justification:** Steady RANS is the KTU-required starting point; incompressible because ship speeds produce $M \ll 0.3$; SIMPLE is robust for marine flows. For VOF, switch to `interFoam`. **Key file contents (2 marks):** - `0/U`: Inlet = fixedValue ($U = (1, 0, 0)$ m/s), outlet = zeroGradient, hull = noSlip. - `0/p`: Inlet = zeroGradient, outlet = fixedValue 0, hull = zeroGradient. - `constant/turbulenceProperties`: `RASModel kOmegaSST;`. - `system/fvSchemes`: `div(phi,U) Gauss linearUpwind grad(U);` (second order). - `system/fvSolution`: SIMPLE with `nNonOrthogonalCorrectors 2;`. **Justification summary:** OpenFOAM's file-based structure allows full control; `kOmegaSST` handles the adverse-pressure stern flow; second-order schemes minimise numerical diffusion. --- **Part (b) — 7 marks** Given: $R_F = 1.81$ N, $R_{VP} = 0.33$ N, $R_W = 0.21$ N, $C_F^{ITTC} = 3.747 \times 10^{-3}$, $S = 0.97$ m², $\rho = 998$ kg/m³, $U = 1.0$ m/s. **Step 1 — Compute dynamic pressure term:** $$q = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^2 S = 0.5 \cdot 998 \cdot 1^2 \cdot 0.97 = 484.03 \text{ N}$$ **Step 2 — Compute CFD $C_T$:** $$R_T = 1.81 + 0.33 + 0.21 = 2.35 \text{ N}$$ $$C_T^{CFD} = \frac{R_T}{q} = \frac{2.35}{484.03} = 4.855 \times 10^{-3}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the form factor $k$ from the viscous-pressure:** $$k = \frac{R_{VP}}{R_F} = \frac{0.33}{1.81} = 0.182$$ **Step 4 — Compute ITTC total coefficient:** $$C_T^{ITTC} = (1+k) C_F + \frac{R_W}{q} = 1.182 \cdot 3.747 \times 10^{-3} + \frac{0.21}{484.03}$$ $$= 4.429 \times 10^{-3} + 4.34 \times 10^{-4} = 4.863 \times 10^{-3}$$ **Step 5 — Compute percentage error:** $$\% \text{ error} = \frac{|C_T^{CFD} - C_T^{ITTC}|}{C_T^{ITTC}} \times 100 = \frac{|4.855 - 4.863| \times 10^{-3}}{4.863 \times 10^{-3}} \times 100$$ $$= \frac{0.008 \times 10^{-3}}{4.863 \times 10^{-3}} \times 100 = 0.165\,\%$$ **Step 6 — Comment:** The CFD result matches the ITTC estimate to within **0.17 %** — an excellent agreement for a minor project. **Two mesh improvements (2 marks):** 1. **Reduce $y^+$** from $\sim 50$ to $\sim 1$ (low-Re) by thinning the first prism layer to $1 \times 10^{-6}$ m — this resolves the viscous sublayer and improves $C_F$ accuracy. 2. **Refine the free-surface region** with VOF (or finer cells at WL) — Wigley has a strong bow wave; finer cells capture the wave system better, reducing $C_W$ error. **Bonus improvement:** Increase overall cell count to 5 M+ and run a **grid-independence study** with three meshes (coarse / medium / fine) using Richardson extrapolation. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Forgetting $y^+$ justification.** Simply stating "I used a fine mesh" loses marks. Always state the *first-cell height in metres* and the resulting $y^+$. > 2. **No ITTC domain citation.** A minor project without a domain-sizing justification is treated as methodologically weak. > 3. **Confusing $C_F$, $C_{VP}$, $C_W$ and $C_T$.** Memorise the decomposition: $C_T = C_F(1+k) + C_W$. > 4. **Reporting only one speed.** A good project shows at least **three Froude numbers** (e.g., $F_n = 0.20, 0.26, 0.32$). > 5. **No validation.** CFD without a literature/ITTC comparison is incomplete. > 6. **Mixing $\rho = 1000$ and $1025$.** Use **998 kg/m³ for freshwater** and **1025 kg/m³ for seawater**. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist** — read this the night before the exam. - ✅ A minor CFD project follows the chain: **Geometry → Mesh → Set-up → Solve → Post-process → Validate**. - ✅ Use the **ITTC domain** rule: $1L$ upstream, $2.5L$ downstream, $1L$ lateral, $0.5L$ vertical. - ✅ First cell height must give $y^+ \approx 30$–$60$ for wall functions, $y^+ \le 1$ for low-Re. - ✅ **$k$-$\omega$ SST** is the *default* turbulence model for ship hydrodynamics. - ✅ **VOF** with two phases is mandatory for free-surface; rigid-lid is acceptable for low $F_n$. - ✅ **Second-order upwind** discretisation for momentum is the KTU expectation. - ✅ The total resistance coefficient decomposes as: $$C_T = C_F(1 + k) + C_W$$ - ✅ The ITTC-57 line is: $$C_F = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} Re - 2)^2}$$ - ✅ Froude number $F_n = U/\sqrt{gL}$ is the key similarity parameter for free-surface flows. - ✅ Always validate $R_T$ and $C_T$ against **ITTC benchmark, SIMMAN, or published data**. - ✅ Common software: **OpenFOAM** (free), **Star-CCM+** and **ANSYS Fluent** (commercial/academic). - ✅ Grid-independence study using **Richardson extrapolation** is the gold-standard validation. - ✅ Report $R_T$ in Newtons, $C_T$ dimensionless, and wave pattern as side-view contours. - ✅ Blockage ratio must be **< 1 %** (ITTC) — justifies lateral domain of $1L$. - ✅ Project should include a **methodology section** (justifying every choice) and a **results section** (with comparison table). - ✅ **KCS, KVLCC2, DTMB 5415, Wigley** are the four canonical benchmark hulls to choose from.
